text:
Dr. Byoung G. Choh (Full)
Interviewee: Choh, Byoung G.
Dr. Byoung Choh immigrated to the United States in 1972, when he was 43 years old. He could come to the states after he passed the ECFMG exam, coming to work at a mental health hospital. Working as both a resident and intern in Cleveland, Ohio, he had learned to adjust to a new environment with his wife. Originally from North Korea, Choh had a rough time during the Korean War, escaping and getting separated from his family. Although he was able to get in contact with them, he was not able to see them ever again because of the corrupt government in North Korea. Choh despises the corruption in Korea and believes that it is best for him and his family to live in America, with freedom. In terms of conserving his Korean culture, Choh believes that it is very important to know how to speak one’s native language, which is why he encourages his grandchildren to do the same. As a Korean immigrant, he feels proud of his accomplishments and hard work and believes that his home is now America.
Keywords: United States; Korea; War; ECFMG examination; Resident; Intern; Cuyahoga Falls; Ohio; Wife; Seoul; Family; Children; Education; English language; North Korea; Money; School; Stepmother; Corruption; Communism

Dr. Byoung G. Choh (Edited)
Interviewee: Choh, Byoung G.
Legacy Project video of Dr. Byoung G. Choh of Cleveland, Ohio, interviewed by his daughter, Theresa Choh-Lee. Dr. Choh discusses how he was able to leave North Korea and escape to the South in 1950 and how no one else in his family was able to join him. He also remembers visiting his hometown in North Korea many years later and reflects on where home is for him.
Keywords: Family; North Korea; Hometown, Liberation; U.S. army; Korean army; United States; Ohio

Jung S. Choh (Full)
Interviewee: Choh, Jung S.
Mrs. Jungsook Choh was born in 1935 in Uhrae-Jin, Hamkyung-Namdo, which is now in North Korea. She recalls her life immediately after the Korean Independence from Japan and the subsequent events during the Korean War which separated her younger brother from the rest of the family.
Keywords: Uhrae-jin, Hamkyung-namdo, Korea; North Korea; Father; Mother; Brother; Middle School; Korean War; Seoul; Tempura business

Dr. Samuel Sang Gook Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Rocky
Dr. Samuel Sang Gook Lee came to New York in 1973, in hopes of beginning a new chapter of his life in the United States. Leaving his life behind in Korea, he shares his challenges of starting over through the same, repetitive process, from intern to resident, and how he came to change and adapt to the ways and cultures of America. Growing up in a poor family in Daegu, South Korea, Dr. Lee shares his stories of living through a period of war and how those wars helped to shape the man he is today. Moreover, he comes to express his true opinions on high expectations and the importance of having a strong, connected family. He holds great love for his children and his grandchildren and hopes that they know that God is always by their side.
Keywords: New York; Vietnam War; Korean War; Japanese Occupation; Second World War; God; Religion; Korea; Grandchildren; Doctor; Family; United States

Dr. Samuel Sang Gook Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Rocky
Dr. Samuel Sang Gook Lee immigrated to the United States in 1973. As with many physicians who immigrated to the US during the 1970s, he had to restart his medical career as an intern in New York.  In this conversation with his son, H. Rok Lee, Dr. Lee remembers the happiest and the saddest memories of his life in the United States.
Keywords: Physician; New York; Medical; Doctor; Father; Intern; United States; Grandchildren; Korea

Kwon Sook Young (Full)
Interviewee: Kwon, Sook Young
Ms. Kwon Sook Young, born and raised in Korea, came to New York in 1973. Expressing the pain and fear she felt while raising her children kids in an unfamiliar country, she comes to highlight many obstacles she had faced due to language barriers and her process of growing and adapting to her new surroundings. Working as a volunteer at the YWCA for over 20 years, she helped many young kids whose parents were too busy to watch over them. Moreover, as the wife of a soldier who fought in the Vietnam War, she expresses her hardships of raising her children alone amidst her fear for her husband's safety. As all of her kids are currently married to non-Koreans, she expresses her opinions on mixed races and her love for her grandchildren, who bring her great happiness. Always looking up to God, she hopes and prays for the best for her family and grandchildren. She hopes to share the beauty of the Korean culture someday and hopes to be remembered as a beautiful and kind grandma.
Keywords: Grandchildren; Korea; Vietnam; Children; Mother; God; Volunteer; Korean culture/language; Non-Korean; Mixed Races

Kwon Sook Young (Edited)
Interviewee: Kwon, Sook Young
Ms. Kwon Sook Young was interviewed by her daughter, Yoon Lee Perera, in New York in November 2012.  Ms. Kwon talks about her love for her first grandchild, her desire to take her grandchildren to visit Korea, and how she wants to be remembered by them.
Keywords: Grandmother; Grandchildren; Korea; Daughter: New York; Teenager; Family; Korean; Hope

Rev. Jung John Kwon (Full)
Interviewee: Kwon, Jung John
Jung John Kwon, a Korean American, immigrated to the United States in 1974 when he was 32. With his determination to pursue his graduate degree in the US, he enrolled at Kent State University, following Columbia University right after. As he shares his family’s journey, living in the United States, he reveals that America has become their new home and is where they feel most comfortable. 11 years later, Kwon became a United Methodist Minister, which he is continuously doing now. Talking about his thoughts on Korea, he believes that respecting his culture is crucial but also wishes Korea to become more open to diversity. He is unable to adjust to all the rapid changes of Korean society, which is why he claims to be most comfortable living in the US.
Keywords: United States; Korea; Graduate Degree; Kent State University; Columbia University; Wife; United Methodist Minister; Money; Korean culture; Children; Grandchildren; Home; English; Language; Korean American; Rituals; Pastor

Rev. Jung John Kwon (Edited)
Interviewee: Kwon, Jung John
The Reverend Jung John Kwon reflects on his journey in the United States. He was interviewed by his daughter Young-Yi Clinton, in New York. "I want them to remember that their grandparents came from a concrete place called Korea and they came to the US with unique culture and language."
Keywords: Korean American; Children; Wife; Graduates degree; Life; Korea; US; Culture; Language; Purpose; Meanings

Rev. Koonae Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Koonae
The Reverend Koonae Lee is the Senior Pastor of the United Methodist Church in Stratford, Connecticut. She was interviewed by her son, Mark Lee, in New York. Rev. Lee remembers the moments before she departed for the United States. Now, she considers herself a proud Korean American and encourages us to feel a sense of pride in our own Korean American identity.
Keywords: USA; Married; Woman; Gimpo Airport; Mom; Dream; Korean American; Heritage; Identity Crisis; Proud; American society

Rev. Koonae Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Koonae
The Reverend Koonae Lee immigrated to the United States at a young age with her husband, Sam Lee, starting their own family in such a truly new environment. As Koonae talks about her husband's multiple stories, she reveals how he overcame all his struggles and, in the end, turned everything back into a positive perspective. Her husband, being mixed between three cultures, Japanese, Korean, and American, talks about the discrimination he faced and how he always overcame those situations. Even though Koonae wishes her husband was in a better state, health-wise, she expresses the love and respect she has for her husband. Because of the pride they have in their racial identity, she believes that they were able to overcome discrimination and make many significant accomplishments to this day.
Keywords: U.S.; Sam; Husband; Fulbright Scholar; Korean American; Japan; Korea; Struggles; Discrimination; Seoul National University; Yonsei University; Language; Deacon; Lee Byeong Gi; Poem; Math; Physics; Harvard; Mother in law; Children; Surgery; Golf; Culture; Positive; Pride

Yong-Hee Silver (Full)
Interviewee: Silver, Yonghee
Jeon Yonghee came to New York in 1961, from Germany, where her father was the ambassador to the Federal Republic of West Germany. As she shares her story of adjusting to a new environment, she speaks of all the hardships she faced in society with the language, raical, and cultural barriers. Although she was devastated after the miscarriage of her first child, light has shone through her once again when her two sons, Julian and Adrian, were introduced to the world. Mrs. Jeon needed to sacrifice her dreams in order to raise her children, but she realized soon enough that her kids, in return, gave back for those sacrifices. Surviving in America as one of a kind, she identifies herself as solely a Korean and not a Korean American, despite the fact that she lived the majority of her life in America. As she gives an insight to the important life lessons she learned from her parents, husband, and teachers, she demonstrates her journey in becoming a confident and determined woman.
Keywords: Korea; Washington D.C.; Korean American immigrant; Japanese; Green Card; Mother; School; Fundraising; Son; Husband; Korean American Story

Yong-Hee Silver (Edited)
Interviewee: Silver, Yonghee
Legacy Project video of Yong-Hee Silver interviewed by her son, Adrian Silver in New York.  Ms. Silver is a daughter of a Korean diplomat, and their family immigrated to the US in 1961. She shares the stories of her parents and the struggles they faced, lessons she learned from her parents, as well as her own personal struggle to bear children and what her children now mean to her.
Keywords: New York; Daughter; Korean Diplomat; Struggles; Lessons; Parents; Children

Dr. Haeng Soon Park (Full)
Dr. Haeng Soon Park, born and raised in South Korea, came to the United States in 1960 to study abroad at Southwestern University in Tennessee. Starting a new life experience in a foreign country, Dr. Park faced great obstacles that have helped her become the woman she is today. She shares her experiences of fulfilling her duties of being a student and a mother of two sons. Looking up to her kids and her desire to become a good role model, she learned the importance of staying strong and not giving up. After moving back to Korea in 1983, Dr. Park began working as a professor in Chonnam University in Gwangju, where she had met many great people and students. Being one of the few women who had made great success in the field of pharmacy, she helps to highlight the fact that women are no different from men, and that they shouldn't hold back from doing what they love. After her retirement, she went on many missionaries to Nepal and other foreign countries, providing education to those who had limited access. Through the sharing of her experiences, working along with many foreign workers, she empathizes the hardships of language barriers and the process of adapting and finding ways to create relationships, even when communication is difficult. Moreover, as she holds great pride and love for the Korean culture, she expresses the importance of learning their language and about the beauty of their people.
Keywords: New York; Nepal; Korea; Women; Study Abroad; Biochemistry; God; Son; education; language; mother; missionaries; determination; hard work; communication; study abroad; Professor

Dr. Haeng Soon Park (Edited)
Dr. Haeng Soon Park, a professor of biochemistry, retired from a university in Korea and then went on to teach in Nepal. She shares the stories of her life and the lessons she learned with her son, Terry Kim in New York.
Keywords: Biochemistry; Professor; Korea; Nepal; Son; New York

Grace K. Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: K. Lee, Grace
Legacy Project video of Grace K. Lee of Minnesota, interviewed by her daughter, Marie Myong-Ok Lee, in New York City. She reflects on her decision to stay in the US after considering returning to Korea despite the difficulties she faced. "No matter what, we are going to survive."
Keywords: Life; Victor; Salary; Visa; Future; Korea; Fatherless; Money; Family; Children; Value; First-generation; United States

Grace K. Lee (Full)
Interviewee: K. Lee, Grace
Grace K. Lee, a Korean American, gives us a breakdown of her life, starting from being born in North Korea and eventually ending up in the United States, where she started a family of her own. She goes on to explain all the hardships she had gone through during her childhood, like being separated from her family from a young age and having to live through two tragic wars, World War II and the Korean War, from when she was young, her ultimate fairy-tale-like dream was to immigrate to the US, where everything seemed to be possible. Taking the chance she had to come to America along with her husband, she explains how she is very proud of all the accomplishments she was able to make with the strength given by her family. Although she regrets that she couldn't give her fullest attention to her children, she hopes that they will always have each other's backs and continue to support each other in any situation.
Keywords: North Korea; Education; South Korea; Brothers; Family; United States; College; Husband; Dream Discrimination; Discipline; Homesick; Sacrifice; Money; Children; Doctor; California; Goal; Masters Degree; Social Work; World War II; Korean War; Regret; Mother; Father; Together; Life; Unique

Myung Hee Jun (Full)
Interviewee: Jun, Myung Hee
Born as the fifth of seven children in Longjing, China Jun Myung Hee's life journey began in 1932. He fled to Seoul at the age of 11 in 1943 to escape communism, traversing the 38th parallel. In a harrowing incident, he nearly perished falling into a frozen river but resolved to survive in free land. Life in Korea during that time was marked by poverty and limited educational opportunities, leading him to work and struggle to attend school. He entered school late during his last year of middle school and the June 25th War lead to a seven-year military service stint, interrupted by a hospitalization due to injury. Reflecting on his upbringing, he recalls the difficulties faced in China under Japanese occupation, where his family struggled despite his grandfather's profession as a doctor. The family's political involvement, particularly through his mother's activism against discrimination, led to her imprisonment during the March 1 uprising. Despite their hardships, his parents played significant roles in aiding the Korean Independence movement in China, resulting in ongoing financial support from the Korean Government. However, his grandfather met a tragic end under Mao's regime, facing execution for his collaboration with the Japanese. His grandfather worked as a doctor, and was only allowed to keep his hospital open in exchange for examining comfort women for STDs. After the Korean War, he embarked on entrepreneurship, which, amidst a challenging economic climate, eventually failed. Following the footsteps of his siblings, many of whom had immigrated to the United States as students and immigrants, he too found his way to America in 1990 at the age of 62. Initially settling in Dallas, Texas, he eventually made New York his permanent home, where he has resided ever since, reflecting on a life marked by resilience, sacrifice, and the pursuit of freedom and opportunity.
Keywords: Longjing, China; Seoul, South Korea; communism; poverty; military service; Japanese occupation; China; activism; imprisonment; uprising; korean government; korean independence; Mao; doctor; comfort women; entrepreneurship; korean war; immigration; Dallas, Texas; New York;

Jun Jinhee (Full)
Interviewee: Jun, Jinhee
Jinhee Jun (Jinhee Choi) was born on December 6th, 1927 in Sincheon, Seoul, under colonial rule. When she was around six, her family relocated to Manchuria, where she attended elementary school (Hong-joong Elementary School) and middle school (Gang-myeong Girls’ Middle School). Up until liberation, she received her education in Japanese, and went by the Japanese name “Takada Jenshin.” Following graduation, she worked as an elementary school’s teacher in Manchuria, where all of her students were Korean, though she instructed them in Chinese and Korean, teaching most subjects. She recalls being neither poor nor wealthy in her youth. Some members of her family were involved with the independence movement; her father, a doctor who had opened a practice in Manchuria, secretly treated independence activists who passed through. His clinic also saw comfort women, though Korean doctors were not allowed to conduct checkups on them, with Japanese medics treating them instead. She also remembers how a family member on her maternal side (Mother? Uncle?) who was heavily involved with the independence movement was imprisoned, and how upon their release they couldn’t see too well because it had been too dark in their cell. Following the end of WWII, many of her friends fled to Japan as sympathizers, but she and her family stayed in Manchuria for a little while longer until moving to Seoul in 1947; she recounts how the few Japanese still living in Manchuria at the time were mostly farmers who did not have the means to go back to their country, and how she felt sorry for them as many ended up moving to the Soviet Union. When the Korean War broke out, she and her family followed the military down to Daegu, where they had no relations, before moving back to Seoul, where she married in Supyo (Supyo Church). Following her sister, she immigrated to the United States in 1979 at the age of 52, living first in Tulsa, Oklahoma, where she made a Japanese friend named Hiroki who helped with her English. She became an American citizen in Tulsa, taking English lessons to pass the citizenship test, and worked at American Airlines for 16 years. When asked if she ever missed Korea, or anyone still in Korea, she said that everyone had already come to America, and that she as well as her children enjoyed life in the U.S. When asked whether she identified as Korean or as Korean American, she said she hadn’t given it much thought, and thought of herself as Korean.
Keywords: Japanese occupation; Manchuria; Teacher; Doctor; comfort women; Tulsa; Oklahoma; Japanese name; Daegu; Seoul; Supyo; activism; imprisoned; military; sympathizers

Theresa K. Rhee (Full)
Interviewee: Rhee, Theresa K.
Theresa K Rhee, born Jun Kyunghee was born on Jaunary 14, 1935 in China (Manchuria). She was the sixth of seven children; she had three sisters and three brothers. Her father practiced medicine, with his clinic having around a dozen beds, and her mother used to be an independence activist. When her mother was 19, she became a leader of the independence movement, and was subsequently jailed for a year by Japanese authorities. Her father, who had graduated from medical school, proposed to her after her release, to which she replied that she couldn’t marry him because she was a “criminal.” Upon learning of her illicit activities, however, Theresa’s father chose to marry her mother because she was an “honorable criminal.” Her mother married when she was 22, and the family moved to Southern Manchuria, where there were Korean communities. Theresa’s family lived there until she was 12, after which they moved to Ganghwa Island, modern-day South Korea; she recounts taking a truck to the Peninsula, then a train at the Tumen River to Seoul, then to Incheon, where they boarded a ferry to Ganghwa Island where her family was originally from. This was her first time in Korea. In 1948, speaking little Korean (interesting), she passed an examination to be placed into Ewha Middle School. When she was 15, the Korean War broke out and the family moved temporarily to Daegu, where she enrolled in Hyosang Catholic High School. At the war’s end, the family moved back to Seoul where she graduated from Ewha High School before beginning studies in English at Ewha University. In 1955-1956, she came to the United States alone by a cargo boat, sponsored by a family she had met through an American chaplain accompanying American GIs posted in Korea (at the time, there was a quota on the number of Koreans who could enter the country). She majored in biology at college in the U.S., and although the plan was to return to Korea after her studies, it wasn’t until 12 years later that she made a visit back to the country. While she was a sophomore in college, her father passed but news of his passing were kept from her by her family so as to not interfere with her studies. She settled in West Virginia in 1962, most of her friends Caucasian. Her mother passed in 1974 to a stroke, but because of difficulties in traveling to Korea at the time, she had to take a flight to Japan before flying to Korea; when she landed in Japan, she received news that her mother had passed.
Keywords: China; Manchuria; Doctor; Parents; Independence Movement; Activism; Resistance; Mother; Kanghwa Island; Ganghwa Island; Ewha; Korean War; GI; Military; Catholic; Chaplain; Criminal; Washington; West Virginia; BIology; Quota; Sponsorship

Woon Sun Choi (Full)
Interviewee: Choi, Woon Sun
Woon Sun Choi was born in Valdivostok, Russia, on April 28th, 1923. When he was seven years old, he along with the rest of his family moved to “Dong-gyeong,” Manchuria. When asked if he moved from Russia to Manchuria due to communist activities, he said no; rather, his family chose to move to Manchuria because they heard it was nice to work and live in. While his family was in transit, however, their possessions (including gold they had exchanged their legal tender for) was stolen by thieves. Upon their arrival to Manchuria, Choi had to work to support his family of four, rather than enroll in school right away. Working in the logging industry, he would cut and sell logs alongside adults to make ends meet. Eventually he was enrolled into elementary school, where he received his education in Japanese, and spoke Chinese and Korean while in Manchuria. He did not choose to take a Japanese name. After liberation, he moved to Seoul alone (his parents went to the North?), where he continued schooling at Hanyang Middle and High school; after finishing his studies, he began training to be a teacher, and was posted to a school in Kangwon Province (unclear if he was posted in Kangwon North or Kangwon South). During the Korea War, he was reported by a neighbor for being involved with communist activities, which he was not, but the report led to subsequent harassment by the secret police. After the war, he was married. In the 1970s, the rest of Choi’s family immigrated to the United States (Tulsa, OK), but he was barred from leaving until a year later due to his alleged involvement with the communist party. He moved first to Tulsa, where he worked at Benihana before getting a job at an electronics factory, where he said the work was better than being a schoolteacher, and that his colleagues (mostly Chinese) were kind to him. When asked if he ever wanted to return to Korea, he said no, and that he’s never wanted to go back. When asked if he identified as Korean or Korean American, he stated that he was a Korean.
Keywords: Japanese occupation; Manchuria; Russia; Valdivostok; Communism; Theft; Seoul; Factory; Tulsa; Oklahoma; Benihana; Electronics; Factory; Korean; Kangwon; Teacher; School; Dong-gyeong; Kangwon-do; North Korea; Reported; Secret Police

Haewon Latorre (Edited)
Interviewee: Latorre, Haewon
Haewon Latorre was born in Korea, moved to Argentina as a toddler, then moved to NYC as a teenager. She identifies herself as a Latin Korean American. She has struggled with bipolar disorder and explored her sexuality as a bisexual woman without the support she longed for from her family and community. She is now happily married and lives in Flushing, NY, to the only Latin American man who can't Salsa (her words).
Keywords: Latin America; Dad; Iran-Iraq War; Korea; Argentina; Hero; Grandmother; Asian; Racism; Black people; Protests; Korean American; Black American; College; United States; Bisexual; Flushing

Haewon Latorre (Full)
Interviewee: Latorre, Haewon
Haewon Latorre, a Latin Korean American, gives us a detailed insight into her life, from when she was a toddler to a fully grown adult, talking about all the ups and downs she faced. She claims that there was too much ignorance about her racial identity growing up, as she looked Asian, appearance-wise, but spoke Spanish, which was odd to many people. It truly angers her how people aren't flexible enough to accept different people, like Haewon herself. Particularly pinpointing Korean Culture, she explains that Korean people are way too demanding in the way they think other Koreans should behave and live, like how her parents would restrict her from doing things she desired to pursue. Revealing the fact that she suffered from bipolar disorder, depression, and ADHD, she further talks about how she wasn't able to receive affection and love from her family during this difficult time. As a fully grown adult, she wishes to spread more awareness of Asians and gain respect for them in a way in which they can freely express themselves.
Keywords: Argentina; Buenos Aires; Dad; Korea; Racism, Black American; Protests; Korean Latin American; New York; English; Korean American; Language; Embarrassment; Ignorance; Ethnic studies; Europe; Assimilation; Acculturation; Requirements; Bisexual; Affection; Love; Acceptance; Bipolar disorder Depression; ADHD; Different

Clara Yoon (Edited)
Interviewee: Yoon, Clara
When her child, born female, came out to Clara Yoon and her husband as a boy, they decided to accept and support his transition. As Clara shares what enabled them to make this decision and her thoughts on his queer identity, the tremendous love she feels for her son becomes so evident.
Keywords: Transgender, Gender Identity, Children, acceptance, Korean American; Parents; LGBTQ; Support; Love; Social Norms; Sexuality; Understanding; Courage

Clara Yoon (Full)
Interviewee: Yoon, Clara
Clara Yoon is the creator of the API Project, a group that seeks to help Asian American parents share their stories of accepting their kids' change in identity and gender. Yoon, herself is a mom of a transgender son who went through a while to understand and support her son. She comes to explain her thought process on the idea of LGBT and the process of accepting and understanding the true meaning of it. As she explains her process of coming to learn and accept her son's transition from a female to the boy, she comes to emphasize the difficulties her Korean family has when exposed to her son's transition. Defining religion as one of the main contributions to opposition against LGBT in many Asian communities, she comes to express her sadness towards what many young kids are faced with while having to deal with their identity crisis. As she feels support from parents is really important, she wishes that more Asian communities will become more accepting of LGBT communities.
Keywords: LGBT, gener, sex, identity. Asian American, Acceptance, religion. Courage, Coming out. bullying, Support, affirmation, parents, API Project, P Flag

Janice Paik - Part 1 + Part 2 (Edited)
Interviewee: Paik, Janice
Janice Paik was born and raised in LA's Koreatown and currently works and lives in downtown LA. In this Legacy Project video, Janice discusses being openly gay growing up in K-Town, grappling with her ethnic and sexual identity, as well as her relationship with her mother, who has been and continues to be, the most important person in her life.
Keywords: Sexuality, gender, mother, LA, Korea Town, Tom Boy, Identity, Racial Identity; Korean language; Lesbian, Korean American; Relationships; Acceptance; Systemic Discrimination; Social Norms; Social Standards; Hardships; Career; Grandmother; family; Coming Out; Sexual Abuse; Resentment; Mom; Self Love; Love Yourself; Acknowledgement; Addiction; Rehab

Janice Paik (Full)
Interviewee: Paik, Janice
Janice Paik, born and raised in LA, shares her story of how she came to realize her true sexual identity as a Korean American lesbian. As she has fought through her disability to open herself up, she feels the desire to guide and support people like her on a journey where they can really express themselves and show, confidently, who they are. Emphasizing the significant role her mother played throughout her life and her career, she really thanks her and truly looks up to her. As she has been through significant obstacles throughout her life, she wants others to recognize the importance of self-love.
Keywords: Sexuality, gender, Korea Town, Identity, LGBT, Lesbian, Family, Race, Drugs, Rehab, Self love; acceptance; Mother; Career; Society

Lisa Quan (Edited)
Interviewee: Quan, Lisa
Lisa came to the US when she was two years old. Her mother left the family at age 5, then she was sent back to Korea at age 11, only to return to her father in Los Angeles at age 14. Despite her turbulent childhood, she graduated from Loyola Marymount University, and her passion is helping others by working in the nonprofit world. Hers is a story of struggle and resilience with a hefty dose of grace.
Keywords: Non-Profit; Community; Social Justice; Asian American; Korean American; Mom; Dad; Divorce; Family; Work; Childhood; Obstacles; Financial struggles; Education; Youth; Resilience

Lisa Quan (Full)
Interviewee: Quan, Lisa
Lisa Quan, born in Korea, grew up through an unstable childhood, juggling life in and out of the US. Following the separation of her parents, she was sent to her grandparents in Korea, where education was not accessible. As her dad struggled financially to support a household, he worked job after job to help bring Lisa back to America. Growing up in a poor neighborhood in West LA, Lisa struggled to receive a proper education and grew up in a limited environment where no opportunities for success were accessible. Attending Loyola Marymount University, she began her journey to pursue her passion for social justice. Emphasizing the great impact females have in her life, she encourages the importance of looking up to strong women and learning from them. Facing great amounts of obstacles throughout her childhood and teenage years, she was able to create an armor of resilience and strength that shapes the woman she is today.
Keywords: Non-Profit; Community; Social Justice; Asian American; Korean American; Mom; Dad; Divorce; Family; Work; Childhood; Obstacles; Financial struggles; Education; Youth; Resilience; Grandparents; Women

Irvin Paik (Edited)
Interviewee: Paik, Irvin
Irvin Paik was born in Bakersville, CA in 1940. His father came to the US in 1905 with the first wave of sugar plantation workers to Hawaii. His mother came to the US in 1914. This is a rare recording of a Korean American who can recollect what life was like during the early period of Korean immigration. Irvin also recollects a high school play that he was in with George Takei, the Japanese-American actor. This interview was conducted in Los Angeles on August 31, 2013.
Keywords: California; Parents; Korean; Grandfather; Farming; Lease; Rent; Land; Profitable Crops; Utah; Quick Silver Mine; WWII; Badges; Musical

Irvin Paik (Full)
Interviewee: Paik, Irvin
Irvin Paik was born in 1940 in Bakersfield, California, and grew up in America his whole life. His mother and father immigrated to the US at a young age and worked in several factories and farms to earn money for their kids. Having that person in America who was not familiar with Koreans, Paik and his family had to live through constantly being asked uncomfortable and annoying questions as well as unfair treatment. Attending a Korean Presbyterian Church, Paik maintained the Korean side of his culture as he spent time with other Koreans around the area. After graduating from UCLA, he became an editor for major production companies, as these companies were looking for minorities to work for them instead of already capable and educated people. By working as an editor and a script clerk, he became very fond of this job and regretted not pursuing it further. Irvin Paik is one proud Korean American, and he truly believes that Korean immigrants, like his parents, have made significant accomplishments to solidify the Korean presence in the States.
Keywords: Blakersfield, California; Los Angeles; Factories; Mother; Father; Korean; Korean Presbyterian Church; Farm; Crops; UCLA; Discrimination; Badges; World War II; Korean War; Hawaii; Angel Island; Army Pictorial Center; Editor; Script clerk; Warner Brothers; Universal; Military; Japan; Korean American; English

Julius Rosen (Full)
Interviewee: Rosen, Julius
Julius Rosen, a WWII veteran, comes to share his prominent experiences as a deployed soldier in Korea. Recalling his memories of Korea during the period of Japanese internalization, Rosen comes to describe the inhuman treatment of the Japanese towards the Koreans and how disgustingly horrid it was. Moreover, reflecting on his journey in Japan, Rosen comes to emphasize the ever-lasting effects of the Atomic bomb ever-lasting and the difficulties in receiving treatment to this day. Showing great love and thanks to his community in Millis, Rosen would not trade a single penny for the people by his side today.
Keywords: WWII, Veteran, soldier, Korea, Japan, Nagasaki, Atomic bomb, Incheon, History; Deployment; Millis American Legion

Julius Rosen (Edited)
Interviewee: Rosen, Julius
Julius Rosen was 18 years old when he was deployed to Korea in 1945 during World War II, just before Japan surrendered. He has clear memories of landing in Inchon and looking at a country devastated by war. He made the mistake of visiting Nagasaki right after the atomic bomb, which caused him to struggle with ill health all of his life. Mr. Rosen passed away six months after recording this video in Millis, MA, where he was born and lived for 86 years.
Keywords: World War II, Korean War, Japan; Atomic Bomb; Veteran, Russia, Cancer; South Korea; Service

Jwa Kyung Shin (Full)
Interviewee: Shin, Jwa Kyung
Jwa Kyung Shin was born in 1914 in Korea. She was 100 years old at the time of the interview, and firmly states throughout her conversation that she attributes all of her life’s activities, blessings, and successes to her faith. She begins the interview by recounting her family’s history with the Christian faith, which began in the 1800s when the first Protestant missions arrived on the Peninsula. In the 1890s, her grandfather, a successful self-made man who had amassed enormous wealth, founded a school in his village with these missionaries, believing that education could be a means for the students to have better lives. While living under Japanese rule, many members of her family were engaged with Korean independence movements. She remembers how her father, educated abroad in New York, was an independence activist who worked alongside the likes of Kim Gu and Rhee Syngman during his time in the United States, and how upon his return to Korea he was imprisoned twice by the Japanese. Her husband began his academic career in Japan studying at an Imperial University, before obtaining professorship at Seoul National University after liberation. She remembers with pride how her during independence movements, her husband was a staunch supporter of Korean independence (she points out, despite his education at an imperial university, he was loyal to the cause of liberation). However, following independence from colonial rule, her husband was kidnapped and taken to North Korea, potentially to work at Kim Il Sung University with which her family had some kind of connection. Despite the hardship of raising six children alone, she takes pride in their perseverance and success, remembering how her kids would pursue schooling rigorously, walking to school no matter the outside conditions. In 1982, she moved to the United States (one of her sons had moved ten years prior, in 1972), and continues to lead a life of faith, crediting her health, children, and satisfaction to God.
Keywords: 100; age; Christianity; missionaries; grandfather; blessings; school; education; Seoul National University; Imperial University; North Korea; kidnapped; independence movements; activists; Kim Gu; Rhee Syngman; imprisoned, United States

Jwa Kyung Shin (Edit)
Interviewee: Shin, Jwa Kyung
Jwa Kyung Shin was born in 1914 in Korea. She was 100 years old at the time of the interview. She survived the Japanese Colonialism and the Korean War, despite the fact that her husband was kidnapped by the North Koreans, leaving her with 6 young children. Despite her overwhelming sense of helplessness, she managed to survive and all of her children graduated from college, some of her children graduated from Seoul National University, and Dr. Kang Lee, her eldest son, received his PhD from MIT and went on to become a successful scientist and an entrepreneur. Ms. Shin remembers her grandfather, a self-made man who amassed enormous wealth then used these funds to start a school for children, invite a Christian missionary into his village to start a church, and hold educational sessions to villagers to teach them how to lead a better life. She credits 6 generation of Christian faith which allowed them to live with thankfulness despite seemingly insurmountable challenges.
Keywords: 100; age; Christianity; missionaries; grandfather; blessings; school; education; Seoul National University; Imperial University; North Korea; kidnapped; independence movements; activists; Kim Gu; Rhee Syngman; imprisoned, United States

Kang P. Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Kang P.
Immigrating to the US in 1972, Kang Lee discusses his journey to achieve his ultimate goal: being the best engineer/scientist Korea can ever produce. Starting at Seoul National University, to Clarkson University, and finally to the one and only MIT, he talks about his hardships in getting to that point and what he had learned from these experiences. Going back to his life in Korea, he notes that his family lived a hard life, as they had no money and North Koreans kidnapped their father. However, Lee does not think of these experiences as hardships but as things that shaped him into the strong person he is today. Though he is not working directly in the mechanical engineering field today, he has vast aspirations to make new technological advancements through the company he had started and hopefully benefit Korea in the future. Thus, he advises future generations to be passionate and hardworking in the things they enjoy doing.
Keywords: Scholarship; Korea; New York; Clarkson; MIT; Mechanical Engineering; Father; School; Hardships; Money; Exams; Aspen Company; Aerogels; Technologies; Family; Education; Obstacles

Kang P. Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Kang P.
Since junior high school, Kang Lee aspired to become one of the greatest scientists Korea has produced. His father, who was the chairman of the Biology department at Seoul National University, was kidnapped by the North Koreans during the Korean War. His mother was left to raise six children on her own. Kang Lee found scholarships that allowed him to attend his junior high school and high school and eventually worked his way through Seoul National University as a private tutor. He came to the USA to attend MIT and receive his Ph.D. In 1984, he founded Aspen Systems, where he is still the CEO. This is an amazing story of struggle and resilience in the face of seemingly insurmountable challenges. Despite many struggles and successes, Kang Lee remains incredibly optimistic and humble.

Byoung Choh (Full)
Interviewee: Choh, Byoung
Byoung Choh was born in 1929 in what is now North Korea, during the Japanese occupation. He faced the prohibition of the Korean language at his primary school, which was enforced by both Korean and Japanese teachers. Despite this, he continued speaking Korean at home. Byoung's teenage years were marked by compulsory military training in high school, a reflection of the turbulent times. The landscape of his homeland dramatically changed in August 1945 when the US dropped atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki, leading to Japan's surrender. Shortly thereafter, the Soviet army occupied northern Korea, introducing communism, while the southern part saw the US army bringing democracy after the Japanese withdrawal. Byoung witnessed the harshness of the Soviet regime, including the treatment of Korean women and the theft of resources. This contrasted with the gentler approach of the American forces. In October 1945, Kim Il Sung was installed by the Soviets as the leader of North Korea. The establishment of the 38th parallel soon followed, setting the stage for the Korean War in 1950. During the conflict, Byoung, realizing the dangers of staying in the North, made a harrowing escape to the South by ship in December 1950. Tragically, he was separated from his family when the US destroyed infrastructure, preventing their planned reunion. Byoung then joined the South Korean army, where he began his military life amidst scarce resources. His service lasted until 1964, during which time he admired General MacArthur for his role in the conflict, despite MacArthur's eventual dismissal by President Truman. Post-military, Byoung married Jung Sook and opened a medical practice in Seoul. Seeking a better life, Byoung moved to the United States in 1972 after passing a special exam for foreign medical doctors. He settled in Cleveland, Ohio, where he has lived ever since. It was only after witnessing the successful establishment of his children in America that he felt reassured about his decision to immigrate. Throughout these challenges, Byoung's faith in God provided him with strength and perseverance.
Keywords: Japanese occupation; Korean war; North Korea; 38th parallel; Soviet Union; US army; democracy; communism; South Korea; family separation; General MacArthur; President Truman; Seoul; Cleveland, Ohio; immigration; medical practice; doctor; faith; Christianity; atomic bombs; Hiroshima; Nagasaki;

Jung S. Choh (Full)
Interviewee: Jung S. Choh
Jung Sook Choh was born in 1935 in Hangyeonnamdo, near the East Sea (동해 바다). She and her father fled to Shinpo to escape the increasing violence of the Japanese occupation. Despite learning Japanese as a child, she no longer remembers the language. To distance themselves from conflict, they moved further south before liberation. After liberation, her father sought a new place to settle and went to Hwanghaedo, where he taught villagers Hangeul in exchange for food. They later joined him and acquired a small home. Her father then served the Cheondoist church in Gaenipo while Jung Sook and her mother farmed. Due to financial constraints, Jung Sook could not initially attend school, but later, her aunt helped her get into middle school. She returned to her mother's home when they had more money, but the Korean War began, preventing them from reuniting with her brother. During the war, the North Koreans issued new currency, rendering the old money useless. Her father sent the old money to Cheondoists in Seoul, which led to his imprisonment when someone was caught. Rumors spread that prisoners were drowned by communists, but her father survived an attempted poisoning in prison by not drinking the soup provided. Her father's colleagues warned them to flee, and they escaped to Seoul, facing hardships such as riding on the roof of a train to Osan. Seeking valid identification, a kind police officer from their hometown provided IDs and housing, allowing them to start a tempura business. They later moved to Chuncheon to be closer to her brother and started a bookstore. During the 1988 Olympics, they located her brother, who had posed as an orphan for special benefits in North Korea. Jung Sook attended school in Seoul, where she met her future husband. Her mother was reluctant to send her to college, but eventually did, though Jung Sook got engaged and did not finish her education. They immigrated to Akron, Ohio, where her husband found a residency at St. Thomas Hospital. Initially poor, their situation improved once her husband became a doctor. They bought a house near the hospital and established a stable life. Jung Sook is grateful for immigrating to the United States, as her children have had good lives and found good partners.
Keywords: Akron, Ohio; Immigration; Korean War; Japanese Occupation; Cheondoism; Cheondoist; Poison; Communism; North Korea; South Korea; Japanese Imperialism; Liberation; Tempura; Train; Busan

Dr. James ChinKyung Kim (Edited)
Interviewee: Kim, James Chinkyung
Dr. James ChinKyung Kim is no ordinary man, containing the spunk and spirit of a teenage boy. Most impressive about this “love-ist” is his ability to maintain an unwavering stance on global peace in a world today that isn’t the most conducive to that end. His passion for his country in the brutal era of the Korean War is what perhaps led him to where he is today, heading two universities in two different communist nations. It takes a special kind of person with a certain level of persistence to do what he did in 1992, founding and establishing with the Chinese government Yanbian University of Science and Technology (YUST). Later in 2009, he would carry out the same feat, invited by North Korea to build Pyongyang University of Science and Technology (PUST). With a resounding voice, full of conviction and authority, Dr. Kim shares about his life, the journey of his faith, and his ongoing dreams for a united Korea.

Namsun Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Namsun
Namsun Lee got sick with tuberculosis at 24 and was sent to a sanatorium in South Korea. While there, she encountered a man praying in the hospital church, later to become her husband. They moved to the United States with their three children, settling in Richmond, VA. She supported the family financially while her husband was studying to be a pastor. She worked at a sewing factory for three years, despite physical pain and racial barriers at work, Namsun remained resilient. She eventually found work at the General Electric company, initially on an assembly line and later in a higher-paying "man's job" assembling TVs. Her happiest memory occurred during a moment of deep despair in Albany, NY. Feeling overwhelmed being a pastor's wife, she ran into the broken bathroom stall of the children’s ministry building. She found solace in hearing children sing praise songs. She walked outside and asked God to to give her joy which lead her to a profound spiritual experience. The feeling of joy washed over her like an ocean wave, sadness was gone and everything felt different. Namsun's faith sustained her through adversity. She imparted its importance to her children, reassuring them in the face of hardship and maintaining a strong family prayer routine. She now cherishes her role as a grandmother, aiming to pass on the wisdom and joy she finds in her faith.
Keywords: Tuberculosis; Korea;  Sanatorium; Minister; Pastor; Wife; United States; Richmond; Virginia; Sewing; Factory; General Electrical Company; Western Electric; Assembly Line; Man's job; Spiritual; Joy; Happiness; Lonely; God; Children; Grandchildren

Namsun Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Namsun
To support her family while her husband was studying, Namsun Lee took on a “man’s job” at a General Electric company in Richmond, VA. Despite the physical demands of this job, Namsun actually points to her time as a pastor’s wife in Albany, NY as a time she “almost died.” Crippled by the anxiety of having to interact with so many people and fulfill the social obligations of a pastor’s wife, she hid inside a bathroom stall in the children’s ministry building. But something transpired there that turned this unpleasant memory into one of the happiest moments in her life.

Rev. Jong Hyung Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Hyung, Jong
Reverend Jong Hyung Lee was born in 1941 in South Korea. He arrived in the US in 1974 at the age 33. After earning his PhD in the US, he returned to Korea to teach in seminaries and churches. Witnessing immigrant struggles, he saw an opportunity in the US for guiding others and creating a community. He founded Korean Overseas Missions Inc. to train church and community leaders and send missionaries abroad. Notable among his missions was establishing spiritual ministry at Myungsung Medical College in Ethiopia. He taught ethics and world view to students, helped establish spiritual ministry, and laid the foundation to train students on whole person medical care. His belief in God stemmed at a young age seeing his grandmother's deep devotion to Christianity and overcoming his personal near-death experience at 23. Grateful for his wife's support, he raised three daughters and now has three grandchildren. After 35 years of ministry, he leaves a legacy of service and faith as a pastor. He seeks to tell his grandchildren the importance of inner character, fostering meaningful relationships, and the transformative power of God. He emphasizes the interconnectedness of humanity, highlighting the potential for personal growth and spiritual change.

Sulja Warnick (Edited)
Interviewee: Warnick, Sulja
Sulja Lee was born in Japan in 1942 during WWII, and her family moved back to Korea after Korea gained independence from Japan in 1945. She married Fred Warnick, a Jewish man from the US when mixed-race marriages were not expected. Her two daughters were born and raised in Korea but immigrated to the US as toddlers. Her daughter Gina Warnick Coletti became an accomplished violist, and Angela Warnick Buchdahl recently became the Senior Rabbi of the Central Synagogue in New York City. In this Legacy Project video, Sulja Warnick reflects on her childhood in Korea, the influence of her parents, her desire to explore worlds outside of Korea, marrying a Jewish man and moving to the US, and raising their children with encouragement to "pick and choose" the best out of Korean and Jewish cultures.

Victor Victori (Edited)
Interviewee: Victori, Victor
Victori was born in 1943 in a small peach-farming village outside of Seoul, Korea. After enduring years of the Korean War as a young child and later serving in the Air Force, he left Korea in 1969 and traveled through Europe, studying the works of old masters, including his most revered icons, Rembrandt, Van Gogh, and Michelangelo. He immigrated to the United States in 1972 and undertook his first project to paint a mural of all the U.S. Presidents. After receiving a personal invitation and thank you letter from President Nixon, Victori delivered the painting to the White House in 1973, where it hangs to this day. During the 70s and 80s, Victori traveled the country as a portrait artist, then started and ran his own gallery on 230 Park Avenue during the 90s. He currently works out of his private studio, where he is focused on further developing his unique style of painting called Multiplism. He is a father to 5 grown sons and lives with his wife Maria, in Rutherford, NJ.

Han Sung Park (Full)
Interviewee: Park, Han Sung
Park Han Sung was born on January 7, 1935, in Andong, a rural countryside without buses or electricity raised by a single mother after her father's premature death. She fondly remembers foraging as a child and cherishes her connection to nature during her childhood. Her grandmother was incredibly kind and loving, and her older sisters often sacrificed for her as the youngest sibling. Her mother, though a woman of few words, was a strong homemaker who could make a meal from a single jujube, ensuring the family never felt the lack despite their father's early death. Han Sung learned to cook from her mother, who maintained a sense of stability and self-esteem in the family. She attended Japanese school as a child and remembers the pain of being unable to perform jesa, a Korean ancestral rite, due to Japanese interference. The Korean War began when she was in 3rd grade, forcing her family to flee to Suwon, then Daegu, and ultimately Busan. During the war, her family faced many hardships, including the theft of heirlooms by communists and the abduction of relatives by the People's Army. Despite the turmoil, Hang Sung formed a close bond with her sister, especially after their brother-in-law was sent to the military. After the war, Han Sung resumed her education in Daegu, experiencing happiness and frequent visits with her mother. Han Sung decided to become a pharmacist to support her mother, and she took pride in being a good daughter, helping with household chores whenever she visited home. After marrying, she got her first job at a wonderful pharmacy with supportive bosses, where she learned financial literacy and gained independence. However, her life took another turn when her husband decided to move to America. Despite her mother's sadness and pleas for her to stay, Han Sung eventually joined her husband, bringing their children. In America, they lived in an apartment with other Koreans, which mitigated her loneliness, but she also recalls being poor during that time. Concerned about her children's future and language skills, she prioritized their English education. Her children eventually attended medical school, and Hang Sung feels that all her sacrifices were worth it for their success. Though she sometimes regrets moving to America and believes she might have been happier in Korea, she finds solace in her children's happiness and achievements.

Sung Do Park (Full)
Interviewee: Park, Sung Do
SungDo Park was born on January 6th, 1933, during the Japanese occupation of Korea. He experienced hardships such as being forbidden to speak Korean and enduring monthly raids of his home as a child. He recalls the month of March as being especially hungry as it was too early in the year to gather greens. His father was a scholar and Korean independence fighter which had a significant influence to Sungdo's life. He recalls his father's commitment to Korea as he was also an army officer and military battalion commander during the Korean War. SungDo credits his parents and their education as an influence in his own career paths. SungDo own experiences during the Korean War was one of survival. He escaped the North Korean draft during the Korean War by escaping to South Gyeongsang and lived in a cave for 3 months. During the chaos of war, he witnessed the devastating toll it exacted especially on young lives like his friends from school. After he graduated medical school he served as a surgeon in the South Korean army for 14 years from 1957- 1972. He came to the US in 1961 for one year as part of his medical school training and realized the opportunity to raise his kids and access to education. He moved to the US in 1978 with his wife and three children and started his practice working in emergency medicine. SungDo values family and hopes to be remembered as a loving and hardworking individual to his eight grandchildren.

Yun Hoon Chung (Full)
Interviewee: Chung, Yun Hoon
Yun Hoon Chung, born in China in April 1938 to a lineage of scholars, moved to China in 1918 during the Japanese occupation. Inspired by a photo of the Empire State Building, he taught himself English with the help of a Japanese American tutor. His family's privileged life in Manchuria shielded them from immediate oppression, thanks to his father's successful import-export business, of soybeans, corn and rice. Despite societal challenges, Yun Hoon's father instilled in him a strong sense of Korean identity and resilience against discrimination. Once back in North Korea, realizing the limitations of education under the dictatorship, Yun Hoon attempted to assassinate Kim Il-sung before fleeing to South Korea with nothing but a 10 won note. Encountering the US and CIA due to his English proficiency, he prioritized education over offers of employment. Struggling to survive, he secured a scholarship for weather forecasting training through a fake college certificate. A few months later, the Korean War broke out and hid from being drafted by North Korea. He was caught often while looking for food but eventually escaped. Once the war endedUpon arriving in the United States, he attended Michigan University, working various jobs to support his family. Despite immense pressure, he compared his previous life in North Korea as more difficult. While working at a packaging company, he was tasked with making improvements on a sugar packaging machine. When the company went under he took it upon himself to create the product and eventually ventured into entrepreneurship. His successful ventures led to the development of innovative products, notably impacting packaging supply systems. He has currently been working on building a new commercial product that will impact the supply chain system and help to eliminate waste.Yun Hoon instilled in his children the same values of freedom, equality, and resilience he was taught by his father. He aspires to contribute to global welfare and Korean unification, embracing his identity as an engineer and inventor. Throughout his journey, he learned to embrace challenges, encourage imagination, and view failure as a stepping stone to growth.

Kim J Chung (Full)
Interviewee: Chung, Kim J
Kim J. Chung was born in October 1935 in Pyongyang. In 1946, at age 11, her family fled to Ongjin, pretending to attend a wedding, with money sewn into their clothes by her mother. They moved to Incheon and then Seattle. She recalls this escape as similar to a scene from The Sound of Music. Within six months, their money was gone due to her father’s frivolous spending. He eventually abandoned the family to work in a gold mine, losing his wife’s trust. Her mother began sewing scarves to sell to soldiers, and Jung Sook helped, improving their living conditions. Her mother was politically active in the North-West Youth Association, opposing communists. During the Korean War, communists imprisoned her mother. Jung Sook’s bravery and the presence of her infant sibling secured her mother’s release. They fled to Kangwondo and then to Jeju, where Jung Sook, at age 13, enjoyed singing in a church choir. After the war, Jung Sook had a tutor with whom she developed a relationship. When he immigrated to the US, she was promised to him. She struggled with immigration exams but eventually passed, cheating on the English test, and traveled by boat to Seattle at age 18. She cared for seasick passengers during the journey. In the US, she faced initial disappointment with her living conditions but pursued her GED at Ann Arbor High School. She married her tutor because she didn't want to live with a man unmarried. She was the first Korean many Americans had seen and received many gifts. Despite poverty, she was grateful to escape the Korean War’s atrocities. Jung Sook and her husband had three children. They faced housing discrimination but borrowed money to start a hasuk (boarding house). They later bought and renovated a seven-bedroom house. Her husband invented and sold a sugar packing machine, prompting their move to Toledo for better business opportunities. Jung Sook has no regrets and attributes her life's comfort to her faith. Initially skeptical of charismatic Christian practices, she experienced a deep faith revival after a pastor's sermon, believing her life is a miracle from God.
Keywords: Pyongyang; North Korea; Seattle; Chicago; Ann Arbor; East Michigan; Michigan; Ongjin; Anti-communist; Scarves; Communist; Tutor; GED; Housing Discrimination; Sugar Packing; Christian; Christianity; Toledo; Jung Sook; Kangwon; Jeju

Chong Taek An (Full)
Interviewee: Chong, Taek An
Chong Taek An was born in 1929 in Hwanghae Province, Korea, which today is situated in the southwestern region of North Korea, bordering the 38th parallel. His childhood was spent in the deep and rural mountainsides, and he recalls how he had to make arduous mountain crossings to get to elementary school. In the winters, he and his friends would ride logs down the mountain slopes to get to and from school. His family lived in Hwanghae until he was in 3rd grade, when they moved to Seoul following a relative who was in the oil-pressing business. In order to make ends meet, An worked instead of continuing school to support his family, working in both the fields and in the oil-press. His family eventually worked in a rice-cake business, but because rice was a commodity during the Japanese occupation, they had to discreetly smuggle grains to be turned into rice cake, and their business was often subject to inspections by the imperial police. Often, the family had to eat millet, corn, or red beans instead of rice because of how scarce it was. During this time, An’s mother worked in food preparation for the Japanese, and An himself was able to be trained as an electrical technician, working on the railroad crossing signals. It was here where he was introduced to Christianity by a fellow contractor. His mother was opposed to him attending church, as this would mean he wouldn’t be able to perform jesa (ancestral rites) for his parents. After liberation, he met his wife (she was at the railroad station), and encouraged by his mother, took her out on a date. They married after the Korean War came to an end, and he recalls how because the bridges across the Han river were still destroyed, he had to ferry across the river to the wedding site. After the war, he found work in a factory, before working in the import-export business. But because of various export quotas that such businesses were subject to during the Park Chung-hee administration, he found himself deeply in debt to the point of contemplating suicide, but stood down, attributing his courage to his faith. After paying his debts, he immigrated to the United States, where he had a relative, and arrived with $500. He worked for an electronics company for a while but was injured one day (electric shock?), which put him in the hospital. After recovering, he moved to New York, where he first worked at a grocery store (potentially greengrocer) before borrowing money from his brother-in-law to open a dry cleaners. His business was in a run-down neighborhood filled with abandoned buildings, and so after a few years he and his wife sold the dry-cleaners to a Jewish businessman and moved to a different part of the city. When asked what he would tell future generations, he said that his generation doesn’t expect of their descendants to struggle as they did, but that they should focus on keeping their faith, and the hope that comes with it.
Keywords: Hwanghae; Mountains; Snow; Rural; Countryside; Oil; North Korea; Rice Cake; Japanese occupation; inspection; electrical technician; Korean War; Jesa; Christianity; Los Angeles; New York; Harlem; Export; Import-Export; Dry-cleaners; dry cleaners; laundromat; smuggling; millet; corn; red bean

Jeong Ae Choi (Full)
Interviewee: Choi, Jeong Ae
Jeong Ae Choi, born in Gyeongui-do to parents from Gwajun, grew up in a family marked by hardship and resilience. Her father, who lost his own father at the age of nine, was raised by his grandfather in poverty and loneliness. Despite these challenges, he excelled in school, and his teacher funded his education, leading him to become a government worker. Jeong Ae's mother, despite living in poverty, managed to send all her sons through college by making the best of their situation. In the sixth grade, Jeong Ae experienced the liberation from Japan, but soon after, her grandmother sold their house to pay for her brothers' education. Unfortunately, they were scammed out of the money, leaving Jeong Ae unable to attend school. When the Korean War broke out, her brother was drafted but did not serve due to his height. Jeong Ae witnessed the war's brutal violence and believes she survived by the grace of God, attributing this to her family's strong Christian faith. Jeong Ae married a man who was caring for his deceased brother's two children. Living in poverty, she worked at handiworks and embroidery, earning the love of her mother-in-law. She emphasizes the importance of family unity, believing that becoming a family requires everyone to work together and live for others. Encouraged by a church member in LA and with family already in the US, Jeong Ae and her husband decided to immigrate. After arriving in America, her husband worked in a factory but was severely injured in an electrocution accident. The Gwangju Uprisings in Korea, which they initially thought were a North Korean invasion, made her fear for the safety of her children left behind in Korea. She eventually brought her children to the US. Jeong Ae began working part-time sewing, which allowed all her children to attend college, for which she is grateful to God. Jeong Ae later started a laundry business, opening earlier than other businesses and running it successfully. Eventually, they bought a retirement home in Jacksonville, where she now enjoys yoga and golf daily. Reflecting on her life, she acknowledges the hardships in Korea but also recognizes the times of peace that followed. Despite her limited English, due to having many Korean friends in the States, she emphasizes the importance of family and faith, values that have guided her throughout her life.

Aiyoung Choi (Full)
Interviewee: Choi, Aiyoung
Choi Aiyoung's narrative is a rich tapestry of resilience, cultural discovery, and unwavering dedication to community service, deeply intertwined with the tumultuous history of East Asia in the 20th century. Born in Hamheung in 1940, she came into the world during a period of great upheaval, with the Korean peninsula gripped by the chaos of war and colonial occupation. Aiyoung's early years were marked by displacement and uncertainty as her family's journey took them from Korea to various corners of Asia. They settled temporarily in Shanghai, where her parents actively participated in the Korean independence movement, offering refuge to fellow compatriots and contributing to the production of national symbols like the Korean flag. Her father's linguistic talents and her mother's musical prowess reflected a family deeply rooted in tradition and resistance. The family's odyssey continued as they moved to Taiwan and then Japan, each new destination bringing its own set of challenges and opportunities. In Japan, Aiyoung embarked on her formal education, graduating from the American School in Japan. Her experiences there, coupled with her family's enduring commitment to Korean identity and independence, shaped her worldview and instilled in her a sense of social justice and activism. After completing her education, Aiyoung ventured to the United States, where she continued her journey of self-discovery and community engagement. She became involved in various advocacy efforts, ranging from voter representation to racial reconciliation. Her work with the Coalition for Korean American Voters and the Black and Korean Mediation Projects exemplified her dedication to fostering understanding and harmony within diverse communities. As a board member of organizations like the Korean American Community Foundation and Community Resource Exchange, Aiyoung played a pivotal role in addressing pressing social issues and uplifting marginalized voices. Her tireless advocacy and leadership left an indelible mark on the Korean American community and beyond. Beyond her professional accomplishments, Aiyoung's personal life was equally remarkable. She navigated the complexities of relationships and motherhood with grace and resilience, ultimately fostering a blended family and embracing her role as a mother and community leader, even going so far as to foster a little Korean boy who her own son later adopted. Choi Aiyoung's legacy is one of courage, compassion, and commitment—a testament to the transformative power of resilience and the enduring spirit of service. Recently, she was able to embark on a journey for peace traversing both Pyongyang and Seoul. As a trailblazer and changemaker, she leaves behind a profound impact on the lives of those she touched, embodying the essence of a life well-lived in service to others.

Han Shik Park (Full)
Interviewee: Park, Hanshik
Hanshik Park, born amidst the tumult of the civil war near Harbin, he experienced hardship and displacement at a young age, fleed to Korea only to be engulfed by the Korean War. Despite facing poverty, Hanshik found love and stability in marriage to his wife, whose family's wealth provided a contrast to his own humble beginnings. Together, they embarked on a new chapter in the United States with meager resources but boundless aspirations. Hanshik's academic pursuits led him to become an assistant professor in Political Science at the University of Georgia, where he dedicated 45 years to teaching and scholarship. His journey was marked by challenges, including acclimating to Southern English and navigating the evolving landscape of Korean immigration to America. Driven by a sense of duty and familial obligation, Hanshik remained in the U.S., honoring his parents' dying wish to work towards Korean reunification. His deep-seated belief in the reunification cause inspired him to advocate tirelessly, even amidst personal sacrifices and financial struggles. A poignant moment in Hanshik's life was his marriage to his beloved wife, a memory cherished for its profound joy and love. However, his experiences extend beyond moments of happiness, as evidenced by his visits to North Korea. Hanshik's encounters with the stark realities of life in North Korea, including witnessing children suffering from starvation, left an indelible mark on him. His humanitarian instincts prompted him to confront North Korean soldiers and advocate for the welfare of the vulnerable, underscoring his compassion and courage. With over 50 visits to North Korea, Hanshik remains a steadfast advocate for reunification, emphasizing the common humanity shared by North and South Koreans. His unwavering dedication to this cause reflects his belief in a brighter future for his homeland, where divisions can be bridged, and peace and prosperity prevail.
Keywords: Pharmacology; Political Science; University of Georgia; Harbin; Reunification; North Korea; One Korea; Great Leap Forward; Mao Zedong; Chiang Kai Shek; Starving; Orphans; Korea; South Korea

Han Shik Park - Part 1 (Edited)

Han Shik Park is no stranger to war. Born near what is now Harbin amidst Chinese civil unrest, Park eventually moved to South Korea after the surrender of the Japanese, but once his family reached the country, the Korean War broke out. These encounters with death and destruction were what inspired him to immigrate to the United States and study—all to figure out why people fight and kill. Now a recently retired professor of political science at the University of Georgia and the founding director of its Center for the Study of Global Issues (GLOBIS), Park is dedicating the rest of his life to reunify the two Koreas—something he longs to see happen in his lifetime.

Sang Gyun Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Sang Gyun
Sang Gyun Kim, a 49-year-old Korean immigrant who has spent 25 years in America, reflects on his experiences navigating cultural differences, discrimination, and the challenges of raising a daughter in a foreign land. Having immigrated with his parents due to the South Korean IMF crisis, Sang pursued education at Georgia State University before establishing his own accounting business as a CPA. Despite his success, Sang faced discrimination while working at his sister-in-law's business and witnessed the tragic consequences of gun violence within his community with his father's close friend passing away after being hospitalised for three years. While tensions between Asian and Black communities once existed, Sang notes a decline in conflict as Korean businesses in predominantly Black neighborhoods have dwindled since the 90s. However, Sang grapples with feelings of isolation in America, missing the sense of community he had in Korea while also reflecting on the freedom that being in the States allows. Sang's own journey is marked by personal loss, including the passing of his mother and the absence of familial support during her eight years of illness. Seeking to instill resilience and conviction in his daughter, Sang reflects on the meaning of home and belonging, yearning to find a place of safety and connection. Despite challenges, he remains committed to preserving his cultural identity, attending Korean mass and seeking community support, albeit with a sense of longing for deeper understanding and solidarity among Korean Americans.

Sang Soo Park (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Park, Sang Soo
Sang Soo Park was born on January 4, 1929, in Kyungsangnamdo. His early years were marked by severe hardship, including extreme hunger that led him to eat dog food, acorns, and foraged shrubbery. Growing up in a community where starvation was rampant, people would greet each other by asking if they had eaten. Despite the harsh conditions, Sang Soo remembers his father as kind and generous, though without a stable trade, which placed a significant responsibility on Sang Soo as the firstborn son to take care of his family. Determined and brave, Sang Soo worked various jobs to support his family. He initially worked in a factory making baskets, then became a carpenter, making tables and chairs for the Sacheon Air Force. During the Korean War, he fled from Sacheon right before his military service. After liberation from Japanese colonization, he faced the ideological conflict between communism and capitalism. He almost joined the communists but was advised by a family friend to stay in the south, a decision that saved his life as many of his friends who went north perished. After the war, Sang Soo became a vegetable salesman, then traded wheat and rice, and eventually ran a restaurant. He took pride in his entrepreneurial spirit, never working under anyone. He even tried cattle ranching, believing it could be profitable. Despite not finishing his education in childhood, he later pursued schooling under Park Chung Hee's regime and eventually earned a master's degree. Sang Soo's younger brother, who was prospering in America, invited him to move to the states in 1965. Sang Soo provided his brother with $20,000 in seed money, encouraging him to start his own company. As the eldest son, Sang Soo bore significant financial responsibility, putting his siblings through school and taking pride in their success. One of his greatest regrets was not allowing his youngest sibling to pursue acting, insisting on architecture instead. In America, Sang Soo faced new challenges. He initially worked at a factory that eventually burned down, leading to a year and a half of hospitalization. After recovering, he moved to Atlanta, ran a grocery store, and converted a movie theater into another grocery store. However, he lost everything during the Rodney King riots, lamenting the lack of police support for Koreans. Fortunately, business owner's insurance allowed him to rebuild. Sang Soo emphasizes the importance of political involvement to ensure the safety and prosperity of the Korean community. Reflecting on his life, he believes that if he had come to America under more challenging circumstances, he might have built a more substantial career. Despite the ups and downs, Sang Soo remains proud of his resilience and the legacy of support and success he fostered within his family.

Seung Nam Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Seung Nam
Lee Seung Nam was born on August 7, 1943, in Seoul to North Korean parents. After liberation, his family lived in Sajik-dong. Despite his young age, he vividly remembers the horrors of the Korean War, particularly fleeing with his family across the Han River, seeing their home burn during airstrikes, and eventually finding refuge in a cramped room in Busan with 15 other people. Despite their poverty, he recalls playing and swimming with friends after school. After graduating from Yonsei University, Lee faced a lack of job opportunities. He completed his mandatory military service and began working in a bank during President Park's presidency. Concerned about his and his children's future prospects, he decided to join his older brother in Atlanta, where he initially struggled with the physically demanding work of landscaping. Lee later opened a grocery store with his sister, but they suffered significant losses due to an armed robbery. Despite these hardships, he believes that earlier immigrants faced even greater challenges. As the vice president of the Korean Association in Atlanta, Lee played a crucial role in addressing safety concerns during the riots that followed the LA riots. With the help of a Jewish woman named Frank, he secured a meeting with Mayor Jackson, during which he demanded better protection for Korean American stores. Pastor Ji's moving speech at the subsequent press conference left a lasting impression on Lee. During the Atlanta Olympics, Lee made a point to welcome the North Korean team, despite being labeled a communist for years afterward. He expressed his belief that Koreans, whether from the North or South, should support each other. One of the hardest aspects of immigration for Lee was leaving his friends behind, especially given the high costs of communication and lack of flights to Korea at the time. He views Korea's development fondly and urges second-generation immigrants not to endure the same struggles as their parents. He also encourages them to contribute to the reunification of North and South Korea.

Kelly Choi's life journey, born in Seoul in 1967, is a testament to resilience, determination, and the pursuit of opportunity. Tragically, Kelly's father's battle with liver cancer ended prematurely when she was just 25, leaving her to navigate the complexities of life without his guidance. Yet, despite the hardships, Kelly found strength in her family, particularly in her mother, who later joined her in America to provide support and care for Kelly's own children. Kelly's journey into the workforce began with a leap of faith, starting at a Korean American law firm despite her limited English proficiency. Her determination and gratitude for the opportunities afforded to her propelled her forward, leading her to pursue a career in real estate to provide for her growing family. Amidst the challenges of adapting to life in America, Kelly grappled with personal struggles, including a battle with panic disorder following the birth of her third child. Yet, through introspection and resilience, Kelly found ways to overcome her challenges, emphasizing the importance of perseverance and self-care to her children.
