Yusun Chang (Full)
Interviewee: Chang, Yusun
Chang Yusun's life story is a journey marked by resilience, sacrifice, and a deep sense of familial duty. Born in 1971 in Mongtanmyun as the youngest of six children, Chang's upbringing was shaped by both privilege and adversity. Despite facing challenges with his eyesight, Chang pursued education, attending high school in Gwangju. However, his dreams of attending a prestigious college were derailed, leading him to explore opportunities abroad. Determined to study in the United States, Chang diligently saved money while serving in the military to fund his education. Upon arriving in the States, Chang faced the harsh realities of financial strain, prompting him to expedite his studies and graduate within 14 months, even obtaining a master's degree. As an immigrant and student, Chang found himself straddling two worlds, navigating the divide between campus life and his cultural roots. Throughout his journey, Chang's heart remained tethered to Korea, particularly to his aging parents. Despite being separated from them since the age of 15, Chang's devotion to his family is unwavering. His deepest sorrow came with the loss of his father in 2007, a figure of strength and compassion who instilled in Chang the values of kindness and community service. Reflecting on his upbringing, Chang cherishes memories of his family's prosperity and the support they received from their community. Despite facing financial hardship later in life, Chang remains grateful for the generosity of those who once benefited from his family's prosperity. Amidst the challenges and triumphs of his life in the States, Chang finds solace in the milestones of his own family, celebrating the joys of marriage and the birth of his child. Yet, amidst the happiness, the shadow of his father's passing looms large, a reminder of the profound impact of love, loss, and the enduring bonds of family. As he looks towards the future, Chang's aspirations include a return to Korea to spend time with his aging mother.
Keywords: education; Gwangju, South Korea; military; immigration; United States; master's degree; student; loss; mourning; financial hardship; family; studying abroad; associate professor in electrical engineering; Kennesaw State University; Georgia Tech;

Yung Duk Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Yung Duk
Kim Yung Duk was born in 1934 in Changjeon, a small fishing harbor in North Korea. During his early education under Japanese colonization, he attended public school until the 5th grade, where speaking Korean was penalized, and students were often pitted against each other. His upbringing in a Japanese-dominated system left a lasting impression, as he still counts in Japanese. Kim's father ran a successful sardine business, but the rise of the North Korean Communist Party in the late 1940s led to persecution of landlords, business owners, and Christians. In 1947, to escape the Soviet influence and imminent danger, Kim's family fled to South Korea, settling first in Kangwondo and later in Daejeon. As a teenager, Kim supported his family by collecting and selling firewood in the local market. Due to the turmoil and displacement, Kim missed formal education in Hangul, the Korean alphabet. His mother, despite having no formal education herself, taught him to read and write Korean. Kim attended an unaccredited school run by poet Hoon Jung, who instilled in him a love for poetry, literature, and music. This period, enriched by dedicated teachers, was the best time of his life and pivotal in shaping his future. Kim's education continued through various relocations caused by political unrest, including the Yeosu-Suncheon Rebellion in 1948, where his brohter was assigned to investigate the incident by the government. His family him him to avoid guerilla recruitment during the Korean War. His older brother's assignment with the US on the POW exchange at Guje Island eventually brought the family to Guje, where Kim attended high school. Kim then attended Seoul National University and served as a civil engineer in the Navy. His exemplary service led to an opportunity to study in the United States. In 1963, he enrolled at the University of Western Ontario, earning a PhD. In 1974, he moved to New York, supported by a missionary scholarship he had received in Korea. Kim's wife, a PhD in medicine and an anesthesiologist, interned in New York, allowing Kim to join her. For four years, he put his career on hold to care for their children, an experience he highly recommends to other fathers. After completing his PhD, he found a job in New York and was soon offered a role as an engineering advisor in Saudi Arabia. There, he learned about Hyundai's contract for an offshore loading station and was recruited to work for Hyundai in Korea. Kim worked for Hyundai for 25 years. After retiring in 1997, he dedicated himself to improving the lives of Koreans in America. He joined the Korea Society board, helped establish Korean language classes in public schools, joined the voter's council, created the Research Center for Korean Community at Queens College, and founded the Korea Chamber of Commerce. His efforts have significantly contributed to the Korean community's development in the United States.

Charles Yoon, born in Seoul in 1963, had a diverse upbringing due to his father's diplomatic career, exposing him to various countries and cultures. Yoon served as president of the Korean American Lawyers Association of Great New York (KALAGNY) and supported organizations like KALCA, which was focused on civic mentorship to young Korean Americans. Steve Choi (Full)
Interviewee: Choi, Steve
Steve Choi, the executive director of the New York Immigration Coalition, holds over 15 years of community involvement. As a first-generation Korean American, his parents' journey from Korea to the US to pursue medical careers deeply influenced his strong will and motivation. With his parents growing up in post-war Korea, along with his grandparents' escape from North Korea, instilled in him a profound appreciation for history and resilience. Raised in a predominantly white neighborhood in 1980s Westchester County, he learned to navigate identity complexities, shaping his advocacy for immigrants today. His time at Stanford University ignited his passion for social justice, prompting an exploration of his Korean American identity. A transformative solo trip to Korea in 1996 enriched his understanding of familial roots and cultural background. Internships at the Korean Youth and Community Center in Los Angeles and the National Korean American Service & Education Consortium (NAKASEC) in Flushing, Queens further deepened his commitment to community organizing and social justice. Pursuing a master's degree in Korean studies at the University of Hawaii, Steve founded and led the Korean Students Association, fostering community and advocacy. His experiences fueled his resolve to address inequalities, leading him to law school with a focus on immigrant rights. Today, as the head of the New York Immigration Coalition, Steve's unwavering dedication to empowerment and justice continues to shape a more equitable future.

Reverend Jean Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Jean
Rev. Jean Kim was born in Hamhung City, North Korea. Living through the Japanese occupation of Korea and also the Korean War, she faced many hardships growing up, as she had to move around from place to place and also work to earn money. As she and her family escaped from Communist North Korea, they lived in Seoul, where one of her brothers joined the army to get an apartment to live in. However, after the breakout of the Korean War, Jean Kim never heard from her brother again. Her family ended up taking the train to Busan, where they lived homeless for three years until Jean moved to America in 1970. She was hoping to get her master’s degree in social work and also worked as a social worker at a mental health community center. She later formed her own Presbyterian Women’s Church and founded foundations to increase awareness for the homeless. She went from place to place, talking about this topic, and published five books. As one of the last things she wants to accomplish, she hopes to make one book in Korean and English, advocating to help out the homeless.

Unzu Lee, born during the Korean War to North Korean refugees, embarked on a journey marked by faith and advocacy. Joanne Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Joanne
Joanne Lee's was born in South Korea before immigrating to the US in 1990 to work as a nurse. Settling in Madison, Wisconsin, she raised two children, both of whom came out as transgender and queer during their teenage years. Joanne, influenced by her religious upbringing, initially struggled to accept her children for who they truly were. Tragically, Joanne's second child, Skylar, took his own life at 16 years old in 2016, prompting Joanne to confront the internal struggles faced by transgender children and their families. The outpouring of support at Skylar's funeral, attended by 500 people, revealed his impact as a racial justice activist and advocate for the black community at a young age.Inspired by Skylar's activism and the need for support for families with transgender children, Joanne became a transgender activist. She emphasizes the importance of love, support, communication, and advocacy for transgender children, striving to foster acceptance and inclusivity within the community. Her mission is to continue Skylar's legacy and promote greater understanding and acceptance of LGBTQ individuals, particularly transgender children.

Andrew Ahn (Full)
Interviewee: Ahn, Andrew
Andrew Ahn, a Korean American filmmaker, centers his work around his identity as a Korean American gay man. Born in LA, he grew up in a large Asian American community but began to understand his identity within the broader context of the Asian American immigrant experience. Attending Brown University, he found solace and inspiration within the Asian American community on campus, where he delved into diverse stories and experiences. Ahn's exploration of multiple identities within the social landscape led him to recognize the tendency of LGBTQ individuals to separate identity from culture. He grappled with his own fear of not being accepted by his family and losing his connection to his Korean identity, deeply rooted in his familial relationships. With a desire to understand the identity of Korean Americans across generations, Ahn aims to celebrate their existence while embracing diversity and various aspects of identity through his films. His short film "Dol" in 2010 marked the beginning of bridging the gap between his family and his identity, as he cast his parents without disclosing the film's theme, leading to conversations about his sexual identity. Ahn's full feature film, "Spa Night," garnered wide support, reflecting his aspiration to foster a sense of belonging and community while promoting solidarity through his work.

Heeja Park Pai (Full)
Interviewee: Pai, Heeja Park
Heeja Park Pai was born on June 7, 1945, in Seoul, Korea, living in Changshin-dong. She was the fifth child born in her family, and had one younger sister, and she recalls being a rather frail child, often the subject of teasing and bullying by her older siblings. It didn’t help that she didn’t like to eat rice, preferring bread instead, which in retrospect she attributes to an early desire to immigrate to the United States. She was five years old when the Korean War broke out, and though she doesn’t have many memories of this part of her childhood, she does remember how the family’s evacuation from Seoul felt more like a camping trip than an evacuation. She completed her primary and secondary education in Korea before immigrating to the United States on July 15, 1972, when she arrived in San Francisco on a student visa to attend SFU. After completing a semester of schooling, she met President Kim Dae Jung in San Francisco, and upon hearing that he’d be traveling to Washington D.C., traveled to DC as well at the behest of her uncle, who was working as a “trader” and had connections in the East Coast. After arriving in DC, she took a taxi to New York where she’d been offered a job at Korean Airlines, but was shortly laid off. Realizing she wouldn’t be able to stay in the United States for much longer without obtaining a more permanent visa, she opted to search for a sponsor when she came across the announcer Lee Kwan-gae, who said he’d offer sponsorship to someone who’d look over his children. She found an unexpected sponsor under a Jewish physician named Dr. Leslie, who had taken an interest in her art (calligraphy), and had been operating a kind of “boarding house” at his residence for students, fully sponsoring their tuition and lodging. Graced with this opportunity, Pai continued her schooling in New York, attending English classes at NYU for two years before matriculating into the Brooklyn Museum Art School, where she studied for 3 years. Soon, she met her husband, and had one daughter and one son; together, the family lived in the Leslie residence for a number of years. Around this time, too, her husband became ill, as did Dr. Leslie, and were in and out of the hospital for a number of years—she attributes her husband’s failing health to his commitment to his business, which required of him long work hours and many business trips which she took in his stead later into his life. Both her husband and Dr. Leslie passed within a short time span; at the time of her husband’s passing, Pai was in Korea and had to quickly secure a ticket back to New York, which had recently experienced 9/11. Both of her kids began families of their own, and Pai recounts a funny anecdote where she went with her son and her daughter-in-law to go apartment-hunting, assuming that she would live with his family, to her son’s (and daughter-in-law’s) surprise: she admits that this was something she wouldn’t ask of him today.
Keywords: Seoul; Korea; Changsin; frail; sick; break; United States; camping; San Francisco; SFU; Kim Dae Jung; Washington; Washington D.C.; New York; Korean Airlines; Lee Kwan-gae; sponsorship; Dr. Leslie; Art; Brooklyn Museum; NYU

Dju Hyun Park (Full)
Interviewee: Park, Hyun Dju
Dju Hyun Park was born into a wealthy high society family in North Korea in 1932. Raised in a compound with her family and servants, she received education in Korean and Chinese before attending a Japanese school during the Japanese occupation. Her grandfather's wealth, derived from pioneering grain and cotton mills, facilitated education for neighborhood children. Tragedy struck when her father died in a factory accident, prompting her mother to remain unmarried to ensure her children's care. As war erupted, the women of the family fled to South Korea, escaping with the help of villagers around the border. Meanwhile, the men established a textile factory in Daejeon but lost it to bombing during the war.Amidst the turmoil of war, Dju Hyun witnessed the invasion of Seoul, that involved seeing bodies and encountering hunger. The family hid the men from being drafted into the North Korean army and navigated war zones in search for food. Escaping to Busan, they made a living selling pollack and knitting sweaters. Eventually, they found refuge with a prosperous member of the Hyun family, and resided in his garage and hallway of the home. Dju Hyun pursued education in pharmacy and eventually sought a new life in the US, prompted by the recommendation of a potential husband studying at the University of Cincinnati. Settling in the US, she prioritized raising her three children, finding pride and fulfillment in motherhood.

Yoon Soo Park (Full)
Interviewee: Park, Yoon Soo
Yoon Soo Park was born on July 4, 1929, in Gimcheon, Korea. His parents, originally from North Korea, were involved in the Korean independence movement during the Japanese occupation before settling in Gimcheon. He and his six siblings grew up during this occupation, experiencing the imposition of Japanese language and military training in school, and working in rice fields. His teachers and parents, who were Christians, instilled in him a strong sense of the Korean language by reading the Korean Bible. When the Korean War began, Park was a student at Seoul National University. He and his brothers escaped and took 10 days to reach their hometown, surviving on potatoes. His family relocated to Busan and later settled in Seoul after their home in Gimcheon was bombed.His academic excellence led him to new opportunities abroad. In 1952, he moved to Canada through a scholarship program by a Canadian missionary organization, earning a master’s degree in nuclear physics from the University of Alberta. He later obtained a PhD in solid-state and semiconductor physics from the University of Cincinnati in the US. He was recruited by the Aerospace Research Laboratory in Dayton, Ohio, becoming the head of the semiconductor research lab. Sponsored by UNIDO, he worked with the Korean Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) to advance Korea’s technological development.Despite his decision to stay in the US, Park continually sought ways to support Korea and share his expertise through training and fundraising for Korean students and professors. In the US, he was active in Korean churches and served as chairman of organizations like the Korean American Scholarship Foundation (KASF) and the Korean-U.S. Science Cooperation Center (KUSCO). He remains grateful for his family, career, and the successes of his children and grandchildren.

Young Hae Han (Full)
Interviewee: Han, Young Hae
Young Hae Han was born in Busan in 1952, the youngest of six siblings. Her oldest sister was twelve years older. Her parents were musicians, and she grew up in a large house with a kiddie house and slides, enjoying a happy childhood. Her maternal grandfather, born in 1890, studied medicine at the University of Chicago and became a doctor in Hawaii, where he provided free services to Koreans and supported the Korean independence movement during the Japanese occupation. He was later exiled to Shanghai, where he died in 1941 after being tortured by the Japanese. Posthumously, he was honored by the Korean government in 1968 and is buried in the Korean National Cemetery in Seoul. Her mother, inspired by her father's legacy, became a famous singer and always wore a hanbok, representing her country with pride. Young Hae started playing the piano as a child and initially loved it, but her enthusiasm waned after beginning formal lessons. In 1975, at age 23, she moved to America from Gimpo, with her father renting a bus for family and friends to see her off. She settled in San Francisco, having chosen the United States over Germany due to the opportunity and the presence of her sister and many European musicians in the States. She attended the San Francisco Conservatory for her master’s degree, enjoying a close relationship with her professors. Despite being very homesick and crying daily, she immersed herself in studies and social activities. At Ewha University, frequent student protests closed the school often, allowing her to bond with friends. In San Francisco, however, she found the city lonely and boring, focusing on studying, practicing, and spending time with her nieces. Young Hae later attended the Eastman School of Music at Rochester University, where the small number of Korean students forced her to learn English. She felt lonely and became sick, but this isolation allowed her to improve significantly as a musician. She didn't experience much racism, as the focus in her community was solely on music. After having two children, Young Hae moved back to Korea in 1985, twenty years after first leaving. Concerned more about her children’s safety than her career, she encouraged them to adapt to their new environment without comparing it to America. Her children, initially enthusiastic about their Korean heritage, attended international school, with her daughter choosing an American name upon arrival in Korea. In America, her daughter faced racial bullying, prompting Young Hae to address the issue by educating her daughter’s classmates about Korea, which had a positive impact. Although she felt her career might have been stunted by motherhood, she believed her life experiences enriched her musical skills. Young Hae values freedom for her students and children, sometimes wondering if she should have pushed them harder academically. She allowed them to experience failure, believing it was an important part of their growth.
Keywords: San Francisco; North Korea; Japan; South Korea; Seoul; Busan; House; Pond; Music; Conservatory; Piano; Rochester University; Eastman School of Music; Vocal; Liberation; Japanese Occupation; Japanese Liberation; Freedom; Loneliness; International School; Ehwa Women's Unviersity; Medicine; Doctor; Hawaii; Shang Hai; Shanghai; Korean Red Cross

This video is the third of 11 SalGu LA Riots stories we will publish in 11 days to commemorate the 25th Anniversary of SalGu (4-29 LA Riots). Commander Blake Chow is the Assistant Commanding Officer - Operations West Bureau, Los Angeles Police Department (LAPD). Commander Chow recalls how he, as a young police officer in 1992, was called in for a nearly impossible task: to stop the thousands of people looting stores and protect the firefighters trying to save stores and lives. Blake Chow (Full)
Interviewee: Chow, Blake
Commander Blake Chow, currently the Assistant Commanding Officer in the Operations West Bureau of the Los Angeles Police Department (LAPD), shares his experiences and reflections on the 1992 Los Angeles riots, a pivotal event that significantly influenced both the LAPD and the communities it serves. With only two years of experience as a young officer at the time, Chow was thrust into the chaos of the riots that erupted following a highly contentious trial verdict. The riots lasted three days, leaving indelible marks on the city, its inhabitants, and the police force tasked with restoring order. Chow vividly recalls the overwhelming challenges faced by the LAPD during those three tumultuous days. The sky, tinged with an orange hue from the fires, served as a backdrop to the widespread looting and violence that stretched the police force thin. Amid the chaos, Chow and his fellow officers were inundated with looting calls, struggling to manage the situation and help those in need. The sense of helplessness was palpable, as officers who joined the force to serve and protect found themselves unable to stem the tide of unrest. Chow reflects on the fear and the focus on safety during the riots, emphasizing that it was only in the aftermath that the full impact of the events began to sink in. The riots revealed a mob mentality that posed a significant challenge to law enforcement. In response, the LAPD has since evolved its strategies for crowd control, learning valuable lessons on how to contain such situations more effectively. However, the events also highlighted the strained relationships between the police and minority communities. Chow acknowledges that the policing model of the late '80s, heavily focused on enforcement to combat the violence of the period, did not foster positive relations with these communities. He specifically mentions the disproportionate losses suffered in Koreatown, a reflection of the complex dynamics and challenges in protecting diverse communities during the riots. Commander Chow also addresses the criticism regarding the LAPD's response, including the controversial arrestment of store owners and the rumored orders to avoid Koreatown. He clarifies his personal focus on addressing the immediate crises, such as looting, and the logistical impossibility of avoiding specific areas given the scale of the emergency. Chow emphasizes the importance of distinguishing between peaceful protest and illegal activities that escalate into violence and destruction, underscoring the need for law enforcement to intervene and prevent the spread of mob mentality. Reflecting on the 25th anniversary of the riots, Commander Chow highlights the LAPD's transformative journey since those dark days. The department has fundamentally changed its approach to community engagement, working closely with diverse groups including Korean American, African American, Hispanic, and LGBTQ communities. These efforts, undertaken in partnership with organizations like Korean Churches for Community Development, have been crucial in rebuilding trust and fostering a more inclusive and responsive policing model. Through this video, part of the SalGu LA Riots stories series, Chow aims to shed light on the personal and institutional impacts of the riots, offering insights into the challenges faced by young officers like himself and
Keywords: Assistant Commanding Officer; Police Officer; Police Department; LAPD; Community; SaiGu LA Riots; Fear; Looting; Helplessness; Fires; Violence; Drugs; Gangs; Homicides; Crimes; Shootings; Enforcement; Diversity; Public Safety; Change

Carol Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Carol
Carol Kim's journey from a high school senior witnessing the tumult of the 1992 SaIgu riots in Los Angeles to becoming a prominent figure in public service and healthcare reflects a profound narrative of resilience, activism, and commitment to community empowerment. Born in Honolulu, Hawaii, and raised in Koreatown, Los Angeles, Carol's early experiences shaped her understanding of racial dynamics, community needs, and the power of advocacy. As an only child to Korean American parents who immigrated in 1973, Carol's upbringing in a vibrant yet complex cultural environment laid the foundation for her future endeavors. Graduating from high school as the SaIgu riots unfolded, Carol found herself at a crossroads, initially considering journalism to address the disparities in media representation she observed during the crisis. The riots, sparked by the acquittals in the Rodney King case and fueled by longstanding racial tensions, deeply impacted Carol. She witnessed firsthand the destruction in Koreatown and the insensitivity of mainstream media towards the affected Korean store owners. This experience, coupled with the memory of earlier incidents like the death of Natasha Harlans, steered her towards a path of activism and public policy. Pursuing higher education at UC Irvine, Carol engaged in student activism, joining the Korean Student Association and the Asian Pacific Associate, and delving into Asian American studies and public policy. Her journey led her to graduate school for Public Policy, further cementing her commitment to serving communities through informed, empathetic leadership. Currently serving as the Vice President of Community Investments and Public Affairs for Healthmet, a Fortune 200 HMO company, Carol oversees a portfolio that includes $170 million dedicated to enhancing healthcare and quality of care across California. Her role exemplifies the breadth of impact that can be achieved when public policy expertise is applied to corporate social responsibility. Reflecting on the racial tensions highlighted by the SaIgu riots, Carol criticizes the oversimplification of Black and Asian dynamics by the media, emphasizing the systemic issues at play. She points to immigration policy and other societal structures that perpetuate inequality, highlighting the importance of nuanced, informed discourse over sensationalism. Carol's activism during the riots, including organizing a significant protest against media misrepresentation, underscores her belief in community mobilization as a catalyst for change. She advocates for consistent civic engagement, not just through politics or public service, but also through voting, volunteering, and participating in dialogues that shape the societal fabric. As the 25th anniversary of the SaIgu riots approached, Carol hoped for a future where Korean Americans and all communities could thrive in diverse industries and sectors, representing a holistic vision of progress and inclusion.
Keywords: SaIgu riots; LA riots; Koreatown, Los Angeles; police; protesting; violence; homicide; public policy; politics; journalism; media; UC Irvine; public service; community; racial tensions; Healthmet; Asian American studies; Honolulu, Hawaii; Los Angeles, California; voting; civic engagement;

Inha Cho (Full)
Interviewee: Cho, Inha
Inha Cho was born in South Pyongan Province in what is today North Korea, and spent his childhood there before crossing the 38th parallel, arriving in the South. His daughter is in the room as the interview is taking place to clarify/ask questions. In 1965, Cho was sent to Vietnam as part of the Korean Marine Corps under the Park Chung-hee administration; although he recalls how many civilians and soldiers at the time were against deployment, many went to fight anyway, as the government promised good pay and benefits. In Vietnam, he sympathized with the villagers, and saw atrocities committed against them by the government; it was here that Cho says his support for the defenseless was turn into a strong sense of conviction against injustices. Upon his return, Cho was trained in traditional Korean medicine, and had a practice before moving to the United States in 1978, where he continued work with acupuncture. He was shocked at how many “bad” Koreans there were in America, or Koreans who extorted and blackmailed fellow destitute Koreans, and took it upon himself to prevent such actions from happening. Much later, during the Saigu Uprising, he heard on the new Korean-language radio channel, “Radio Korea,” that Koreatown was the site of uprising and looting following the acquittal of the LAPD officers involved with the beating of Rodney King. With a couple other Korean men, many younger than he, Cho and his crew drove five cars, each with one to three armed individuals, to wherever Radio Korea communicated was in need of backup. He told his crew not to shoot live rounds at anyone, but Cho himself was shot twice, in the thigh and ankle. He recounts how he wrapped his thigh in a plastic grocery store bag while the driver tried to get him to a hospital, but found Koreatown to have been barricaded off, with police moving away from the neighborhood to protect the “white” neighborhoods. Eventually the crew arrived at the ER, only for none of the hospital staff to give him care until he yelled at the staff that he was bleeding profusely. He credits God for saving his life that night, as well as his wife’s sincere prayers. Upon reflection, he found it difficult to accept how a country which boasts strength in diversity could also be the site of intolerable systemic injustice, although 25 years after the Uprising, he states that he continues to believe in the innate goodness of people, though he regrets how the mass media at the time of the Uprising portrayed it as a race conflict between the Korean and Black communities in Los Angeles; he believes that responsibility fell on everyone.
Keywords: SaIgu riots; LA riots; Koreatown, Los Angeles; police; Vietnam; violence; shooting; politics; journalism; media; public service; community; racial tensions; Los Angeles, California; voting; civic engagement; racism; diversity; North Korea, Radio Korea, Marine Corps, injustice

Inha Cho (Edited)

Inha Cho, president of the Korean Veterans Association in 1992, recalls gathering veterans of the Korean Marine Corps to go into the areas of rioting in order to protect Korean Americans and their livelihoods. He explains that the U.S. reserve army and local law enforcement protected white communities only, which naturally exposed Koreatown to looting and riots. He credits God for saving his life, as well as his wife’s sincere prayers.

Jinho Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Jinho
This video is the second of 11 SaIGu LA Riots stories we will publish in 11 days to commemorate the 25th Anniversary of SaIGu (4-29 LA Riots). Jinho Lee, a journalist at Radio Korea (KBLA-AM 1580) in 1992, recalls how Radio Korea became a makeshift command center during the LA riots. Korean Americans called the radio for help, information, and resources after realizing there was no response from the police and fire department. Jinho believes that the root issue behind the LA riots was that the government neglected South Central LA, but is hopeful that people have learned from their mistakes. He urges Korean Americans to continue to become educated and involved in politics and to learn from the Black community how to have a strong voice—recorded in LA in partnership with Hyepin Im of Korean Churches for Community Development.
Keywords: Radio Korea; Los Angeles riots; Saigu; racial tension; LAPD; economic hardship; racial dynamics; Los Angeles, California; government; South Central LA; police; reporting; Rodney King; community; journalism;

Jinho Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Jinho
Jinho Lee's journey into journalism began when he moved to the U.S. in 1980 and later joined Radio Korea in 1991. Initially aspiring to attend law school, Lee's career took a pivotal turn during the Los Angeles riots of 1992, a period that offered him an unexpected crash course in journalism. The scarcity of manpower at Radio Korea during the riots provided Lee with the freedom to cover a wide range of stories, significantly shaping his decision to pursue journalism as a career. This experience allowed him to witness firsthand the complexities of racial tensions and economic disparities within the city. Lee's reporting during the riots revealed a multifaceted narrative that extended beyond the surface-level racial conflicts between Black and Korean communities. Through interviews with Black individuals, Asians, and the LAPD, Lee uncovered a consensus that the root causes of the unrest lay in governmental neglect, economic hardship, and a lack of educational and job training opportunities in the area. Despite the racial dynamics highlighted by the media, Lee believed the core issues were more closely tied to systemic neglect by the city government, affecting both Black and Korean residents, rather than a simple racial animosity. One of Lee's significant observations was the frequent violence against store owners, a reality that underscored the daily risks faced by Korean Americans in certain neighborhoods. The conditions he encountered upon moving to LA, reminiscent of a "third-world country," with prevalent trash and graffiti, further emphasized the city's neglect. The situation was so dire that Radio Korea staff, including Lee, had to take extraordinary precautions, sharing a single bulletproof vest for reporting in dangerous areas and driving old cars that they could afford to lose to damage. The Rodney King incident and the subsequent trial, which highlighted police brutality and the systemic injustices faced by African Americans, also impacted Lee's perception, making him feel that Koreans too were victims of the city's failures. Lee's experiences during the LA riots transformed Radio Korea into a critical community resource, acting as a makeshift command center that provided Korean Americans with vital information and support in the absence of adequate police or fire department responses. Reflecting on the riots, Lee acknowledges the deep-seated issues of government neglect in South Central LA but remains hopeful that lessons have been learned. He emphasizes the importance of political engagement and education for Korean Americans, urging them to draw inspiration from the Black community's resilience and advocacy for a stronger collective voice. Through his career and insights, Jinho Lee exemplifies the role of journalism in bridging communities and fostering understanding amidst adversity.
Keywords: Radio Korea; Los Angeles riots; Saigu; racial tension; LAPD; economic hardship; racial dynamics; Los Angeles, California; government; South Central LA; police; reporting; Rodney King; community; journalism;

Richard Choi (Full)
Interviewee: Choi, Richard
Richard Choi (Young Ho), co-founder of Radio Korea, played a pivotal role in providing crucial coverage and aid during the 1992 Los Angeles riots, also known as the 4.29 incident. Arriving in America in 1974, Choi embodied the immigrant ethos of hard work and prosperity. However, the devastating events of 4.29 shattered the illusion of security for the Korean community. The riots, initially a conflict between the Black and White communities, ensnared Koreans, leaving them caught in the crossfire. In response to the chaos, Radio Korea intensified its coverage, only airing coverage about the riots for two weeks straight, and forgoing advertising- their only avenue of income. The station became a lifeline, facilitating communication among listeners, coordinating aid efforts, and rallying volunteers. Choi's leadership guided the station as it transformed into a hub of community support and resilience. President Bush's visit to Radio Korea was on the main events providing hope and healing to the Korean community. Post-4.29 Choi witnessed a surge in political engagement among Koreans, as they recognized the need for representation and empowerment. As violence escalated, Radio Korea faced a crucial decision: advocate for peace or support self-defense. Radio Korea, grappling with the dilemma, ultimately endorsed the protection of Koreatown, believing it was necessary to prevent further devastation. Choi stands by the decision, recognizing the imperative of community defense in the absence of external assistance. Richard Choi's role during the 1992 riots epitomizes the resilience and unity of the Korean community in the face of adversity, showcasing the power of media, collective action, and unwavering determination in times of crisis.
Keywords: LA Riots; 4.29; Radio Korea; April 29th, 1992; FEMA; Community Aid; George Bush; Gun Violence; Riots

Richard Choi (Edited)
Interviewee: Choi, Richard
Richard Choi is the current vice chairman of Radio Korea, and was the vice president of Radio Korea in 1992. He recalls the shift in mindset during the LA riots: from one of survival to one of protecting Koreatown and the community. He explains the central role that Radio Korea had during SaIGu and beyond: receiving and relaying information related to emergencies, fires, and looting during a time described as being “like a war zone.” The process of setting up a temporary FEMA center beautifully illustrates how the many different parts of the Korean American community came together during a time of crisis to support and serve one another.
Keywords: LA Riots; 4.29; Radio Korea; April 29th, 1992; FEMA; Community Aid; George Bush; Gun Violence; Riots

Han Sung Chang (Full)
Interviewee: Chang, Han Sung
Chang Hansung's arrival in America in 1987 shattered his preconceptions, as he encountered a reality starkly different from his expectations. Anticipating a serene and orderly environment, he was confronted with the harsh reality of urban life, where theft and danger lurked at every turn. In the volatile atmosphere of Koreatown, where safety was a luxury, solidarity among Koreans became paramount. As rumblings of impending riots grew louder, the Korean community mobilized to defend itself. Faced with the failure of law enforcement to respond, they took matters into their own hands, barricading roads and protecting their businesses. Pastor Kang Jong Min's prescient warnings had foretold the unrest, prompting proactive measures to safeguard lives and property. In the crucible of chaos, alliances and divisions emerged. Virginia Hughes, representing the Black Association, pleaded for restraint amidst the turmoil, fostering dialogue amid simmering tensions. Amidst the chaos, acts of intercommunity support offered glimmers of hope amidst the chaos. Reflecting on the upheaval, Chang Hansung identifies a fundamental lack of understanding and communication between communities as underlying causes. The historical transformation of Koreatown from a Black-dominated neighborhood to a multicultural enclave underscores the need for acknowledgment and reconciliation. Drawing lessons from the experience, Chang Hansung emphasizes the importance of political empowerment and preparedness in averting future crises. He warns of the potential for unrest in the wake of natural disasters and advocates for proactive measures such as obtaining business insurance to safeguard against unforeseen calamities.
Keywords: LA Riots, 4.29, Radio Korea, April 29th, 1992, Community Aid, Gun Violence, Riots, Japantown, Chinatown; Ministry; Christianity

Han Sung Chang (Edited)
Interviewee: Chang, Han Sung
In 1991, Han Sung Chang joined a youth group that provided protection services to Korean Americans, especially shop owners who were most at risk when they closed their shops at night. This group rapidly expanded from 24 members to over 360 members shortly after SaIGu started. In the absence of any intervention from the police and fire department, the youth group began to move out to protect Koreatown and the community. Reflecting on the events of the LA riots, Han Sung calls for the Korean American community to unite as one, in order to prevent tragedies of a similar kind.
Keywords: LA Riots, 4.29, Radio Korea, April 29th, 1992, Community Aid, Gun Violence, Riots, Japantown, Chinatown; Ministry; Christianity

Hyepin Im (Full)
Interviewee: Im, Hyepin
Hyepin Im's journey from a child immigrant to a prominent nonprofit leader is a testament to the power of faith, resilience, and advocacy. Arriving in the US in 1974 at the age of 7, Hyepin's early life was deeply influenced by her family's dedication to ministry. Growing up as a pastor's kid in Santa Ana, California, she faced the unique pressures and expectations of a religious household, compounded by the scarcity of Asians and Koreans in California at the time. Her parents were instrumental in leading tens of thousands to Christ, instilling in Hyepin a profound sense of faith and duty. Despite the challenges, including the struggle of living up to her parents' monumental achievements, Hyepin found her own path. Her experiences, from taking speech classes in high school to witnessing the complexities of community and racial dynamics firsthand, shaped her understanding of advocacy and leadership. The Saigu Riots of 1992, a pivotal moment in her life and in the Korean American community, profoundly influenced her. As an MBA student at USC during the riots, she was disheartened by the negative media portrayal of Koreans and felt a strong desire to correct the narrative and support her community. Hyepin's dedication led her to found the Korean Churches for Community Development (KCCD), focusing on empowering the Korean American community politically and economically. Her work, particularly around the Saigu campaign, seeks to honor the memory of the riots and ensure that the Korean American experience is recognized and respected within the broader tapestry of American history. She has faced challenges, such as the lack of empathy from other community leaders and the oversight of significant anniversaries by national figures, but these setbacks have only fueled her commitment to advocacy and unity. Through her leadership at Faith and Community Empowerment (FACE), Hyepin Im continues to work towards a vision where the Korean American narrative is not only heard but also valued as an integral part of America's story. Her life’s work is a beacon of hope for not just her community, but for all those who strive for justice and understanding across diverse cultural landscapes.
Keywords: ministry; christianity; faith; religion; evangelism; Koreatown, Los Angeles; racial tensions; saigu riots; MBA; University of Southern California; Korean Churches for Community Development (KCCD); Faith and Community Empowerment (FACE); nonprofit; advocacy; leadership;

Hyepin Im (Edited)
Interviewee: Im, Hyepin
Hyepin Im, an MBA student in 1992, recalls how the media falsely portrayed Korean Americans as the main aggressors during the LA riots, despite the 400 million dollars in property damage to Korean-owned businesses. The injustices inspired Hyepin to advocate for more political and economic empowerment of the Korean American community. She is the founder and president of Korean Churches for Community Development (KCCD). She hopes that SaIGu, a painful moment in history, will be redeemed as a catalyst to unite communities to achieve the American Dream together.

Joe Ahn (Full)
Interviewee: Ahn, Joe
Joe Ahn's journey from a young immigrant to an influential community organizer and advocate for Korean American businesses is both inspiring and poignant. Immigrating from Seoul to Los Angeles in 1976 at the age of 8, Joe's family navigated the challenges of adapting to a new culture while establishing their livelihood through various small businesses. Despite the language and cultural barriers, Joe's parents persevered, engaging in entrepreneurial ventures ranging from dry cleaners to managing a motel. Joe's academic pursuit at Fullerton College, where he majored in African American ethnic studies and political science, was driven by a deep interest in the Civil Rights movement and the shared struggles of minority communities in America. This interest was further fueled by the absence of an Asian American studies program, motivating Joe to engage deeply with the histories and challenges of other marginalized groups. His college years were marked by significant achievements, including becoming the first Asian elected president of the student body and initiating Asian week to celebrate Asian culture. These experiences not only showcased Joe's leadership skills but also his commitment to fostering understanding and appreciation for diverse cultures. The Saigu Riots were a turning point for Joe, personally and professionally. Working at the California State University system during the riots, he witnessed firsthand the devastation in Koreatown, including the damage to his parents' bakery. His efforts to protect his family's business underscored the dire situation many Korean American business owners faced, battling not just the immediate threats of violence but also the long-term economic impacts. In the aftermath of Saigu, Joe's work with Mike Wu, a mayoral candidate who vocally supported the Korean community, highlighted his dedication to political advocacy. Despite Wu's loss, Joe remained undeterred, focusing on legislative efforts to support Korean-owned businesses. His collaboration with attorney Paul to introduce a revitalization bill aimed at providing loans and tax credits for small businesses exemplified his commitment to the community's economic recovery. Joe's story is a testament to the resilience of the Korean American community in Los Angeles. His efforts to navigate the complexities of political advocacy, coupled with his personal experiences during the Saigu Riots, underscore the challenges faced by immigrant communities in seeking justice and recognition. Through fear, anger, and ultimately, legislative action, Joe Ahn contributed significantly to the ongoing struggle for equity and inclusion, marking an important chapter in the history of Korean Americans in Los Angeles.
Keywords: saigu riots; civic engagement; politics; Fullerton college; African American studies; political science; guns; violence; small businesses; legislation; immigration; Korean American; Koreatown, Los Angeles; elections; mayors; California Statue University system; economic development;

Joe Ahn (Edited)
Interviewee: Ahn, Joe
Joe Ahn recalls feeling both fear and anger during SaIGu: fear that his father would get hurt during the riots, and anger that the people who were most affected by the public’s expressed frustration with the government were the people who had the least resources. As businesses went bankrupt in Koreatown, new laws were created that effectively made it very difficult for the common types of Korean-owned businesses to reopen. Joe Ahn played a critical role in helping to pass a revitalization act that included components such as: loans and tax credits for Korean-owned businesses.

John Lim (Full)
Interviewee: Ahn, Joe
Born in Seoul, Korea, John Lim moved to the U.S. in 1967 at the age of 10, following his father who had arrived two years earlier to pursue graduate studies. Settling in Los Angeles' Koreatown, John grew up in a vibrant, ethnically diverse environment that shaped his early years. As the youngest of four siblings, John immersed himself in sports and built connections within his Korean community through church, while also navigating the broader multicultural landscape of his school life. John's academic journey took a pivotal turn during his high school years, leading him to focus intensely on his education in college. This determination paved the way for his successful career as a corporate lawyer, specializing in corporate and real estate law, and representing large corporations and institutional lenders. The tragic shooting of Natasha Harlans, a young African American girl, by a Korean American store owner, underscored deep-seated tensions between communities. This event, alongside the broader social justice issues it highlighted, deeply impacted John. In response to the aftermath of the 1992 Los Angeles riots—often referred to by the Korean American community as SaIGu—John leveraged his legal expertise to serve his community. Recognizing the urgent need for legal assistance, he assembled a pro bono team of over 80 Korean American lawyers to form an ad hoc law firm. This initiative focused on aiding victims with a range of issues from small business loans to insurance coverage, demonstrating a year-long commitment to rebuilding and advocacy. John's work during this period was not just about legal assistance; it was deeply intertwined with navigating complex social issues. The challenges faced by Korean American store owners, particularly concerning the Alcoholic Beverage License (ABL), highlighted the intricate dynamics between minority communities and the systemic issues at play. John's leadership in mobilizing legal support was a testament to his belief in justice and equity, beyond the immediate legal battles. Reflecting on SaIGu, John emphasizes the broader context of social justice, pointing out the role of media in shaping perceptions and the ongoing struggle against being labeled as foreigners, despite efforts to assimilate. His experiences and insights underscore the complexity of identity, community, and justice in a multicultural society. Currently, John Lim's roles extend beyond his legal practice. As a partner at LimNexus LLP, the immediate past Board Chair of the Council for Korean Americans, and the past Board Chair of Asian Americans Advancing Justice—Los Angeles, John continues to influence and contribute to discussions on social justice, equity, and the empowerment of Korean Americans and broader Asian American communities.
Keywords: lawyer; pro bono; Saigu riots; racial tension; systemic ssies; Alcoholic Beverage License (ABL); Korean Americans; legal practice; advocacy; Asian Americans Advancing Justice; Koreatown Los Angeles; LimNexus LLP; Council for Korean Americans; Brown vs. Board of Education;

John Lim (Edited)
Interviewee: Ahn, Joe
John Lim, then president of the Korean American Bar Association, recalls his experience of SaIGu and how he was moved to mobilize a team of over 80 lawyers to provide pro bono legal services for Korean Americans in the aftermath of SaIGu. John notes that the media focused on the tension between Korean Americans and African Americans, but believes the fundamental issue was and is about social justice and equity for all. He is currently a partner at LimNexus LLP and is immediate past Board Chair of the Council for Korean Americans, as well as the past Board Chair of Asian Americans Advancing Justice—Los Angeles.

Michael Woo (Full)
Interviewee: Woo, Michael
Born and raised in Los Angeles, Michael Woo's family was among the first Chinese American families to settle in Monterey Park, California, during the 1960s. This early experience in a multicultural yet tense environment shaped Woo's understanding of racial dynamics, setting the stage for his future endeavors. His decision to attend UC Santa Cruz and pursue urban planning was deeply influenced by the economic and racial tensions he observed, including riots that underscored the societal need for thoughtful urban development and racial reconciliation. Despite growing up in the multiethnic Crenshaw district and experiencing less overt racial tension in his youth, Woo's journey was not devoid of challenges related to his ethnic identity. His Americanized upbringing and loss of the Cantonese language pointed to a broader narrative of assimilation and cultural disconnection experienced by many second-generation immigrants. However, it was his venture into politics that truly confronted him with the harsh realities of racial prejudice, marking a turning point in his personal and professional life. Elected as the first Asian American city council member in Los Angeles, Woo's political career was both groundbreaking and challenging. His background as an urban planner and his vision for revitalizing Hollywood played crucial roles in his election, demonstrating the potential for targeted local policies to resonate with diverse urban populations. His outspoken stance against LAPD Chief Gates following the Rodney King case, despite the political risks, underscored his commitment to addressing systemic issues within the city's law enforcement and his broader advocacy for racial justice. The riots that engulfed Los Angeles became a defining moment in Woo's career, not only in terms of the immediate political and social response required but also in reflecting on the city's underlying fractures. The smoke that filled the air, serving as a metaphor for the city's pervasive unrest, highlighted the interconnectedness of Los Angeles' communities despite their apparent separateness. Woo's insight into the city's emergency response infrastructure during the riots further emphasized the need for systemic improvements to address urban crises effectively. Woo's decision to relinquish his council seat to run for mayor, though ultimately unsuccessful, did not diminish his impact on the city's political landscape. As the first Asian American elected to the Los Angeles City Council and a pioneer in calling for police reform, Woo's contributions to racial reconciliation and urban planning have left lasting marks. Now serving as the dean of the College of Environmental Design at California State Polytechnic University, Pomona, Woo continues to blend his political leadership skills with his urban planning expertise, shaping future generations of urban planners and designers equipped to tackle the complex challenges of modern cities.
Keywords: urban planning; politics; city councilmember; election; Bill Clinton; Monterey Park, California; Chinese American; Cantonese; LAPD; SaIgu riots; Rodney King; California State Polytechnic University; Los Angeles City Council; Koreatown Los Angeles; racial tensions; systemic issues;

Michael Woo (Edited)
Interviewee: Woo, Michael
Michael Woo was the first Asian American elected to the Los Angeles City Council, and served from 1985 to 1993. Despite risk to his political career, Michael was the first Los Angeles City Council member to call for the resignation of the LAPD police chief after the Rodney King case, and thereafter became a leader in racial reconciliation efforts. Michael is currently the dean of the College of Environmental Design at California State Polytechnic University, Pomona, for which he has combined his political leadership ability and urban planning background to successfully execute his position.

TC Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Woo, Michael
TC Kim’s family immigrated from Korea to the U.S. in the mid-70s when he was just 14. Settling first in Tucson, Arizona, his family, led by his father, a church pastor, embarked on a mission to establish new churches for the Korean community. This mission saw TC moving across the U.S., from Arizona to Texas and finally to Los Angeles, California, navigating through the complexities of cultural shock, racism, and the challenges of assimilation into American society. In his early years in the U.S., TC faced the harsh realities of being an outsider. Being the first Asian kid in his school in Tucson, he encountered racism firsthand. His unique way of coping with bullying—claiming to be related to Bruce Lee—reflects his early struggles with identity and belonging. These experiences were compounded by frequent moves due to his father's church-building efforts, forcing TC to adapt to new environments repeatedly, from adopting and then dropping a Southern accent, to adjusting to the diverse cultural landscape of Los Angeles. Education played a significant role in TC's life, with expectations set high for him as the eldest son. At UCLA, he pursued majors in Psychology and Anthropology, diverging from the traditional career paths expected of him. His father's influence was profound, inspiring TC to become a journalist through involvement with the Christian Press, a newspaper publication his father started. The Los Angeles riots of 1992 were a pivotal moment in TC's life, occurring shortly after the death of his father. The chaos and violence of the riots brought significant loss and fear into his life, with his family's bookstore being looted. TC's response to the crisis—capturing the events through photography, assisting in translation for victims, and his subsequent involvement with the LAPD's Community Police Advisory Board (CPAB)—demonstrates his deep commitment to his community and to fostering understanding and cooperation between the police and the community. TC's personal life has been marked by both tragedy and resilience. The loss of his wife to breast cancer, his experiences as a single parent to five boys, three of whom serve in the military, and his engagements with single-family ministries and support for the divorced and widowed, reflect his enduring strength and commitment to family and community. His founding of the United Taekwondo Organization, with its emphasis on Christian faith and responsible use of skills, along with his work to support victims of crime, illustrates his ongoing efforts to contribute positively to society. Despite the hardships and losses, TC Kim's story is one of hope and progress. His involvement in initiatives like the CPAB and his efforts to improve police-community relations highlight the potential for positive change.
Keywords: journalist; anthropology; psychology; culture shock; pastor's kid; Christinaity; Koreatown, Los Angeles; Saigu Riots; police; LAPD; Arizona; Texas; California; Community Police Advisory Board (CPAB); photography; United Taekwondo Organization; community; ministry; breast cancer; widow; divorce; family; victims;

TC Kim (Edited)
Interviewee: Woo, Michael
TC Kim, a journalist in 1992, hit the streets to capture photos during the LA riots, even though his wife asked him to stay home. Despite being chased at one point by angry looters who noticed his camera, TC remained committed to his work as a journalist. While he has been personally and profoundly affected by the losses and grief of SaIGu, TC remains hopeful of the progress that has been and will be made. He notes that among many different organizations founded in 1993, the year after the riots, the LAPD launched the Community Police Advisory Board (C-PAB), which facilitates direct communication, connection, and collaboration between the police and leaders in the community.

Andy Marra (Edited)
Interviewee: Marra, Andy
Andy Marra is a Korean American adoptee and leader in LGBTQI advocacy. In this continuation of the Love/Hate Project series, Andy talks about what the process of coming to terms with her identity was like and why being a transgender individual added a layer of fear in looking for her birth mother. “I was deathly afraid of coming out to my family, or the prospect of coming out to my family, even though I knew that at a very young age, I wanted to look for them and know more about my story.” This is the third installment in the Love/Hate series of the Legacy Project, highlighting the Korean Adoptee experience and its connection to mental health.

Andy Marra (Full)
Interviewee: Marra, Andy
Being adopted at six months old to a white family from South Korea, Andy reveals many aspects of his life that effectively changed him into the person she is today. Identifying as a transracial adoptee, she explains that throughout his teen years, he struggled "to navigate so many different identities and communities all at once." It was a lot to take in for both her and her adoptive parents, but through all of this, she sends great gratitude to her biological mother, who supported her throughout the whole transitioning process. Meeting her biological mother for the first time in Korea, Andy explains the fact that her mom already knew that she was different from the others through her birth dreams. However, she didn't use that fact to hate on her and never see her again; she only gave encouragement and love, which Andy greatly needed at the time. Andy reveals that it was because of her biological mom that she was able to gain more confidence in her queer identity.

The path to self-discovery can be complicated perhaps even more so for an adoptee. Jae was adopted at 4 months old from Seoul, South Korea, yet she did not come to terms with her Asian identity until college. Now, there’s no question about it. As a Korean adoptee, it’s never been easy opening up that part of herself to others. She shares, “It could get really dark.” This is the first installment in the Love/Hate series of the Legacy Project, highlighting the Korean Adoptee experience and its connection to mental health. Adopted to two Caucasian parents at just 4 months old, Jae expands on her journey of fully discovering herself and understanding her Asian-American identity. She reveals how she never really knew that she was Asian until college and how she regrets not being able to connect with her ethnic culture sooner. Even though she is grateful to have such amazing people by her side, she dislikes that she can't freely open herself up to them without having to worry. She is taking therapy in hopes of bettering her mental health; she is happy with the life she is living now and is still on the path to self-discovery. Showing gratitude towards her adoptee experience, she describes her journey as complex, challenging, special, and loaded.

Michael McDonald was adopted to the U.S. at three months old. When he visited Korea in 2007, Michael learned that he was named after the hospital he was born in. He shares that for adoptees, believing in themselves is most important when dealing with feelings of alienation from societies that don't accept them. This is the fourth installment in the Love/Hate series of the Legacy Project, highlighting the Korean Adoptee experience and its connection to mental health. Follow us on these stories to hear about those in our community who have fought to overcome these complex struggles.
Keywords: South Korea; New Jersey; Adoptee; Parents; Adoptive Identity; Culture; Kang Dong Gil; Love-hate relationship; culture shock; believe; own identity

Michael McDonald (Full)
Interviewee: McDonald, Michael
Michael McDonald, born in Masan, South Korea, was adopted by an American family at three months old. Growing up in a rural town in New Jersey, he talks about his experiences during his childhood and teenage years and how his adoptive identity came to affect his life. Carrying two different racial identities, he reveals that there were many hard moments in his life, feeling alone and afraid of how he could transform into the man he desired to be. Experiencing life in both America and Korea, he realized there would never be a place where he completely fit in, but he had to try his best to form a new environment where he felt the most comfortable. Now, as a fully grown adult, he shares that believing in oneself is key for adoptees who may feel alone. He also advises possible adoptive parents to provide several opportunities for their children to meet up with other adoptees who may have had similar experiences, as he, himself, felt that it had helped him cope with the feeling of alienation.

Michael Pulliam (Edited)
Michael Pulliam clearly remembers the time when he was punched squarely in the face right after he boarded the school bus. He was bullied physically and psychologically all through elementary school, and it has further enforced his feelings of otherness. He has battled and survived suicidal thoughts and attempts, but some of his friends did not survive the depression and loneliness, leaving him with guilt, rage, and despair. He feels he has been tested to his limits and feels stronger for surviving the battles.

Michael Pulliam (Full)
Michael Pulliam shares his experience recognizing his identity as Korean American adoptee. Facing harsh racism and bullying from an early age, Michael comes to share how his struggles have shaped the person he is today, both mentally and physically. Moreover, expanding on isolation, Michael highlights the obstacles of finding friends he truly felt he belonged with and could rely on. Further sharing his experience with mental abuse, Michael comes to share how he has overcome his feelings of depression and despair through his childhood and even through the scars of the deaths of his friends.

Alex Myung Wager (Edited)
“I certainly have a love/hate relationship with being adopted, but I think it’s a little different in the sense that I’ve been given many more opportunities to openly live as a gay male because I was adopted…” Born in Daegu, South Korea and raised in Albany, New York since he was four months old, Alex Myung Wager struggled with his identity as not only a Korean adoptee but also a gay man. It wasn’t until his trip to Seoul about ten years ago that Myung finally learned of his birth family. Now, after coming out to his adopted family and gaining acceptance from them, Myung grapples with the question of whether or not to reach out to his birth family, unsure of how they might perceive his sexuality or if his half-brother even knows about him. This is the eighth installment in the Love/Hate series of the Legacy Project, highlighting the Korean Adoptee experience, and the connection to mental health.
Keywords: Daegu; South Korea; Albany; New York; Korean Culture; Camp; Gay Identity; 10th Grade; Biological Parents; Half Brother; Love/Hate; Adoptee; Opportunity; Struggle; Support

Alex Myung Wager (Full)
Adopted at four months old to an American family in New York, Alex Myung elaborates on his life as an adoptee but also as a person with a queer identity. Growing up, Alex reveals that he didn't really face any struggles as an adoptee, but more so because of his gay identity. Coming to terms with his gay identity was more of a bigger problem for him than thinking about his adoptive identity. However, as he became an adult and started attending conferences like KAAN, Alex explains that he could then understand that all adoptees were not the same and had such different experiences, where not all of them were as bright and positive as his. Because he was raised in such a supportive environment, he didn't realize that other adoptees might have been in different situations. Hence, he wants to advise people not to judge adoptees on how they think because they were all raised in different environments with different situations. Furthermore, Alex hopes that he could have the chance to meet his biological family one day whenever he is mentally prepared.

Adopted. While sharing her journey of understanding her true identity as a Korean American Adoptee, Lee-Ann shares some of the struggles she faced living in a community where she had no one to relate to or genetically connect with. Growing up with the idea of becoming a "perfect" child, she emphasizes some of the standards she thought she had to live up to to fill in the missing gap of her non-biological Korean sister. Moreover, as a person who identifies as an adoptee, Lee-Ann hopes to bring more recognition and empowerment to the adoptee community and create a society where adoptees can feel a sense of belonging as real people and not just some form of commodity. Lynn Richards-Noyer (Full)
Interviewee: Richards- Noyer, Lynn
Lynn Richards Noyers was born in Gwangju, Korea and was adopted into a German Jewish English family when she was 9 years old. Despite the late adoption, she quickly adapted to life in Long Island with her siblings. However, her teenage years were marked by struggles with an eating disorder and an abusive marriage in her early twenties. During these challenging times, she began to question her identity and her friends became her pillars of support, offering strength and companionship. Curiosity about her birth family intensified after becoming a mother herself, leading her to participate in a televised search with Global Overseas Adoptee's Link (GOAL). It proved to be successful and she was able to connect with her birth mother via Skype, a momentous occasion that stirred intense emotions. A face to face meeting in Korea followed, but language barriers and cultural disparities hindered their relationship. Despite her longing to connect with her roots, Lynn found herself growing apart from her birth family due to these challenges. She holds mixed feelings about this, balancing gratitude for knowing her origins with frustration at the communication barriers. Lynn found support and solace in groups like Also- Known-As, Inc. and other Korean American networks, where she could share her experiences with others who had undergone similar journeys. Through these connections, she found a sense of belonging and community, enriching her journey of self-discovery and identity.

She also talks about her past with an abusive ex-husband and expresses her gratitude for the incredible support of her girlfriends and groups, such as Also-Known-As, Inc., for helping her through some of her hardest times.

Meg Campbell (Full)
Interviewee: Campbell, Meg
Meg Campbell, a 38-year-old nurse at Camp Sejong, was born in Seoul, South Korea, and adopted at the age of five. She grew up in Upstate New York alongside three older adoptee sisters, navigating the complexities of adoptee life. The isolation of her environment often weighed heavily on her, yet her relationship with her sisters, especially her oldest sister Joy, provided a stabilizing force. During her middle school years, Meg grappled with her identity and experienced a notable strain in her relationship with her parents. She perceived that they favored her over her sister Beth, which led to significant familial tension and resulted in Meg not speaking to her parents for six months. This strain was evident even years later, as her parents did not attend her wedding, which occurred 8 or 9 years ago. Despite these challenges, Meg has forged strong bonds with her adoptee sisters, who have been a constant source of support. These relationships have instilled in her a desire to connect with her birth siblings, preferring this connection over discovering who her birth parents are. Currently, Meg is married with two children and has no interest in searching for her birth parents. Identifying more with her American upbringing than her Korean heritage, Meg considers the relationships with her adoptee sisters as pivotal in shaping her life. Her experiences have left her curious about potential siblings but content with the family dynamics she has cultivated with those around her.
Keywords: korean adoptee; family; strained relationships; adoptee sisters; Camp Sejong; tension; identity; isolation

Meg Campbell (Edited)
Interviewee: Campbell, Meg
Meg Campbell grew up in Upstate New York with her 3 other adopted sisters, where she felt a strong sense of isolation and loneliness due to the strained relationship with her parents. She credits her sisters, in particular her older sister Joy, whose relationship with Meg has help ground her.

Michael Mullen (Full)
Interviewee: Mullen, Michael
Michael Mullen, a 47-year-old from Seoul, South Korea, has a poignant narrative marked by complex family dynamics and personal discovery. Adopted at the age of three from an orphanage, he grew up in Spokane, Washington—a predominantly white area—alongside three domestically adopted siblings who are white. Michael remains close with his adoptive family, although one of his adoptive sisters has ceased contact due to personal issues. Despite several moves back to Korea as an adult, Michael initially refrained from searching for his birth parents to avoid potentially painful revelations. Recently, he accessed his adoption papers for the first time, learning that his biological mother had been killed and his aunt, unable to care for him, had left him at a police station. This information discouraged him from further inquiries to avoid the emotional toll of confirming these details. Michael is married with two sons, ages 12 and 15. Growing up, he struggled to connect with his Korean identity but has embraced it more as an adult, particularly after engaging with other adoptees. His turning point came when he gave a presentation on his adoption story at Queens College, where he found comfort and community among fellow adoptees, leading him to realize the importance of this connection in his life. Now, Michael is keen to share his adoptee story more broadly, participating in the Legacy Project's Love/Hate series, which explores the Korean adoptee experience and its ties to mental health. Through sharing his journey, Michael aims to lessen the stigma around discussing adoption and to foster understanding and solidarity among adoptees.
Keywords: korean adoptee; orphange; Spokane, Washington; white family; adopted siblings; birth parents; Seoul, South Korea; KAAN; stigma; adoptee community; identity; mental health;

Duk Sun Chang (Full)
Interviewee: Chang, Duk Sun
Chang Duk Sun's life journey reflects the complexities of immigration, parenthood, and the pursuit of a better life. Born in Jeollanamdo, he experienced both prosperity and adversity in his early years, from the affluence of his grandfather's wealth to the struggles of surviving the aftermath of the Korean War. Joining the navy provided Chang Duk Sun with opportunities to travel but also posed challenges in finding stability and starting a family. Immigrating to the United States brought its own set of challenges, including court orders threatening his return to Korea. However, with the support of his German wife, Chang Duk Sun was able to navigate these obstacles and build a life for his family in America. Despite the hardships of immigration, Chang Duk Sun is grateful for the opportunities his daughters have as Americans. He treasures their education and achievements, yet harbors regrets about not being able to provide more financial support. His dedication to his daughters is evident, as he fondly recalls their graduations and employment milestones as the happiest moments of his life. He regrets not being more present and affectionate towards his daughters during their upbringing. Reflecting on his career as a gem cutter and later working in various locations like Hawaii and Las Vegas, Chang Duk Sun demonstrates resilience and adaptability. Despite the challenges he faced, he found solace in knowing that his daughters were healthy and thriving. Now, divorced from his first wife but content with his new partner, Chang Duk Sun remains focused on his daughters' well-being and feels grateful for the blessings he has, even as he grapples with the guilt of past decisions.
Keywords: Yeosu Rebellion; Law School; Lawyer; Hawaii; Gemcutter; Navy; Brazil; Korean War; 6.25; Education

Duk Sun Chang (Edited)
Interviewee: Chang, Duk Sun
Duk Sun Chang struggled all his life working as a gemcutter, a back-breaking occupation. He reminisces about the toll the hard labor has taken on his body and the stress of struggling financially. After 40 years struggling to make ends meet, he expresses his sadness and regret in not being able to leave an inheritance for his two daughters, but he is proud of them and hopeful that their accomplishments which came from their own hard work will be important life-lessons for them.

Myung Sook Cha (Full)
Interviewee: Myung Sook, Cha
Cha Myungsook, born on October 7, 1955, in Gwangju, grew up in a large family with eight siblings. Despite being the eighth of nine children, she maintained close ties with her family, especially her eldest brother who had moved to the United States when she was young. Motivated by a desire to support her grandparents after they faced financial hardship due to a con, Myungsook immigrated to America with hopes of earning enough money to assist them. However, she soon realized the challenges of making a living in a new country. Initially planning to return to Korea once she obtained a green card, she eventually settled down after getting married. Myungsook worked as a nutritionist and later at her older brother's stationary store, always driven by the belief that hard work would lead to success. Despite her intentions to return to Korea, she found herself adapting to life in America, albeit with a lingering sense of unfulfilled obligations. As her daughter pursued higher education, Myungsook harbored hopes of her becoming a lawyer, although she ultimately respected her daughter's choices. Reflecting on her experiences during the Gwangju uprisings, she views protests as a last resort, advocating for political action to prevent such unrest. Myungsook's dream is to establish a bed and breakfast where weary travelers can find respite—a testament to her compassionate nature and desire to provide comfort to others.

Myung Sook Cha (Edited)

Myung Sook Cha came to the US with the intent of earning enough money to go back to Korea to take care of her father, only to discover that life in America was not as profitable nor verdant as she hoped and believed. After suffering major disappointments in her life, including a failed marriage, “I just worked diligently like an ant”, she recounts.
