Nellie Sung (Full)
Interviewee: Sung, Nellie
Nellie Sung's life journey epitomizes the complexity and depth of the adoptee experience. Born in Seoul, South Korea, Nellie was adopted by an American family when she was only 7-8 months old and raised in the twin cities of Minnesota. Despite the love and middle-class comforts provided by her family, including an older biological sister, Nellie grappled with a profound sense of alienation. Being the only person of color in her Catholic school further intensified her feelings of not belonging—a sentiment echoed throughout her youth. Nellie's parents encouraged her connection to her Korean heritage, evident from their thoughtful gifts like a Hanbok for Christmas and her involvement in Korean School and Korean adoptee camps. However, she faced significant challenges, including battling depression triggered by harsh bullying during her formative years. College marked a pivotal shift in Nellie's life. She embarked on a journey of self-discovery, pursuing a major in Women's Studies and later a master's degree in Racial and Ethnic Studies from Trinity College Dublin. Her senior year took her back to South Korea for a semester at Yonsei University, a challenging but ultimately transformative experience that allowed her to blend into a crowd for the first time. Nellie's professional life flourished upon her return to the United States, writing for the Korea Times and teaching in Korea before settling in large cities like New York. Her personal life also prospered, marrying in 2004 and starting a family. Throughout, she remained deeply engaged in social justice, particularly through her involvement in an Asian Women's Organization. A poignant moment in her life was when, at age 22, she escorted an adoptee on a flight to the U.S., reuniting with this individual years later through social media. This encounter stirred a mix of emotions about Korea's adoption practices, reflecting her complicated relationship with her birth country. Nellie's journey underscores a powerful narrative of searching for identity, facing cultural dissonance, and ultimately forging a path that honors both her origins and her own unique life story.
Keywords: Adoptee; adoption; Minnesota; middle class; alienation; isolation; bullying; depression; belonging; identity; self-discover; Women's Studies; Yonsei University; study aborad; Trinity College Dublin; Racial and Ethnic Studies; Asian Women's Organization; korean school; reunion

Nellie Sung (Edited)
Interviewee: Sung, Nellie
“…I was so scared of what her life might be like. I was very upset with Korean people and society for just the whole industry…you’re the only developed country that has exported children.” Adopted from Seoul as a baby, Nellie Sung, the only person of color at her schools in Minneapolis, never felt like she fit in. It wasn’t until her senior year of college that Sung’s curiosity brought her back to South Korea to study abroad for a semester at Yonsei University, where unfamiliarity with the culture made it tough for her to adjust. Sung ended up volunteering at the orphanage where she was adopted, and then, on a trip back to the U.S., escorted a newly adopted baby — with whom she would heartwarmingly reunite many years later.

Seungjin Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Seungjin
Seungjin Lee, born in 1978 in South Korea, immigrated to the United States in 1988 at the age of ten with his family after a decade-long process initiated when he was born. Settling in the Bronx, New York rather than Flushing, his parents chose this to encourage quicker assimilation into American culture. They sought a better life away from the political instability of South Korea during its dictatorship era, fearing their children might be influenced by communist ideologies. Seungjin's father, who had previously run a glassmaking factory near the DMZ, ventured into owning a dry cleaners in the U.S. despite the family's initial financial struggles with only $2,000 to their name. The cultural shift included exploring American cuisine, with his father experimenting with various foods, from Vienna sausages to clam chowder. While Seungjin's older sisters faced challenges integrating into American society, Seungjin himself adapted quickly, seemingly oblivious to any discrimination, and was indifferent about the move. At home, religious and ideological differences presented challenges, with his mother being very religious and his father critical of the church. Unique among Korean immigrant stories, Seungjin’s father encouraged his children to pursue arts rather than conventional paths like law or medicine. Seungjin himself graduated with a double major in Fine Arts and Management Throughout his life, Seungjin has felt blessed. He is now a father to three children and values the sacrifices his own father made, which he reflects upon deeply, especially during familial events like the burial of his grandfather in Korea, a moment that connected him physically and emotionally to his heritage. In his Legacy Project reflections, Seungjin recounts the arduous transitions and sacrifices, highlighting his father’s journey from North to South Korea during wartime and their shared struggles and resilience upon moving to the U.S. These narratives underscore the profound impact of his family’s migration on his personal and professional life.
Keywords: North Korea; Korean War; immigration; assimulation; Bronx, New York; dry cleaners; American food; communism; fine arts; management; gratitude; burial; sacrifice; resilience

Seungjin Lee - Part 1 (Edited)

In the first half of this two-part Legacy Project installment, Seungjin Lee, now a father himself, provides an intimate retrospective on his father’s sacrifices and trials. From Vienna sausages to daycare, Lee, who immigrated to the Bronx at the age of ten, recalls the many day-to-day challenges he and his family faced after his father’s decision to leave South Korea with only $2,000 in his pocket. “Those are the sort of struggles that I couldn’t even fathom, having a family of my own now, with children.”

Yung Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Yung
Yung Kim, born in 1960, has a rich history shaped by his family's experience through various governmental changes in Korea. His grandfather witnessed the transition from Korea's Imperial government established in 1903 to Japanese occupation, and the subsequent division of Korea post-WWII. Yung’s parents, originally from different parts of Korea, met in Seoul, influencing Yung's sense of identity, which was further shaped during college when he received a scholarship from a North Korean organization. In 1982, Yung moved to the U.S., where he has since been an active member of his local Korean church for 35 years, engaging deeply in various roles from teaching youth about Korean culture and biblical studies to participating in the choir. His commitment extends beyond the church as he volunteers at a senior center and is involved in Korean civil movement organizations advocating for justice in Korean society. Professionally, Yung’s journey began with a bachelor’s degree in mechanical engineering in Korea, followed by a transition to computer science in the U.S. where he eventually worked for IBM for 15 years. Post-IBM, he explored international opportunities, connecting with a Korean company and the Mexican state department on projects related to waste water treatment. Throughout his life in the U.S., Yung has focused on human rights and societal justice, inspired by the respect he received upon his arrival. His early years in the U.S. were challenging, marked by depression and struggles with language and cultural integration, but support from his wife and work colleagues, as well as his faith community, provided him with a strong support system. Yung's commitment to social change is also evident in his involvement with Father’s School, a program that encourages Korean fathers to abandon authoritarian roles for deeper emotional connections with their families. His niece, Nina Joung, interviewed him for the Legacy Project, highlighting his efforts to bridge cultural gaps within the Korean-American community. Yung's story is a testament to his dedication to his heritage, community service, and the pursuit of justice.
Keywords: Korean church; church community; IBM; mechanical engineering; computer science; immigration; language barrier; depression; human rights; advocate; non profit organization; volunteering; Father's School Organization; korean community; North Korea; WWII; South Korea; justice;

Yung Kim (Edited)
Interviewee: Kim, Yung
In this special #FathersDay Legacy Project video, Yung Kim, who attended and helped develop the Father School program in New York, shares what he's learned about fatherhood and family. Yung Kim, interviewed by his niece Nina Joung, came to America at 22 years old, volunteering at nonprofit organizations while serving at his church as a youth group teacher. In this Legacy Project, Kim shares how while serving, he saw a need to help bridge the cultural gap between the 2nd gen students and the 1st gen parents. Kim also shares his experience being involved in civil movement organizations where they advocate justice in Korean communities. Father School is a special educational program that was started in 1995 at Duranno Bible College where men are encouraged to challenge the traditional Korean authoritarian father role in order to emotionally connect better with their wives and children.

Hyun Joon Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Hyun Joon
Hyun Joon Lee, born in Seoul and raised in Indonesia, embarked on a richly diverse educational and cultural journey before settling in the Bronx, New York. Her path to the U.S. began unexpectedly when she met her future husband at Yonsei Medical School in 1991. It was love at first sight; they dated for 10 months before getting engaged, and she moved to the U.S. the day after their wedding in 2000, primarily motivated by her relationship. Despite her initial lack of intention to move to the U.S., Hyun adapted quickly to life in New York, bringing with her a deep-rooted value system influenced by her Korean upbringing. Growing up in Indonesia, she attended an international school and was fluent in Indonesian, but the core of her community and values remained distinctly Korean. This foundation was further reinforced during her time in Korea for college, making her transition there seamless. Hyun’s professional journey is as multifaceted as her personal life. She initially attended Cornell for summer schools, despite her father's reservations about her becoming too Americanized, and eventually pursued a career in integrative medicine and aesthetics in the U.S. Over the past 10 years, she has not only practiced but also founded a nonprofit organization aimed at combating obesity in America, seeking to make a significant impact beyond her clinical work. Her life in America has been the longest she has spent in any country, and she views herself as highly adaptable, capable of assimilating under any circumstances. This adaptability was tested during a challenging 2.5-year period in her marriage, which was particularly difficult as her move to the U.S. was motivated by her relationship. Despite these challenges, she and her husband worked through their issues, reinforcing her belief in resilience. Hyun identifies strongly with her Korean heritage, yet her views are progressive, supporting gay rights and abortion rights, which she feels align with her humanity and values rather than national identity. This sometimes puts her at odds with her conservative, Republican, and Christian parents. Nonetheless, her relationship with her parents remains strong; they have been a constant source of support, with her mother visiting frequently to help with her children—aged 16, 14, and 4. In this Legacy Project feature, Hyun Joon Lee reflects on how her unique experiences—from Seoul to the Bronx, from Yonsei to Cornell—have shaped her life's mission to leave a lasting impact on public health in America.
Keywords: medical student; love; relationship; marriage; Yonsei University; Indonesia; International school; immigration; private practice; nonprofit; obesity; Cornell University; family; Bronx, New York; Scarsdale;

Lillian Chung (Full)
Interviewee: Chung, Lillian
Lillian Chung's journey reflects the intertwining of cultural heritage and personal discovery. Born in Seoul in 1969, her family's immigration to San Diego in 1971 marked the beginning of her American experience, guided by the presence of her aunt and uncle. Her father's pursuit of education in the Philippines redirected their family's path, away from the traditional family business in Korea. Lillian's immersion in Korean American culture blossomed at Camp Conifer, where she found a community of Americanized Korean American youths. While racism was not a predominant memory from her childhood, Lillian's cultural awakening came during a Yonsei summer program in college, offering her a deeper understanding of her Korean roots. Her journey took a romantic turn when her mother and her future mother-in-law orchestrated a meeting with her now-husband while they both resided in Boston. Professionally, Lillian pursued educational psychology, finding fulfillment as a school psychologist. Since moving back to the West Coast from the East Coast, she finds solace in the slower pace of life. Reflecting on her experiences, Lillian acknowledges the evolving landscape of Asian representation, particularly noting the differences her children encounter compared to her own upbringing.

Lillian Chung (Edited)
Interviewee: Chung, Lillian
In the first installment of our Los Angeles Legacy Project stories, we introduce to you Lillian Chung, a school psychologist based in California. Lillian shares her story of immigrating from Seoul to California as a young child with her family, meeting her husband on a unique blind date set up by their mothers, and now as a mother raising her third generation children as proud Korean Americans.

Sylvia Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Sylvia
Sylvia Kim, born and raised in Toronto, Canada, currently lives in Irvine, California with her family. At 37 years old, Sylvia has cultivated a diverse and impactful career, transitioning from practicing law to spearheading innovative philanthropic efforts. She was born into a Korean family in the multicultural neighborhood of Scarborough, where she was the only Korean until 8th grade. Her father owned a convenience and later a video store, embedding early experiences of community and entrepreneurship in her life. Sylvia’s educational and career trajectory led her from studying Criminal Law to roles as a public defender and prosecutor, before moving into policy research and access to justice within the nonprofit sector. She currently holds a significant position as the Chief Innovation Officer at the Asian Pacific Community Fund, where her primary responsibility is to incubate the first national Asian American Community Foundation. The shift to philanthropy aligns with Sylvia's realization of the unique challenges and opportunities facing Asian Americans, the fastest-growing population group in the US. This demographic shift, she notes, has created a disconnect between institutions and the community, something she aims to address in her work. Sylvia's personal journey into her cultural identity began following an inquiry into her Korean American background, which led her to visit Korea for the first time as an adult. Discovering that her family roots trace back to North Korea, she learned more about her family's history, including her father's role in founding one of the first Korean Presbyterian churches in Canada. Inspired by her heritage, Sylvia co-founded HanVoice, a nonprofit focused on North Korean refugee rights, which has been a significant part of her life’s purpose. On a more personal note, Sylvia has faced family challenges, notably a strained relationship with her brother, which led to emotional and physical confrontations. These incidents have shaped her commitment to being open about her struggles, using her platform to promote authenticity and healing. Sylvia’s story also intertwines with her professional and personal life, highlighting her desire to reclaim and pass on her Korean identity to her children, reflecting on her regrets about not being more available to them due to her demanding career. Her story was captured in a Legacy Project video recording, hosted by Suzan Lee Paek in Newport Beach, CA, providing a platform for Sylvia to share her multifaceted experiences and insights.
Keywords: Toronto, Canada; Christianity; convenience store; law school; lawyer; prosecuter; nonprofit; advocacy; verbal abuse; physical abuse; family; Irvine, California; healing; identity; North Korea; HanVoice; Asian Pacific Community Fund; philanthropy;

Jessica Paek (Full)
Interviewee: Paek, Jessica
Jessica, a 26-year-old born and raised in Los Angeles, has led a life marked by diverse experiences and a continuous search for personal fulfillment. Spending most of her early life in Orange County, she enjoyed the benefits of growing up in a multicultural community, where she largely escaped the pains of racism. Her educational journey included a stint at a boarding school near Akron, Ohio, for 1.5 years, which broadened her perspectives further. Initially, Jessica ventured into the world of fashion, interning at Saks Fifth Avenue's marketing department before college. This experience sparked an early interest in a fashion career. However, her academic pursuits led her to study African history in college, where she initially thought she would become a historian and then a teacher. After completing her thesis, she realized that academia was not her calling and shifted her focus to writing, particularly within the esports and gaming industry. Jessica’s career in gaming evolved as she began working in developer relations and business development for a game discovery platform. Her journey through the professional world has been fulfilling, reflecting her desire to engage deeply with her passions. On a more personal note, Jessica embraces her identity as a tattooed Korean American, challenging the traditional taboos against body art in Korean culture. She has extensive tattoos, including a significant piece around her thigh, which was met with unexpected support from her parents. These tattoos, alongside her Asian heritage, have sometimes led to uncomfortable invasions of personal space, prompting Jessica to learn to assert herself and occupy her space confidently, despite occasionally feeling the need to cover up around family. Jessica has also navigated challenges with mental health, including high-functioning depression, OCD, and anxiety, exacerbated by her birth control. Through therapy and self-exploration, she has learned to find happiness amidst these struggles. In a recorded Legacy Project video hosted by Suzan Lee Paek in Newport Beach, CA, Jessica discusses her tattoos and career transitions, from pursuing a degree in Historical Linguistics, engaging in linguistics research, to embracing her role in the gaming world. Her story is one of resilience, adaptation, and the ongoing quest to live authentically.
Keywords: tattoos; taboos; gaming industry; esports; writing; mental health; identity; OCD; Los Angeles, California;

Youngsong Martin (Full)
Interviewee: Martin, Youngson
Youngsong's journey from South Korea to the United States is a testament to her determination and adaptability. Arriving in the summer of 1979, at the age of 21, Youngsong left Korea in pursuit of better opportunities, aspiring to further her studies and enter academia, yet she ultimately carved a path in business. Her early experiences in the U.S. were challenging, as she struggled with the language barrier and cultural differences, but she quickly adapted, absorbing and understanding U.S. culture within her first year. Driven by a survivor mentality, Youngsong moved from Hawaii to Indiana, where she earned a business degree. Her time in Indiana was transformative, as she learned practical skills such as sewing, which led to her first job at a dry cleaner's, altering clothes. This experience fueled her passion for fashion, and she later moved to San Francisco to attend the Fashion Institute of Design and Merchandising (FIDM). Despite financial constraints, Youngsong's tenacity shone through as she juggled multiple jobs and an intensive course load to support herself and complete her education swiftly. Youngsong's career in fashion quickly escalated after graduation. She turned down a teaching offer at FIDM to work directly in patternmaking, setting the stage for her ascent in the fashion industry. She held roles ranging from an assistant designer at Jessica McClintock to head designer at another fashion house, before eventually founding her own brand. Recognizing the shifting dynamics of the fashion industry in 1988, marked by private equity's increasing influence, Youngsong made a pivotal decision to leave the fashion world in 2001 after a successful career highlighted by her appearance on the cover of Women's Wear Daily. After taking a year to reassess her career goals, Youngsong transitioned into the wedding industry, becoming an event designer. Her company, Wildflower Linens, has achieved tremendous success. Her reputation and unique background as a Korean American woman helped her make significant strides, even leading to an invitation from the White House to assist with events during the Obama administration, a role she fulfilled for eight years. Throughout her life, Youngsong's views on her Korean heritage have evolved. Initially critical of Korean cultural traits, she has grown to appreciate the deep resilience and empathy embedded in her culture, recognizing these qualities in herself as she navigated numerous challenges and successes in the U.S. Today, Youngsong is a celebrated figure in her field, known for her creativity, resilience, and the unique perspective she brings as a Korean American woman in mainstream industries.
Keywords: fashion; Wildflower linens; event planning; interior design; patternmaking; immigration; Indiana; San Francisco; FIDM; Fashion Institute of Design and Merchandising; womenswear; resilience; entrepreneur; business;

Sukhee Kang (Full)
Interviewee: Kang, Sukhee
Sukhee Kang's journey from arriving in the United States in 1977 to becoming the first Korean American mayor of Irvine, California, is a remarkable story of resilience and dedication to the American Dream. At 23, fresh out of college and newly married, Sukhee arrived in the U.S. with no clear goal and faced immediate challenges. For three months, he struggled to find employment, his savings dwindling. His break came when he secured a job at Circuit City, selling electronics. This position not only helped him practice English but also propelled him to become the top salesman within four months, earning a company-sponsored trip to Hawaii with his wife. Sukhee's career at Circuit City spanned 15 years, during which he also became deeply involved in community service and advocacy. His work with the Korean American Coalition and as chair of the Korean American Scholars Foundation marked the beginning of his commitment to ensuring that the voices of Korean Americans were heard. In 2004, his political journey began in earnest when he ran for city council in Irvine, California, personally visiting over 20,000 homes to fundraise and distribute marketing flyers. Despite facing racism and feeling isolated during his early days in the U.S., especially during low moments such as questioning his future at Newport Beach, Sukhee's community work and political involvement grew. However, not all political endeavors were successful; his 2012 run for Congress did not go as planned. Nonetheless, he continued to champion community integration and understanding through initiatives like the Irvine Global Festival. Beyond his public service, Sukhee's personal life reflects his diverse interests and the importance of family. Once a child violinist who was steered towards economics by his father, he now enjoys Chinese calligraphy as a hobby. After a significant career in politics, family has become his focal point, cherishing his role as a grandfather to his son and daughter's children. Sukhee Kang's story is not just one of achieving personal success but also about making substantial contributions to the community, illustrating the impactful journey of an immigrant pursuing and realizing the American Dream while helping to weave the fabric of his adopted community.
Keywords: immigration; politics; mayor; Irvine, California; public service; salesman; economics; University of South Korea; city council; Irvine Global Festival; Circuit City; calligraphy; family;

He shares his story of being the first Asian American salesman at Circuit City, and eventually getting into politics.

Jinhee Ahn Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Ahn Kim, Jinhee
Jinhee Ahn Kim's life story is a testament to the power of adaptability and embracing change. Born on the outskirts of Seoul, near the North Korean border, Jinhee's early life was influenced by her father, who taught martial arts for the army. The family moved to Cincinnati when her father was invited to teach martial arts for the ROTC, marking the beginning of Jinhee's diverse and adventurous journey. Jinhee's academic path started in pre-med at Harvard University but soon pivoted to economics, reflecting her willingness to adapt and explore new avenues. After college, she ventured into management consulting, then shifted gears drastically by entering the fashion industry in New York. Her personal life took her across the globe, living in England, Singapore, Hong Kong, Tokyo, and Hawaii, following her husband’s academic pursuits and broadening her own horizons. While in Hawaii, Jinhee took another bold step by applying to law school at the age of 40. Though she practiced law briefly, her entrepreneurial spirit led her to develop a company focused on early education materials and apps for young children, which she later sold after significant success.Now residing in London, Jinhee is the cofounder and CEO of UYU.gg, an elite brand and pro team in the billion-dollar esports industry, which traces its roots back to Korea. Her career in fashion has influenced her designs for uniforms for esports teams, blending her creative skills with emerging cultural trends from Korea. This role has Jinhee traveling the world, supporting her team members and immersing herself in the global gaming community. Throughout her life, Jinhee has embraced uncertainty and the unknown, never allowing fear to hinder her pursuit of new adventures. From business to fashion to law and finally technology and gaming, Jinhee Ahn Kim has not only adapted to but also thrived in diverse professional realms. Her story is not just about career changes but about the continuous pursuit of growth and the understanding of cultural currents, which she adeptly navigates and influences.
Keywords: entreprenuer; founder; esports; gaming industry; fashion industry; education materials; ROTC; immigration; Cinncinati, Ohio; Singapore; Hong Kong; London; Hawaii; business; economics; family; Seoul, South Korea; resilience;

Tae Hun Yo (Full)
Interviewee: Ahn Kim, Jinhee
Tae Hun Yo's journey from Seoul to becoming the Executive Director of Baseball Operations for Asia reflects a story of profound resilience, adaptation, and success. Born in Seoul and having moved to the U.S. in 1981 following the death of his mother, Tae and his family settled first in Michigan and later in Denver, Colorado. His early experiences in America were filled with learning and adaptation, engaging actively in sports and other extracurricular activities during his school years. After graduating from the University of Denver with a degree in business, Tae embarked on a significant career at Time Warner Cable in Los Angeles. Over 15 years, he rose to become a VP of Broadband Interactive, specializing in innovations like Video on Demand. His tenure at Time Warner was marked by accolades for his contributions to the Roadrunner and VoD services. In 2009, following a major merger and a career sabbatical, Tae penned an unpublished memoir titled "Halfway There," reflecting on his life's journey. The sports world drew him back through his work with Chan Ho, a childhood friend and baseball player, which inspired Tae to help more Asian players gain visibility and success in the U.S. baseball leagues. Today, as the Executive Director of Baseball Operations for Asia and working alongside top baseball agent Scott Boras, Tae has managed prominent Korean MLB players like Shin-Soo Choo, Hyun-Jin Ryu, and Chan Ho Park. His transition from business to sports management illustrates how Tae successfully linked his business acumen with his passion for baseball, creating a fulfilling career path. Tae advises young people to live passionately and maintain a balanced life while pursuing what they love—a principle he embodies through his own career choices. His story is not only about professional achievement but also about personal growth, cultural integration, and the pursuit of one's passions, inspiring the next generation of Korean Americans and others around the globe.
Keywords: baseball; Seoul, Korea; immigration; Denver, Colorado; sports agent; Time Warner Cable; Broadband Interactive; Video on Demand; Roadrunner; Korean MLB; Major League Baseball; Chan Ho Park; Shin-Soo Choo; Hyun-Jin Ryu; career sabbatical; family; Christianity

Originally having worked at Time Warner, Tad shares his story of how he eventually found his way linking his business experience and his deep passion for baseball to cultivate a career he truly loves – something he wishes for all future generations of Korean Americans.

Nancy Choi (Full)
Interviewee: Choi, Nancy
Nancy Choi, born in Culver City, California, has carved a diverse and impactful career path leading to her current role as CFO and co-founder of Carbonadi, a company known for its Italian sipping vodka. A Berkeley economics major, Nancy's early career spanned from being an actuary to investment banking. Her career took a significant turn when she shifted to real estate development, and a pivotal meeting through a friend eventually led her into the vodka industry. Nancy's personal life is equally rich with narratives of growth and reconciliation. Raised as a latchkey kid by a devout Christian mother, Nancy had complex feelings towards her upbringing, which oscillated between resentment and appreciation. This relationship evolved as Nancy delved deeper into her faith, which also helped her reconnect with her mother, who had always been the dominant decision-maker in her family. Her father, who passed away in 2001, had wished for her not to marry a Korean man—a wish Nancy did not fulfill. Marrying into a family with three sons, it took her twelve years to gain full acceptance. The generational differences in parenting styles have significantly shaped Nancy's life. As a third-generation Korean American, she has navigated the challenging dynamics between her and her first-generation mother, ultimately finding a path to reconciliation. Today, Nancy reflects on these experiences with a sense of accomplishment and understanding, acknowledging the journey that has shaped her into a successful businesswoman and a bridge between her cultural and familial legacies.
Keywords: Christianity; mother; family; resentment; reconciliation; vodka; alcohol industry; Carbonadi; investment banking; finance;

Nancy Choi (Edited)
Interviewee: Choi, Nancy
“She literally said I’m sorry." As the mother of a third-generation Korean American daughter, Nancy Choi has faced coming to terms with the different styles of parenting that have come from her own mother, who is first generation. Navigating the cultural differences between first, second, and third generations – Nancy shares her experience finally reconciling with her mother while finding her own path as a wife, mother, and now CFO of Carbonadi Vodka, which she co-founded.

Sung Tse (Sung Min Kim) (Full)
Interviewee: Tse, Sung
Sung Tse's journey began when she immigrated to the United States from her homeland at the age of 8 in 1974, settling in Los Angeles with her family. As the eldest sibling with two younger brothers, Sung experienced a childhood shaped by her parents' ambitions and values. Her father, a skilled pattern maker, initially moved to the US sponsored by an Italian suitmaker in Pennsylvania. However, disliking the cold climate, he relocated the family to LA. Despite financial challenges, which left them "house rich, money poor" after purchasing a home, the family persevered. Growing up in a strict household, Sung started to rebel as she aged. At 18, she made the significant decision to leave home with her then-boyfriend, who would later become her husband. By 20, she was married, and by 21, she had bought a house. Choosing to delay parenthood, Sung didn’t have children until she was 31, a decision born from a deep desire to be intentional about her life choices. She longed for and was blessed with a daughter, who later transitioned and identified as transmasculine. This experience profoundly shaped Sung's understanding of identity, prompting her to learn about nonbinary identities and fully accept her child. Her son's transition was a pivotal moment for Sung, challenging her to confront and question her deeply held Christian beliefs. The struggle was initially met with resistance from her church community, which suggested "praying the trans away." However, Sung and her husband embarked on a journey to understand and navigate the gender spectrum, eventually finding support in an LGBTQ+ Christian community that taught them the meaning of grace. Sung's reflections on her family’s experiences underscore the importance of living authentically. Her son's poignant question, "What if I never get to live as the person I was meant to be?" resonated deeply with her, catalyzing a shift from a conservative to a more progressive Christian viewpoint. This transformation was not just about accepting her son but also embracing the change within herself. Today, Sung is an advocate for her children to live their true, authentic selves, cherishing the personal growth and new perspectives her family's journey has afforded her. She holds her faith and love for her children as central to her identity, a testament to her resilience and evolving understanding of what it means to truly love and accept.
Keywords: lgtbq+ community; coming out; non-binary; sexuality; identity; gender fluid; christianity; family; progressive; conservative; faith; love and acceptance; transgender;

From initially being met with “praying the trans away” from fellow church members, Sung shares how she and her husband have learned how to navigate the gender spectrum alongside their son as parents, Christians, and allies in love and support.

Doug Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Doug
Doug Kim, born in 1956 in Minnesota, embodies a narrative of cultural resilience and identity exploration. Growing up in a Midwestern American environment, his early years were marked by significant challenges due to his Korean heritage. His childhood was shaped by frequent bullying in Minnesota until his family relocated to Muenster, Indiana, when he was in fourth grade. This move highlighted his cultural differences even more, escalating conflicts with peers to the extent that his father enrolled him and his siblings in Judo school to learn self-defense. The cultural disconnect was exacerbated by his parents’ lack of communication about their cultural struggles, leaving Doug feeling isolated and misunderstood. During his junior year of high school, another relocation brought Doug and his family to Detroit, Michigan, where he finally found a sense of community within a local Korean population. Despite excelling in extracurricular activities, including roles as Sophomore and Senior Class President, a member of the marching band, and Editor-in-Chief of the school newspaper, Doug’s parents prioritized academic excellence above all else. This focus on academics was a source of tension, as Doug felt pressured to fulfill traditional expectations over his own interests. Doug’s understanding of his cultural identity deepened over time, particularly regarding his parents' insistence on marrying within the Korean community—a stance rooted in their historical experience of cultural suppression during the Japanese Occupation. Their desire to preserve Korean heritage led his mother to found Saejong Camp, aimed at helping Korean-American children connect with their heritage and each other. Doug’s academic and professional life has been dedicated to exploring and teaching Korean-American studies, further influenced by his parents' remarkable journey from Korea to the United States in 1949, as part of a small group of Korean immigrants. His work at San Francisco State University and his long tenure as Director of SaeJong Camp reflect his commitment to educating others about Korean history and culture. Doug often reflects on the strength derived from understanding one’s roots and the adversities faced by ancestors, a perspective that has guided him through his own journey of self-discovery and cultural advocacy.
Keywords: immigration; identity; East Asian studies; SaeJong Camp; Japanese Occupation; medical school; family; South Korean cultural values; cultural advocacy; self-discovery; Judo; Korean heritage; Midwest;

Doug Kim (Edited)
Interviewee: Kim, Doug
Doug Kim was born in 1956 in Minnesota. As a youngster growing up in the Mid-West he was frequently bullied by his peers for being Asian and was at a loss understanding how he was different (than Chinese or Japanese)… or why anyone should take pride in being Korean American. Thankfully a course in college exposed him to the rich traditions and roots of Korean history and culture which began a life-long pursuit to better know his Korean identity. He went on to teach Korean American Studies at San Francisco State University and has served as Director of SaeJong Camp – a Korean heritage summer camp in Michigan – for 17 of its 44 years. “To know where we come from; to know the adversities that our ancestors faced; that blood flows in us. That is a huge source of strength.”
Keywords: immigration; identity; East Asian studies; SaeJong Camp; Japanese Occupation; medical school; family; South Korean cultural values; cultural advocacy; self-discovery; Judo; Korean heritage; Midwest;

Young Song Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Young Song
Dr. Young Son Kim, born in Jeonju, South Korea in 1928, experienced a challenging childhood marred by personal loss and the backdrop of the Japanese occupation. At just 10 years old, he faced the death of his father and later his sister, a series of events that profoundly shaped his aspirations. Coming from a large family of eight children and enduring years of financial hardship, Young Son resolved to become a doctor, a dream aimed not only at improving his family’s situation but also at serving others. His journey to fulfill this dream led him to the United States, facilitated by an enduring friendship with an American GI named Wally. With Wally’s assistance, Young Son secured admission with a full scholarship to North Park College in Chicago in 1949, beginning an academic and professional trajectory that included studies at Northwestern University and medical school at the University of Minnesota. Despite his academic success and professional dedication, including working night shifts as an x-ray technician to support himself, Young Son’s desire to return to Korea and contribute more directly to his community faced political barriers. His and his late wife’s outspoken stances on human rights and democracy led to repeated denials of his visa applications by the South Korean government, thwarting his plans to work and teach in Korea. Throughout his life, Young Son remained a devout Christian, a faith inherited from his father, who converted after encounters with missionaries. This faith deeply influenced his life and service, leading him to become an elder in his Presbyterian church, where he served in various capacities, including on the church board. In sharing his story with his son, Doug, for the Legacy Project, Young Son reflects on the intertwined paths of personal ambition and advocacy, acknowledging the complex challenges of navigating identity, career, and activism across continents. His life story underscores the profound impact of resilience and commitment to one’s principles in the face of adversity.
Keywords: immigration; Japanese Occupation; Democracy; activism; South Korea; medical school; American GI Soldier; family; Christianity; missionary; doctor; Presbyterian church;

Young Song Kim (Edited)
Interviewee: Kim, Young Song
“My story is somewhat different because I came to the United States to prepare myself to go back to Mission Hospital in Jeonju. But my stance, and my late wife’s stance, on human rights in Korea, made it impossible to go back.” In this Legacy Project, Young Song Kim shares his story with his son, Doug. Young Song was born in 1928 in Jeonju, South Korea and grew up living through the Japanese occupation. After the tragic death of both his father and sister when he was just 10 years old, he made the decision to become a doctor. In 1949, after receiving a mysterious acceptance letter from North Park College in Chicago with fully paid tuition – which he later discovered was because of his American friend Wally – Young Song now had the opportunity to make his dreams come true. He completed his undergraduate and medical education and eventually raised his family in America. However, his repeated attempts to return to his native Korea to work and teach full-time as a heart surgeon were continually rejected by the South Korean government because of his pro-democracy and human rights activism.
Keywords: immigration; Japanese Occupation; Democracy; activism; South Korea; medical school; American GI Soldier; family; Christianity; missionary; doctor; Presbyterian church;

Jin Young Choi (Full)
Interviewee: Choi, Jin Young
Jin Young Choi was born in 1937 in Manchuria, under the harsh conditions of Japanese occupation. Her early years were marked by cultural and educational restrictions, where speaking Korean was banned, and all schooling was conducted in Japanese. Despite these challenges, her family celebrated the end of the occupation and returned to their native Korea. Unfortunately, their relief was short-lived as they soon found themselves amidst the turmoil of the Korean War. During the war, Jin Young's family faced severe hardships. The trauma of losing her youngest sister to starvation deeply affected Jin Young, but her mother's resilience and insistence on the importance of education remained a guiding force. This maternal encouragement spurred Jin Young to pursue academic excellence against all odds. In a historic achievement for Korea, Jin Young became the first individual to receive a Fulbright Scholarship after the war, in 1961. This opportunity took her to the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill to study English Literature. During her time in the United States, she not only faced academic challenges but also navigated the complexities of racial segregation prevalent during that era. Her professors and peers were often surprised by her background and how she managed to excel despite coming from war-torn Korea. Jin Young thrived in her studies and enjoyed her graduate school experience immensely. It was also during this time that she met her future husband, a Korean mathematics student who distinguished himself from his peers through his openness and diligence. After obtaining her master’s degree in 1963, Jin Young embarked on a teaching career in North Carolina, where she offered courses in American literature, a rare feat for an Asian woman in the 1960s. Her teaching journey continued until she returned to Korea with her husband and their three children, following an offer for her husband to take up a professorship in Korea. Back in Korea, Jin Young continued to break barriers; she became a professor of English and American literature, balancing her professional commitments with her roles as a mother and a daughter. Despite her success, she often reflected on her regret for not being more attentive to her aging mother during these years, a sentiment that stayed with her throughout her life. Jin Young's legacy is multifaceted—her children and grandchildren not only achieved high academic honors, including doctoral degrees and valedictorian titles, but they also carried forward her values of perseverance, gratitude, and kindness. Additionally, Jin Young has authored several books, with one becoming a bestseller for over 40 years in Korea. Demonstrating her lifelong commitment to giving back, she donated all the royalties from this book to her religious community. Through her enduring efforts in education and her principled stance against war, Jin Young Choi has left an indelible mark on her family and the broader community, advocating for social engagement and never standing by in the face of injustice. Her story is a profound reminder of the impact one individual can have on shaping a legacy of resilience and dedication.

Jin Young Choi (Edited)
Interviewee: Choi, Jin Young
For this week’s #LegacyProject, we interview Jin Young Choi, the first person to ever receive a Fulbright Scholarship from Korea. Jin Young was born in 1937 in Manchuria which was also under Japanese occupation at the time. After celebrating the end of the occupation, her family moved back to their native country, Korea, but soon she witnessed the Korean War breaking out. In this Legacy Project, Jin Young recalls the extreme difficulties her family faced during the war and having to watch her mother bury her youngest sister, but also how her mother continuously encouraged her to focus on her education. She ended up becoming not only the first person to get a Ph.D. in English Literature from Seoul National University but also the first Fulbright Scholar from Korea. In 1961 when the Fulbright Commission reopened after the war, Jin Young was selected to study at the University of North Carolina for graduate school.

Chris Detrych (Full)
Interviewee: Detrych, Chris
Chris's story is a narrative of identity, belonging, and self-acceptance. Born in Seoul, South Korea, Chris was adopted and brought to Detroit, United States, in 1985. From an early age, his adoptive parents wanted to ensure Chris was aware of his origins and encouraged him to embrace his Korean heritage, a testament to their supportive and inclusive approach to parenting. Despite their efforts, including exposure to Korean culture through books, audio sets, and local culture camps, Chris faced a complex journey of self-discovery and acceptance. The dichotomy of Chris's early life was marked by the loving environment at home and the challenges he encountered outside, particularly at school where he was often singled out for being Asian and on the smaller side. His struggle with identity deepened as he rejected his Korean heritage during his childhood and adolescence, feeling alienated even within spaces meant to celebrate it, such as Korean culture camps. High school and college presented new opportunities and challenges for Chris. He began to find a sense of community with Asian peers and joined an Asian American organization, steps that marked the beginning of a shift in his journey towards self-acceptance. However, college also highlighted the complexities of his identity; living with a Korean roommate who spoke Korean with friends made Chris feel further estranged, spotlighting the nuanced layers of his adoptee experience. Today, Chris's journey has taken a new direction as he embraces his role as a mentor for adoptees in Chicago. This decision reflects his desire to be the positive figure he felt was absent in his own youth. Mentorship has not only allowed Chris to impact the lives of others positively but has also been transformative for him, fostering a sense of accountability, respect, and confidence in himself. It's a role that emphasizes the importance of self-compassion and forgiveness, values Chris holds dear as he navigates his path to self-discovery. Chris's return to Korea in 2016, for an adoptee conference, was a significant milestone. It was an experience that underscored his complex feelings of belonging, feeling simultaneously like a tourist in Korea and out of place upon returning to the U.S. This trip highlighted the ongoing nature of his journey, one that is less about searching for birth parents and more about understanding himself and his place in the world. Despite the progress Chris has made, he acknowledges the loneliness that often accompanies him, a testament to the deeply personal and sometimes isolating process of grappling with one’s identity. His stoic demeanor coexists with the encouragement he offers his mentees to be open about their feelings—a practice he admits to finding challenging for himself. In his final remarks, Chris comments on how the adoptee experience is one of both gratitude and grief, reflecting on this nuanced and complex journey adoptees individually go on. Chris is currently the President of Korean Adoptees of Chicago (KAtCH) and also volunteers with Connect-A-Kid, a non-profit mentoring organization for transracial adoptees.
Keywords: adoption; immigration; isolation; identity; self-compassion; forgiveness; culture camps; adoptee community; Seoul, South Korea; Detroit, Michigan; Chicago, Illinois; gratitude; grief; self-discovery; family;

John Hong (Full)
Interviewee: Hong, John
John Hong's journey from Seoul, Korea, to the suburbs of Chicago, US, is a story of adaptation, resilience, and identity. Born in Seoul, John moved to Chicago when he was just two years old and spent his early years growing up near Wrigley Field. His childhood was marked by independence, as he learned to take care of himself from a young age while both his parents worked—his father as a salesman with commendable English skills and involvement with the Peace Corps, and his mother transitioning from a job at the US Embassy in Korea to new work in the US. Despite moving to the suburbs, where he lived near a cornfield, John's connection to his Korean heritage remained strong, especially through the Korean church he attended every Sunday, which he describes as being with "his own people." This sense of belonging and identity was further reinforced during a trip to Korea with his daughter, highlighting the deep-rooted connection they both felt to their Korean heritage. John's educational journey took him through a predominantly white high school, where he felt out of place, to the University of Illinois, where he found a sense of community with a Korean Christian group. His extracurricular activities varied from being a DJ, radio show host, and participating in a play. John's acting pursuits even led him to roles as an extra in movies and singing the National Anthem at Wrigley Field. The narrative of John's life is also one of personal and spiritual growth, notably influenced by the death of his cousin, which catalyzed his relationship with God. This event marked a turning point, leading him to embrace his faith more fully during his junior year of high school—a faith that had been part of his upbringing but not fully accepted until then. John's professional life is as varied as his personal experiences, having transitioned from practicing law to entrepreneurship. He founded a company that customizes decorations for cellphone cases, showcasing his innovative spirit. His advice to his children encapsulates the lessons learned from his life's journey: trust your network and the people who love you, embrace failure as a stepping stone to progress, and move forward without fear.

Settling in Lacrosse, Michigan, she faced the challenges of racism and discrimination but found strength in her unwavering faith and determination to succeed. Despite the initial hardships, Chang Hee Yung's family found stability and built a life in America. Her husband's employment at an air conditioning company provided financial security, allowing them to purchase a small house and create a nurturing environment for their children. Reflecting on her past, Chang Hee Yung cherishes the memories of unwavering support of her mother-in-law and her husband's deep love. Throughout her life, Chang Hee Yung's faith remained a source of strength and solace. Despite the passing of her beloved husband, she finds comfort in her children and the memories they share. Her deepest desire is for her children to maintain their unity and steadfast faith, embodying the values instilled by their family and to continue their faith As Chang Hee Yung navigates life's challenges, she continues to draw strength from her unwavering faith and the enduring love of her family, embodying the resilience and determination that define her remarkable journey.

David Chang (Full)
Interviewee: Chang, David
For this week’s Legacy Project video recorded in Chicago, David Chang is interviewed by his daughter, Loren Chang. Davids life story is one of resilience, cultural pride, and the pursuit of personal dreams under challenging circumstances. Born in Korea as the oldest of four children, David's early years were spent in Seoul, growing up in a middle-class family amidst the extended family network, including his grandmother. For a time, his family lived near the DMZ, close to an army base, which David recalls fondly of early childhood memories. The family's move to Lacrosse, Wisconsin, marked a significant shift in David's life. There, he formed close friendships at school and continued to grow up with a deep sense of pride in his Korean heritage. His father, a figure of immense respect and inspiration to David, managed the pressures of raising a family without complaint. This stoicism and dedication left a lasting impression on David. Despite the typical pressures of Korean society, David's parents, who worked multiple jobs to support the family, never imposed strict career expectations on their children. They entrusted their faith in God for guidance. This upbringing, coupled with living in a household that valued hard work and resilience, significantly shaped David's outlook on life. David's journey to becoming a doctor was intertwined with his family's narrative. Learning that his father had once aspired to attend medical school deepened David's commitment to his chosen path, albeit with guilt as he pursued his residency while his parents worked tirelessly at their hardware store. This period was marked by a sense of responsibility and regret, as David wished to alleviate his parents' burdens. Tragically, by the time David was financially stable enough to support his parents, his father passed away. This loss was compounded by his father's unfulfilled dream of seeing David's accomplishments. Throughout his life, David's strong Christian faith, nurtured from a young age in the church, played a crucial role. It was this faith that partly inspired his aspiration to become a missionary doctor. Now, as a reflection of his journey and the lessons learned, David emphasizes the importance of embracing challenges and new opportunities to his family. He advocates for the courage to be different, to be unashamed of one's identity, and to always carry pride in one's heritage and personal story.
Keywords: Seoul, Korea; DMZ; military; Korean War; doctor; medical school; Lacrosse, Wisconsin; immigration; hardware; regret; family; Christianity; church; plastic surgeon; missionary doctor;

Jay Yoo (Full)
Interviewee: Yoo, Jay
Jay Yoo's journey from a young boy in Seoul to a successful family man in the United States encapsulates a story of resilience, growth, and eventual reconciliation with his past. Born in Seoul, South Korea, in 1977, Jay moved to Chicago with his family, leaving behind his maternal grandparents who had raised him when he was born. The transition was challenging; at just five years old, Jay found himself shouldering adult responsibilities, looking after his younger brother while their parents worked tirelessly to provide. This early imposition of adulthood left Jay with deep-seated emotional scars, a burden he carried into his adult life. Despite outward success, Jay struggled with the invisible weight of his childhood trauma. Counseling and the support of his church community and understanding wife have been crucial in his journey towards healing, yet gaps remained in his emotional wellbeing. The loneliness and anxiety of his youth, marked by racism and a constant struggle to fit in, were juxtaposed with moments of joy, such as the cherished letters from his grandmother in Korea. Jay's story is also one of perseverance and hard work. His parents, determined to build a better life, eventually owned their own businesses, a testament to their resilience and entrepreneurial spirit. However, this success came at a personal cost to Jay, who sacrificed his education and personal aspirations to support his family's endeavors. As an adult, Jay reflects on his experiences with a nuanced understanding. He recognizes the sacrifices made by his parents and the strength he gained from his challenges. Marriage and fatherhood have offered him new perspectives and motivations. He is determined to provide his children with the support and opportunities he lacked, emphasizing the importance of understanding their heritage and the struggles of their ancestors. Jay's return visits to Korea, beginning when he was nine or ten, reconnected him with his roots and offered moments of joy and belonging that were rare in his childhood. His involvement in a diverse high school and a Christian community provided a sense of belonging and acceptance that he had longed for. Today, Jay has come to terms with his past and the decisions made by his parents. He has found forgiveness and focuses on living in the present, cherishing the blessings of his family and financial stability.

Miae Lim (Full)

Born in Busan, Korea, and now residing in Chicago at the age of 52, Miae Lim's journey to the United States began at the age of six. Her family's relocation was precipitated by her father's political activism in Korea, which placed them in danger following the loss of the opposition party in an election. Her father's death when she was 16, a tragic outcome of a violent altercation in a restaurant, marked a profound moment in her life. Despite his imperfections and his desire to distance their family from the Korean community, Miae remembers her father as a complex figure who allowed her the freedom to express herself. Miae's narrative is also one of familial resilience and personal guilt, especially following the death of her younger sister in a fire shortly after Miae moved out of their shared college apartment. This loss, combined with her father's earlier death, left Miae grappling with deep-seated feelings of guilt and sorrow. Yet, it was her mother's unyielding strength, stoicism, and ambition that offered a beacon of hope. Her mother's journey from factory worker to successful business owner, all while raising a family amidst tragedy, stands as a testament to her bravery and tenacity. Professionally, Miae Lim has carved a notable path in the hospitality industry. From bartending through college, where she pursued a degree in criminal justice with aspirations of joining the FBI, to owning nightclubs and Japanese restaurants, Miae's career reflects her adaptability and entrepreneurial spirit. Despite setbacks, such as not getting into her chosen law schools, Miae's journey through the business world from New York to Las Vegas, and eventually back to Chicago, showcases her resilience and dedication to forging her own path. As Miae reflects on her life at 52, she acknowledges a growing sense of pride in her identity and accomplishments. Her experiences have led her to embrace her Korean heritage more fully, finding strength and a sense of belonging within the Korean community. Her story is one of overcoming adversity, embracing one's roots, and the power of self-acceptance. Miae's life, marked by both profound losses and significant achievements, underscores the importance of resilience, the value of family, and the unyielding pursuit of one's aspirations, all while navigating the complexities of identity and belonging.

Matt Fischer (Full)
Interviewee: Fischer, Matt
Born in Korea, to a mother who was unable to care for him, Matt Fischer was left on the side of the road, subsequently living in an orphanage until the age of 7 when he was adopted by the Fischers, a family in a small town in Michigan. Despite the initial joy of joining a new family, Matt's journey was fraught with challenges. He struggled with a sense of belonging, faced favoritism within his adoptive family, and endured academic and emotional setbacks imposed by his adoptive parents. The turning point in Matt's life came when he was unceremoniously expelled from his home at 18, leading to a period of instability and hardship. However, his life took a positive turn when he was welcomed into the Dennell family, who provided him with the support and love he had been denied. This new family became his anchor for over three decades, illustrating the profound impact of chosen family and unconditional support. Matt's journey through education was marked by determination and resilience. Despite being pulled out of honors classes and transferred schools to hinder his academic success, he thrived in a community college before transferring to the University of Michigan for chemical engineering. His collegiate years also allowed him to reconnect with his Korean heritage through basketball and other community activities. His personal life flourished alongside his academic and professional achievements. Meeting his wife in a biology class, their shared journey through medical school and residency, and their subsequent marriage highlight the importance of companionship and mutual support. The decision to adopt from Hope International, the same agency through which he was adopted, reflects Matt's gratitude for his own adoption and his desire to provide a child with the opportunities he was afforded. The process of adopting his daughter, Chloe, and the cautious emotional investment until the adoption was finalized, underscored the complexities and emotional nuances of adoption. Matt's commitment to multiculturalism and his desire for his children to embrace a diverse cultural identity are central themes in his life. His sons' identification with their Indian heritage, through their mother, and the family's immediate acceptance of Chloe illustrate the beauty of a multicultural family dynamic. Matt's attempt to connect with his birth parents during a visit to Korea, and his wish for his ashes to be sent to Korea upon his death, signify a full-circle moment in his journey of self-discovery and acceptance of his Korean identity. Matt Fischer's story is a testament to the power of resilience, the value of diverse cultural identities, and the profound impact of both biological and chosen families in shaping one's life.

Daniel Chung (Full)
Interviewee: Chang, David
Dan Chung was born in Seoul, Korea, in 1977 and moving to the United States at just five months old, Dan's early life was marked by a legal hurdle—living as an illegal alien due to his parents' oversight in securing his citizenship. It wasn't until his wife took the initiative years later that Dan became a U.S. citizen, a status his parents have yet to obtain. The Chung family relocated from Korea in search of a better life, driven by Dan's father's disillusionment with the militarized state of Korea at the time and a defining incident that underscored his distrust of authority. This fierce independence and skepticism towards authority were traits Dan inherited and values deeply, influencing his decision to eschew corporate life in favor of running an organization dedicated to aiding North Korean refugees. This work, challenging and illegal in China, has been a testament to Dan's ingenuity and the spirit of resistance instilled in him by his upbringing. Growing up on the South side of Chicago, Dan's childhood was far from conventional, with memories of playing with a gun in the back of his parents' dry cleaning business and an early exposure to the realities of living in a rough neighborhood. Despite the lack of academic pressure from his parents and becoming known as the "King of Detention," Dan reflects positively on the freedom and responsibility he was given to learn from his mistakes—a freedom he cherishes and extends to his own children. Dan's professional journey as the founder of Crossing Borders is inspired by his awakening to the plight of North Korean refugees during a famine, a cause to which he has dedicated nearly 17 years of his life. His adventures, including a nerve-wracking experience in China that highlighted the precarious nature of his work, underscore his commitment to this cause. Raised in the diverse suburb of Niles, Dan enjoyed a childhood where race was not a divisive issue, a stark contrast to his teenage years in Indianapolis, where he felt the sting of being a minority and learned to defend himself with humor and wit rather than violence. These experiences shaped his outlook on life and his approach to parenting, emphasizing faith, curiosity, and the value of community over material success. Dan hopes to instill in his children the importance of engaging with others, understanding diverse perspectives, and building a strong network of supportive relationships. To him, true wealth lies not in solitary achievements but in the richness of community and the bonds of love and care that unite people.
Keywords: North Korea; famine; Crossing Borders; China; refugees; green card; Niles, Illinois; Chicago, Illinois; Indianapolis, Indiana; bullying; racism; family; community; non-profit; Trump; rebellious; citizenship; dry cleaners;

Ji-Yeon Yuh (Full)
Interviewee: Yuh, Ji-Yeon
Ji-Yeon Yuh was born in Seoul, Korea in 1965. She moved to the U.S. at the tender age of five, following her parents' pursuit of higher education and professional opportunities. Her father, on a student visa studying Theology, and her mother, a medical doctor, created a new life in Chicago, navigating the challenges and opportunities of immigrant life. In the early years, Ji-Yeon's family life was deeply intertwined with the Korean American community in Chicago, primarily through their church and her father's international student connections. Despite these communal ties, her early life in the U.S. was marked by a sense of isolation from her extended family in Korea and the cultural shock of adjusting to American life. This adjustment was further complicated by language barriers within her own household, leading Ji-Yeon to seek solace and education in American children's television programs. Ji-Yeon's educational journey, from attending an elite private school where she first encountered class consciousness, to her transformative years at Stanford University, reflects her evolving understanding of her identity and societal perceptions of race and beauty. Despite facing challenges related to her immigrant status and racial identity, Ji-Yeon's academic and social experiences at Stanford marked a significant shift in her self-perception and social interactions. Her career path diverged significantly from the expectations set by her family. Initially drawn to the prospect of medical school, Ji-Yeon discovered her passion for writing and journalism at Stanford. This passion led her to explore the fields of history and journalism, focusing on immigration and the Asian American experience. Her work, particularly a widely-read column on the culture wars, reflects her commitment to filling the gaps in Asian American literature and history. Ji-Yeon's professional and personal life continued to be a reflection of her multifaceted identity as an Asian American woman, a journalist, and an academic. Her research on military brides and her subsequent role in Asian American studies highlight her dedication to understanding and teaching the complexities of immigration, race, and gender. Ji-yeon became a founding faculty of the first Asian American Studies department at Northwestern Univeristy. Her life is a testament to the resilience and adaptability of the immigrant experience, and her contributions to journalism and academia offer valuable insights into the Asian American experience.

Bonnie Oh (Full)
Interviewee: Cho-Oh, Bonnie Bongwan
Bonnie Bongwan Cho-Oh's remarkable journey from Seoul, Korea to the United States is a testament to resilience, intellectual pursuit, and profound personal growth amidst adversity. Born in Seoul and arriving in the U.S. during the winter of 1956, Bonnie's early life was marked by personal tragedy, notably the death of her younger brother at the age of four—a pivotal event that deeply impacted her and inspired her book, "Phoenix in a Jade Bowl." Growing up in Korea during the 1940s, Bonnie experienced the challenges of living under an authoritarian regime amid political and intellectual persecution. Her father, a pioneering human rights lawyer, faced political persecution for maintaining their Korean surname during the Japanese occupation, a stance that left young Bonnie as the sole Korean-named student in her class, subjected to punishment and isolation. These early experiences of oppression and injustice fueled Bonnie's resolve to fight against inequity, a theme that would define her life's work. Her father's transition from a persecuted lawyer to a judge advocating for unfairly punished independence activists, and their home serving as a sanctuary for released prisoners, further influenced her commitment to justice. Bonnie's academic journey in the United States began with her independent applications to prestigious colleges, including Vassar, Radcliffe, and Barnard, leading to her acceptance and attendance at Barnard College on scholarships and financial aid. Her time at Barnard not only advanced her educational pursuits but also introduced her to her future husband, setting the stage for her next life chapter. Despite initial expectations of domestic life, Bonnie's husband encouraged her to pursue graduate studies at Georgetown, an endeavor that would see her navigate motherhood, academia, and eventually a teaching role at a local Catholic girls' high school. Her academic career was punctuated by a move to St. Paul, Minnesota, and later, significant advancements including a Danford fellowship that supported her Ph.D. studies at the University of Chicago. Bonnie's professional journey saw her championing Asian studies and advocating for Asian American students, culminating in her tenure at Loyola University Chicago and later roles at St. Mary’s College and Georgetown University. Throughout, Bonnie remained steadfast in her core values as a wife and mother, attributing her family's successes to her foundational principles. Reflecting on a life of achievements and contributions, both personal and academic, Bonnie, now approaching her 85th birthday and mourning the recent loss of her husband, expresses gratitude for the opportunities and support she received in the United States. Her story is a powerful narrative of overcoming adversity, pursuing academic excellence, and making impactful contributions to society, all while holding dear the values of family and community.

Yuri Doolan (Full)
Interviewee: Doolan, Yuri
Yuri Doolan was born in 1988 on an Air Force base in Songtan, South Korea, to an American father and a Korean mother, Yuri's earliest memories are imbued with the warmth of his mother's love amidst the backdrop of a military camp town. Despite the early divorce of his parents and an abrupt move to Cleveland, Ohio, these formative years in Korea left an indelible mark on Yuri, shaping his sense of identity and belonging. In Ohio, his now single immigrant mother raised him and his brother, with little to be seen of his father. Growing up mixed race in the United States presented its own set of challenges and revelations, particularly during his schooling years when he first became acutely aware of his unique heritage. Yuri's journey into academia was fueled by a desire to understand his own history and that of others like him. His pursuit of Asian American history at Ohio State University was not just academic; it was deeply personal. This drive led him to Northwestern University for his Ph.D., where he explored the stories of mixed-race Koreans, inspired by his own experiences and his mother's struggles as an immigrant and a woman of color. His work seeks to bridge gaps between mixed-race students and the broader Asian American community, highlighting the nuanced experiences of identity, migration, and belonging. His current book project, The First Amerasians: Mixed Race Koreans from Camptowns to America, epitomizes his commitment to telling the stories of mixed-race Koreans, tracing their journey from U.S.-occupied South Korea to adoptive American homes. Through his academic and personal journey, Yuri has not only sought to understand his own identity but has also worked to provide a platform for those with similar backgrounds to share their stories. His dedication to making academic writing impactful and accessible to a broader audience underscores his belief in the power of storytelling to foster understanding and empathy. Yuri's personal life, marked by the complexity of coming out to his deeply Christian mother, further illustrates his navigations through the intersections of identity, family, and acceptance. Each step of Yuri's journey reflects a commitment to understanding and compassion, both in his academic pursuits and personal relationships. Now an Assistant Professor in the Department of History and the Women’s, Gender, and Sexuality Studies Program at Brandeis University, Dr. Doolan continues to inspire with his dedication to exploring the multifaceted stories of Amerasians and their journeys of identity, belonging, and resilience.

Anne Joh (Full)
Interviewee: Joh, Anne
Wonhee Anne Joh's narrative is a compelling reflection on identity, class, and culture, deeply rooted in the immigrant experience of a Korean American family. Born in South Korea in 1966, Anne immigrated to the United States in 1976, initially settling in Las Vegas before moving to Chicago in 1978. Growing up in a time and place where diversity was scarcely recognized, Anne and her family found solace and community within the church, despite the palpable class divides and internalized racism even within this sanctuary. The early experiences of exclusion and observation of intra-community divisions profoundly influenced Anne's perspectives and academic pursuits. Witnessing first-hand the avoidance by a Korean dentist at a gas station, Anne became acutely aware of the social dynamics and emotional complexities within the Korean American community. These observations fueled her interest in the dynamics of social relationships, the emotions governing these interactions, and the stark contrast between the model minority myth and the realities of many Korean American families, including her own. Driven by a desire to challenge and redefine the narratives surrounding Korean American success, Anne focused on raising her sons with the freedom to pursue their passions, unconstrained by the pressures of societal expectations. Her personal and academic journeys intersected in her exploration of theology, initially sparked by her father's political activism in Chicago and his conservative theological views. Despite her initial reluctance, Anne's engagement with feminist theology and black liberation theology at seminary opened new pathways of thought and inquiry, particularly around the Korean concept of Jeong and its complex emotional and social implications. Anne's approach to theology and culture is profoundly interdisciplinary, reflecting her commitment to teaching that fosters transformation through shared experiences. As a professor at Garrett-Evangelical Theological Seminary, she emphasizes the power of facilitation over instruction, guiding her students through explorations of identity, culture, and theology that transcend traditional academic boundaries. Her personal experiences with love, parenting, and the challenges of navigating divorces within a community steeped in stigma have further deepened Anne's insights into the human condition. These experiences have informed her writing, which aims to be accessible and engaging to a broader audience, and her collaborative work with global communities, reflecting a desire for her work to have a wider impact. Dr. Wonhee Anne Joh's story is one of resilience, reflection, and the relentless pursuit of understanding the complex layers of identity, culture, and emotional ties that bind communities. Through her academic work and personal journey, she continues to explore and articulate the nuances of Korean American identity, the transformative power of theology, and the universal quest for belonging and acceptance.
Keywords: Garrett-Evangelical Theology Seminary; christianity; Chicago, Illinois; racism; classism; Korean American; immigration; identity; feminist theology; systemic theology; black liberation theology; divorce; marriage; family; model minority; academia; jesus; love; diversity;

Mary Kim was born in Hwanghae Do during Japanese occupation, she faced linguistic suppression and turmoil at a young age. Eventually settling in Wisconsin before moving to New York and later Illinois, Mary juggled familial responsibilities while striving for personal and professional growth. Despite not having the opportunity to attend college earlier in life, she pursued higher education at a community college, studying accounting and carving a path for herself in the workforce. Mary's entrepreneurial spirit shone through as she engaged in various ventures, from clothing alterations to working in the gas industry, and eventually transitioning to a career in insurance and real estate, despite initial reluctance. Following her husband's passing in 2004, Mary faced the challenges of loneliness and linguistic decline but found purpose in running an elderly daycare, where she continued to make a positive impact on her community. Through determination and resilience, Mary Kim's journey reflects the immigrant experience and the pursuit of the American dream, embodying the values of hard work, adaptability, and perseverance in the face of adversity.
