Taneka Jennings (Full)
Interviewee: Jennings, Taneka
Taneka Hye Wol Jennings' journey from her birth in Cheongju, South Korea, to becoming a beacon for Asian American and adoptee human rights work in Chicago is a narrative of resilience, exploration, and community. Adopted at just 3 months old by a white American family in New Jersey, Taneka's early life was marked by a sense of solitude as she navigated her identity as a Korean adoptee without a community of similar backgrounds. Despite the diverse environment she grew up in, which leaned more towards an immigrant identity, Taneka often found herself grappling with her parents' expectations to assimilate into white culture. Her college years brought these challenges into sharper focus as she moved from a diverse setting to a homogeneous, conservative Christian college in the Midwest. This shift triggered an identity crisis for Taneka, as she struggled to find her ethnic identity within a space that did not celebrate diversity. However, her academic pursuits were fulfilling, laying a complex foundation for her personal growth. The pivotal moment in Taneka's journey came with her move to Chicago after college, where she began to actively seek a sense of self. Her involvement with KAtCH (Korean Adoptees of Chicago) initially bewildered her but ultimately provided a gateway to exploring her roots and connecting with others who shared similar experiences. Furthering her engagement, Taneka took Korean language and cooking classes, immersing herself in the culture of her birthplace. Today, Taneka serves as the Deputy Director at the HANA Center in Chicago, a role that allows her to deeply engage in Asian American and adoptee human rights work. Her commitment to community, advocacy, and social services is a testament to her journey of self-discovery and healing. Taneka's narrative underscores the importance of community in navigating one's identity and the power of personal growth through advocacy.Her late adoptive father's philosophy of living life without regrets resonates deeply with Taneka, who seeks to find beauty in chaos and live a life of gratitude and purpose. Representing 1 in 17 Korean Americans as an adoptee, Taneka's story is a poignant reminder of the collective experiences that shape the Korean American identity and the necessity of challenging societal norms to forge a path of inclusion and understanding.
Keywords: New Jersey; Korean American; adoptee; Chicago, Illinois; Cheongju, South Korea; human rights; identity; Christianity; Korean Adoptees of Chicago; HANA Center; deputy director; social services; self-discovery; immigrant; community;

James Wang (Full)

James Jinhwang Wang's life journey is marked by resilience, sacrifice, and a relentless pursuit of opportunity amidst the tumult of war and personal ambition. Born in Pyeongyang in 1940, James witnessed the ravages of the Korean War firsthand, losing two siblings and enduring the hardships of separation from his father and displacement from his hometown. These traumatic experiences left indelible marks, shaping his determination to overcome adversity. With aspirations of academic achievement, James sought higher education and a master's degree, only to confront the limitations of educational opportunities in Korea at the time. Undeterred, he navigated a path into the workforce, driven by the hope of contributing to his country's development. Recruited by Park Chung Hee after the Blue House raid, James found himself drawn into governmental service and awarded the opportunity to study abroad in the United States. His journey led him to the University of Illinois in Chicago, where he pursued higher education and gained valuable insights that he later brought back to Korea. He returned to the states in order to pursue starting his own business, but he grew tired, and eventually ran a Kumon in order to pay for his daughter's college education. Despite achieving financial success, James grappled with the weight of familial expectations and personal regrets, particularly regarding his relationship with his mother and his limited time with his daughters during their childhood. However, his pride and joy now lie in the happiness of his granddaughter. Driven by a steadfast belief in the reunification of Korea, James remains committed to contributing to this vision and dreams of making a business to aid in Korea's reunification. In his later years, James finds solace and purpose in nurturing the happiness and well-being of his granddaughter, emphasizing the importance of contentment over material success. His life story exemplifies resilience, optimism, and the enduring pursuit of a brighter future for himself and his loved ones.

James Jin-Han Wang was born in 1940 in what is now the capital of North Korea, Pyeongyang. Crystal Kang Ahn was born into a deeply traditional Korean family, with her father transitioning from a historian to a pastor. Her marriage to a Korean man who grew up in Nashville, Tennessee, and her move to Chicago for better opportunities highlight her continuous quest for identity and belonging. In Chicago, Crystal sought a healthier relationship with her Korean heritage, engaging with the Hana Center and leading its education department. Her journey reflects a reconciliation with her past, acknowledging her parents' efforts and overcoming resentment. Crystal's life is also marked by her miraculous survival from a heart condition at birth, a story that deeply influenced her faith and sense of purpose. Today, Crystal navigates her identity with a newfound perspective, embracing her Koreanness and Americaness in a balance that continues to evolve.

Jeannie Wang's life journey from Busan to America is one of adaptation, sacrifice, and the pursuit of a better life. Dreaming of working in an embassy, Jeannie majored in political science but had to relinquish her aspirations due to poverty. Jeannie faced the challenges of racism in America head-on, fiercely defending her daughters against discrimination. Despite the hardships, she found motivation in the simple joys of life, like sharing a meal of fried chicken. spurred by watching her own children struggle in classes, she started a Kumon business which she ran with her husband for 20 years. Reflecting on her experiences, Jeannie emphasizes the importance of human connections above all else. Her happiest moments revolve around her daughters, while the loss of her grandmother remains her saddest memory. She regrets not being more involved in her children's lives but takes pride in their ability to navigate different cultures seamlessly. Through it all, Jeannie's journey underscores the resilience and determination needed to thrive in a new country while cherishing the connections that make life meaningful.

His work focuses on empowering Korean American youth, fostering a sense of community, and advancing human rights. William's experiences and the support of his community have been pivotal in shaping his views on activism and the potential for societal change. Regina Park (Full)
Interviewee: Park, Regina
Regina Park's journey is one of resilience and determination, shaped by her experiences as a child in Manchuria and her later life in Korea and the United States. Born in Harbin in 1944, Manchuria, Regina's family fled to Korea after the fall of the Japanese Empire. She was able to live in both Suwon and Seoul, but subsequently faced racism because of her Northern accent. In Manchuria, Regina's parents ran a hotel and gained respect in the community for their generosity and integrity. They sheltered political refugees, earning Regina's family land in Suwon from one of them. Her aunt immigrated to Canada ending up with a roommate who wanted to marry Regina, and traveled to Korea to gain Regina's family's approval. Upon immigrating to Chicago, Regina faced challenges in finding employment due to her limited English skills. With determination and her mother's encouragement, she learned key punch and secured a job at Rothschild, eventually transitioning to computer work. Regina's dedication to learning and hard work led to promotions and success in her career. She attributes her achievements to the values instilled by her husband's friend: respect, response, and responsibility. Through her journey, Regina demonstrates the importance of resilience, perseverance, and continuous learning in overcoming obstacles and achieving success in life. She lives with no regrets because she believes that all experiences add to one's growth.
Keywords: Manchuria; Chicago; Suwon; Farmers; Farmland; Hotel; 3.1; March 1st Movement; Key Punch; Computers; Rothschild; Truman College; Government Workers; Seoul; Faith; Missionaries; Christianity; Soldiers; The People's Army; North Korea; Japanese Occupation; Korean Liberation; Korean War; Canada

Regina Park (Edited)
Interviewee: Park, Regina
Born in 1944 in the city of Harbin in what was then called the Manchuria region of China, Regina Park experienced the hardships of the Korean War at a young age. Her memories of the war include fleeing from Pyongyang, North Korea to South Korea with her family in the dead of night and receiving milk porridge from American soldiers on the street in order to survive. After meeting her husband through her uncle, Ms. Park applied for a green card and moved to the U.S. in her late twenties to start a new life. Her story is one of incredible resilience, courage, and tenacity.

Ohn Choe (Full)
Interviewee: Choe, Ohn
Ohn Choe, currently serving as the incoming board chair of Korean American Story, has dedicated approximately four years to the organization. This marks his first experience as a board chair, reflecting his commitment to the community. Born in Korea in the 80s, Ohn moved to Flushing, New York, in 1987. Growing up in a neighborhood of immigrants, he shared a bed with his sister in the living room but never felt excluded, surrounded by a diverse population. Despite facing challenges like waiting until 9 pm for dinner due to his working mom and skipping fillings due to lack of health insurance, Ohn recalls a childhood where cultural inclusivity prevailed. His father's journey, immigrating to the U.S. to further his education in architecture but ending up as a day construction worker, left a profound impact on Ohn. Witnessing his father's frustration and struggles after a successful career in Korea, Ohn gained insight into the complexities of the immigrant experience. An incident in Westchester, NY, where his mother faced mockery at an auto-shop, marked the first time Ohn felt disconnected from the culture. High school in Hunter on 94th street meant almost three hours of commuting daily, and graduating in 2001 with aspirations for Wall Street led him to Georgetown University. College prompted Ohn to explore his Korean identity, joining clubs to understand the significance of being part of a Korean community. Post-graduation, his finance career lacked personal connections, and the predominantly white leadership didn't foster a sense of belonging. However, a turning point came when he watched short films by Swallowtail Studios’ Flushing Web Series, providing a profound sense of visibility and belonging. At the age of 30, he left his finance job, traveled, and visited Korea for the first time since he was 19, finally feeling a sense of belonging after years in the corporate world. Ohn emphasizes the importance of Korean American Story's mission, recognizing the internalization of struggles and the impact of ingrained values. He believes the organization plays a crucial role in challenging traditional ideals and providing a platform for diverse narratives.
Keywords: Flushing, Queens; immigrant; finance; Happy Cleaners; Swallowtail Studios; Korean American Story; Georgetown Universty; Hunter College Campus Schools; Westchester, New York; racism; car acident; identity; career; South Korea

Vivian Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Vivian
Vivian Lee, originally from Toronto, Canada, moved to the United States 17 years ago and recently became a U.S. Citizen, a deeply emotional process for her as she never anticipated becoming American. Her family, among a few Koreans in Toronto, moved to Canada due to geopolitical tensions around 1971, and Vivian was born in 1974. Growing up, Vivian's family owned a Baskin Robbins Ice Cream Store after her father became an accountant and bought the franchise. Spending her teenage years helping at the store, she experienced the hardships firsthand. Her parents owned the store for over 30 years, eventually selling it, and even today, Vivian can't drive past it without feeling a mix of suffering and gratitude. Vivian's grandparents immigrated to Canada and became citizens, and her parents noticed their own transformation during visits to Korea, recognizing the bold move of becoming immigrants in the 60s and 70s. At a young age, inspired by an Asian Canadian woman newscaster on TV, Vivian aspired to be a journalist. She became a reporter at 18, working in Halifax for the summer. The spirit of possibility in the U.S. motivated her to become an American. Vivian started her career in New York at WNBC in 2002 and then joined NY1 as an anchor and reporter in 2008. Korean American Story resonates with Vivian, bridging the cultural similarities between Korean Canadian and Korean American experiences. She joined as an emcee for their gala and recalls a favorite memory of being surrounded by women engaged in a panel of Korean American authors. In her mid-30s, Vivian left her job due to visa issues but found a more meaningful position. Feeling a sense of purpose, she started volunteering, breaking free from a cycle of working without personal fulfillment. Vivian's journey encapsulates the complexities of identity, immigration, and the pursuit of meaning.
Keywords: Toronto, Canada; Baskin Robbins; South Korea; reporter; journalist; journalism; U.S. citizenship; United States; family; hardship; identity; feminism; Korean American Story; Korean Canadian; Korean American; culture;

Jessica Park (Full)
Interviewee: Park, Jessica
Jessica Park, a 23-year-old residing in New York, is the Project and Communications Manager for Korean American Story (KAS). Beginning as a graphic design intern at KAS, she gradually expanded her role to include social media management and involvement in ROAR Story Slams. Jessica appreciates the diversity of projects at KAS, involving video productions and more, keeping her engaged and on her toes. Born in Arlington, Virginia, and raised in Stafford, Virginia, a half-rural, half-suburban area, Jessica grew up in a predominantly white neighborhood. Her parents owned a deli, and she has an older sister who is a dentist in Texas. Despite the rural setting, Jessica's upbringing in Northern Virginia included experiences with a sizable Asian community, attending mega churches with fellow Koreans. During college at the University of Virginia, Jessica initially avoided Asian clubs and associations, opting for involvement in arts-related activities. After reconnecting with a friend, she joined the Korean Christian Intervarsity Club. A semester abroad in Korea proved life-changing for Jessica, sparking a deep interest in Korean culture and language. College posed challenges as Jessica balanced her time between studio painting, ministry leadership, and K-pop dancing. Initially exploring pre-med and law, she ultimately pursued graphic design, facing rejection from a design school but finding an internship at KAS. Jessica's close relationship with her mom and older sister led her to grapple with serious topics like death and menopause, contributing to anxiety and depression. Christianity played a crucial role in coping with these challenges, providing a source of strength. Drawing parallels with the struggles depicted in the film "Happy Cleaners," Jessica's parents faced language barriers and systemic oppression while striving for success in the U.S. Her dad's battle with pancreatic cancer during her elementary school years and the subsequent closure of their deli added to their challenges. Upon graduating college, Jessica returned home to assist with the deli but chose to pursue a career at KAS. Despite her parents' reluctance, she strives to prove her ability to support herself and contribute to the family's success. Jessica reflects on the life of a deli kid and how that impacted their family and her dreams for the future.
Keywords: deli; immigrant; language barrier; church; megachurch; intervarsity; Korean American Story; University of Virginia; Arlington, Virginia; New York; South Korea; study abroad; art; design; graphic design; painting; ministry; leadership; Happy Cleaners; family; Korean culture; Korean language;

Julian Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Julian
Julian Kim, a 33-year-old married man with a one-year-old son named Ian, reflects on the transformative journey of fatherhood. Initially hesitant about having children due to a troubled relationship with his alcoholic and abusive father, Julian's perspective changed when he met Yumi, his wife. Yumi's influence allowed him to envision a future with a family. Julian's childhood was marked by resentment towards his father and struggles with anger and frustration. Battling suicidal thoughts, he found solace and healing through his faith in God. The birth of his son Ian shifted Julian's focus from himself to the responsibility of parenthood, realizing the selfishness of contemplating suicide. Despite the challenges, Julian's life story unfolds with resilience. Born in Flushing, Queens, his family faced hardships, including a move to Korea for seven years. This disrupted his education but fueled his passion for art, eventually leading him to pursue a career in filmmaking. The movie "Happy Cleaners" is a reflection of Julian's life, with each character representing different aspects of his journey. Overcoming financial struggles and identity crises, Julian's perseverance led to a successful career in filmmaking. His exposure to filmmaking during his thesis project and collaboration with Korean American Story further solidified his place in the industry. Currently employed at a big corporation with staff benefits, Julian dreams of saving up for a house – an aspiration that seemed unattainable in his earlier years. His deep appreciation for his wife, Yumi, and her unwavering support gives him strength. Julian's ultimate longing is for lasting childhood friendships, a desire rooted in his constant relocations during upbringing. Julian Kim's story is one of overcoming adversity, finding purpose in fatherhood, and pursuing dreams against all odds.
Keywords: Mental health; suicidal thoughts; anger management; identity; immigration; language barrier; education; ESL; Flushing, Queens; South Korea; Korean American Story; creative arts; filmmaking; cartoon drawing; marriage; childrearing; fatherhood; family; Happy Cleaners; financial struggle;

AJ Valente (Full)
Interviewee: Valente, AJ
AJ Valente, a 27-year-old Filipino American born in Toms River, New Jersey, has been an editor for Korean American Story since 2016. Initially invited to collaborate on a motion graphic project for the KAS gala with videographer and photographer Janice Chung, AJ has continued his involvement with the organization ever since. Drawing inspiration from the Legacy Projects, AJ identifies deeply with the shared narratives of individuals who, despite diverse backgrounds, find commonality in their struggles, familial bonds, and the quest for identity in a complex world. This connection fuels AJ's empathy for the Asian American and immigrant experience, transcending cultural boundaries. Whether it's the story of an older grandmother or a father grappling with familial relationships, AJ relates these narratives to his own experiences, fostering a profound understanding of diverse perspectives. AJ recognizes the intricacies of Korean American history, acknowledging its specificity to each family yet united by the shared goal of establishing a unique and valuable place in America. Growing up in New Jersey, AJ's pride in his Filipino heritage was instilled by his parents. Despite attending private school with a consistent class structure from first grade to eighth grade, he experienced minimal racial concentration during his formative years. While AJ has visited Korea and China multiple times, his connection to the Philippines, his ancestral homeland, remains limited to a single visit. Having attended the New Jersey Institute of Technology (NJIT), AJ, grounded in Christian faith, joined the Korean Campus Crusade for Christ, exploring his passion for design and media through graphic work. His journey reflects a commitment to understanding and showcasing the diverse stories within the Asian American community.

Kwan Chung (Full)
Interviewee: Chung, Kwan Ho
Kwan Ho Chung, born in 1937 amidst the turbulence of the Second Sino-Japanese War, is a testament to resilience and determination. As the youngest of six siblings, with two sisters married before his birth, Chung's early years were marked by significant historical events, including the liberation from Japanese annexation in 1945. He vividly recalls the celebrations in Gangnam, Seoul, as people rejoiced in their newfound freedom, a stark contrast to the earlier policy-enforced dispersion to avoid bombings similar to those in Tokyo. The end of World War II didn't bring peace to Chung's life; the Korean War erupted during his middle school years, forcing him and his family into a three-year refugee existence in Busan. Despite these hardships, Chung's spirit remained unbroken. His academic journey led him from Seoul National University's pre-med and medical programs to the United States in 1967, seeking opportunities that mirrored his father's educational path at the University of Pennsylvania. Settling initially in Jersey City, Chung faced financial hardships, even struggling to afford hospital stay for the birth of his son, Henry, in 1968. Despite these challenges, he persevered, furthering his medical career and ensuring his sons received top-tier education at UPenn and Harvard, respectively. Chung's life reflects a deep connection to his heritage, marked by the dualities of loss during the Japanese occupation and the Korean War, and the triumphs of his later years in America. His pessimism towards the political climate in South Korea, shaped by fears of North Korean aggression, contrasts with his personal achievements and the legacy he's built for his family. His biography, "Father's Footsteps," not only chronicles his father's journey but also cements his own legacy of pursuing opportunities and overcoming adversity. At 82, Chung embodies a life well-lived, bridging continents and generations with his story of resilience, perseverance, and the pursuit of a better life for his family.
Keywords: Second Sino-Japaenese War; Korean war; Gangnam, Seoul; Seoul, South Korea; Busan; Tokyo; refugees; bombing; typhoid fever; poverty; Jersey City; medical school; education; University of Pennsylvania; Harvard University; "Father;s Footsteps"; Japense occuption; annexation; physician; family;

Kwan Chung (Edited)
Interviewee: Chung, Kwan Ho
Kwan Ho Chung was born in South Korea in 1937, during the year the Second Sino-Japanese War began. Growing up, he heard stories from his mother about his father’s college education in the United States at the University of Pennsylvania. He made his way to America to retrace his father’s journey, visiting the University of Pennsylvania campus in person and learning about his father’s educational history there. Mr. Chung would spend years piecing together his father’s story in America and publish a biography titled “Father’s Footsteps.” Eventually, Mr. Chung would also come to live in the United States, in search of more opportunities for his sons and a new life in a new country. His father’s story motivated Mr. Chung to seek opportunity and fulfill his own ambitions, continuing a remarkable legacy of determination and perseverance.

Stella Gill (Full)
Interviewee: Gill, Stella
Stella Gill's life story is shaped by the upheaval of the Korean War, which altered her childhood forever. Raised in Cheongju, her earliest memories are of her father dropping her off at school, accompanied by her mother and younger sister. However, everything changed with the outbreak of the war, forcing them to flee with only rice and seek refuge in random empty houses to escape the bombings. The war took a toll on her family, separating them from her father, who never returned home. Despite their hope of his eventual return, they eventually moved to Seoul. Stella remembers her father's trumpet playing, a cherished memory that faded after the war, leaving people wondering where the "trumpet man" had gone. Upon immigrating to the United States and starting her own family, Stella received a letter from her father, revealing he had remarried and had children in North Korea. This revelation left her feeling a profound sense of loss and anger, unable to properly reconnect with him before his passing. Despite the hardships, Stella found solace in music, teaching her own children piano and nurturing their talents. Her happiest moments revolve around her children's achievements, from getting into dream schools to excelling in music.
Keywords: Korean War; Refugee; Blankets; Trumpet; Music; Education; North Korea; 38th Parallel; Dancing; Mother/Daughter; Bombing; South Korea; Cheongju; Seoul; Golf

Stella Gill (Edited)
Interviewee: Gill, Stella
Stella Gill was attending kindergarten and learning how to play the piano when she recalls the Korean War breaking out when she was just 4 years old. After several years of living as refugees, her family finally returned home only to find that their father never came back. Stella went on to get married and settled with her new family in America. However, 25 years ago, she received a mysterious letter in the mail sent from North Korea that turned out to be her long lost father. Communicating through letters until his death, she describes the emotions she felt at that time learning about her father’s new life and family.

Stephen Gill (Full)
Interviewee: Gill, Stephen
Stephen Gill, originally named Gill Moon Geun, embarked on a journey from his birthplace in the rural area of Geumsan, South Korea, to becoming a respected member of the United States community. His early life was marked by resilience; after leaving home to attend high school in the bustling city of Daejeon, he furthered his education at Seoul Business College and Graduate School. His academic pursuit in business laid the foundation for his eventual career in public service, where he served diligently during his college years. In 1968, seeking new opportunities, Stephen moved to Tokyo, Japan, despite not knowing the language. There, he immersed himself in business studies, a challenge that prepared him for his next chapter. After spending four years in Japan, he returned to Korea briefly before making a pivotal move to the United States in 1973, a decision driven by a promotion that promised a new beginning for him and his future family. Stephen's life in the U.S. was not without its trials. After leaving his company in the 1980s, he ventured into entrepreneurship by operating a Hallmark card store. This period brought unforeseen challenges, including three alarming robberies—one of which nearly cost him his life. These incidents tested his resolve but also highlighted his determination to seek justice, leading him to court to confront his assailants. Despite these adversities, Stephen's commitment to his family's future never wavered. His decision to become a U.S. citizen in 1987 was a testament to his dedication to providing a stable and secure life for his children. His story is one of perseverance, the pursuit of the American dream, and the unwavering strength of family bonds. Stephen Gill's journey from Geumsan to the United States embodies the spirit of courage and the enduring hope for a better future.
Keywords: Tokyo, Japan; Geumsan, South Korea; Seoul Business College and Graduate School; Japanese Occupation; entrepreneurship; Hallmark card store; small business; robberies; American dream;

Stephen Gill (Edited)

Stephen Gill was born in the rural area of Geumsan, South Korea as Gill Moon Geun. After graduating from high school in the city of Daejeon, he attended Seoul Business College and Graduate School. Linda Chen (Full)
Interviewee: Chen, Linda
Linda Chen, now 47, was born in St. Gabriel, California, marking a significant generational shift in her family as the only sibling born in the United States. Her childhood was rooted in the backroom of her mother's grocery store, Happy Valley, in East L.A., where she spent considerable time under her father's care. This early environment, juxtaposed with her parents' vocal disappointment over her not being a boy, shaped Linda's perception of herself and her role within the family. Despite these challenges, Linda's upbringing instilled in her a sense of resilience and independence. Her parents, though hardworking and unable to participate in many of her school activities, demonstrated love in their own unique ways. Linda's mother, for instance, showed affection through financial support rather than time—a contrast to the more communal parenting Linda observed in other Korean families. This realization sparked a reflective journey for Linda, particularly in how she chose to parent her own children with a blend of honesty and a different approach to nurturing. Linda's exploration of identity deepened through her engagement with yoga. Initially resistant, yoga became a sanctuary for Linda, especially during periods of depression. It offered her a discipline of meditation devoid of religious connotations, which profoundly impacted her mental health and worldview. Her marriage to Paul, a Chinese American, enriched her cultural identity. Their shared American identity and mutual respect for each other's heritage underscored the multifaceted nature of identity in the melting pot of the U.S. However, growing up, Linda grappled with her identity due to her family's isolationist tendencies and her personal struggles with weight and self-image, exacerbated by a lack of healthy lifestyle options and extracurricular engagement. Linda's quest for cultural connection led her to George Washington University, where she pursued Korean studies, and later, to a summer program at Yonsei University in South Korea. This journey, however, brought its own set of challenges, including a brutal sexual assault that left her grappling with trauma and alienation. Despite these hardships, Linda's resilience shone through. She not only continued to embrace her Korean heritage but also became a vocal advocate for recovery and rape awareness, using her experiences to empower others.
Keywords: sexual assault; rape; Yonsei University; racism; identity; discrimination; Seoul, Korea; Los Angeles, California; yoga; meditation; healing; depression; mental health; Chinese American; Korean American; Korean language; grocery store; entrepreneurship; immigrant;

Linda Chen (Edited)

Linda Chen was born in the United States, a few years after her parents immigrated to America and began operating a grocery store in East L.A. Alexander Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Alexander
Alexander Kim, a political figure in his mid-40s from Orange County, California, embodies the spirit of Generation X with a versatile career that spans public service, entrepreneurship, and community development. Born and raised in Los Angeles, Alexander's journey into politics began in college, spurred by the Saigu riots. This pivotal moment awakened a realization in him: the critical lack of Asian American leadership within the United States. It was this realization that propelled him to pivot his academic focus towards political science, aiming to fill the void he identified. Alexander's professional life kicked off in the mayor's office of Los Angeles, where he served as a law clerk. This role was just the beginning of what would become a deeply committed career in politics, dedicating over a decade to serving his community, often working upwards of 100 hours a week. His dedication was driven by a desire to see a unified Korean community that could effectively advocate for its needs and interests within the larger American society. Parallel to his political career, Alexander has been instrumental in his family's foray into entrepreneurship. The purchase of an ice cream store by his father, an engineer by profession, marked the family's entry into the business world. This venture evolved over time, from managing the ice cream store to owning a pizza business, and eventually to real estate investments that led to the creation of a retail center. This center wasn't just a business venture; it was a platform for immigrants from diverse backgrounds, including Latinos, Vietnamese, Middle Easterners, and Koreans, to establish and grow their own businesses. Beyond his professional endeavors, Alexander's commitment to community service is evident through his involvement with the Goodwill Cooperation Society. This nonprofit, founded by Korean American professors, focuses on fostering understanding and connections between African American leaders and the Korean community, especially in the aftermath of the L.A. riots. This initiative reflects Alexander's deep-seated belief in the power of education and cross-cultural exchange to heal and unite communities. Currently, as a consultant for the local government, Alexander leverages his extensive experience in politics and community service to bridge the gap between the government and local communities, businesses, and nonprofit organizations. His work is a testament to his unwavering dedication to making a positive impact, guided by the lessons of his past and the needs of his community.
Keywords: saigu riots; african american community; racial tension; Orange County, California; Los Angeles; Korean Americans; law clerk; politics; entrepreneurship; small business; ice cream shop; immigration; community; Goodwill Cooperation Society; LA riots; local government; public servant; community service; nonprofit organization; political science;

Joseph Jeon (Full)
Interviewee: Jeon, Joseph
Joseph Jeon's journey from Seoul, South Korea, to a prominent position in academia in the United States is a testament to his dedication and passion for literature and cultural studies. Born in 1971 in Seoul, Joseph's life took a pivotal turn when his family decided to immigrate to the United States in 1973. The move was initially intended for his father to complete a medical residency in Barberton, Ohio. However, what was supposed to be a temporary stay blossomed into a permanent residency, setting the stage for Joseph's future achievements. Growing up in Buffalo, New York, Joseph's early life was marked by a transition to American culture, which included becoming a naturalized citizen and adopting the name Joseph at the age of 5. His father's profession as a doctor and the family's early years of adaptation to American life laid the foundation for Joseph's broad perspective on cultural identity and integration. Joseph's academic path was significantly influenced by his exposure to John Barth's "The Floating Opera," leading him to pursue higher education at Johns Hopkins University. There, he delved into English literature, a passion that he further cultivated by attending graduate school at Berkeley. His academic pursuits were not just about personal achievement; they were stepping stones to a broader objective of understanding and teaching cultural dynamics through literature and cinema. Now, Joseph Jeon holds a prestigious position as a professor in the English Department at the University of California, Irvine. More than that, he serves as the director of the Center for Critical Korean Studies at UCI. His work at the UCI Center for Critical Korean Studies embodies this commitment, as it aims to centralize and elevate Korean studies on campus and beyond, fostering a deeper understanding of Korean culture and its global impact.

Nancy Yoon (Full)
Interviewee: Yoon, Nancy
Nancy Yoon's life story encapsulates a journey of resilience, cultural identity, and the quest for connection and representation. Born as the youngest of five daughters, Nancy's narrative begins with her family's immigration to Los Angeles' Koreatown in the mid-1970s, a pivotal move initiated by her father, a former captain in the Korean army. Charmed by the United States during his visits, he, with the sponsorship of Nancy's uncle, relocated his family from South Korea, seeking a better future. Despite the initial financial struggles marked by their humble beginnings in Koreatown—illustrated by an incident at the airport involving a doll stuffed with money meant for safekeeping—the family persevered. Nancy's parents undertook multiple jobs, her father working for the county and her mother in a clothing factory, to support their family in a foreign land. This tale of migration and hard work set the stage for the Yoon siblings' achievements, with Nancy's sisters rising to prominent positions in banking and entrepreneurship. However, Nancy's journey diverged into a rich tapestry of experiences that spanned finance, entertainment, and a profound engagement with her cultural roots. The loss of her father to stomach cancer 20 years ago and her mother in a tragic car accident six years prior left Nancy feeling like an "orphan," a transformative phase that led her to redefine her life's purpose around love and community service. Nancy's career trajectory is notable for its diversity—from finance to roles at Disney Channel and Fox and Family, to marketing and eventually hosting and interviewing on the red carpet for significant events like "Crazy Rich Asians." Her international experience, living in Europe with her Austrian husband, Thomas, whom she met through her sister, added layers to her global outlook, despite the challenges of adapting to a new culture far from Koreatown. Following her mother's death, Nancy sought a new direction, leading her to a developmental role at a seminary, her first religious job, symbolizing a journey back to her spiritual roots and the importance of community. She founded the Facebook group Asians in LA, transforming it into a vital network and platform for Asian American voices in social media, politics, entertainment, and more. Her work culminated in the launch of "DiverCity" on Charter Spectrum News 1, a show segment celebrating diversity and now she is a QVC host at HSN. Nancy's life, marked by the passing of her parents and her visionary work within the Asian American community, reflects a narrative of overcoming personal loss, embracing cultural heritage, and making a significant impact on society.
Keywords: death; loss; mourning; community; diversity; Asians in LA; entertainment; Germany; Los Angeles, Koreatown; Austrian; QVC; HSN; ghost; church; religion; god; family; sisters; interviewer; TV and camera;

May Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, May
May Lee's journey from a childhood marred by racism and discrimination in Columbus, Ohio, to becoming a beacon of empathy and justice in journalism is both inspiring and compelling. Born in 1966 to a Korean American family, May's early years were between the United States and Korea, ultimately settling in Dayton, Ohio. Her father, a psychiatrist with proficient English skills, and her upbringing in a home filled with love provided a foundation of stability and comfort. Despite these comforts, May faced significant challenges growing up as an Asian American in the 1970s, including bullying and an identity struggle that would profoundly shape her character and future. May's educational journey took her to an all-girls boarding school in Connecticut, a welcoming bubble that starkly contrasted her experiences with racism and discrimination. It wasn't until college in California that May began to explore her identity more fully, connecting with other Asian and Korean individuals and stepping out of her cultural isolation. This exploration was the beginning of a lifelong journey of embracing her heritage and confronting the challenges head-on. As a journalist with over 30 years of experience, May has lived and reported from all over the world, using her personal experiences of racism and discomfort to connect with and tell the stories of others who have faced similar challenges. Her career was shaped by a calling to broadcast journalism—a divine intervention, as she describes it—that led her to never pursue another career path. Throughout her career, May has sought to tell stories with authenticity and empathy, leveraging her platform to advocate for social justice and to be a voice for the Asian community in media. One of the most notable moments in May's career was her interview with Monica Lewinsky, which taught her the valuable lesson of not judging others at face value. This experience, among others, reinforced her commitment to digging deeper into stories and presenting them with genuineness and empathy.Despite the anti-Asian sentiment that made it difficult for her to find her first on-air job, May's determination never wavered. Her career milestones include confronting racism head-on, such as when she stood up to verbally abusive men while covering a protest in Ohio. Today, as the host of The May Lee Show, she continues to delve into Asian and Asian American stories, fostering open and honest conversations that challenge stereotypes and promote understanding. May Lee's story is a testament to the power of resilience, faith, and the relentless pursuit of justice. Her experiences of overcoming racism, coupled with her dedication to truth-telling and advocacy, have not only shaped her into the journalist she is today but also serve as a beacon of hope and inspiration for many.
Keywords: racism; discrimination; Dayton, Ohio; boarding school; broadcast journalism; Oprah Winfrey; Monical Lewinsky; television host; interviewer; identity; journalist; Asian American community; media; isolation; The May Lee Show;

Alison Choi (Full)

Alison Choi's journey of self-discovery weaves through the vibrant streets of Hong Kong to the diverse landscapes of the United States, mirroring her complex cultural heritage and identity exploration. At 23, Alison's life is a rich tapestry of experiences shaped by her unique position as a third-generation Korean American born in Hong Kong. Her parents, 1.5 generation Korean Americans, established their lives in Hong Kong due to career opportunities, particularly her father's role in finance focusing on the Korean Market. Despite their American cultural roots, the Choi family's life in Hong Kong was predominantly English-speaking, with a nuanced infusion of Korean traditions that Alison sensed but couldn't fully articulate. Growing up, Alison's identity was multifaceted—American by birth and upbringing, Korean by heritage, yet immersed in Chinese culture. This complex identity tapestry was further complicated by her schooling in an international environment where she was one of the few Americans of color, predominantly interacting with white Americans. This environment led to a nuanced struggle with her Asian identity, which she felt was overshadowed by the more dominant Chinese cultural experiences of her youth. Alison's transition to Pomona College in the United States marked a significant turning point in her journey of self-identification. College life presented her with the challenge—and opportunity—to delve into the depths of her Asian American identity. Through rigorous academic pursuits, including a self-designed concentration on Gender, Race, and Class, Alison embarked on a quest to understand the historical and cultural underpinnings of her heritage. Her college years were transformative, sparking a passion for Asian American activism and leading her to intern and work with organizations that focus on Asian American issues, such as an Asian American theater company. Her work post-college, especially at the East West Theater, and her activism became platforms through which she navigated and embraced her Korean American identity, marking the first time she felt truly connected to this aspect of her heritage. Alison's journey underscores the significance of understanding one's roots and the power of education in bridging the gap between intellectual knowledge and personal identity. Her story is a testament to the ongoing process of self-discovery, highlighting the importance of exploring and acknowledging the diverse facets of one's identity, heritage, and the impact of intercultural experiences on personal growth.

Pak Myung Sook (Full)
Interviewee: Pak, Myung Sook
Pak Myung Sook's life story is marked by resilience and the pursuit of hope amid adversity. As the eldest of five children, she witnessed her father's abduction during the Japanese occupation, leaving her mother to care for their family alone. Despite hardships, she was fondly remembered by her Japanese teachers, but the liberation of Korea brought clarity to the disrespect she faced. In her youth, she was urged to prioritize learning English over Japanese, something she reflects on as a sign of the changing times. Memories of her father singing hymns during drunken moments remain cherished, highlighting moments of solace amid turmoil. The wartime trauma lingers, as she still wakes in fear, haunted by the loss of her child during the Korean War. Fleeing to Busan, Myung Sook endured illness and hardship, finding solace and her happiest moment in encountering Christ. Immigration to the United States with her sister followed, seeking new opportunities after her husband's passing. Despite challenges, she remembers her husband with deep affection, illustrating the resilience and strength that define her journey. She repeatedly recounts memories of deep hardship, and reminds the viewers that war is something that must not be repeated, and the Korean people must remain peaceful.
Keywords: Korean War; Japanese Occupation; Japan; Busan; Seoul; Large family; Familial death; illness; faith; church; jesus; immigration; business

Pak Myung Sook (Edited)

Pak Myung Sook was born in 1929 in Seoul, South Korea, during a time when the country was under Japanese rule. Ms. Pak’s mother sought strength in her religious faith, helping her entire family to become devoted Christians After growing up during a time of cultural and social repression, Ms. Pak then experienced the horrors of war, suffering the loss of her child when she fled to seek refuge. Her stories depict how important it is to find comfort and strength in one’s family and keep moving forward, no matter what.

Audrey Jang (Full)
Interviewee: Choi, Alison
Audrey Jang's story is one of resilience, cultural navigation, and the quest for belonging amidst complex familial and immigration circumstances. At 22, Audrey's life encapsulates the nuanced identity of a young woman born in Gwangju, South Korea, raised between the echoes of her Korean heritage and the reality of her life in California, USA. The early years of her life were marked by a significant transition when her family moved back to the US following her grandfather's passing in Korea, a country she associates with summer visits, and the warmth of her extended family. Audrey's academic journey took her from a Catholic school in California to a boarding school in Connecticut, and eventually to Pomona College, a decision driven by financial pragmatism that blossomed into a grateful acknowledgment of the opportunities it afforded her. Her education was not just a path to academic excellence but a means to escape, to broaden her horizons, and to forge a future filled with possibilities. The separation of her parents when she was just eight—a decision spurred by her father's dedication to his family in Korea and subsequent stay—left Audrey and her family in a liminal space, not divorced yet living separate lives across continents. This family dynamic, coupled with the challenges of growing up in a low-income household without a green card, added layers of complexity to Audrey's identity and belonging. Audrey's journey into her Korean American identity deepened through her involvement with the Korean American Coalition (KAC), a turning point that awakened a deep-seated desire to connect with her cultural roots and community. Despite the challenges of navigating her identity and belonging in various educational environments where Korean American peers were scarce, her time at KAC marked the beginning of a meaningful engagement with her heritage. The challenges of being undocumented, the anxiety over her immigration status, and the fear of being forced to leave the only home she knew underscored Audrey's college years. Yet, it was her major in Public Policy and her internship at KAC that eventually carved a path for her to contribute meaningfully to the Asian American community. As the Strategic Partnerships Coordinator with KAC, Audrey became a vital link in voter outreach, census participation, and fostering inter-organization relationships, embodying the role of a community advocate and a public face for the coalition. Audrey's narrative is a poignant exploration of identity, belonging, and the ongoing journey of self-discovery. Straddling two worlds, she grapples with the dichotomy of her birthright and the reality of her upbringing, reflecting on the essence of what it means to be Korean, American, or both. Her story is a testament to the strength found in the face of adversity, the search for one's place in the world, and the profound impact of cultural and familial ties that shape our understanding of who we are and where we belong.

Kam Redlawsk (Full)
Interviewee: Redlawski, Kam
Kam Redlawsk's narrative is a profound testament to resilience, self-discovery, and the power of advocacy born from personal struggle. Born in Daegu, Korea, in 1979 and adopted into a loving family in Chesterfield, Michigan, in 1983, Kam's journey is one of contrasts—between cultures, identities, and the challenge of living with a rare genetic condition. Her childhood, though filled with love and a typical middle-class upbringing alongside three brothers, was marked by an early confrontation with feelings of difference and isolation. Growing up in a predominantly white area, Kam grappled with her identity and the visible scars left by chicken pox and a cleft lip, which made her the subject of teasing at school. This sense of not fully belonging followed her into high school, where struggles with identity and acceptance became more pronounced. College became a turning point for Kam, offering a broader view of the world and sparking an interest in various subjects, from history to creative studies. However, it was also during this time that she began experiencing symptoms of what would later be diagnosed as GNE myopathy (previously known as Hereditary Inclusion Body Myopathy), a rare muscle disorder affecting only about a thousand people worldwide. This diagnosis came after a challenging journey through misdiagnoses and physical setbacks, including the onset of foot drop at 18, which gradually led to her reliance on a wheelchair. Despite the challenges, Kam's spirit and resolve only grew stronger. She pursued her passion for automotive and product design, earning degrees and eventually working for companies like Mattel and Motorola, where she contributed to impactful projects, including the design of the world’s cheapest prosthetic knee joint. Moving to California for work, Kam founded a nonprofit organization dedicated to her condition, leveraging her experiences for advocacy and awareness.Kam's journey through the physical manifestations of her disease has been a constant cycle of grief, acceptance, and determination. Refusing to be defined by her disability, she has embraced life with vigor—skydiving, scuba diving, and traveling extensively. Her artistic talents and commitment to advocacy have found expression in blogging, creating art as a visual journal, and writing a children's book about her disability, sharing her story to inspire understanding and empathy in others. Married to Jason, a fellow Korean adoptee and video game designer, Kam's life in California represents a deliberate choice for safety and a better quality of life. Facing the future with courage, Kam is determined to continue using her voice, her art, and her intellect to make a difference, embodying the belief that living fully is not about the limitations we face but about the boundless spirit with which we confront them. Kam Redlawsk's story is a beacon of hope, showing that even in the face of rare and challenging circumstances, one can lead a life rich with purpose and passion.
Keywords: disability; wheelchair; GNE Myopathy; Korean adoptee; cleft lip; rare genetic condition; identity; rare muscle disorder; product design; California; community; empathy; children's book; diagnosis; resilience; isolation;

Kam Redlawsk (Edited)

Kam Redlawsk was born in Daegu, South Korea in 1979 and adopted by an American family in Michigan in 1983. It was during college that Ms. Redlawsk was diagnosed with what is known today as GNE myopathy, a rare genetic disease that leads to weakness and wasting in one’s muscles and affects only around one thousand people worldwide. Debora’s academic journey was interrupted as they took time to address personal traumas and questionings, often described as "Korean trauma"—a deep-seated, ineffable pain rooted in their cultural heritage. A pivotal moment in Debora's journey was attending a pansori concert, which profoundly impacted them and rekindled their connection to Korean musical traditions. This experience was instrumental in Debora's decision to redirect their studies towards Traditional Korean Music. Through this musical journey, they began to reclaim their cultural identity, finding solace and strength in the rich history and expressive depth of Korean arts. Now deeply engaged in the study of Traditional Korean Music, Debora is also exploring themes of colonialism and elitism, which are intertwined with the historical context of Korean diaspora communities. This academic and personal exploration has not only helped Debora reconnect with their Korean roots but has also empowered them to reshape their identity and influence through music and activism, fostering a deeper understanding of their place within and beyond the Korean American community.

L. Song Richardson (Full)
Interviewee: Richardson, L. Song
L. Song Richardson, the current Dean and Chancellor’s Professor of Law at the University of California, Irvine School of Law, was born in El Paso, Texas. Her upbringing was uniquely shaped by her parents: a Korean American mother and an African American father who served as a Lieutenant Colonel in the U.S. Army. Richardson’s childhood was spent mostly on army bases, cultivating a deep sense of discipline and resilience under her mother's strict guidance, who heavily emphasized the importance of education and music. Growing up in a military family with a strong Korean influence, Richardson and her two younger brothers were raised in a tightly-knit, disciplined environment, marked by frequent road trips where the family avoided rest stops to prevent drawing attention to their mixed racial background. Despite the diverse settings of army bases, it was not until college that Richardson faced significant questions about her race and identity, which sparked her interest in the dynamics of race and society. Initially aspiring to be a doctor, Richardson discovered her passion for psychology in college, which eventually led her towards a career in law. After realizing her desire to advocate for civil rights, she pursued law school, became a public employed defender, and later a law professor teaching in Chicago. In 2014, she joined UCI, where she was later appointed Dean. In this Legacy Project, Richardson reflects on the complexities of growing up in a mixed-race family during times of significant social tension in the U.S. and her journey towards understanding the pivotal role of race in shaping personal and professional identities. Her career at UCI includes joint appointments in the Department of Criminology, Law and Society, and the Department of Asian American Studies, emphasizing her continued commitment to education and the intersection of race, law, and civil rights.

Cedric Stout dives into his rich cultural tapestry as the son of an African American father and a Korean mother. Raised in Fayetteville, North Carolina, a military town, his early years were characterized by the love of his parents, particularly his father, who lived through the era of segregation and the Civil Rights movement. Navigating the complexities of middle school, Cedric explored various facets of his identity, grappling with different interests and phases. However, it wasn't until high school that he discovered his core identity lay not solely in race but in his Christian faith. Despite some regrets about not delving earlier into his family's ethnic background, he now finds himself on a journey of understanding and embracing his roots, propelled by a desire for self-fulfillment. At the age of 27, Cedric ventured to South Korea, driven by a yearning to comprehend his mother's culture more deeply. The move dispelled preconceptions, revealing that the culture he thought he knew was a mere snapshot of his mother's experience. While being biracial has brought the pressure to represent both sides, Cedric has grown to appreciate the beauty of embodying multiple cultures. His decision to move to Korea was not only about familial connections but also an opportunity to explore his cultural heritage and advance his professional pursuits in an environment he felt connected to. Living in Korea, Cedric grapples with the dichotomy of self-assurance in his identity and a persistent longing for a sense of belonging. Despite recognizing the impossibility of changing people on a grand scale, he champions the idea that one doesn't need to be fully Korean to embrace and appreciate the culture, reinforcing the notion that identity is a nuanced and personal journey.
Keywords: African American; Civil Rights Movement; Segregation; Military; North Carolina; New Jersey; South Korea; Racism; Identity; Mixed Ethnicity; Christianity; Family, Living Abroad

Becky White, a 30-year-old with a distinct heritage, was born near Omaha, Nebraska, to a South Korean mother and an American father. Raised in a military family, she experienced constant relocation, limiting her interactions with peers and fostering a close bond with her family. Growing up amidst her parents' disparate personalities and dealing with racism in the Deep South, Becky developed a strong sense of individuality, forming her primary community within her family. Becky's journey took a pivotal turn when, at 16, she discovered her uniqueness in high school, leading her to explore her Korean identity. Motivated to understand her roots and bridge the gap with her mom, Becky moved to Korea, where her experiences studying at Yonsei University and teaching for nine months shaped her perspective on culture and injustice. To address the shared traumas of half-Koreans, she founded "The Halfie Project," using social media to build a community that empowers individuals to embrace their unique identities. Through this platform, Becky has come full circle, from a life initially defined by isolation to creating her own space and advocating for self-acceptance.

Paul Jean's journey from San Francisco to Korea is marked by a complex relationship with his Korean identity. Kara Bos (Full)
Interviewee: Bos Kara
Kara Bos, born in Korea, was adopted at nearly 3 years old and raised in Michigan. Eventually settling in Amsterdam, she married a Dutchman and is now a Dutch citizen. Growing up as the sole Asian in her community, she faced limited discrimination until later in life when she began seeking her biological mother, exposing corruption in adoption paperwork. Years of searching and going back and forth to Korea, she had a breakthrough when a DNA match came back positive – her half-nephew. Through this connection, she was able to make a family tree and find her assumed father and half-sisters. In her pursuit of roots, Kara initiated a complex legal process by filing a paternity suit against her father, leading to a tense meeting just days before his passing. The encounter, marked by hostility and confusion, left her devastated, with her father refusing to acknowledge her through photographs and concealing his face with a mask and sunglasses. Media attention and a documentary offer provided some help in searching for her mother, though the pandemic disrupted collaborative efforts, leaving her with unanswered questions. Despite these hurdles, Kara emphasizes the significance of confronting traumas for personal healing. Her ardent hope is that her children will navigate life's challenges with courage, embracing their diverse identities proudly. She wishes for them to confront any potential racial injustice with confidence in their Korean, American, and Dutch heritage, creating a legacy of resilience and pride.

Kara Bos (Edited)
Interviewee: Bos Kara
Kara Bos was born in Korea and adopted by an American couple in Sheridan, Michigan, when she was about 3 years old. She had never felt the need to find her birth family until her daughter turned two years old. Her love for her two children and wish for them to know their origins sparked the search for her birth mother and her Korean identity. Ms. Bos’ adoption journey opened her eyes to the realities of the adoption process. As she uncovered more documents and confronted conflicting information, she realized that it is not always as straightforward or honorable as she had believed. Years of searching and going back and forth to Korea, she had a breakthrough when a DNA match came back positive – her half-nephew. Through this connection, she was able to make a family tree and find her assumed father and half-sisters. As the search for answers and who her mother could be went on, Ms. Bos was met with closed doors by her assumed family. The continued refusals ultimately led Ms. Bos to file a paternity suit in 2019, something she had never expected to do. By June 2020, it was recognized by the Seoul Family Court that this man is her father. The pushback Ms. Bos faced by her birth family was turbulent and painful, but she is grateful she met them and have a basic understanding of who and where she comes from. Kara Bos hopes that her children view her exploration of her identity as something courageous and through her experience they will also find pride in who they are as Koreans.

Bobby Choy (Full)
Interviewee: Choy, Bobby
Bobby Choy, a 43-year-old New York native, had a unique upbringing as the youngest of three brothers. Born to immigrant parents who met and married in New York, Bobby's childhood in 1970s New York was marked by an interesting mix of cultural influences. Growing up in Manhattan and Queens, he found solace in his religious upbringing, often feeling most comfortable at church. Despite being a 70s baby, Bobby's childhood in New York was far from the city's current reputation. He struggled with his identity and often felt like a spectator in school, disconnected from the larger conversation. Moving 18 times by the time he reached high school, Bobby became a loner, focusing on music, guitar, and writing poetry as constants in his life. Bobby applied and got accepted to Laguardia Performing Arts High School, fulfilling a promise from his parents to let him stay in the city alone for specialized education. However, the racial challenges he faced growing up in New York prompted him to seek an escape. Attending a Christian College outside of Boston became a harsh reality check, leading to a loss of faith during his first year. Discovering Korea's vibrant indie music scene on a trip to visit his brother, Bobby extended his stay and eventually moved to Korea in 2011 with just a guitar and one suitcase. Feeling safe in Korea, he found a harmonious combination of safety and music that has kept him grounded there for over ten years—making it the longest he has ever lived in one place. Despite initial concerns from his parents, Bobby's attachment to Korea grew, and he now desires for his son to learn Korean, regretting not learning more about his heritage from his father before he passed away. Reflecting on his journey, Bobby Choy expressed these experiences in his song "Everything Always," which includes a tribute to his brother with a song called "Stupidhead."

Interviewee: Royalty, Hayden
Hayden Royalty, born and raised in Southern California, embarked on a transformative journey that led them to Korea eight years ago. They initially struggled with their Korean identity, feeling like the only Korean in school. Moving to an Asian community and finding fellow Koreans at church provided a sense of connection. Throughout their life, language played a significant role in communicating with parents in Korean and English. While studying Women and Gender Studies in university, Hayden began to explore their identity, eventually claiming their Korean American heritage. They grappled with societal expectations, wanting to be proud of their Korean roots while noticing a lack of cultural pride among non-Korean friends. Academically, they pursued Biology and Chemistry with parental expectations of becoming a doctor or pharmacist. However, with poor grades, they were eventually kicked out of college. Questioning their sexuality led them to the LGBTQ+ center and a shift to Women's Studies, reigniting their academic journey. Hayden's passion for representation and frustration with the lack of Asian and queer voices in media led them to start a YouTube channel. Despite being a private person, sharing intimate details became a therapeutic outlet with a greater purpose of representation, even though they knew their parents were against it. Later after moving to Korea, Hayden found solace in the safety and lack of hardships, even coming out to coworkers in a culture that often holds prejudices against the queer community. Now in their late twenties, Hayden reflects on their growth in Korea, recognizing the importance of creating resources that are lacking in their youth. As they prepare to leave Korea for graduate school, Hayden remains motivated by the desire to contribute to social work, inspired by the lack of available resources during their upbringing. As their peers start families, Hayden continues to advocate for representation and understanding in their journey of self-discovery.

As he grapples with his identity, Danny questions the label "Korean American," feeling caught in cultural purgatory, particularly as Koreans sometimes refer to him as a foreigner (Gyopo). Despite the complexities, his determination to redefine comedy in Korea and create opportunities for others reflect

Stephen Wagner (Full)
Interviewee: Wagner, Stephen
Stephen Wagner is a 31-year-old Korean American who grew up in the culturally limited town of Merino, Nevada. Now living in Asia for nearly six years, Stephen's background is a blend of his South Korean mother's immigrant story and his American father's mixed heritage of German and Native American roots. Despite facing racism in his hometown, Stephen considers himself blessed, cherishing the rich tapestry of cultures that shaped his upbringing. Stephen recalls experiencing the best of both worlds. His mother's efforts to teach him Korean, however, took a backseat to his active childhood focused on sports. Highschool introduced him to a dual cultural experience, alternating between American friends on weekdays and Korean friends on weekends. Post-college, burdened with student debt, Stephen discovered an opportunity to teach English in Asia, leading to a transformative six-year journey in China and Korea. Living abroad granted him confidence, freedom to explore, and a sense of specialization he felt lacking in the US. Despite initial culture shock in China, he embraced the language and culture, particularly drawn to the feeling of being unique and interesting. A visit to Korea prompted Stephen to reconnect with his roots and extended family, inspiring a move to Korea, where he was captivated by the individualistic culture, fashion, and nightlife. Weathering ups and downs during his two years in Seoul, he successfully established a business, providing a stable passive income. Reflecting on his journey, Stephen describes himself as more open and experienced. He navigated odd jobs and discovered his strengths in sales and business, acknowledging that complete fluency in Korean might elude him. Despite living abroad, he values maintaining close relationships with his family. As he looks forward to returning to the States, Stephen finds happiness in staying close to his family, a source of fulfillment and joy.

Mike Kim, a San Francisco native, spent his childhood in the Bay Area and later moved to Korea about 5-6 years ago. Despite limited proficiency in Korean, he made the decision to move to Korea, facing concerns from his parents about assimilation and career impact. Over time, his parents became proud of his contributions to Korea. Reflecting on his youth, Mike acknowledges the challenges of reconciling his dual identity as a gyopo. His first year in Korea brought frustrations, leading him to realize that the issue lay in his mindset rather than Korea itself. Despite previous clashes, he developed regret and guilt, understanding the sacrifices his parents made during immigration. Notably, Mike founded the Korea Legacy Committee, a non-profit organization in Korea. This initiative aids seniors by providing meals every weekend and involves the youth in meaningful community contributions. Mike's journey highlights the evolution of his cultural appreciation and the impact of his dual identity on his life and career.

Dr. Jerome Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Dr. Jerome
Jerome Kim's life story reflects a rich tapestry of cultural heritage and professional dedication, grounded in his roots as a third-generation Korean American with a deep connection to Hawaii. Born in Honolulu in 1959, Jerome's familial background is significant, with his grandmother being one of the first Koreans born in Hawaii in 1905. This early wave of Korean immigrants established a foundation that would become increasingly diverse with subsequent migrations in the 1980s. Raised in a multicultural environment, Jerome spoke English at home and was exposed to a strong Japanese influence, learning Japanese in elementary school and watching Japanese TV channels with English subtitles. His education continued at the University of Hawaii, where he took Japanese history classes, reflecting the layered multicultural influences of his upbringing. Jerome's familial legacy includes a rich historical record on his father’s side, with detailed biographies and photographs of his grandfather as a Korean independence fighter. This heritage played a foundational role in his identity, both personally and professionally. Jerome's career path was significantly shaped by financial and familial circumstances. Despite aspirations for medical school, financial constraints led him to join the US Air Force, which offered to pay for his medical education. This decision led him to prestigious institutions such as Yale and Duke for his medical training. His military service during the Gulf War initiated his involvement in medical research, working under Bob Redfield, the former head of the CDC, on HIV vaccines. This work continued as Jerome moved to Thailand to conduct HIV clinical trials for the US Army, showcasing his growing expertise in global health challenges. His career progression brought him to Washington, D.C., where he served as the Deputy for a new unit before eventually becoming the Director General of the International Vaccine Institute (IVI) in Seoul, South Korea. Throughout his career, Jerome has navigated changes in global health landscapes, working collaboratively across cultures and nations. His experiences reflect a commitment to understanding and leveraging global partnerships to address health crises, a mission that continues to drive his work at the IVI. Jerome's personal and professional journeys highlight his dual identity as both a dedicated American military officer and a global health leader, deeply influenced by his multicultural upbringing and the historical context of his family. His story is a testament to the impact of heritage, education, and service on shaping a life devoted to global health and cooperation.
Keywords: Hawaii; immigration; Korean Independence Fighters; biographies; U.S. military; Japanese culture; medical school; medical training; clinical trials; HIV vaccine; CDC; Washington D.C.; International Vaccine Institute (IVI); Gulf War; U.S. Air force;

Dr. Jerome Kim (Edited)
Interviewee: Kim, Dr. Jerome
Dr. Jerome Kim is the Director General of the International Vaccine Institute (IVI) in Seoul, South Korea. He is a third generation Korean American and from a family with long established roots in Hawaii. His grandmother was one of the first Koreans to be born in Hawaii and his grandfather was a Hawaiian correspondence and community organizer for the Korean Independence Movement. English was the primary language spoken in his home with his grandparents speaking Korean with each other and recalls being required to learn Japanese from the third to fifth grade. Upon graduating college, Dr. Kim joined the Air Force to help him go to medical school at Yale by the suggestion of his cousin. He had desires to continue his education following his residency and fellowship at Duke but was pulled to Texas to begin officer training in 1990 due to the Gulf War. The war was short lived and the Air Force sent him to Baltimore seeing that he was a good fit to provide assistance for HIV vaccine trials and research with the former head of the CDC, Robert Redfield. As he transitioned from the Air Force to the Army, he continued this research to further explain why the vaccine was proper protection as well as how to combat further mutations. Throughout his career, Dr. Kim has moved every two to three years and worked with a variety of different people. It was always an eye opening experience and continued to shed light on the possibilities of working together with other nations while understanding each other’s differences. As we become more of a global society, Dr. Kim hopes to further his work with different countries and to collaborate with them to reach a common goal.
Keywords: Hawaii; immigration; Korean Independence Fighters; biographies; U.S. military; Japanese culture; medical school; medical training; clinical trials; HIV vaccine; CDC; Washington D.C.; International Vaccine Institute (IVI); Gulf War; U.S. Air force;

Jason Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Jason
Jason Lee's journey from Queens, New York, to the vibrant streets of Korea, is a testament to his pursuit of dreams and opportunities. Born in Queens, he moved to an affluent New Jersey suburbs until his family's separation and faced sudden changes. He moved from a diverse neighborhood of blacks and Hispanics to a predominantly white neighborhood, then back to areas with diverse demographics. Jason discovered his passion for performing, specifically dance, in college near New York City. Battling low self-esteem, he overcame his genetic belief of lacking rhythm, inspired by an episode from the Oprah TV show. Dance became his escape, leading him to international performances and paving the way for his career. Despite initial family reservations, Jason's breakthrough in a national Intel ad displayed prominently on an NYC billboard demonstrated their shift in perspective after his uncle drove his grandparents and family from Long Island to NYC to view it. Enduring nights on 42nd Street benches during tough times, he persevered through auditions and rejections for years, eventually becoming a pioneer for Asian Americans in dance. A turning point came when record labels eyed Jason, enticing him to leap of faith and move to Korea. Despite some unexpected turns, he grew resilient as a performer. While he maintains a connection with his dad's side in Seoul, he feels closest to his grandmother on his mom's side, considering her his hero for braving hardships to seek the American Dream. As a proud Korean American, Jason rejects conventional labels, viewing himself as a custom-cut diamond. He emphasizes the importance of maintaining cultural heritage from Korean and American perspectives. Having faced skepticism in the U.S. due to his appearance, Jason encourages authenticity and individuality in a racist industry, advocating for people to be loud and unashamed. Ultimately, Jason Lee's story reflects a fusion of cultures, resilience, and the pursuit of one's authentic self in the face of challenges.
Keywords: Flushing, Queens; Dancing; Choreography; Auditions; Dance; Entertainment Industry; New Jersey; Divorce; Family; Relationships; Rejection; American Dream; Korean American; Identity; Individualism; Performer; Seoul, Korea; Resilience

Jason Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Jason
Jason Lee was born in Queens, New York but ended up moving around a lot with his family. He hated dancing because his friends said he didn’t have rhythm and wasn’t born with it – so he believed it to be a genetic thing. While attending college, he had the opportunity to see the Oprah Winfrey Show where they brought a choreographer to teach the basic steps of dancing. He felt inspired and put months of practice into simple choreographies that soon sparked his love for dance and eventually joining a street dance crew in Time Square. Being the only Asian in the crew and lacking experience compared to his peers, he knew he had to overcompensate by using his sense of humor and character. But performing in front of people was pure exhilaration for Jason that it gave him the confidence he never had. He recognized the lack of Asian representation in the dance industry and went to any audition and casting he could find to prove to himself that he could make it as well as show that Asian Americans can dance. Since then, he has travelled internationally to perform behind artists and has even appeared ads. Jason currently resides in South Korea and since arriving, has realized the sacrifices his grandmother and family had to make when immigrating to the US. As life furthers in Korea, Jason hopes to continue to champion his Korean and American identities to overcome any obstacles he may face.
