Bernie Cho (Full)
Interviewee: Cho, Bernie
Bernie Cho is a prominent music executive born in 1971 in Pittsburgh, PA. Growing up in Jamestown, NY, Bernie embraced the snowy environment and developed a love for skiing. Raised by first-generation immigrant parents, he navigated cultural ignorance more than overt racism and witnessed the dedication of Korean Americans, including his parents, to practicing medicine to secure citizenship. Despite familial expectations to pursue a medical career, Bernie resisted stereotypes through his passion for sports. A Dartmouth College attendee, he explored various majors before discovering his interest in Asian studies and film. Bernie's immersion in East Asian courses exposed the lack of representation in the entertainment industry, motivating him to shift his major to Government and Asian studies. Fascinated by MTV and the music industry, Bernie, though tone-deaf, found his niche in adjacent roles like videography. The lack of Asian representation fueled his determination to contribute to the industry's diversity. Before beginning his graduate career in Korea, Bernie landed a job teaching English to music label executives by helping them understand incoming trends and terms. Through an unexpected meeting at a party and his passion for music and MTV, he was offered a position to work for Korea’s first music television channel, thus launching his career in the industry.  Reflecting on his 25+ years in the music industry, Bernie recalls the transformative shift when music became a visual experience. Witnessing the explosive growth of Korean music, he considers it his most challenging and proudest achievement. His multicultural background and language skills provide a unique perspective on Korean American identity. Passionate about advancing the Korean music industry, Bernie aims to pay it forward by offering wisdom and advice to those seeking guidance. Today, he remains dedicated to fostering multicultural perspectives and contributing to the industry's evolution.

Kesung Anderson (Full)
Interviewee: Anderson, Kesung
Kesung Anderson, a unique individual of dual heritage—half Swedish, half American—navigated a dynamic childhood marked by relocations from Virginia to New York, Korea, and eventually settling in Minnesota for two decades. After completing a few years of school in Korea, his family moved to Minnesota to be with his grandparents. In contrast, his mother continued working as a Korean folk ballet teacher in Korea. His father was employed in patent law in the U.S. As the new kid in middle school, Kesung remembers being picked on and treated like an outcast. He was scrawny, didn’t like to curse like the other kids, and still wore clothing from Korea, making him very aware and self-conscious of his differences from his peers. One day, he had the opportunity to participate in the running event at school, and surprised everyone when he began to pass his classmates. Kesung was ultimately recruited for the track team and beat the top runner at a sectional meet, thus helping him grow out of his shell and gain confidence and respect from his peers. Overcoming adversity, he became his high school's first MVP in track and field, discovering confidence through sports. He went to Bethel University for college and encountered a more accepting environment. Choosing a Christian college, Kesung entered the biology field, initially aspiring to be a doctor but later transitioning into Medical Sales, accumulating 15 years of sales experience. A significant life change came when he moved to Korea, leaving behind familiar surroundings for an unfamiliar culture. Despite initial challenges adapting to the social scene, Kesung refrained from forming friendships with other foreigners, determined to build a network among Koreans. His sense of identity flourished in Korea, and he became comfortable as a unique individual, a stark contrast to his earlier struggles. Reflecting on his journey, Kesung emphasizes the importance of staying close to one's culture. He recognizes the significance of cultural ties, offering insights that could have boosted his younger self's confidence. Today, living in Korea, Kesung cherishes his distinct identity and encourages others to embrace their roots.
Keywords: Multiracial; Multicultural; Track & Field; Sports; American; Arlington, Virginia; New York; South Korea; Minnesota; Bullying; Bethel University; Medical Sales; Networking;

Eric McDaniel (Full)
Interviewee: McDaniel, Eric
Eric McDaniel, a Korean American adoptee, moved from Kansas City, Missouri, to Korea in 2008, driven by a desire to reconnect with his birth family. Endearingly known as "McLee," he faced a challenging early childhood in Korea, enduring abuse, homelessness, and abandonment, and eventually dropped off at an inadequate orphanage in Incheon. Adopted at four, Eric vividly recalls meeting his American family and the immediate sense of security and love they provided. On the car ride from the airport, his mom opened a photo album showing four Polaroid photos – one of a car, a big house, his Mom and brother smiling, and his bed. Having vivid memories of being abandoned, it finally clicked with him that he was getting a second chance, and this was the family he wanted. Overcoming language barriers in the Midwest, he grappled with racism but emerged resilient, excelling academically and athletically in high school. However, the pursuit of acceptance led him to compromise his identity, accepting derogatory terms like "Chino." University marked a pivotal shift as he reconnected with his Korean heritage through his first Korean friend, rekindling a passion for Asian culture. Embracing his mixed identity, Eric immersed himself in Korean language, food, and lifestyle. The catalyst for a deeper exploration of his roots came when he decided to find his birth parents. With flashbacks of Korea coming back to him, he made up his mind to go to Korea as an English teacher and use it as an opportunity to search for his birth parents. The search was successful, and he had the chance to rekindle a relationship with his parents and extended family while discovering he had half-siblings adopted to France. Reconnecting with his mother and father, he forgave his father, providing a second chance for a meaningful relationship. Uncovering truths about his past, including a misattributed birthday, brought relief and understanding. Eric shares how second chances allowed him to love his Korean family again and thanks the internet and global adoptee community for helping him locate his half-siblings. Now residing in Korea for 13 years, Eric has made it his second home, dividing his time between his separated birth parents. Eric cherishes the moments of reconciliation and regrets not embracing his Korean identity sooner. His story is one of resilience, forgiveness, and a profound connection with the cultural tapestry of Korea.
Keywords: Korean American Adoptee; Incheon, South Korea; Orphanage; Abuse; Neglect; Birth Parents; Homelessness; American family; Language Barriers; Midwest; Kansas City, Missouri; Baseball; Academics; Cultural Ignorance; Bullying; Forgiveness; Asian Culture; Korean Heritage; Trauma; Half-siblings; Resilience;

Joseph Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Joseph
After his family immigrated to America in 1976, Joseph Kim and his brother grew up being one of the only non-white students in their community.  His parents, both international students, faced challenges, with his mother initially giving up education to care for the family. Joseph's family moved to the U.S. when he was four, and despite financial struggles, his father pursued an MBA, working tirelessly at an army supply shop while attending night school. Joseph and his brother essentially raised themselves during this period. His parents moved to a poor area in Chicago, qualifying for government aid, and Joseph faced challenges fitting in at school. Though his parents worked hard to help them fit in, he still endured teasing because of his race and financial status. He recollects when an old teacher at school called him a racial slur–extremely shocked and angry, fourteen-year-old Joseph did not know how to react. Though initially contemplating more extreme action to take his anger out on that adult, he settled on a more peaceful resolution. As a young man, he found two communities for himself: one within the Asian American church, and one in the rock genre of music. His dream was to become a musician, but his mother gave him the choice to continue his education after two years of pursuing music. Eventually, he became a professor. Through living in Japan for seven years, Joseph realized the different ways his identity was viewed; in Japan, people saw him as simply a Korean who speaks English, but once he moved to Korea he felt more American. He is currently a professor of nonverbal communication at the school of business of Sungkyunkwan University in Seoul, South Korea. He also has a company called H2H, which collaborates with major Korean corporations with a global presence to enhance employee communication skills. . He is a single father with two teenage sons; he hopes that through his own Korean American story, his sons will realize how making their own choices will shape their lives, and how the ability to make those choices can empower them.
Keywords: Seoul, South Korea; business school; mba; graduate school; Education; night school; Chicago, Illinois; University of Illinois; Japan; H2H; Sungkyunkwan University; Language barrier; family; racism; racial slurs; government aid; financial struggles; professor; identity;

Brian Kuh (Full)
Interviewee: Kuh, Brian
Brian Kuh, a 34-year-old born in Korea and raised in Chicago, embarked on a dynamic journey that shaped his identity as a bridge between two cultures. Moving to the United States at 6, Brian faced the challenge of adapting to new environments, attending ten different schools from first to 12th grade. His father, a pastor, led the family's move to Denver, Colorado, where Brian spent 12 years and found solace in church. Despite the constant transitions and being the new Asian student at school, the church provided Brian with a sense of community and a platform to embrace his Korean heritage. In university, he pursued his passion for Communication, Writing, and Music, aspiring to enter the music industry. Brian's journey took a turn when he moved to Korea after his F1 Visa expired to seek a career in the entertainment industry. Before pursuing his dreams, he fulfilled his military service. This experience provided insights into Korean culture and deepened his understanding of his parents, realizing the prevalence of fear as a driving force in Korean society. Now bilingual in Korean and English, Brian serves as a bridge between cultures, excelling in translating and hosting as an MC. Despite feeling more connected to his Korean roots, he acknowledges his unique position as a Korean who speaks English fluently. His acceptance of Christ as an adult and his experiences in Korea and church revealed his vulnerabilities and shaped his perspective on brokenness. Brian Kuh's narrative reflects a journey of cultural duality, where he navigates the complexities of being a Korean immersed in American culture and vice versa, ultimately finding strength and purpose in bridging the gap between the two worlds.

Brian Kuh (Edited)
Interviewee: Kuh, Brian
Brian Kuh was born in South Korea and moved to America at the age of six. He moved around frequently throughout his childhood, which always made him the new Asian student at school. By second grade, he started to realize how much his peers were teasing him and even recalls having rocks thrown at him. Instead of harboring any form of bitterness, Brian just wanted to feel like he belonged and continued to have a lingering feeling of being “in the middle”. With other Koreans, they would say he is too American. With Korean Americans, they would say he was too Korean. Eventually, he realized being between two cultures is a uniqueness not many people have and would allow him to become a bridge connecting two communities. 11 years ago, Brian returned home to Korea and went to fulfill his civil duty in the military. Throughout his experience, he was able to understand Korean society, his roots and his parents more. Upon completing his service, he went on to create his own curriculum to translate Korean music into English and teach the artists the pronunciations. This furthered his career to become a translating MC for fan-meetings, concerts, and other shows. As he continues his life in Korea, he hopes to continue being a bridge to help communicate between Korean and American cultures within the music industry.

Joshua Hwang (Full)
Interviewee: Hwang, Joshua
Joshua Hwang, a Philadelphia native raised in a diverse community, grew up as one of five boys with a father as a pastor's household and mother who ran two dry cleaning businesses. Despite the diversity, he encountered racism and had to toughen up physically, participating in various sports to navigate his environment. Growing up, Joshua distanced himself from his Korean identity, avoiding Korean culture and food. He related more to American influences, spending time away from home and having few Asian friends. His parents, deeply rooted in the Christian faith, caused Joshua to rebell from the standard ideas of being a PK, or pastor’s kid, and tried to avoid the Korean American community as much as possible. The youngest of five boys, Joshua found connection and comfort with other diverse friends but avoided association with Koreans to prevent gossip and protect his parents' reputation. Later in life, after quitting his job, his parents bought him a plane ticket to Korea while simultaneously booking a trip to Hawaii for ten days. They knew that if he took the trip to Hawaii, he would consider living out in Korea for three months with his brothers. Initially resistant, Joshua's perspective shifted during a church visit in Korea, where he felt a spiritual calling to stay. He embraced the opportunity, met his wife during the three months, and started teaching before transitioning to full-time work. His entrepreneurial spirit led him to own a bar, start an events company, and eventually venture into diamond sales, opening doors that transformed his life. For Joshua, the journey was never about proving his Korean identity to others. Instead, he fully embraced being a Korean American, finding love and fulfillment in Korea while building a successful career and has now lived there for seven years. His story reflects the transformative power of embracing one's identity and the unexpected opportunities that can arise when stepping into the unknown.
Keywords: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania; family; pastor’s kid; church; Christianity; dry cleaning business; korean food; korean culture; racism; identity; Hawaii; South Korea; cultural barriers; entrepreneur; small business owner

Mandy Hwang, originally from Tulsa, Oklahoma, has called Korea home for the past decade. In college, Mandy pursued her passion for music and aspired to move to Korea to explore K-pop. However, upon arriving, she took a teaching job, discovering a newfound passion that shifted her focus. In her quest for community, she engaged with diverse groups of people, fostering connections and broadening her social circle. Living in Korea has deepened Mandy's respect for her mother and has allowed her to envision a long-term life in the country. While she didn't always feel like a true Korean American growing up, Mandy now feels a profound connection to Korean culture, having explored and embraced this significant aspect of her identity. Korea, for her, is not just a place of residence; it has become a heartfelt home.

Greg Norrish (Full)
Interviewee: Norrish, Greg
Greg Norrish, born in Seoul in 1986, was adopted to the US at the age of 3 months. Growing up in Northeastern Connecticut for 23 years, he later spent 8 years in Sacramento, CA, before moving to Korea four years ago. At 35, he reflects on a journey that traverses identity, family, and a newfound connection to his Korean heritage. Raised in a loving and supportive family with a civil engineer father and a mother who is a teacher and social worker, Greg's adoption through SWS shaped his multicultural family experience. His curiosity about his heritage intensified as he contemplated a move to Korea, prompting discussions about his adoption and identity. In his formative years, Greg found his place among those focused on enjoying life, gradually exploring his identity more deeply in high school. Although he proudly identified as Korean in his youth, questions about his identity surfaced around the age of 18. It wasn't until he was 27 in Sacramento that he made his first Korean friend, sparking admiration for her pride in her identity. Greg obtained a master's in Public Health from George Washington University, complementing his interest in the food industry. Despite having his first taste of Korean cuisine at 28, his move to Korea in 2017 became a pivotal moment as he grappled with the desire to find his birth mother and learn her story. While the search narrowed to two potential matches, the heartbreaking realization that she had passed away brought acceptance. Greg now acknowledges the need to delve deeper into his roots, research further, and hopes to meet his birth family someday. Currently engaged in a new business venture in the food industry, Greg, alongside his wife, has found comfort in Korea, where the pressure to explain his life in detail has lifted. Embracing his Korean American identity, he acknowledges its significance and encourages fellow adoptees to navigate their journeys at their own pace. Today, Greg's story stands as a testament to the evolving nature of identity and the diverse paths one can take to find comfort and belonging.
Keywords: Korean adoptee; Seoul, South Korea; Sacramento, California; Connecticut; identity; korean food; public health; masters degree; George Washington University; food business; entrepreneur;

Greg Norrish (Edited)

Greg Norrish was born in Seoul, South Korea in 1986 and was adopted to Northeastern CT when he was about 3 months old. Throughout his childhood, he distinctly remember being perpetually aware of his differences. By the age of 18, he began to process his identity in multiple ways and continued until he was nearing his late twenties, which was when he chose to come to Korea. Greg had no idea of what he was hoping for when he came to Korea. To a certain degree, he wanted to find a purpose while trying to live out the inflated dreams he made throughout his childhood but he was also feeling pressured because he told people back home his decision was to explore his Korean roots. After living in Korea for four years, he stopped worrying about his adoption because he found himself being able to feel comfortable in his own skin and living life through work and meeting people who embraced him as he is. As Greg continues life in Korea, he hopes to continue to process and explore his identity through each moment that comes naturally in his everyday life.

Chris Packard (Full)
Interviewee: Packard, Chris
Chris Packard, born in 1984 in Toledo, Ohio, grew up in a suburban Midwest town along the Great Lakes. Raised by a first-generation Korean American mother and an American (white) father, his childhood was marked by a cultural blend. His father, a computer engineer, and his mother, who managed various Korean small businesses in their town, navigated their multicultural household. Joining the U.S. Army at 19 in 2004, Chris served six years, including a challenging 26-month deployment in Iraq. The experience left him with depression and PTSD, but finding a supportive community helped him overcome these challenges. Chris moved to Orange County, California, for community college after his military service. However, he felt a stronger connection to his roots in Ohio, finding comfort in a small town. After an invitation from his older brother, Chris spent 6-7 years in Seoul, South Korea. Working in a friend's restaurant for two years, he eventually co-owned a bar and restaurant with his brother. Chris doesn't view Korea as a forever home. Despite the familial connections, he still considers himself homeless, emphasizing his challenge in defining a sense of belonging. Language plays a pivotal role in Chris's identity. Limited proficiency in Korean creates a barrier, making it difficult for him to embrace his Korean heritage fully. However, he proudly identifies as an American and a Korean American. While he believes a stronger connection with Korea could come through language, Chris is content with his current self and expresses happiness with who he has become. His journey highlights the complex interplay between identity, language, and cultural connection.
Keywords: Biracial; Multicultural; multiracial; US military; PTSD; military service; depression; Midwest; Toledo, Ohio; Iraq; deployment; Orange County, California; community college; family; homelessness; identity; language barrier; cultural barrier; korean language; Seoul, Korea; entrepreneur; restaurant; bar;

Chris Packard (Edited)
Interviewee: Packard, Chris
Chris Packard was born and raised in Toledo, Ohio to a Korean mother and American father. He recalls growing up with a true rural Midwestern life filled with baseball and bicycle rides but also vivid memories of helping his mother run her small businesses – something he considers a defining part in his childhood. Chris served in the military in the Middle East and went through a period of finding himself and maneuvering depression. He ended up traveling across the US on his motorcycle to search for his own network and soon found himself going back to where his roots were, Ohio. Ohio always felt like home but Chris has grown to find comfort in Korea, where he currently resides. As he furthers his life in Korea, Chris hopes to continue to explore what being Korean means for him and his identity as a proud Korean American.

Madison Jay (Full)

Madison Jay (Edited)
Madison Jay was born in Korea in 1995 and was adopted to Arizona to a two-parent household with an older brother, who is also a Korean adoptee. Her parents were never shy in exposing her and her brother to Korean culture and read them books about adoption or Korean American identity as well as sending them to Korean heritage camp. As she went through high school and college, people knew her as the adoptee and would question her Koreanness. She didn’t speak the language, eat Korean food, or even know the latest K-Pop band causing her understanding of beauty and self to become unclear. Going to Korea to teach English was not part of Madison’s life plan because she didn’t feel ready to open that can of worms and was scared of how she would be viewed. But she came to realize she needed to escape Arizona and have a conversation with herself to confront her own identity. As Madison lives her life in Korea, she hopes to continue to experience Korea fully and to put out good energy.

Cory Lemke (Full)
Interviewee: Lemke, Cory
Cory Lemke was born in Jeonju, South Korea, and grew up in northern Iowa, where he was adopted and raised. Currently residing in Seoul for nine years, he teaches English and does some editing work as well. Adopted at six months old, Cory spent his childhood in Iowa before moving to Tucson, Arizona, just before high school and college. With six members in his family, including two older siblings and a younger sister, he shares a special bond with his youngest adopted sister, navigating life's challenges together. Growing up, Cory's family, who was white, did not actively incorporate Korean culture into their lives. Their and the local community’s colorblind upbringing in Iowa, two and a half hours from Des Moines, resulted in an environment where race wasn't a prevalent consideration. However, this changed when Cory moved to Tucson, where he began experiencing overt racism and a shift in how others perceived his identity. Cory attended Arizona State University, studying Chinese and Political Science for four years. Before settling in Korea, he lived in Taiwan and explored various Asian countries. Korea became the last stop in his travels, driven by a desire to potentially meet his birth family and teach English. His reunion with his Korean family was a straightforward process, facilitated by a case worker who located them in Jeonju within a day. Though there were initial anxieties about the meeting, he feels fortunate for their openness to him and recognized that having the opportunity to physically see where you come from is a powerful sensation unique to adoptees. Getting close with his birth family had its challenges, mostly due to differences in socialization and values. Today, Cory acknowledges the infinite ways one can express their Korean identity. Cory Lemke currently works with Democrats Abroad, a job which has defined his time in Korea. He feels that he identifies as both Korean and American, but doesn’t necessarily feel Korean American as a whole – he simply does Korean things in America, and American things in Korea. Over the years of observing the Korean people in his life, Cory has learned that there is no singular definition of what it means to be Korean or Korean American.
Keywords: Korean adoptee; Iowa; rural; Jeonju, South Korea; Tucson, Arizona; Arizona State University; identity; racism; birth family; teaching English; living aborad; Democrats Abroad; family reunion;

Jeanne Jang (Full)
Interviewee: Jang, Jeanne
Jeanne Jang sits down with her son Owen as he learns more of his mother’s story in this interview. Jeanne Jang, a 51-year-old Korean American, shares her journey from immigrating to the U.S. at the age of 7 to grappling with identity, language barriers, and subtle racism. Born in Seoul, Korea, Jeanne's family settled in Virginia Beach, where she initially spoke only Korean, feeling the need to learn English when interacting with her American classmates. Despite her father's proficiency in English, Jeanne learned the language through school and playing with neighborhood kids. After a year there, she moved to Flushing, Queens, where she embraced the rule of speaking Korean at home and English outside. Though private schooling posed academic challenges growing up, attending Smith College provided a liberating environment. Courses on racism resonated with her, making her feel seen and heard. Growing up amidst a diverse community, Jeanne faced both overt and subtle racism, dealing with societal beauty norms that didn't align with her appearance. As a mother, Jeanne reflects on her wish to connect her children more with Korean opportunities and communities. However, her current sadness stems from witnessing her grandfather's struggle with Alzheimer's, seeing him lose confidence and certainty. She admires her father as a trailblazer and risk-taker, highlighting the challenges and triumphs that have shaped her life. Her father’s relationship with her son and her relationship with her father has reminded her of the importance of self-acceptance and being comfortable with who you are.
Keywords: Virginia Beach; Flushing, Queens; immigration; family; self-acceptance; identity’ Alzheimer’s; language; Seoul, Korea; racism; Smith college; anti-Asian hate; Black Lives Matters;

Jeanne Jang (Edited)

In our first Remote Edition of Legacy Project, Jeanne Jang sits down with her son Owen as he gets to know more of his mother’s story in this interview. John Park (Full)
Interviewee: Park, John
John Park spent only six years in Seoul, South Korea before moving around the world due to his father’s job as a diplomat. He moved to Canada, New Zealand, Singapore, and eventually landed in the United States. In this sitdown interview with his son Owen, Mr. Park recalls his highschool experiences in Virginia and remarks on his feelings of being an outsider looking in.It was only later in his college years when he was able to find a sense of belonging through his martial arts “tribe”. In this interview, he expresses how he discovered his happiness well into his 30s and also shares advice for Owen as they consider their next chapter in their lives. John experienced a nomadic childhood due to his father's diplomatic career, finding a sense of home in New Zealand for five years. As the youngest of three siblings, his family faced fragmentation, with his siblings returning to Korea, marking a period of emotional detachment his Korean American heritage, especially after moving to the states. Living in New Zealand from ages 8 to 13, John witnessed its unique diversity, primarily due to their inclusive approach towards their indigenous population too. However, a move to Virginia at 14 revealed stark racial segregation in Washington, D.C., contrasting with the inclusive environments he had previously encountered in Singapore and New Zealand. In high school, feeling like an outsider, John immersed himself in college at the age of 16, discovering solace and friendship through martial arts. While lacking a spiritual connection to his Korean American heritage, John approaches it intellectually and seeks to expose his son to both aspects. As an Asian American teacher, John takes pride in creating a safe space for his students, aiming to foster a supportive environment. His journey reflects the complexities of identity, belonging, and the pursuit of understanding and acceptance.
Keywords: New Zealand; diplomat; Virginia; Washington, D.C.; racial segregation; racism; teacher; identity; family; inclusivity; Singapore; Canada

John Limb (Full)
Interviewee: Limb, John
John Limb, a 51-year-old born in Brooklyn, New York, experienced an Americanized upbringing where speaking Korean at home was not emphasized. His parents, both doctors who met at the hospital in the 60s, consciously chose not to teach him Korean to ensure fluency in English. Attending Roselyn high school, where he was one of three Korean families in a predominantly white high school, John's cultural background wasn't a focal point in his early years. Despite his parents' challenging immigration experience, they maintained a primarily English-speaking household, contributing to a strained relationship with his father. However, as John entered college, their connection improved. His mother, a constant support, played a crucial role in John's life, along with his brother, with whom he grew closer after high school and college. John pursued his education at Cornell, then entered the professional world with a consulting job before attending law school at Boston University. His career took him from Seattle to New York, where he worked in One World Trade Center during the tragic events of 9/11. Later, he transitioned to in-house work at AIG. In 2017, John's life took a turn when introduced to makkeouli at a Korean American Story barbecue by Julie Young. Impressed by the drink, he connected with Alice, the maker, and became business partners and now runs Hana Makkeouli. This experience ignited John's passion for building a company, trying new products, and producing a unique Korean item not widely available in the U.S. While his parents never pressured him to follow a specific career path, John is proud of his family and his business partner. He expresses how grateful he is for Erin and her sister’s ability to embrace their Korean heritage and hopes that they find a path that will bring joy and fulfill them in every way possible.
Keywords: Brooklyn, New York; Consulting; immigration; Americanized; Cornell University; Boston University; AIG; 9/11; Korean American Story; family; law school; lawyer; entrepreneur; brewery; Hana Makkeouli

Judy Hong (Full)
Interviewee: Hong, Judy
In this Remote Edition of Legacy Project, Judy Hong sits down with her daughter, Erin, and shares her journey as a working Korean American woman. Originally from Seoul, Korea, Judy Hong immigrated with her family to New York in Queens when she was 12. Despite tearful beginnings, the move aimed for better education and business opportunities. She struggled during her first year in America and would practice saying, “I don’t speak English,” to avoid conversations. Judy, now a proud US citizen since 2009, still vividly recalls her strong memories of Korea. After college, initially aspiring to be a journalist, Judy's career took an unexpected turn when she joined a Korean investment company, translating materials and gaining exposure to finance. Pursuing her interest, she earned an MBA, leading to a remarkable 20+ years at Goldman Sachs, specializing in consumer goods analysis. She is Head of Investor Relations in the cannabis industry at Canopy Growth. Throughout her career, Judy faced subtle sexism and racism, navigating pressures to succeed that stemmed from her internal drive. Her father's venture into a Manhattan clothing store added to the family's narrative of resilience and determination. While the journey to America presents challenges, Judy views it as a source of strength, instilling in her the belief that she can overcome anything. Despite her success, she humbly remains focused on empathy, being there for others, and making a positive difference in people's lives. With pride in her family and her remarkable career trajectory as an Asian American woman who once didn't speak English at age 12, Judy strives to be remembered for her impactful and compassionate contributions.
Keywords: Immigration; MBA; business school; journalism; investment; cannabis; clothing store; entrepreneur; family; racism; sexism;

Faith Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Faith
Faith Kim's narrative is a profound exploration of identity and resilience shaped by her Korean American heritage and her family's military background. Born in 1999 in Deerfield, Illinois, and raised in Peoria, Faith experienced a significant cultural isolation during her early years in a predominantly white school, where she was one of only two Asians in her grade. This isolation led to an identity struggle, as she grappled with her dual Korean and American identities without a substantial community of peers who could relate to her experiences. Despite these challenges, Faith's engagement with her Korean heritage deepened in middle school when she began exploring K-pop and K-dramas. This cultural engagement sparked her interest in learning the Korean language through Rosetta Stone, even as she communicated with her mother in a mix of Korean and English. Her parents, who immigrated to the United States in their early teens, provided a model of perseverance and adaptation. Faith's academic and career path reflects a blend of her interests and her family's legacy of service. After a year at Bradley University, she attended the Air Force Academy, choosing to study Behavioral Science. Her decision was influenced by the structured environment and clear career path the military offered, aligning with her need for direction and stability post-college. The Air Force not only provided a career but also a supportive community where she found a strong Korean American network that helped her reconcile her bicultural identity. Graduating from the Air Force Academy stands out as one of her most cherished achievements, symbolizing both personal growth and academic success. Her current role in the Air Force's Cyber career field continues her family's tradition of service—her father served in the Army before transitioning to civilian roles aiding underserved communities, and her brother is also in the military. Today, Faith embodies the Korean American identity that once confounded her, understanding it as a strength that allows her to appreciate both her cultural heritage and the opportunities America has afforded her family. Her experiences have taught her the crucial lesson of self-worth, encouraging her not to underestimate her abilities but to approach challenges with confidence. Faith has four more years committed to the Air Force, during which she plans to continue developing her skills and serving her country, while her family continues to make impactful contributions in various social sectors, from education to social justice. Her story is a testament to the power of embracing one's heritage and the profound impact of a supportive community in overcoming personal and cultural challenges.
Keywords: Air Force Academy; U.S. military; Rosetta Stone; Air Force; Korean American identity; community; college; Bradley University; Behavioral Science;

Faith Kim (Edited)
Interviewee: Kim, Faith
Faith Kim was born and raised in the Chicago suburbs of Illinois. She went to a predominantly white school, and struggled with the concept of being both Korean and American. She began to connect with her heritage in middle school when she began watching K-dramas with her mother and learning Korean through Rosetta Stone. Her father and brother both served in the military; though she originally had no intention of joining, she liked the opportunities it presented in terms of both education and serving her country and followed her family’s footsteps. It was scary to be in a completely new environment but she was able to meet other Korean Americans with similar backgrounds to her and she learned a lot about herself and her identity as a Korean American. Now, she is an officer in the Air Force and in the cyber career field. For Faith Kim, being Korean American means embracing both sides of her culture and being able to appreciate her parents’ journeys in relation to her own. Her experiences in the Air Force have taught her not to underestimate herself, but to have confidence because if she works hard, she can be capable of amazing things.

Seonwoong Hwang (Full)
Interviewee: Hwang, Seonwoong
Seonwoong Hwang's journey from South Korea to the United States encapsulates a profound narrative of cultural transition, spiritual dedication, and personal growth. Born in Seoul in 1984 and raised in a deeply religious family, Seonwoong spent his formative years in Bucheon and later Incheon. His father's role as a pastor and his mother's active church involvement profoundly influenced his upbringing and career choices. After two decades in Korea, Seonwoong moved to the U.S. to broaden his educational and cultural horizons. He pursued further theological studies, driven by a desire to deepen his understanding of the Bible and church teachings. The birth of his son, which he describes as the happiest day of his life, marked a significant milestone, bringing new joys and responsibilities. Seonwoong appreciates several aspects of life in America that differ markedly from his experiences in Korea. He values the cultural diversity and the more relaxed social norms regarding age and lifestyle, which contrast with the more rigid expectations he faced in Korea. Additionally, he finds that being a pastor in the U.S. offers a more receptive environment due to the more religious nature of American society compared to the secular leanings of Korean culture. Despite his positive experiences in America, Seonwoong still feels a deep connection to his roots. He misses the rich culinary traditions, the convenience of urban life in Korea, and most poignantly, the proximity to his parents and extended family. His current life in Tennessee, with its welcoming community, pleasant weather, and beautiful natural surroundings, offers him a comforting and supportive environment. Professionally, Seonwoong is now a pastor for a college ministry in Nashville, focusing on guiding Korean American youth. He aims to provide a safe space where they can explore their cultural and spiritual identities. His life’s work is shaped by a commitment to serve through his faith, hoping to influence his congregation and his own children to pursue their passions while maintaining their faith and cultural identity. Seonwoong's story is one of navigating multiple worlds—balancing the demands of his faith, the challenges of immigration, and the responsibilities of family and community leadership—all while fostering an environment where future generations can thrive without losing sight of their heritage.
Keywords: Bucheon; Incheon; Seoul, South Korea; ministry; church; Christianity; pastor; Nashville, Tennessee; youth pastor; college ministry; social norms; theology;

Dr. Doug Hong (Full)
Interviewee: Hong, Doug
Dr. Doug Hong was born in 1941 to what he described as a very average family. By the time he was in elementary school the Korean War had broke out, and to this day he carries with him vivid memories of seeing bodies on the streets along his neighborhood. Two days after the North Korean army entered Seoul, he and his friends saw a bomb fall on a neighboring street, and felt the buildings around them shake. As his family prepared to evacuate, they realized that they wouldn’t be able to cross the Han River as its bridges were all destroyed, so they instead went into hiding in the mountains for three months, in fear that they would be forced into the North Korean army should they be captured. After three months, they were able to return to their house but it wasn’t long after that the North Korean army, this time backed by the Chinese military, once again invaded Seoul; this time, the family was able to cross the Han on makeshift wooden bridges, and take a train south to Busan. Hong stayed in Busan until he graduated from elementary school, by which time the war had ended, and returned to Seoul alone to attend middle school, before coming back to Busan to attend high school. He once again returned to Seoul to attend college, and at his father’s suggestion, enrolled and graduated from medical school, after which he completed mandatory service and decided to immigrate to the United States in 1970, hearing of the medical training he could receive while there. He landed in Chicago, then head to Ohio to complete an internship at Ohio State, and attended Vanderbilt for residency, specializing in radiology at the behest of his senior. After completing residency, he was offered a full-time faculty position in their medical school, and taught for three years before joining a private practice where he worked until his retirement. Through it all, he credits God and the strangers who helped him adjust to his new home in America for his life’s work.
Keywords: Korean War; North Korea; invasion; Seoul; bombs; train; Han River; Han; bridge; Busan; elementary; middle; high; college; medical school; residency; internship; faculty; professor; Ohio; Columbus; Vanderbilt; Tennesse; Nashville; Radiology; church

Dr. Doug Hong (Edited)
Interviewee: Hong, Doug
Dr. Doug Hong was born in Korea in 1941. By the time he was 9 years old, the Korean War had begun. He recalls being faced with extreme hunger that he caught insects like cicadas and flies to eat. As he was fleeing south with his family towards Seoul, bombs destroyed bridges causing them to seek refuge in a mountain in the outskirts of Seoul. His dad and uncle hid in a cave to make sure they wouldn’t be caught and forced to join the North Korean army, while the rest of his family found shelter nearby. Three months went by and UN troops reclaimed Seoul. Dr. Hong still remembers the horrid sights that laid in front of his eyes on his walk back home. Looking back at his life, Dr. Hong was able to experience so many wonderful things: having 3 children, 9 grandchildren, deciding his specialty in radiology, becoming a resident and eventually a faculty member at Vanderbilt, and even having his own private practice. He doesn’t believe any of it was done solely on his own, but by the support he received from everyone around him. He hopes that he can continue to live happily and with gratitude with the rest of his family as they continue to grow and succeed in all parts of the country.
Keywords: Korean War; North Korea; invasion; Seoul; bombs; train; Han River; Han; bridge; Busan; elementary; middle; high; college; medical school; residency; internship; faculty; professor; Ohio; Columbus; Vanderbilt; Tennesse; Nashville; Radiology; church

Amanda Assalone (Full)
Interviewee: Assalone, Amanda
Amanda Assalone was born in 1978 and adopted at the tender age of three months by a family in Tulsa, Oklahoma, Amanda's early life was marked by a sense of uniqueness; she and her adopted sister, also from Korea, were the only Koreans in their area. The adoption, facilitated by Dillon International, was the beginning of Amanda's journey into her Korean American identity, a journey that would take her across the United States and deep into the heart of her heritage. Despite being adopted into a loving family, Amanda grew up with a narrative that painted her birth family's decision as one driven by financial hardship. It wasn't until much later in life, after completing her Ph.D. and experiencing significant personal milestones—including marriage, divorce, and a deep dive into her professional life—that Amanda would uncover the true reason behind her adoption. Her birth parents, already having five daughters, had hoped for a son, leading to Amanda's adoption. This revelation came through efforts facilitated by her adoption agency and the Korean consulate, culminating in an emotional reunion with her birth mother and sisters in a nursing home. These encounters were marked by a mix of joy, discomfort with the lavish attentions of her birth sisters, and a deepening connection to her roots.Amanda's journey is also a story of personal growth and community engagement. Moving to Jersey after getting married at around 30, and later to Atlanta following her divorce and academic achievements, Amanda found solace and identity within the Korean American community. Her work with organizations such as Asian Americans Advancing Justice, the Korean American Coalition, and the Korean Adoptee Association in Georgia became pillars of her life. These experiences, coupled with the profound impact of events like the Atlanta Spa Shootings, underscored the importance of community for Amanda, offering both safety and a sense of belonging. Her personal trials, including her divorce and the challenges of reconnecting with her birth family, have been transformative. Learning about her true origins and grappling with language barriers as she improves her Korean have been parts of her journey toward self-discovery. Amanda has also found comfort and connection in embracing Korean cuisine, which she considers both a remedy for her physical well-being and a link to her cultural roots. Amanda's story is one of overcoming misconceptions and fabricated narratives to find truth and connection. Her experiences reflect a broader narrative of adoptees navigating the complexities of identity, heritage, and belonging. Now settled in Atlanta, Amanda's involvement in the Korean American and adoptee communities represents not just a personal achievement, but a commitment to fostering understanding and support for others on similar journeys.
Keywords: adoptee; Dillon Internation; family reunion; birth mother; Tulsa, Oklahoma; Seoul, South Korea; Asian Americans Advancing Justice; Korean American Coalition; Korean Adoptee Association; postdoctorate; doctor of philosophy; Atlanta, Georgia; phd

Amanda Assalone (Edited)
Interviewee: Assalone, Amanda
Amanda Assalone was born in Seoul, South Korea, and adopted into a white family in Tulsa, OK. She always believed she was given up for adoption because her birth mother was young and could not financially raise a child. Amanda never intended to search for her family and always wished them well. So when she received an email saying they’d been found, she quickly learned the truth. Her story had been fabricated and she was given up for adoption because she was not a boy. Amanda currently resides in Atlanta, GA and looks back at each moment in her life happening for a reason. She is not filled with any regrets and is thankful to be surrounded by the Korean American and Korean American adoptee community in Atlanta. As she continues to learn to live life for herself and not others, she hopes to embrace her identity more and more.

Choong Shik Cho (Full)
Interviewee: Cho, Choong Shik
Born in 1935 in Seoul, South Korea, during the Japanese occupation, Choong Shik Cho's early years were marked by significant historical events that shaped his life and career. The occupation lasted until he was 11, during which he and his peers were required to speak Japanese at school, despite maintaining their Korean language and culture at home. The liberation of Korea brought a brief period of peace before the outbreak of the Korean War when Cho was 16, further disrupting his education. However, despite these challenges, Cho graduated from high school in 1953, at the end of the war, without having to take exams, a testament to the turbulent times. Choong was the youngest of eight siblings, with his brothers and sisters pursuing careers in medicine. Choong, however, chose a different path. His aptitude for mathematics led him to pursue civil engineering, a decision influenced by the destruction he witnessed during the Korean War, particularly the destruction of the Han River Bridge, which he dreamt of rebuilding someday. In pursuit of higher education, Cho moved to the United States, enrolling at Oklahoma University. His journey to the U.S. was not easy; he arrived with minimal financial resources and limited English proficiency, traveling by Greyhound bus from Los Angeles to Oklahoma City. His determination was evident through his work as a surveyor on the Pennsylvania Turnpike during summer breaks and as a furniture mover during the 1956 Republican Convention in San Francisco, where he earned enough money to support his education without needing to wash dishes. Cho completed his civil engineering degree in 1958 and went on to earn a Master’s Degree in Structural Engineering from Kansas State in just one year. His career took him to a chemical company in Virginia and eventually to New York, where he married in 1962 in an arranged marriage. Despite the arranged nature of their union, Cho found happiness with his wife, whom he found beautiful and kind. Choong Shik Cho's professional journey led him to establish the Hanil Construction Company and contribute significantly to his native country's development. His retirement in Atlanta, Georgia, has been peaceful, and he takes pride in the growth of the Korean American community there, from 30,000 when he first arrived to over 100,000 people today. Cho's life story is a remarkable testament to resilience, hard work, and the impact one individual can have on both their homeland and adopted country. From enduring the hardships of war and occupation to achieving professional success and giving back to his community, Choong Shik Cho's journey reflects the profound changes Korea underwent in the 20th century and the diaspora's role in shaping Korean identity abroad.
Keywords: civil engineer; Seoul, South Korea; Korean War; Japanese Occupation; Oklahoma University; Hanil Construction Company; Atlanta, Georgia; Han River Bridge; immigrant; economic success; arranged marriage;

Choong Shik Cho (Edited)

Born in 1935 during the Japanese occupation of Korea, Choong Shik Cho recalls constantly struggling and facing hunger. Although his family didn’t have much, he remembers his parents’ deep devotion to providing him and his seven siblings with food and support. Fast-forward to 1954, Mr. Cho immigrates to the states with $120 in his pocket and a desire to further his education. James "Uzuhan" Han was born and raised in Washington, D.C.. With his formative years in Maryland amidst a diverse cohort of Black and Latino students, James found early on that his surroundings offered a safe, yet profoundly insightful environment. James's transition to Atlanta in 2016 marked a new chapter in his pursuit of music. The city's vibrant scene, affordability, and relative scarcity of Asian artists presented a unique canvas for James to distinguish himself. It was here that he became "Uzuhan," evolving from his roots in a Christian rap group to a solo artist who not only celebrates his Korean heritage but also seeks to bridge cultural divides through his work. His marriage in Atlanta and the development of his career underscore the city's significance in his narrative. Reflecting on his journey, James acknowledges the sacrifices his parents made, allowing him the privilege to chase his dreams. His music not only serves as a conduit for his own exploration of identity but also aims to foster understanding and solidarity between Asian and Black communities. Through his art, James seeks to build bridges, hoping to contribute to a world that values diversity and mutual respect.

Jennifer Chung (Full)

Jennifer Chung was born in South Korea and raised in San Francisco, Jennifer's life was steeped in the arts from an early age, surrounded by the melodies and movements of her parents' professions. Her mother, a traditional Korean dance teacher, and her father, a Korean DJ, laid the foundation for what would become Jennifer's lifelong passion. However, her family's split when she was five introduced early hardships, particularly financial instability and the burden of responsibility that fell on Jennifer's shoulders. Growing up, Jennifer navigated the complexities of her identity as a Korean American in San Francisco, a city known for its diverse Asian American community. Despite the socio-economic challenges and the pressure to excel for the sake of her family, she found solace and expression in the arts. Her involvement in theater, leadership clubs, and eventually musical theater in high school became not just extracurricular activities but essential parts of her journey toward self-discovery and growth. Jennifer's story is also one of reconciliation and forgiveness, particularly in her relationship with her father, who moved back to Korea. Their eventual reunion opened doors to understanding and healing, highlighting the profound impact of family dynamics on personal development. Faith played a significant role in Jennifer's life, guiding her through moments of doubt and decision-making, particularly in her choice to pursue the arts against the backdrop of financial insecurity. Her college years at UC Irvine, where she studied Drama, marked the beginning of her public artistic journey, propelled by a viral video of her singing Alicia Keys. This exposure led to performances across the U.S., opening doors to a career that many only dream of. Yet, Jennifer's path was fraught with uncertainty, reflecting the dilemmas many artists face when choosing passion over stability. Jennifer's move to Korea, prompted by a desire to reconnect with her roots and a quest for direction, brought her closer to her faith and her artistic ambitions. It was also where she met her future husband, leading to a whirlwind romance that saw them married within three months of their engagement. Relocating to Atlanta, Jennifer continued to make strides in her career, from recording albums funded by Kickstarter to landing roles in television, including an episode of HBO's "Lovecraft Country." The Atlanta Spa Shootings added a somber note to Jennifer's narrative, underscoring the racial tensions that have long pervaded American society. For Jennifer, this tragedy was a call to advocacy, emphasizing the need for representation and support for people of color in the creative community.

John Song (Full)
Interviewee: Song, John
John Song was born in Hackettstown, New Jersey, then later moved to Atlanta, Georgia. Born into a Korean American family, John's early years were marked by a sheltered understanding of race, finding solace and community primarily within the confines of his church, where his father served as a deacon. These early experiences, nestled within a tight-knit community, highlighted the importance of cultural rituals and the shared experiences of food and fellowship, offering John a sense of identity amidst a predominantly non-Asian environment. The transition from New Jersey to Georgia introduced John to a contrasting reality, where his Korean heritage was not only more visible but celebrated among peers. This shift was empowering, yet it came during a tumultuous period marked by his father's battle with cancer and eventual passing. The complexities of these years were compounded by personal rebellions; alongside his sister, John veered from the expected path, engaging in behaviors, such as drinking and smoking, that distanced him from academic pursuits and traditional markers of success. Despite the backdrop of financial stability provided by his parents' success in the art dealing world, John's personal journey was fraught with challenges. His expulsion from Georgia State University served as a wake-up call, sparking a profound moment of introspection during a psychedelic experience that would pivot his life's direction. Confronted by the imagined disappointment of his late father, John sought redemption through education, achieving a degree in business and, for the first time, recognizing the value of hard work. John's redemption arc continued in Atlanta, a city that offered him the creative space to flourish. Learning to shoot films and music videos, he tapped into the burgeoning digital landscape, eventually finding companionship and partnership through the Asian American YouTube community. His trips to Korea, marked by personal milestones and cultural exploration, further deepened his connection to his heritage and fueled his aspirations. Now residing in Atlanta as a digital content creator and producer, John's mission transcends personal success; he envisions a world where Korean Americans and Asians more broadly can pursue their artistic and creative passions without fear or reservation. His story, marked by loss, rebellion, and renewal, is a testament to the power of self-reflection, the importance of cultural roots, and the endless potential for transformation. John's future goals include reconciling with his family's past and nurturing a space that celebrates Asian representation in the arts, hoping to inspire others to navigate their identities with courage and authenticity.
Keywords: Hackettstown, New Jersey; Atlanta, Georgia; Korean American church community; identity; cancer; personal rebellions; smoking; drinking; Georgia State University; psychedelic; YouTube; digital content creator and producer; artist; freelancer;

John Song (Edited)
Interviewee: Song, John
John Song would describe his childhood as atypical. He and his sister were latchkey kids who didn’t follow the traditional path a Korean American kid should take and experienced lots of bumps on the road. At 11 years old, John’s father passed away from cancer; six days later, his 17-year-old sister gave birth. Instability and lawlessness were John’s lifestyle. After getting high at a party, John found himself on his mother’s front porch looking at the sunrise and hearing a voice asking, “What do you think your father would think of you right now?” Feeling broken and vulnerable, John confronted himself and turned his life around. Today he lives in Atlanta, GA as a freelancer creating and producing digital content. He hopes to see more Asian representation in creative fields and make space for them to feel unafraid to pursue whatever careers they want.

Katheryn Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Katheryn (Jo Gil Ja)
Katheryn (Jo Gil Ja) Kim was born into a yangban family in Gyeongju City, South Korea, in 1940, Katheryn's early years were marked by the strictness and high expectations characteristic of noble families. Being the seventh of nine children, she grew up with the affection and attention typical for the youngest siblings, yet was not spared from the hardships that befell her family and country. The Korean War left an indelible mark on Katheryn's life, not just through the loss of her home and the devastating impact on her country, but personally through the tragic death of her older brother and the resulting profound hearing loss—a hidden wound she would only discover years later. Despite these early trials, Katheryn's spirit remained unbroken, a testament to her resilience. Her journey took a significant turn when she met her future husband on what was seemingly a routine blind date arranged by her parents. Despite her initial reluctance to marry and her bold step to run away from home to escape the expectations placed upon her, this encounter led to a union that would become her bedrock. Marrying at 22, Katheryn embarked on a new chapter that would take her far from the familiarity of Daegu to the unknown streets of Pittsburgh, USA, with her husband and two babies in tow. Adjusting to life in America was no small feat for Katheryn. Beyond the cultural shock and the challenges of navigating a new country, she faced the daunting task of managing a household without the support network she had back in Korea. The anecdote of losing—and fortunately finding—her firstborn son in an airport bathroom encapsulates the initial chaos and panic of her transition to American life. Yet, these hurdles did not deter Katheryn. Instead, they underscored her ability to adapt and persevere, traits undoubtedly honed by her earlier life experiences. Katheryn's story is also one of love and partnership. Her husband's unwavering support was crucial as she navigated homesickness, cultural barriers, and the daunting task of upholding Korean traditions while accommodating the expectations of her in-laws. Their journey together, until his passing in 2016, is a poignant reminder of the power of companionship and mutual respect in facing life's challenges.
Keywords: yangban family; Gyueonju City, South Korea; Korean War; hearing loss; arranged marriage; family; culture shock; homesickness; immigration; cultural barriers;

Katheryn Kim (Edited)

Katheryn Kim was born in 1940 into a yangban, or a noble, family in Gyeongju City and grew up in a strict and traditional home. Mila Konomos (Full)
Interviewee: Konomos, Mila
Mila Konomos, born in Seoul, South Korea in 1975 and adopted at six months by a white American military family stationed in Japan, has navigated a life marked by profound transitions and deep-seated yearnings for connection and identity. Despite a stable and loving upbringing, Mila wrestled with the loss of her Korean roots and suffered from acute separation anxiety in her infancy, reflecting early on the painful severance from her birth culture. Her childhood, while filled with love, was also shadowed by a persistent grief for the family and heritage she lost—a longing that persisted even after reuniting with her birth family. This reunion brought its own complexities, highlighting that some losses remain irreplaceable and introducing new dimensions of loss and understanding. Mila's journey into motherhood has been both a source of healing and a mirror to her own experiences of loss and belonging. Determined to provide her children with the sense of identity she struggled to secure for herself, Mila has fostered open dialogues about her adoption history, ensuring her children have the opportunities to explore their heritage and personal stories. Her approach to parenting is deeply influenced by her own narrative as a Korean adoptee, embodying her commitment to giving her children what she felt was missing in her own upbringing: a solid understanding of their origins and the freedom to question and learn about their family history. The path to finding her birth family was fraught with apprehension and the emotional toll of a seven-year search, filled with hope, despair, and relentless uncertainty. Mila's decision to embark on this journey was not taken lightly but spurred by a close friend that connected her with a social worker that worked at an adoption agency. Mila was in college at the time. Eventually, she decided to search even though she was scared, but realized she wanted to know the truth. Mila found out that the agency had all the information of her birth family, she found out she wasn’t abandoned in a clinic and but that her mother had to give birth to her with C-section and stayed at the hospital for a week, and then forcibly removed by her by her aunt. Today, Mila's narrative is one of resilience and empowerment, marked by her ability to navigate the intricate dynamics of loss, identity, and belonging. Her experience as an adoptee and a mother has fortified her sense of rootedness and belonging, not just in a geographical sense but in the deep, unconditional acceptance she finds within her family.
Keywords: adoption; Korean adoptee; family reunion; grief; loss; isolation; identity; military family; separation anxiety; foster; adoption agency;

Mila Konomos (Edited)
Interviewee: Konomos, Mila
Mila Konomos was born in Seoul, South Korea, and adopted into a white American military family stationed in Japan at six months old. As an infant, Mila would cry and screech nonstop. It wasn’t until her adoptive mother was flipping through tv channels she immediately stopped crying when she heard something familiar, Korean. This story was often humorously shared with Mila but for her, it’s a story about grief and severance. Now a mother of two, Mila addresses being an adoptee impacts how she raises her children. Her sense of rootlessness drives her desire to never have them experience the same things she has and to be secure in their identities. For Mila, parenthood is often a reminder of her grievances but she has found a greater sense of belonging in the family she is raising and has learned being herself is enough.

Rayanne Williams (Full)
Interviewee: Williams, Rayanne
Rayanne Williams' journey from being adopted into a white American family at four months old to her present-day reflections on identity and heritage encapsulates a complex narrative of growth, discovery, and acceptance. Initially resistant to her Korean heritage, Rayanne's early years were marked by a desire to assimilate and distance herself from her Asian roots. Growing up in Vermont, she found herself to be the only Korean in her community, leading to a childhood and adolescence where she grappled with her distinctiveness. This resistance extended to her name, as she disliked retaining her Korean name as her middle name, symbolizing a connection to an identity she wasn't ready to embrace. Rayanne's perspective began to shift during her first trip to Korea at 18, facilitated by her supportive adoptive parents and the Holt International Agency. Amidst the emotional journey and the camaraderie with fellow adoptees, she faced the reality of her adoption story, including the inaccuracies surrounding her birthday and the daunting prospect of exploring her adoptee identity further. Despite these revelations, Rayanne wasn't ready to fully engage with her Koreanness, a sentiment that persisted until the more recent challenges posed by the COVID-19 pandemic and the rise in Asian hate, which forced her to confront her ethnicity in ways she hadn't before.Her parents' encouragement to reconnect with her Korean roots—manifested through cultural immersion and culinary explorations at home—laid the foundation for Rayanne's gradual appreciation of her heritage. Yet, her desire for a deeper connection with the Korean community has been hampered by barriers, including her non-religious stance which made integration into church-centered Korean circles challenging. This longing for belonging led Rayanne to immerse herself in studying Korean and engaging with the broader cultural community, efforts that introduced her to a diverse group of people but also highlighted the external expectations placed on her because of her appearance. Rayanne's struggle with her identity is multifaceted; she identifies as Asian without hesitation but finds the label of Korean, with its rich historical and cultural implications, more difficult to claim. Her move to Atlanta for college, where she pursued a major in music and met her husband, marked a significant chapter in her life, offering new environments and experiences yet also bringing into focus the unresolved aspects of her adoption. Rayanne's ongoing journey involves reconciling with the possibility that she may never know her biological family. Despite the pain of seeing other adoptees reunite with their relatives, she has found solace in the community of Koreans and Korean American adoptees. Through their stories, Rayanne has begun to find peace and a forward-looking perspective, embracing the uncertainties and possibilities that lie ahead.
Keywords: korean adoptee; Holt International; Vermont; Atlanta; Pennsylvania; korean heritage; Korean American; Korean language; family; birth family search; music; community; isolation; KAAN; Korean Adoptee Adoptive Family Network; Asian hate; COVID19;

Rayanne Williams (Edited)

Rayanne Williams was adopted into a white American family at four months old. Throughout her childhood, Rayanne wasn't very interested in her Korean identity and just wanted to blend in. Sarah Park (Full)
Interviewee: Park, Sarah
Sarah Park, Korean maiden name Kim Yu Jeong, was born in 1985 in Seoul, South Korea, the eldest child of two (one younger brother). She lived with her family in Korea until she completed middle school, and has memories of her parents always putting her in a place to receive the best education; she believes that she attended around 10 schools before college, an oddly high number she attributes to her parents’ wishes for her. In moving around different schools within Seoul. Sarah had an early understanding of how society was stratified, and with it a strong conviction that, wherever she was, she ought to work toward creating a more equal and fair society. Although her family originally had plans to move to the DC area, they chose to move to Atlanta instead, in part due to the city’s exposure during the Olympics, but also because they knew a few people who had immigrated to Atlanta before them. Sarah remembers conversations she had with her younger brother about how it was sad to see her parents work so hard in a new country, having to adapt to all of its stresses, but in retrospect feels that wherever her parents worked, they would have been met with equal stressors, whether it be in Korea or in the U.S. While working/volunteering at a conference in (or right after?) college, she met her future husband, who she said was one of the few people who “treated her for who she was,” rather than treat her how the rest of society did: as a young Asian woman. Her husband, who was born in America to parents who immigrated in the 1970s, would go on “volunteering dates” with her as they continued running into each other at various events. They married, and have kids together who Sarah insists on raising “not identically” to how she was raised; among her many reflections today is how she can raise good human beings, as well as which parts of her Korean and American identities she thinks they’ll take after in becoming civically responsible citizens. In her own words, she wants her kids to grow up like fish, acknowledging that there are both bigger and smaller fish out there, but that at the end of the day they should swim with the current in stride, with no more stressors than necessary. Today, she continues her volunteering and civic work in Atlanta, and when asked by her friends why she’s chosen to remain in the American South (with all of its connotations), she believes that there’s a reason God placed her family there, to carry out work for the Korean American community and the rest of the Atlanta community for future generations so that they might live in a more fair society.
Keywords: Seoul; Korea; middle school; 1.5; high school; volunteering; atlanta; georgia; DC; Washington; Olympics; conference; date; husband; children; kids; equality; equity; fairness; fish; South; God

David Shin (Full)
Interviewee: Shin, David
David Shin was born in 1965 in South Korea and immigrated to Toronto, Canada, at the tender age of four. David's early life was shaped by the hardworking ethos of his parents—an electrical engineer father and a mother with a master’s in Math and science. Despite his father securing a job at a TV manufacturer, David’s childhood was marked by financial constraints, evident in his wearing hand-me-downs and being unable to afford Little League participation. David's perception of racism during his childhood was minimal, often encountering curiosity about his ethnicity rather than outright discrimination. His father’s endeavor in the real estate sector, starting with a 16-unit apartment complex acquired with a modest 10% downpayment, exposed David to the harsh realities of financial management and investment risks. This experience, coupled with observing his family’s frugal lifestyle despite their asset wealth, significantly influenced his career trajectory and personal life choices. Choosing to diverge from his father's path, David pursued engineering, dedicating himself to academic excellence and leadership, as evidenced by his role as president of the Korean American Student Association. This commitment to education and community engagement continued with his decision to attend law school, where he not only honed his legal acumen but also gained insight into the demanding work culture of law firms. David’s personal life has had its share of challenges, including two divorces and a departure from the church, reflecting a journey of self-discovery and changing beliefs. His move to Houston and foray into real estate—despite initial reservations—signify a departure from past influences and a step towards carving his own path. Today, David reflects on his immigrant family’s sacrifices and the financial struggles that shadowed his upbringing with a nuanced understanding. The legacy of being "asset rich, cash flow poor" has imparted in him a deep-seated caution towards spending, inherited from his parents who, despite their successes, remain frugal. David's story encapsulates the immigrant experience of striving for a better life for the next generation, navigating the complexities of cultural integration, financial stability, and personal fulfillment.
Keywords: asset management; law school; law firm; real estate; frugality; immigration; Houston; Toronto, Canada; divorce; family; financial struggle;

David Shin (Edited)
Interviewee: Shin, David
David Shin recalls his dad expressing their family’s immigration to Canada as being “for the sake of his children” despite their struggles with money. Watching his father balance a difficult life of buying properties and paying mortgages– “asset rich, cash flow poor”– led David in a completely different direction towards engineering and law school. He was able to make his own successes during his time in Houston, earning his way up in the world, but still struggled with the guilt ingrained in him from his experiences at Baptist churches. David So (Full)
Interviewee: So, David
David So's journey from South Korea to the United States encapsulates the intricate tapestry of identity, community, and purpose that defines the life of a 1.5-generation immigrant. Born in 1987 in Korea and relocating to the U.S. in 1995 with his family, David's earliest memories of arrival are marked by the sweltering heat of a Louisiana summer on July 14th, a day that symbolized the beginning of a new chapter. The Korean church in Louisiana became a beacon for David's family, serving not just as a place of worship but as a vital community center for Korean Americans navigating their new lives. Despite the church's central role in his early years, David's relationship with it became fraught as he grew older. The politics and gossip that often permeated church life led him to take a break from the institution, a decision that coincided with a tumultuous period in his 20s characterized by drinking, driving infractions, and an arrest. However, David's narrative is one of redemption and growth. Recently, he has found his way back to church, seeking solace and community once more, an endeavor that coincides with a significant personal milestone: his proposal to his girlfriend of 13 years. David's commitment to his heritage and community took a decisive turn in 2015 when he attended a Korean Festival in Houston. Inspired by the vibrant celebration of Korean culture, he seized a job opportunity in Houston in 2016, immersing himself in the Korean American community. His involvement with organizations such as the Korean Festival Houston (KASH) underscored his dedication to his roots. Professionally, David has carved a niche for himself by running an insurance brokerage that demystifies the concept of insurance for Korean immigrants, a vital service given the unfamiliarity of many in the community with the American insurance landscape. Beyond his professional achievements, David's life is deeply intertwined with his family. His mother, who runs a store, and his father, who supports her while working on his doctorate, exemplify the hardworking spirit that David has inherited. Today, he stands at the intersection of his American present and Korean heritage, navigating the complexities of being a pastor’s kid and a business owner dedicated to serving his community. David dreams of expanding his business to reach a broader, multiethnic, and multilingual American audience, continuing his mission to foster a healthier business environment for immigrants.
