Sarah Chase (Full)
Interviewee: Chase, Sarah
Sarah Chase's journey reflects a blend of cultural exploration, educational pursuits, and a passion for teaching. Growing up in Maryland within a multicultural family, she experienced a childhood enriched by travel and exposure to diverse experiences. Initially studying biochemistry and premed at the University of Maryland, Sarah eventually found her calling in education. As a teacher, she found fulfillment in nurturing young minds, a passion further ignited during her time teaching English in China. However, her journey to fully embrace her Korean heritage was marked by moments of self-discovery. Despite attending Korean school as a child, it wasn't until her first trip to Korea in high school that she realized the significance of her roots. Subsequent visits to Korea deepened her connection to her cultural identity, particularly in understanding the nuances of Korean family dynamics. Sarah's pride and joy lie in her role as an educator, where she witnesses the growth and development of her students. Despite initial feelings of being an imposter, she now embraces her place in Korea, recognizing the value of her experiences and contributions. Her story serves as a testament to the power of self-discovery, cultural exploration, and the profound impact of education in shaping lives. Through her dedication to teaching and her journey of personal growth, Sarah continues to inspire those around her.
Keywords: Maryland; Seoul; Wasian; Mixed Race; Biracial; Teaching; Education; Seoul; Korea; Premed; Biochem; University of Maryland; Hierarchy; Siblings

Sarah Chase (Edited)
Interviewee: Chase, Sarah
For now Korea-based Sarah Chase, being Korean was first understood in the form of a dinner table split between American food and Korean food, taking shoes off in the house, and the smell of her grandmother’s jjigae wafting down the block on her walk home from school. Sarah wanted more, though. She wanted to speak Korean with her relatives, craving stories to relate to, to feel more valid in her Korean identity, but found that the closer she got to the culture, the more shame she felt to not be fluent in Korean and struggled to feel deserving of living in Korea. Upon her second move to Korea, Sarah feels much more stable and accepting of her self, with the notion that she’s just going to do her best, to witness herself with more compassion.
Keywords: Maryland; Seoul; Wasian; Mixed Race; Biracial; Teaching; Education; Seoul; Korea; Premed; Biochem; University of Maryland; Hierarchy; Siblings

Kayla Kim Votapek (Full)
Interviewee: Votapek, Kayla Kim
Kayla Kim Votapek, originally from New Jersey, is an anti-racist facilitator and theatre producer. Born in Taechon, South Korea, she is a Korean American adoptee whose birth mother worked in a textile factory and had an affair with her boss, resulting in Kayla's birth. Adopted at four months old, she was raised in an Italian family, where her neighbors were considered family, and she was closest to her dad. Her sister is also a Korean adoptee. Growing up, her parents would visit her school to educate students about adoption, explaining "airplane day" and what a non-normative family looked like. Although she initially felt most included in the arts community, this changed in high school when her involvement in theatre highlighted her differences. She often felt conflicted about connecting with her Korean heritage, fearing it would be seen as a betrayal to her adoptive family, a sentiment her mother also shared, fearing Kayla might choose her birth mother. Kayla's involvement in anti-racist healing work allowed her to meet other adoptees for the first time, where she felt truly understood. Within six months, she traveled to Korea to explore her heritage further. Her interest in learning about her lineage was influenced by her father, who was passionate about family histories. Her artistic inclinations were nurtured by her aunt, and she was encouraged by her drama teacher to pursue theatre. She triple-majored in theatre, psychology, and education at Rutgers University, aiming to become a theatre teacher. Her work as an anti-racist facilitator focuses on taking action rather than just education and approaching conflict with humility. Kayla pursued a master’s degree in London to enhance her facilitation skills, and during this time, she organized a walkout in response to racist remarks by the head of the school. This activism enabled her to engage more deeply in anti-racist work and explore her AAPI identity. Kayla believes that anti-racism work is a path to self-actualization. During the COVID-19 pandemic, she moved to Korea to learn more about her heritage. Holt, the adoption agency, found her birth mother but reported that pursuing contact was detrimental to her birth mother's health. Although she intended to continue her search, the death of her adoptive mother led her to put it on hold. She remains determined to find more about her origins while ensuring her birth mother's well-being. Ultimately, Kayla strives to be seen as a complete human being beyond just her identity.
Keywords: Siblings; Italian; Adoption; Korean American Adoptee; Holt; Birth Mother; National Theatre; Theater; Anti-Racism; Anti-Racist Healing Work; TCG: Theatre Communications Group; AAPI; Asian American; Identity; Korea; New Jersey

Kayla Kim Votapek (Edited)
Interviewee: Votapek, Kayla Kim
As an anti-racist facilitator and overall creative, Kayla Kim Votapek leads a life of perpetual acceptance of growth and movement towards understanding, but getting there has come with its own challenges. Having grown up in an Italian American family of New Jersey, Kayla found closeness at home, yet felt unseen as an adopted Korean in a mostly White upper class town. To work through questions of identity, Kayla found the freedom to express herself through the arts and performance. This further ignited a passion in her to help others feel seen and heard, to facilitate spaces that nurture communities through action, communication and reparation.
Keywords: Siblings; Italian; Adoption; Korean American Adoptee; Holt; Birth Mother; National Theatre; Theater; Anti-Racism; Anti-Racist Healing Work; TCG: Theatre Communications Group; AAPI; Asian American; Identity; Korea; New Jersey

Kyu C. Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Kyu C.
Kyu Lee's story is a compelling journey of cultural rediscovery and professional success, straddling the worlds of Seattle and Seoul. Born on a U.S. military base in Yongsan, Korea, Kyu was a U.S. citizen from birth due to his father's position in the U.S. Army. His family relocated to Mercer Island, Washington, drawn by the promise of good education and a nurturing environment for raising a family. Growing up in a predominantly Jewish neighborhood with few Asian Americans, Kyu’s early exposure to his Korean heritage was limited. Kyu's love for sports led him to the University of Washington, where he played football—a testament to his integration into American culture. His career began at Sony in 2001, but it wasn't until 2004-2005, during his encounter with the Korean film "The Brotherhood of War," that Kyu truly connected with Korean cinema. This film not only introduced him to the depth of Korean content but also sparked his mission to bring Korean films to a global audience. His enthusiasm for the film led to its distribution by Sony, marking a significant step in promoting Korean cinema in the U.S. In 2009, Sony recognized Kyu's passion and expertise by transferring him to Korea to handle local language production. However, his return was met with cultural shocks and the realization that his acceptance was more due to his professional position than his personal identity. Despite these challenges, Kyu continued to forge ahead in the industry. His career took a dramatic turn in 2012 with the global phenomenon "Gangnam Style." Kyu played a pivotal role in introducing Psy to Scooter Braun, leading to a series of opportunities that propelled his career in the media industry. This experience not only heightened his profile but also solidified his role as a cultural bridge between Korea and the U.S. By 2013, Kyu's influence and experiences culminated in the publication of a motivational book in Korean, aimed at inspiring youth. Today, Kyu continues to work in film and distribution, navigating the highs and lows of the industry with a commitment to uplift and create opportunities for others. His journey from a young boy in Mercer Island to a key player in international media is a testament to the power of embracing one's roots and the impact of cultural exchange.
Keywords: music industry; Sony; media and entertainment; Mercer Island, Washington; Seoul, South Korea; military base; gangnam style; PSY; korean film; film industry; korean culture;

Kyu C. Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Kyu C.
An immigrant to Mercer Island, Washington, Kyu Lee recalls not knowing anything about Korean culture aside from the obvious, he and his family were all born there. It wasn’t until after college that Kyu began to be exposed to Korean entertainment. In 2005 during a vacation to the motherland, he watched “Taegukgi: The Brotherhood of War” for the first time and was blown away. Working for Sony at the time, Kyu flew home and enthusiastically told his team about the film. It was quickly picked up and distributed in the States, making it the first Korean film screened in the U.S. entertainment market. Sparking Kyu’s pride and desire to introduce Korean content to the States, he quickly built his network from there, ultimately leaving him responsible for the U.S. viral sensation “Gangnam Style”. Life is still a crazy rollercoaster ride for Kyu as he continues to work in film and distribution. As he rolls with the punches, he hopes to continue to uplift and provide opportunities to those around him.
Keywords: music industry; Sony; media and entertainment; Mercer Island, Washington; Seoul, South Korea; military base; gangnam style; PSY; korean film; film industry; korean culture;

Michael Hurt (Full)
Interviewee: Hurt, Michael
Michael Hurt's story is a rich narrative that spans continents, cultures, and academic disciplines. Born in Germany and raised in Ohio, Michael, of Korean and African American descent, grew up in a diverse community which shaped his early interest in mixed-race identities. His academic journey led him to Korea on a Fulbright scholarship after college, sparking a long-term commitment to the country that began in 1994. During his stay with a host family and later academic pursuits at Berkeley, Michael delved deeply into Korean national identity, which culminated in his dissertation on the portrayal of national identity in Korean textbooks—a project completed in 2014. Michael's academic interests are intertwined with his passion for photography, particularly street and fashion photography, which he pursued actively from 2002 to 2007. Michael's work extends beyond the visual, as he critically engages with social issues in Korea. His research on the significant yet controversial topic of prostitution and sex work in Korea, which once constituted a notable part of the country's GDP, led to public exhibitions and scholarly articles. These efforts, however, were met with resistance from locals who felt his work cast a negative light on their society. Identifying as a visual sociologist, Michael combines his talents in photography with his academic interest in race, gender, and societal observation. His unique position as both an insider and outsider in Korea provides him with a distinctive perspective that enriches his work, allowing him to navigate and document the complex interplay of cultural identity in a modern and rapidly changing society. His story underscores the challenges and enrichments of living between cultures, exploring how personal identity and societal roles converge in the globalized world.
Keywords: ethnography; visual studies; photgraphy; academics; Berkeley; Phillip Andover Academy; Dayton, Ohio; Seoul, South Korea; race; gender; sociologist; fashion photography; prostitution; sex industry; national identity;

Michael Hurt (Edited)
Interviewee: Hurt, Michael
Michael Hurt identifies as a visual sociologist, melding his draw towards street fashion and photography with his passion for observation and studies in race and gender. With a mixed background, his mother Korean, father African American, Michael’s interest in identity started early, carrying him through his academic years, eventually bringing him to live and work in Korea since the 1990s. During his initial move, Michael had a desire to figure out what it meant to be multiracial, but instead discovered his American-ness. To him, Korea at a baseline was just a place with a culture and society, things that he was becoming defined as an outsider to. Eventually Michael’s work gave him opportunities to feel more tied into Korea’s society, allowing him to uniquely navigate in both cultural spaces; inside and outside.
Keywords: ethnography; visual studies; photgraphy; academics; Berkeley; Phillip Andover Academy; Dayton, Ohio; Seoul, South Korea; race; gender; sociologist; fashion photography; prostitution; sex industry; national identity;

Sangmin Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Sangmin
Sangmin's life story reflects a journey shaped by cultural transitions and a deep-rooted connection to his faith. Born in Korea, he spent his early years moving frequently due to his father's role as a pastor in various small towns. This nomadic childhood took a significant turn when his family immigrated to Virginia when Sangmin was ten. Financial challenges were a constant aspect of his upbringing, with both parents working multiple jobs to make ends meet. These early experiences instilled in Sangmin a strong drive to achieve financial stability, leading him to study business management in college. However, despite his initial pursuit of a career in business, Sangmin's path took a spiritual turn in his late twenties. After 12 years in the U.S., he returned to Korea and followed in his father's footsteps, becoming a pastor after attending seminary school at the age of 28. His deep connection to Korea was reaffirmed over the years, notably during international sporting events like the Olympics and the 2022 World Cup, during which he found himself rooting for Korea over the U.S., despite holding a U.S. passport. Today, Sangmin leads a Korean-American church in Seoul, where he serves a community that bridges his two cultural identities. He is married with daughters and is keen on raising them bilingual, ensuring they maintain a connection to both cultures. His journey from a business student to a community leader in the ministry highlights a life led by a blend of ambition, cultural heritage, and spiritual calling.
Keywords: faith; ministry; pastor's kid; pastor; seminary school; immigration; Korean American culture; cultural identity;

Sangmin Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Sangmin
The son of a pastor, Sangmin moved from Korea to McLean, Virginia at a young age. Reflecting on his parents’ struggle to provide for their family, Sangmin resolved to become an entrepreneur and make a lot of money. However, after moving to Korea in his late twenties, Sangmin found a new, though familiar calling in the ministry. Following in his father’s footsteps, Sangmin became a pastor at Jubilee, a church in Seoul. Having served at Jubilee for upwards of seven years, Sangmin was made the leader of King’s Cross Church, a new, English-speaking church in Seoul created by Jubilee, where he still serves today.
Keywords: faith; ministry; pastor's kid; pastor; seminary school; immigration; Korean American culture; cultural identity;

Tae Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Tae
Tae Kim was born in Korea and moved to the US with his family at age three. His father was recuited by the US army in Korea and was able to to move the family to the US. Initially living in Kansas, the family moved to Los Angeles to escape discrimination and racism. Tae grew up in LA during the 1980s, experiencing the LA riots, which he recalls as a pivotal event that strengthened the Korean community's reliance on each other. His parents emphasized their Korean heritage despite living in the US. Tae worked as an auditor for the Department of Veteran Affairs for several years and engaged in community service, volunteering with organizations like AA, NA, and Criminal Gangs Anonymous. He moved back to Korea to assist his extended family with problems. Despite speaking Korean with his parents, he faced significant language barriers and culture shock upon his return, particularly struggling to communicate in everyday situations. Seeking support for his mental health challenges, Tae found a lack of resources and cultural stigma surrounding mental health in Korea. He started a Facebook group to address these issues, which quickly gained a following. Tae then developed Gidae, an app supported by the Ministry of Health and Welfare, designed to provide anonymous access to mental health resources.He credits his success to his commitment to stay in Korea which gave him the motivation and drive to adapt and learn. After three and a half years, Tae has successfully adjusted to life in Korea. He is now married, has a son, and is happily running his business, grateful for the life he has built despite initial hardships.
Keywords: Korea; US; Immigration; LA; Racism; LA Riots; Korean American; Community; Auditor; Accounting; Volunteer; Community Service; Korean; Culture Shock; Language Barrier; Mental Health; Application; Gidae; Ministry of Health and Welfare; Identity; Support; Resources

Tae Kim (Edited)
Interviewee: Kim, Tae
One night in Seoul, Tae Kim found himself in a Burger King, struggling to order his meal. Despite being able to speak Korean fluently in his home of Koreatown LA, he quickly found that it just did not translate so smoothly in his new home of Korea. As he tried paying for the meal he didn’t even want, Tae felt the realness of culture shock. Doubt and fear permeated as he began to settle into the new city, leading him to ask friends about finding mental health counseling. To his surprise, his struggle was met with judgment. Certain that he couldn’t be the only one, Tae took action. Starting with a Facebook group to provide a safe outlet for people struggling with their mental health in Korea, it became clear that he really wasn’t alone. So he made an app called “Gideb” where people could remain anonymous and find the right resources and access to the mental health support they need. Now, Tae is grateful to be living in Korea with his wife and son, happily running his company.
Keywords: Korea; US; Immigration; LA; Racism; LA Riots; Korean American; Community; Auditor; Accounting; Volunteer; Community Service; Korean; Culture Shock; Language Barrier; Mental Health; Application; Gidae; Ministry of Health and Welfare; Identity; Support; Resources

Namjun Cho (Full)
Interviewee: Cho, Namjun
Namjun Cho was born in Korea and relocated to the United States at the age of 8, spurred by his parents’ desire to provide him and his siblings with superior educational opportunities. His father was involved in international business, while his mother managed the household. Despite the promising prospects, Namjun struggled to adapt to his new environment, particularly wrestling with the cultural and linguistic barriers that distanced him from his peers. The challenges of integration were further complicated by a sense of isolation due to language barriers, which often left him unable to express his perspective or connect deeply with others. This struggle extended into his attempts to join the U.S. military, where his ambitions were thwarted by citizenship requirements, blocking his entry into both the armed forces and the ROTC program. In a turn back to his roots, Namjun joined the South Korean military in late July of 2020. However, his years in the U.S. had impacted his proficiency in Korean, with his understanding reduced to 70-80%. This linguistic gap, combined with his perceived American identity, isolated him further, making his basic training particularly grueling. The cultural dissonance he experienced was so profound that it not only hampered his ability to connect with fellow soldiers but also subjected him to bullying. Through his experiences, Namjun has navigated the complex identity of being caught between two worlds—feeling both Korean and American without full acceptance by either community. This ongoing journey reflects his continuous struggle to find a place where he truly belongs, marked by his efforts to bridge the diverse aspects of his identity.
Keywords: immigration; South Korean military; US military; military training; language barrier; bullying; international business; identity;

Namjun Cho (Edited)
Interviewee: Cho, Namjun
Born in Korea and moved to the U.S. by age 8, Namjun Cho’s parents wanted to provide a better education for their children. Seeing how much diversity that the States provided, Namjun’s parents decided to lengthen their stay to see their kids graduate. Along the way, Namjun had a hard time in school, feeling little sympathy from the community as he would find himself in conflict with other students; never being able to share his side of the story because of the language barrier. After years of adjusting to America, Namjun then returned to Korea in late-July of 2020 to fulfill his military duties. Because of his need to learn English to get by in the States, Namjun found himself now struggling with his native-tongue and self-proclaimed American identity, which earned him no support yet again. Caught in between, Namjun went into training and service feeling Korean, and has come out on what feels like the distant other side of being American.
Keywords: immigration; South Korean military; US military; military training; language barrier; bullying; international business; identity;

Christine Pennell (Full)
Interviewee: Pennell, Christine
Christine Pennell, born in 1969, was discovered at a railroad station in East Daegu, South Korea, around the age of 2 and was adopted to the United States at 3.5 years old. Raised in a white family, Christine felt a strong sense of belonging, never treated differently despite her awareness of her Korean roots. Her first significant interaction with another Korean occurred in Montreal in 1976, which marked the beginning of her journey to embrace her ethnic identity, even as she grappled with being fetishized by white men. Christine married at 21 to a man whose sister was her friend and whose family was well-known to her father, a local teacher. The relationship blossomed from these close family ties. However, inspired by the documentary "Lion," Christine joined a Korean American Adoptee Facebook group in 2018, which led her to a DNA test and the subsequent discovery of her sister living in Belgium. The sisters, who coincidentally both had children the same years, forged a deep connection, sharing photos, engaging in video chats, and eventually meeting in person. Christine's visits to Belgium became monthly occurrences, and she grew increasingly attached to her Korean heritage, leading her to spend more time in Korea. After 50 years in the United States, she decided to relocate permanently to Korea, supported by her family back in the States with whom she keeps in touch via frequent video calls. Now a dual citizen, Christine enjoys her life in Korea with her four children and a granddaughter, though she admits the transition from an American mindset presents ongoing challenges. Christine's story is one of rediscovery and connection, bridging continents and cultures as she finds peace in her Korean American identity. After her divorce in 2013 and her experiences of reconnecting with her biological roots, Christine has embraced her full identity, finding satisfaction and peace in her dual heritage and the new chapters of her life in Korea.
Keywords: adoption; Daegu, South Korea; identity; asian fetishization; Korean American adoptee; korean heritage; dual citizenship; family reunion; Belgium; divorce;

Christine Pennell (Edited)
Interviewee: Pennell, Christine
Christine was raised as an American in a white family— far from where she was originally found, a train station in Daegu, Korea. Despite feeling fortunate for her loving adoptive family, she still felt and looked different, influenced by classmates that bullied her. Years later, in 2018, Christine saw an online post about a welcome home program. Inspired by the documentary Lion, in which a man found his family on Google, she discovered the Korean American Adoptee Facebook group and was able to travel to Korea for the first time. The feeling of being home removed an unknown tension she had felt in the US. The following year, she received confirmation from a DNA test that she has a sister living in Belgium. Reunited through the internet, they immediately felt a connection. They met for the first time in Korea, in an emotional reunion that prompted monthly trips to Belgium before COVID. Having reunited with her homeland and family, Christine has found satisfaction and peace in her Korean American identity.
Keywords: adoption; Daegu, South Korea; identity; asian fetishization; Korean American adoptee; korean heritage; dual citizenship; family reunion; Belgium; divorce;

Albert Kim #61 (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Albert
Albert Kim was born and raised in Flushing, Queens, NY, to parents who had immigrated there in 1982. With both parents working daily—his father owning a grocery store and selling traditional Korean medicine—Albert was predominantly cared for by his grandparents, who also moved to Queens. Initially speaking only Korean, Albert learned English when he started elementary school. Throughout his youth, Albert was deeply embedded in Korean-American communities, moving first to New Jersey during middle school and later to Virginia for high eventful during high school due to his father’s business needs. He attended the University of Virginia, majoring in Journalism, and was actively involved in traditional Korean performing arts, including Samul Nori (a traditional drum group) and pansoori (a genre of musical storytelling), from elementary school through high school. After graduating in 2011, Albert used his graduation gift money to buy a one-way ticket to Korea, where he has lived since. In Korea, Albert pursued careers in sales and the fashion industry, even learning to make a hanbok (traditional Korean attire). His time in Korea was not without challenges; from 2017 to 2020, he developed alopecia, exacerbated by intense work-related stress. Despite these difficulties, Albert finds freedom and contentment in Korea, embracing his dual citizenship and the cultural heritage that has shaped his identity.
Keywords: flushing, queens; korean traditional medicine; small business; ESL; new jersey; virginia; samul nori; korean traditional drums; pansoori; journalism; fashion industry; sales; alopecia; dual citizenship; immigration; seoul, south korea;

Albert Kim #61 (Edited)
Interviewee: Kim, Albert
Born in Flushing, Queens, Albert Kim was raised speaking Korean by his grandparents throughout his time in elementary school. Even after his grandparents moved out, Albert maintained a connection to his Korean heritage through the practice of Samul Nori, traditional Korean drums. These connections grew stronger after Albert and his family moved to New Jersey while he was in middle school, and Albert found himself surrounded by even more Korean Americans in his community. Upon moving to Korea in his adulthood, Albert grappled with living with alopecia in a culture highly sensitive to physical appearances. Having moved around a lot throughout his life, Albert has learned to enjoy life by living in the moment.

Denny Hong (Full)
Interviewee: Hong, Denny
Denny Hong, born in Korea on a U.S. army base, spent his childhood oscillating between the United States and Korea due to his father's military assignments. This nomadic lifestyle took him to various cities like Memphis, North Carolina, and Atlanta, alongside stints in Seoul. Denny finally settled in Memphis during his high school years, where he faced racial slurs that were initially unfamiliar to him. However, after physically confronting some of his bullies, these incidents subsided. Denny attended Valparaiso University, and post-graduation, at 21, he moved back to Korea, planning only a year-long stay to work for his uncle before pursuing seminary school. Yet, living in Korea sparked a change in Denny's path. Realizing he did not want to become a pastor, he found himself teaching English and embracing the flow of continuous new opportunities that kept him in Korea. Denny's journey led him from Busan, where he lived for 5-6 years, to Seoul, where he stumbled into a broadcasting career, a field he has remained in ever since. In Seoul, Denny has been particularly struck by the dynamism and the plethora of opportunities, something he doubts would be as accessible in the states. Over the past 20 years, Denny has built his life in Korea, finding community among gyopos—other Korean expatriates who have returned from abroad—and embracing his identity as a "third culture kid." This unique blend of cultural experiences has helped him craft a personal culture that defies traditional definitions. Now well integrated into Korean society, Denny states that apart from his immediate family in the U.S., including his parents and brother, there are few ties pulling him back to America. He has learned to let go of the expectations others have placed on him, as well as the ones he had for himself. Today, Denny lives comfortably undefined by others, embodying a hybrid identity that resonates with many expatriates navigating life between cultures.
Keywords: gyopo; US military; military family; racial slurs; Valparaiso University; Seoul, South Korea; Busan, South Korea; broadcasting; teaching english; third culture;

Denny Hong (Edited)
Interviewee: Hong, Denny
Born a Korean American expat in Korea, Denny Hong grew up in various international and military schools in Korea, the U.S., and Germany. Denny’s family finally settled down in Memphis when he was in high school. While living in the states brought its own set of struggles, including racism, Denny has never let the challenges of his past define him. After returning to Korea to reconnect with his family and roots, Denny found himself enjoying life in Korea, keenly observing its shifting culture. Denny continues to live in Korea, and he now works as a radio broadcaster.

Zach Benson (Full)
Interviewee: Benson, Zach
Zach Benson, born in Busan, South Korea, was adopted at three months old and raised in Iowa. He lived there until after graduating from college. Zach's adoptive family provided a nurturing environment, particularly as he faced learning disabilities and a speech impediment during his childhood. Despite these challenges, Zach grew very close to his adoptive mother, who played a crucial role in his educational development. He was surrounded predominantly by white peers and, apart from his aunt and uncle's adopted Korean children whom he met at family reunions, he had little exposure to Korean culture or friends of Korean descent. Zach’s life took a significant turn after college when he moved back to Korea with a plan to find his birth mother. His adopted mother initially felt uneasy about this plan, creating some tension between them. However, this eased when Zach found his birth mother in 2011 with assistance from Holt International, a remarkable reunion that also led to his adopted mother meeting her and feeling a sense of familial connection. Living in Korea, Zach felt at home despite the language barrier, calling Korea his true home. He embraced his roots, integrating into the community and even teaching English in Busan, where he also met and connected with his birth mother. This encounter sparked a desire to find his birth father as well, learning that his birth mother had remarried and he had half-siblings. Zach's academic and personal growth became evident through his high school and college years. Despite a rocky start with a 1.6 GPA in his freshman year of high school, he graduated with a 3.7 GPA. He discovered a passion for breakdancing, which not only boosted his confidence but also led to the formation of a dance crew and a breakdancing club at Central College. This hobby turned into a significant part of his identity, with performances across the United States. Post-college, Zach volunteered at World Relief, where he met a North Korean that inspired him to return to Korea. His time in Korea extended as he lived in various places, including Busan and Daejeon, working as an RA at a Korean international school to stay close to his birth mother. His journey also took him abroad to Spain and Mexico for four years, and his extensive travels to over 70 countries have led him to feel at home in hotels as much as anywhere else. Now, having lived in Korea for 6-7 years, Zach's journey from Iowa to Busan has been a profound exploration of identity, heritage, and the complex dynamics of family, both adopted and biological.
Keywords: Busan, South Korea; korean adoptee; family reunion; birth mother; learning disability; speech impediment; breakdancing; dance competitions; Holt International;

Zach Benson (Edited)
Interviewee: Benson, Zach
Born in Busan, but raised an adoptee in Iowa, Zach Benson grew up looking and speaking differently from his mainly white peers. Zach struggled with fitting in and participating in class until he discovered a passion for breakdancing in high school. When Zach was 23, he came to Korea to learn more about his heritage and search for his birth mother, with whom he was finally able to reconnect. Zach later moved to Daejeon, where he worked as an RA at a Korean international school, to spend more time with his birth mother.
Keywords: Busan, South Korea; korean adoptee; family reunion; birth mother; learning disability; speech impediment; breakdancing; dance competitions; Holt International;

Aaron Choe (Full)
Interviewee: Choe, Aaron
Aaron Choe, originally from San Jose, California, moved to Korea in 2008 after falling in love with the country during a visit with his sister. Raised primarily by his grandmother, Aaron spoke Korean at home and was largely exposed to Korean culture through church, despite being the only Korean in his school. His initial fascination with Korea began in high school when he discovered 90s K-pop, which ignited his curiosity about his heritage. After graduating, Aaron's passion for subcultures and his longing to connect with his roots prompted him to relocate to Korea at the age of 24. Initially, he stayed with his sister and enrolled in Korean language classes at Yonsei University, supporting himself through tutoring and various odd jobs. During this challenging period, Aaron's father became embroiled in a Ponzi scheme, losing his life savings, which inadvertently anchored Aaron in Korea. In Korea, Aaron pursued his interests in photography and film, starting as a photo assistant. He aspired to become a cinematographer and focused on telling authentic stories that convey deep, cultural understandings. Over time, Aaron noted significant differences in film set culture between Korea and the West and has since endeavored to blend these practices in his work, striving to take the best aspects of Korean set culture to Western environments. Despite the geographic distance from his family, which he regrets due to his father's recent passing, Aaron has found a strong sense of identity and pride in his Korean heritage. Now more comfortable in his own skin, Aaron prefers to use his Korean name, Yoonsuk, more frequently. As a filmmaker, director, producer, and DJ, Aaron has successfully bridged his American upbringing with his Korean roots, fully embracing his dual identity and encouraging others to experience the richness of living in Korea.

Barbara Uni Lee Potter (Full)

Barbara Uni Lee Potter, born in 1943 in San Francisco was one of the first Korean Americans to live in the States. She identifies as an American Korean, reflecting the fusion of her diverse cultural heritage. Raised in the Korean Presbyterian Church, Barbara found a sense of belonging within the tight-knit Korean community of her childhood. Adopted by parents facing fertility issues, she grew up in a loving household where her aunt played a significant role in her upbringing. Despite initially only speaking English when starting school, Barbara's childhood was rich with cultural experiences. She fondly remembers Ahn Chung Ho's wife, who became a cherished figure in her life, and recalls acting in movies as an extra with Philip Ahn, only aware of his fame and not his father Ahn Chung Ho's. Barbara's journey took her to Pennsylvania in the 1960s, where she encountered racism for the first time, highlighting the contrast with her upbringing in LA. Her mother was the first person to sell kimchi in LA. At the age of 13, Barbara learned of her adoption from Ahn Chung Ho's wife, a revelation that reshaped her understanding of her identity and family history. Despite this, meeting her biological parents later in life brought a sense of closure and peace. Barbara Uni Lee Potter's story is one of resilience, cultural discovery, and the enduring bond of family, showcasing the complexity and richness of her American Korean identity.

Irvin Paik (Full)
Interviewee: Paik, Irvin
Irvin Paik's life story is a compelling narrative of overcoming challenges and embracing one's passions amidst societal struggles. Born in 1940 in Bakersfield, California, and later raised in Los Angeles, Irvin grew up as a second-generation Korean American in a time when cultural tensions and discrimination were rampant. Growing up, Irvin's experiences were shaped by the complexities of racial identity and societal acceptance. His interactions with a Japanese classmate were discouraged by his family, reflecting the isolated sentiments many immigrant families felt during that era. Despite this, Los Angeles was bustling with development, including the notable electric streetcars that characterized the city's landscape at the time. Irvin discovered his passion for theater early in life, a passion that provided him a refuge and a voice. By junior high, he was deeply involved in drama, a commitment that continued through his high school years and well into his college days at UCLA. Here, Irvin not only participated in but thrived by winning an award in a Shakespeare competition, highlighting his talent and dedication to the arts. His creative pursuits extended beyond the stage. Irvin explored photography and attempted to establish a career in it. However, he faced significant barriers due to discrimination, which forced him to adapt and find alternative pathways. His resilience led him to the army, where he served as a combat photographer in Vietnam and later worked as a script supervisor in Long Island City. These roles allowed him to hone his skills in a different aspect of visual storytelling. Post-military, Irvin transitioned to filming nature documentaries and took advantage of new opportunities as the Justice Department pushed for increased hiring of minorities. This shift enabled him to pursue film editing, a field in which he could further exercise his creative expertise. Reflecting on the achievements of Koreans in the modern entertainment industry, Irvin remains an advocate for pursuing one’s dreams regardless of the challenges. His life serves as an inspiration to young people, particularly in minority communities, to follow their passions and contribute their unique voices to the broader cultural tapestry.
Keywords: Bakerfield, California; isolation; UCLA; drama; theater; Shakespeare competition; photography; Vietnam War; US military; documentaries; justice department;

Alex Chang (Full)
Interviewee: Chang, Alex
Alex Chang's journey is deeply rooted in a rich heritage and a profound commitment to community service. Born in 1959 in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, Alex's early childhood was marked by his parents' separation when he was just two years old. He moved to Venice, California, where he spent most of his formative years, from fourth grade through high school. Despite growing up in the U.S., it wasn't until junior high that Alex met other Korean kids, which sparked a growing pride in his heritage. By the time he attended UCLA for his undergraduate studies, and later USC for his master's degree, Alex had fully embraced his Korean identity. This pride was further expressed through his involvement in the nightlife scene, where he promoted Asian dance clubs, creating a safe space for the Asian community. Alex's connection to his Korean roots deepened with the discovery of his familial legacy of resistance against Japanese occupation. His grandfather, Ik Jun Hwangbo, who received a Presidential Citation in 2017, and his great-grandfather, Chung Kul Whangbo, awarded the 2011 Medal of Patriot, were both recognized as Korean independence fighters. This revelation came around the same time Alex learned that the Korean government was searching for the descendants of 103 independence fighters, enhancing his commitment to his heritage. Since 1999, Alex has been an active volunteer at the Korean American council, helping to document the history of Korean Americans. In 2015, he was recognized with a Certificate of Recognition from the city of Los Angeles for his contributions, particularly in preserving the stories of Korean American pioneers. His leadership as the current president and chair of the Korean American Pioneer Council underscores his dedication to collecting and archiving these significant histories, ensuring they are passed down to future generations. Alex's life has also been influenced by his Japanese godparents, who have played a significant role in his upbringing. Additionally, a surprising family discovery revealed that his grandmother had two more children, leading Alex to connect with cousins he never knew he had. Overall, Alex Chang's life story is a testament to the power of heritage and the impact of community service. His efforts not only honor his ancestors' struggles and triumphs but also ensure that the legacy of Korean Americans is remembered and celebrated.
Keywords: community service; Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania; Venice, California; Japanese occupation; UCLA; korean identity; Korean American; korean independence fighters; Korean American Pioneer Council; nightlife; club promoter;

Dr. Samuel Sang Gook Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Samuel Sang Gook
Dr. Samuel Sang Gook Lee was born in Daegu, South Korea, in 1935. The oldest of five, he was born in the midst of the Japanese occupation and recounts seeing many Japanese soldiers around his town and school. His elementary school, which was modeled after the Japanese schooling system, had a mix of both Japanese and Korean teachers, and began the day with a recitation of a pledge of loyalty toward the emperor. Once a week, students would walk up a mountain to a Japanese shrine and pray facing east, where the emperor’s throne lay. In every home was a kamidana, a mini-shrine meant for Shinto worship, which was encouraged by the teachers. Dr. Lee also remembers how the Japanese would take all of what the Koreans produced, particularly the rice, which some families tried to hide to perform jesa. With little food to go around, many families resorted to eating the scraps discarded from pressing soybeans into oil, and when faced with a shortage of oil, Korean men were forced to cut pine trees for their resin. During the occupation, Dr. Lee’s given Japanese name was “Iwamoto Sang Gok,” and Japanese was spoken at home by everyone except for his grandmother. It was at church that his Sunday school teachers would try to teach in Korean, which at the time he didn’t understand the gravity of. His paternal great-grandfather was the first Christian in his family, and his maternal grandfather participated in the Samil Independence movements both from Manchuria and Korea, where he’d send money to independence fighters. Dr. Lee’s mother he recalls as being a very sensitive person who passed away at the young age of 41, and from whom he believes he inherited his own depression and anxiety which he says never truly went away. The youngest of her family, she had two sisters and brothers, and was popular in her town for having been educated in Seoul during a time when it was uncommon for women to be highly educated at all, much less in the capital. His father, on the other hand, ran a bike shop, and married his mother when he was 20; she was 19-20. After his mother passed, his maternal aunt lived and took care of his family for a year before returning home. During the Korean War, Dr. Lee was a “house boy” who took care of menial tasks for a general and major, safe and away from the frontline, where for three years he worked for the American military; he was 15-16 at this time.
Keywords: Dr. Samuel Sang Gook Lee; Daegu; South Korea; 1935; Japanese occupation; shinto; emperor; rice; church; Samil; Samil Movement; Manchuria; bike shop; Seoul; depression; anxiety; mental health; house boy

Dr. Samuel Sang Gook Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Samuel Sang Gook
Dr. Samuel Sang Gook Lee was born in Daegu, Korea in 1935 during the Japanese occupation. Recalling the Emperor’s Oath that Korean students had to recite every day in school, Dr. Lee reflects on feeling shame for obeying Japanese rule. He was forced to learn Japanese and obtain a Japanese Sur name during the occupation. His local church was the only place where he found community and positivity. Later when he was 15 years old, Dr. Lee served as a house boy for 3 years where he ran errands for the U.S. soldiers on their military base. It was during this time that he suffered from severe depression and anxiety, which he carried with him throughout the rest of his life. Since immigrating to the US, Dr. Lee continues to pray every day to bring healing to himself and his family impacted by generational trauma.

Kwon Sook Young (Full)
Interviewee: Kwon, Sook Young
Kwon Sook Young was born in North Gyeongsang Province, five years before liberation from Japanese occupation. She remembers how her family bought a brewery from the Japanese as they left, a business they operated until she was 10 years old, which was around the time that the Korean War broke out. At the insistence of her grandmother, the family fled to Andong, North Gyeongsang, before deciding to go south to Busan squeezed into a freight train carrying cargo. As the train headed south, it was met with bombardment, the effects of which knocked a water kettle on her, and buried her underneath a bags of rice, resulting in a broken rib. Although her family believed her to have died, she managed to crawl out from underneath the rice, and was able to make it to Busan where, to her (and her grandfather’s) dismay, there was very little to do. Her grandfather asked her and her older sister to at least try selling some pears, though this was met with little success, both because other, more older vendors had established themselves in the markets, and also because she and her sister would eat all of the pears. She notes how, during the war, she saw very little damage to church structures. Eventually, she enrolled into school in Daegu, where she spent her time while the war passed; upon expressing a desire go to Seoul to continue her education, her family deemed it unnecessary for a girl to continue schooling. She remembers how whenever she went back to visit her family, she’d be careful not to wear her school uniform to not agitate the older, conservative generation. Soon, she met her husband—a future doctor—and it was he who wanted to immigrate to the United States to pursue medicine, moving the family to New York in 1973 with three kids in tow. She recalls how difficult life in America first was, unable to help her children with homework not because it was difficult, but because she didn’t understand English yet. She also remembers the apartment the family moved into: a small two-bedroom, the rent for which took up 2/3 of the family’s monthly income. To make ends meet, she bought a used sewing machine to repair clothes, then to sew dresses; sometimes, her children would help her after school. She doesn’t remember all of the jobs she worked, simply because she worked too many odd jobs to remember. What kept her going was hope that her husband’s career would soon pan out, and as he finished up his internship and residency, the family eventually moved to Scarsdale. Her children, though, attended Bronx Science, because that was where all of their friends were going. During this time, too, she had been working at a newly opened YWMCA, looking after children. When her son, HJ, married and had his first child with Theresa, she moved in to live with them for a while, and recounts how she’d occasionally butt heads with her daughter-in-law over generational differences, though she’s grateful that things have smoothed out over time. In her golden years, Kwon says that she often reflects on the time she has left on this Earth, and while she’s not afraid of death, it’s something she’s constantly reminded of as she sees her loved ones pass, watch news of others’ passings, and witness who still remains at her church.
Keywords: Kwon Sook Young; Korean War; Kettle; Gyeongsang; North Gyeongsang; Andong; train; Busan; brewery; pears; New York; Flushing; Brooklyn; doctor; residency; Scarsdale; age; old age; death; church

Kwon Sook Young (Edited)
Interviewee: Kwon, Sook Young
While fleeing for their lives, Kwon Sook Young vividly recalls the evacuation journey with her family from Andong to Busan aboard a freight train. Amidst the chaos, a heart-stopping moment occurred when Kwon found herself surrounded by bags of rice as a large water kettle came crashing down upon her from a nearby bomb explosion. Her motionless body led her family to believe she died; however, against all odds, they discovered her crawling out alive and breathing. From attempting to sell Korean pears to nearby soldiers to her father narrowly escaping execution, the Korean War served as just one chapter in Kwon’s life of survival. Kwon eventually relocated to New York, and a profound sense of displacement enveloped her as she grappled with the challenges of language barriers and cultural shock. Today, Kwon’s thoughts often gravitate towards themes of mortality and aging, contemplating how to gracefully navigate the inevitable aftermath. Despite the passage of time, she continues to reside in New York alongside her family, desiring nothing but the best for them and hoping they may lead long and healthy lives.

Bomin Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, HJ, interviewer, Kim, Bomin
Bomin Kim was born in Seoul and raised by her grandparents after her parents separated when she was young. Her childhood was marked by the loving care of her grandmother, contrasting with her authoritative grandfather. Born two months premature, doctors had advised terminating the pregnancy, but her grandmother's belief in a Korean superstition about her long fingers and toes indicated she would live a long life. In 1987, at age 11, Bomin migrated to Sydney, Australia, with her grandparents, influenced by her uncle's migration there. She initially struggled with English but excelled in math and made friends with other migrant children, despite one Korean Australian boy being embarrassed by her presence. Bomin lived with her grandparents until she was 35, feeling well cared for by her grandmother, whose teachings about kindness and a non-materialistic life grounded her. She began traveling in her twenties, seeking more life experiences, which led her to a job with a U.S. company and a move to San Francisco at 35. In the Bay Area, she started using dating apps to find a partner but eventually met her husband, Chris, through a friend's introduction. After dating for 13 months, she expressed her desire to marry, and he proposed shortly after. She was initially worried about her in-laws but found them welcoming and kind. The most challenging part of moving to the U.S. was leaving her grandmother, who had dementia and lived in a nursing home. Having visited her every week, Bomin felt deeply saddened by her grandmother's death in 2016, marking the saddest moment of her life. Bomin was shocked by the homelessness and social issues in the Bay Area, and was moved to begin volunteering. The rise of anti-Asian hate crimes during the pandemi led her to wish to raise a politically aware and compassionate child, fostering love towards people who are different. Despite feeling grounded in the U.S., Bomin contemplates returning to Australia due to the political climate in New York. She envisions a more loving and inclusive society and hopes to instill these values in her future family.
Keywords: Australia; Korea; Divorce; Sepatation; Racism; Discrimination; Physician; Marriage; Stop Asian Hate; Asian Hate Crimes; New York; San Francisco; Bay Area; Travel; Sydney

Chris Y Park (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, HJ, interviewer, Park, Chris Y
Chris Park was born in Grand Coulee, Washington, and grew up playing with his siblings and cousins, especially enjoying time with his aunt's family in Kansas. Despite spending a significant part of his life in Texas, he recalls not experiencing much racism until college, feeling welcomed and comfortable in Texas, where his parents have lived for 45 years. His high school had a small number of Asian Americans, including one other Korean American, which made him eager to meet more Asian Americans in college. At Yale, Chris became deeply involved with the Korean Students Association (KSA) and founded the largest Korean American dance event on the East Coast, called Black Out. Initially, KSA had many Korean Americans who didn't speak Korean, but this changed with an influx of Korean immigrants fluent in Korean. As the KSA became more insular, Chris joined the Asian American Student Association to be more politically active. Chris's mom chose not to return to Korea due to the country's sexism, but he discovered a rich history of women's rights before the Joseon Dynasty. He acknowledges that contemporary Korean society is rooted in patriarchy but believes that understanding historical contexts can inform modern progress. He feels that Korean Americans lack a comprehensive education about their history, often overlooking significant periods before Japanese colonization. Chris views his dad as a great role model despite the stereotypical aloofness often attributed to Korean parents. He aims to instill humility in his daughter, especially given their proximity to wealth. His high school experience in San Antonio exposed him to a diverse range of economic backgrounds, which he values. His mom was saddened by Chris's lack of relationship with her father, highlighting a generational gap in familial connections. Chris's reflections on his upbringing and cultural heritage shape his aspirations for his daughter's education and values.

Issun K Park (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, HJ, interviewer, Park, Issun K
Issun Park, born in October 1942 in North Korea, spent her early years in Hangyumdo before moving to Seoul in 1944. Her father was the dean of Seoul National University, which provided them with housing, giving her fond memories of the neighborhood where she played with pet rabbits on the school yard. Her father and mother were both highly educated. Her father went to Kyoto University at 14, while her mother graduated high school, uncommon for the time. On June 24th, the family had to flee Seoul amid bombing, crossing the Han River on a rainy night and taking refuge at a friend’s house. During the Korean War, her oldest brother was drafted, causing her mother much distress. The family relocated to Busan, living in a tiny room. Her oldest sister, thirteen years her senior, was attending medical school and her boyfriend would bring them oranges. Due to communist influences at Seoul National University, her father took a job with the US Army in Japan as an interpreter, and the family joined him after traveling by boat and train. Her two brothers joined later, while her sister stayed in Korea after getting married. Issun first attended a communist-run Korean school in Japan but later transferred to a Korean government school. They kept their ethnicity a secret due to mistreatment from the Japanese. He was later transferred to Okinawa, where Issun attended an American high school on the base, enjoying her time there despite the cultural differences. In 1962, the family moved to San Francisco to join her brother, who was pursuing a PhD in journalism. Issun audited a class at Berkeley before moving to Illinois, where she attended Southern Illinois University. That same year, her future husband, a medical doctor, arrived in the US. Initially hesitant about marrying him, she was convinced by her siblings after he proposed following their second date. Issun eventually settled in San Antonio, Texas, and despite not living long in Korea, she considers herself Korean American. Though she feels at home in the US, her youngest son, after visiting Korea, expressed a desire to move there, enjoying the experience of not being a minority. She has never felt out of place in America and believes she is not a traditional Korean, allowing her sons to choose their professions freely. She and her husband speak Korean at home and have attended the same Korean church for 40 years. Her happiest memory is the birth of her first child, while the saddest is her mother's death. Notably, her husband delivered their third child as he was the only doctor available in town. Her father remains the most influential person in her life.
Keywords: North Korea; Seoul National University; Medicine; Doctors; Doctor; Journalism; UC Berkely; Illionois; Korea; Immigration; Japan; Okinawa; Base; Military Base; Education; Base School; Communist; Communism; Korean War; Japanese Occupation; Banana; People's Army; Han River

Issun Park’s life has been shaped by unexpected relocations and experiences. Born in Kaesong, North Korea, she spent most of her childhood in Seoul. Myung Kun Park (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, HJ, interviewer, Park, Myung Kun
Myung Kun Park was born in Hwanghae, North Korea, in 1934. His childhood memories are marked by the Japanese occupation, which lasted until he finished elementary school. During World War II, he witnessed Japan's initial successes and eventual defeat. In 1945, with Japan's surrender, Korea was liberated, and he began learning Hangul from his father. However, Korea's impending division cast a shadow over the celebration. His family faced turmoil; his father was imprisoned for criticizing the communist party, complicating their plans to move south. Myung Kun attended middle school under a harsh communist regime, which caused him significant stress and a stutter that later disappeared. For high school, he transferred to another county where his background was unknown, excelling academically and joining the Young Men’s Communist Group. During the Korean War, Myung Kun evaded the draft with the help of non-communist hospital staff. When UN troops liberated his hometown, he joined the South's defense forces at 16. He served in a reconnaissance battalion, finding faith during his service. After being wounded, he was discharged at 17. With his parents trapped in North Korea, he reunited with surviving siblings in Suwon. Despite hardships, Myung Kun completed three years of high school in one year while working as a security guard for a US military unit. He passed the college entrance exam and entered Seoul National University’s premed program, eventually excelling in medical school. Supporting himself as a live-in tutor, he later moved to the US for better opportunities, working in Poughkeepsie and meeting his future wife during his fellowship. Due to visa issues, he returned to Korea before securing a job in Washington, D.C., but faced a hiring freeze. He then worked in Canada as an intern before settling in Eastern Washington as a family practitioner, eventually obtaining a green card. Myung Kun later joined the University of Kansas as a teaching professor and then the University of Texas, where he became head of the Pediatric Cardiology Unit. Grateful for his wife, whom he credits to God's grace, Myung Kun is proud of his three sons who attended Ivy League schools. He reflects on his parents' absence during his success, which saddens him. One of his happiest moments was being accepted into Seoul National University, a prestigious school he later realized. Despite not identifying as a man of faith, he considers himself a believer and appreciates the life he has built.
Keywords: Japanese Occupation; Korean War; 6.25; North Korea; South Korea; Political Prisoner; Vietnam War; Singapore; Japanese Imperialism; Liberation; Communist; Communism; People's Army; Soldier; Doctor; Pre-med; Seoul National University; Medical School; Pediatric Cardiology; Faith; Christianity

Amidst suspicion, Park reluctantly became an informant, navigating the Korean War’s outbreak and his brother’s disappearance. Bree Yoo-Sun McLuen (Full)
Interviewee: Choh, Ann, interviewer, McLuen, Bree Yoo-Sun
Bree Yoo-Sun McLeun's life is a testament to resilience and identity exploration across cultural boundaries. Born in Seoul, Korea, in 1986, Bree was adopted at just 3.5 months old and immigrated to Minnesota, where she was raised in a predominantly white community. Her father, a Baptist Minister, ran a nonprofit for youth in Minneapolis, and her family was deeply involved in the church, shaping much of her early environment. Growing up, Bree experienced a profound sense of isolation, intensified by her attendance at a wealthy public school where she often felt the pressure to show gratitude for her adoption, summed up in the family mantra, "don't bite the hand that feeds you." This environment contributed to Bree's struggles with anger during her childhood, marking her as a high-risk kid. Despite these challenges, she found solace and success in sports, eventually choosing figure skating as her focus and turning professional at the age of 17. Her interest in her Korean heritage was supported at home; she attended Korean culture camp, and her maternal grandmother was particularly keen on exploring Korean culture with her. This familial support played a crucial role in her identity formation and later influenced her academic and professional choices. Bree pursued an undergraduate degree in English from North Park with the initial intention of going to law school but later shifted her focus to social work, inspired by her own experiences as an adoptee. During her time at grad school at Loyola, she faced an unexpected pregnancy with her son Carter, which led her to defer and later reapply to her program. Carter's birth marked a turning point for Bree, deepening her engagement with the Korean American community to ensure her tri-racial son—Black, Korean, and Native American—had a strong sense of identity and community support. Her professional journey included an internship with Hanul Family Alliance, driven by a desire to provide Carter with more resources and opportunities. The challenges Bree faced in connecting with her biological family, including a birth search at age 13 that ended in disappointment, further motivated her dedication to helping others navigate their own journeys of cultural and personal discovery. Today, Bree's life story encapsulates the ongoing journey of embracing one's roots while forging a path forward, underscored by her commitment to her son and her community. Her experience highlights the complex dynamics of adoption, the search for belonging, and the powerful impact of motherhood on personal healing and community connection.
Keywords: korean adoptee; Minneapolis; Christianity; identity; isolation; social work; Loyola University; North Park College; ice skating; Hanul Family Alliance; Korean American community; multiracial family; healing; birth search; birth mother;

Matthew Miller (Full)
Interviewee: Choh, Ann, interviewer, Miller, Matt
Matt Miller's life narrative is a poignant illustration of identity, community, and self-discovery. Born near Anyang Province, Korea, in May 1974, Matt was adopted at nine months old and flown to Chicago, Illinois. Raised in Elgin, Illinois, Matt is the youngest of four children in a family that included two biological children of his adoptive parents and his birth sister, who shared early memories of their birth mother and took care of Matt, giving him a unique connection to his origins despite their new surroundings. Matt's adoption through Holt International introduced him to adoptee gatherings, which played a role in his upbringing. However, it wasn't until his early to mid-twenties, during his involvement with a Korean American church in Naperville, that Matt experienced a profound sense of belonging. This community was pivotal in helping him embrace his Korean heritage, providing a sense of family and acceptance that was both healing and transformative. During his graduate studies, a significant encounter further shaped his identity. Matt met a Korean scholar who studied adoption, and during a conversation where Matt expressed his concerns about not knowing the Korean language, history, or culture, the scholar reassured him that "there isn't one Korean experience." This affirmation was a turning point for Matt, helping him to hold onto his Korean identity with pride rather than longing. Matt's academic career has largely focused on the experiences of Asian Americans, with his dissertation exploring how Asian Americans navigate cultural experiences. He has published extensively on the subject and has become a mentor and guide for many Asian American students, creating a safe space within predominantly white academic environments. Married for 22 years and the father of three children, Matt met his white wife at a small, conservative bible college with a notably low Asian presence. His journey of self-discovery and acceptance has also prepared him for a future trip to South Korea—a journey he plans with apprehension but sees as necessary for a deeper connection to his roots. Today, Matt Miller's life reflects a journey from a sense of isolation to one of community and identity affirmation. His experiences underscore the diverse tapestries of the Korean American experience and highlight the profound impact of community and personal interactions in shaping one's sense of self.
Keywords: korean adoptee; Chicago, Illinois; Elgin, Illinois; Holt International; Korean American heritage; graduate school; academia; Asian American studies; bible college; multiracial family; biological sister; identity; self-discovery;

Soon-Young Oh (Full)
Interviewee: Oh, Soon-Young
Soon-Young Oh was born in Gimhae, South Korea, a small city near Busan. Immigrated to Minneapolis, Minnesota in 1978 via international adoption when she was almost 5 months old. White family, 2 older brothers who were quite older. City was majority white. Always aware of having been adopted; parents would tell her that she was “chosen” to be a part of their family. Minnesota has a large population of Korean adoptees, and every summer Soon-Young would go to cultural camps catering to the adoptee population. She doesn’t remember any bullying or teasing as a child. In 2005, she went back to Korea for the very first time, and met her biological father, though she had no expectations of any reconciliation; she explains that this was the beginning of her journey of navigating her adult identity. Meeting her birth father, who explained the circumstances of her adoption beyond what she had known through various paperwork her parents had shown her, settled certain questions she’d had. She was unable to meet her birth mother, as her birth mother had given a false name during the adoption process, but Soon-Young says that she’s come to peace with this long ago. In 2007, attended the IKAA convention; in 2008 Soon-young moved to Korea to teach, a time she described as being challenging and fulfilling. She met her husband in Korea during this time. Her husband is also a Korean adoptee, though she says that his story was different from hers in how he was adopted into a Korean American family living in Chicago, and so grew up immersed in the language. She has a child who she reminds to take pride in his Korean heritage; in 2016, the family moved briefly to Korea, where her son attended daycare surrounded by other Korean 3-year olds. She feels that it’s very important in identifying as part of the Korean adoptee community, and the broader Korean American community, because it’s crucial in navigating one’s personal journey with others. She hopes that her son sees her as a person who worked to both be a part of, and be a builder of, such a community, even when it can be daunting to do so. When asked about what she’s most proud of, she responds by saying that she’s proud of staying in touch with the adoptee community, and putting herself in difficult situations (such as moving to Korea to teach, settling in Chicago) that encouraged her to grow as an adult. She explains that she takes pride in her name, Soon-Young Oh, which she discovered in 2005 on her first trip back to Korea; rather than going by a name given to her by an adoption agency, she felt it important to reclaim the one given to her by her birth parents.

Joseph Oh (Full)
Interviewee: Oh, Joseph
Joseph Oh's life is a profound journey of cultural identity and familial connections. Born in an orphanage in Incheon, South Korea, Joseph and his brother were adopted at the age of 5 by a White couple in the United States. However, after the early loss of his adoptive father, Joseph was readopted by a Korean American family in Chicago. This new chapter reintroduced him to his Korean roots; his adopted parents, who were deeply immersed in Korean culture, were strict but tried to instill a strong sense of heritage. Living with his Korean grandmother, Joseph often found himself caught between two cultures, speaking English while his parents spoke Korean. The family rarely discussed the topic of adoption, and his grandmother's harsh words about "bad blood" made it difficult for Joseph to express his feelings or seek understanding about his identity. Despite these challenges, the Christian faith and the Korean church community played significant roles in his upbringing, providing him a sense of community and connection to his Korean heritage. Joseph's journey back to Korea as an adult—to teach English and search for his biological parents—marked a significant period of self-discovery. Although the search through Holt International did not lead to a reunion with his birth parents, it allowed him to connect deeply with other adoptees, helping them as a translator during family reunions. These experiences, particularly helping others meet their birth families, brought him a sense of fulfillment and an imagined connection with his own birth mother. Finding love with another Korean adoptee in Korea was a transformative experience for Joseph, giving him a profound sense of being seen, heard, and belonging. His marriage and the birth of his child have been sources of immense joy and pride, anchoring his life in the love and connection he long sought. Despite the complexity of his familial relationships, including the distance from his biological brother who now lives in Canada, Joseph has found closer ties with his adopted family following the passing of his adopted mother. His life's journey reflects a tapestry of loss, rediscovery, and deep personal growth, with his son and wife standing as his greatest sources of happiness and accomplishment.
Keywords: korean adoptee; death; readoption; Incheon, South Korea; orphanage; Korean Christian Community; Holt Internation; birth search; marriage separation; remarried; family; korean translator; teaching english;

Theresa Choh-Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Choh-Lee, Theresa
Theresa Choh-Lee was born in Seoul, Korea, she immigrated to the U.S. in 1972, the youngest of three siblings. Growing up in Cleveland, Ohio, she navigated the challenges of straddling two worlds - at home, immersed in Korean language and cuisine, attending Korean church on Sundays, while during the week, surrounded by a predominantly white community. Her educational journey was marked by a significant transition, starting kindergarten in Korea and then abruptly moving to the U.S. for first grade. With no ESL support, Theresa had to adapt to an English-speaking environment, supported by kind teachers who assisted during challenging financial times. Moving to a new town in junior high, Theresa was the only minority, experiencing a sense of isolation and alienation. Theresa's path took a turn when she attended college, opting for an unconventional major in paper science engineering with a scholarship. Post-graduation, she entered the workforce in process engineering, eventually marrying and pursuing an MBA sponsored by her employer. This led to a successful career in brand management at various consumer goods companies. Discovering her Korean American identity was a transformative experience for Theresa, facilitated by Pastor Charles Ryu. Marriage in 1990 brought a move to Hoboken, where Theresa and her husband, a DINK couple, shared household responsibilities evenly. The birth of their first child prompted a move to live with Theresa's parents for what was intended to be two years but extended to 25 years. In 2008, faced with a job loss, Theresa's husband HJ proposed founding a non-profit organization, leading to the creation of Korean American Story (KAS). Theresa grappled with the fear of becoming the sole breadwinner but recognized the opportunity to make a difference through KAS. Her current concern revolves around ensuring the sustainability of KAS beyond her husband's role as executive director. Theresa reflects on her journey with pride, acknowledging the impact of KAS and wishing she had such support during her formative years. Her story encapsulates the complexities of cultural assimilation, career challenges, and the evolving dynamics of family life.
Keywords: Immigration; Seoul, Korea; doctor; family; Cleveland, Ohio; isolation; alienation; identity; MBA; business school; brand management; breadwinner; 2008 financial crisis; Korean American Story; non-profit; nontraditional; hardship; paper science engineering

Theresa Choh-Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Choh-Lee, Theresa
Born in Seoul, Korea, Theresa Choh immigrated to Ohio with her family in 1972. In her senior year of college while interviewing for jobs she went to New York City, where she met her now husband, HJ Lee. Their lives continued to grow together until the 2008 financial crisis, when both HJ and Theresa lost their jobs. Amidst searching for other jobs, HJ came to her one day with the idea of creating a non-profit which would become Korean American Story; though Theresa was initially hesitant, both set out to put the project into place. Dong Hyeon Jeong (Full)
Interviewee: Jeong, Dong Hyeon
Dong Hyeon Jeong was born in 1983 in Gyeonggido, South Korea, and later moved to the Philippines in 1987, where he spent most of his formative years due to his parents' missionary work. In the Philippines, Dong's parents were deeply involved in building churches and seminary schools, nurturing a strong sense of community and spiritual dedication. Despite being an only child, Dong's childhood was rich with cultural experiences, though he often visited relatives back in Korea. Dong identifies himself as Korean American Filipino, reflecting his unique multicultural upbringing. His early exposure to different cultures was largely positive, although he experienced preferential treatment in the Philippines due to his lighter skin—a stark contrast to the racial discrimination he would later face in the United States. In pursuit of higher education, Dong moved to Atlanta, Georgia, in 2003 on an F1 international student visa to study for a Master in Divinity. This transition marked a significant change in his life, introducing him to the harsh realities of racial prejudices in the U.S. The incident where he was derogatorily called a "Chink" and told to go back to his country was particularly jarring and served as a painful introduction to the racial dynamics in America. Despite the challenges, Dong adapted, learning cultural nuances and innuendos, and even enhancing his Korean language skills through his involvement with a Korean church in Atlanta. As a youth pastor, he was deeply engaged with the Korean American community, providing guidance and teachings in Korean. Dong has now been living in the United States for 19 years, currently residing in Skokie, Illinois. His reflections on his journey show a deep appreciation for the diversity and equity within the Korean American community. He values how his perspective is sought after and included in discussions, illustrating his integration and acceptance into the community. On the personal front, Dong's life is enriched by his family. He is married to a white woman, and together they have navigated the complexities of an interracial relationship, confronting and overcoming cultural differences and familial expectations. They have two children, a boy and a girl, to whom Dong regrets not teaching the Korean language more extensively, recognizing the importance of passing on his heritage. Today, Dong continues to promote diversity and celebrates various identities within his community, drawing on his rich, multicultural background to foster understanding and acceptance among different cultures.
Keywords: South Korea; missionary; Philippines; seminary school; churches; Master in Divinity; Korean American church community; Skokie, Illinois; korean language; Tagalog; biracial kids; mixed marriage; Filipino culture; youth pastor; Atlanta, Georgia;
