Anne Joh & Alex Joh-Jung (Full)
Interviewee: Joh, Wonhee Anne
The interview begins with a conversation between Alex Joh and his mother, Wonhee Anne Joh; the first question posed by Alex is why his mother has become so interested in K-pop and Korean dramas as of late. Anne responds by stating that when she was growing up in the 1980s in the United States, she gravitated towards alternative bands (she mentions Depeche Mode) as she didn’t feel that there was a space for her in mainstream pop culture. As Korean culture became more visible, Anne began to consume Korean media. The conversation transitions to reflections on their family (Anne, Alex, and his older brother Josh), and the growth that the three of them went through together; temporarily having a white father figure, and Anne, aware of the structures of white racism, being vigilant in her children having access to their biological father, despite financial stresses at the time. Alex asks his mother if she worries he’ll grow up to be a mediocre “Korean man,” to which Anne confidently responds that she doesn’t believe so. The conversation pivots again, with Alex asking Anne if she’d ever consider marrying again? She responds that she won’t, as it took her so long to find herself, and that in every past relationship she’s had to shrink herself in relation to her partner. The interview then swaps; Anne asks Alex how he felt having a mother who was a proclaimed feminist whando worked. When the family lived in Oklahoma, Alex says that at times he felt as if something was missing, but not bad, more so due to a missing father figure. Yet he also felt a deep admiration/respect for Anne, seeing her work so much to support the family. Anne reiterates the question of the feminist mother; Alex responds by saying that it benefited him, shaping his worldview today. He even says that his friends think she’s very cool. Anne says that from her perspective, society today feels very hospitable to Asian men, and asks Alex if he ever felt “not at home” growing up within general American culture, where white masculinity was so dominant. Alex says that because he didn’t grow up around majority white classmates, there was a “different” experience of masculinity he was exposed to through other minority groups in the United States. Today, Alex says he feels comfortable being Korean American. Anne asks if there are any issues today that Alex struggles with, with respect to self-acceptance. He responds that he feels comfortable with himself today, and Anne recalls jokingly how he used to say that he wished she was more of a tiger mom, making certain decisions for him. Studying Business Economics and Anthropology were points of self reflection, and he questions his motivations behind his choice of major in college. He says he’s notified his job that he’ll be working part-time for a year to professionally focus on his other hobbies, such as writing poetry, which Anne is an ardent supporter of. She tells him that he should do whatever it is that brings him joy, especially given how migrant populations often forfeit the arts to survive. Someone on set asks Alex if he has any wishes for his mother, to which he states he wants her to remain healthy, and live long, but not too long. The pair laugh.
Keywords: Interview; Alex Joh; Wonhee Anne Joh; K-pop; Korean dramas; 1980s; Alternative bands; Depeche Mode; Mainstream pop culture; Korean media; Family; White father figure; White racism; Biological father; Korean man; Feminist mother; Oklahoma; father figure; Asian men; Self-acceptance

John Anthony Ranum (Full)
Interviewee: Ranum, John Anthony
John Anthony Ranum's life story begins in Pyongyang, North Korea, where he was born just before the tumult of the Korean War swept through the nation. At the tender age of ten, he found himself a refugee of war, tragically separated from his mother and left to navigate the harsh realities of life on the streets and in various orphanages. Despite these early hardships, John's life took a remarkable turn in Daegu, South Korea. It was there that he met his future adoptive brother, a U.S. soldier stationed at a local airbase. This chance meeting, initiated by John's kind act of helping the soldier find transportation, eventually led to John being smuggled into an airbase and later adopted by the soldier's family from Lockport, Illinois, in September 1955. Transitioning to life in the United States, John rapidly adapted and excelled in his studies, completing eight years of Catholic school in just four. His academic prowess continued through high school, culminating in his graduation in 1963. John harbored aspirations of becoming a pilot and even reached out to a senator for sponsorship to the Air Force Academy. However, his height fell short of the academy's requirements, which thwarted his initial dreams of flying. Undeterred, John joined the U.S. Air Force, eventually being stationed back in Korea at Osan Airbase, where he endeavored to find his biological mother—a quest that remained unfulfilled among the countless orphans of the Korean War. His military career also included a stint as a military police officer and later, tours in Vietnam, where he served as a non-commissioned officer of investigations. Throughout his life, John was deeply influenced by his adoptive family. His adoptive mother, a resilient woman of German and French descent, and his Norwegian adoptive father, a gentle carpenter, provided him with a stable and loving home. The profound connections he formed with his adoptive siblings also shaped his perspectives and aspirations. After retiring from a commendable 25-year tenure as a police officer in Bolingbrook, Illinois, John cherishes the family he built with his Korean wife—three daughters and five grandchildren, reflecting a life full of challenges overcome and joys multiplied. John Anthony Ranum's journey from the streets of Daegu to the suburbs of Chicago is a poignant testament to the unpredictability of life and the enduring strength of familial bonds, both given and chosen.
Keywords: korean war; separation; adoptee; adopted; refugee; orphanage; North Korea; Lockport, Illinois; adopted family; birth search; US military; US airforce; military officer; police officer; family; grandchildren; South Korea;

Becky Belcore (Full)
Interviewee: Belcore, Becky
Becky Belcore was born in Seoul and adopted into a Minnesotan family when she was one year old. Minnesota at the time had the most Korean adoptees than all the other 49 states combined. She lived there until her family moved to the southern states of Virginia and, eventually, Alabama. Her adoptive parents originally wanted to take in a White child, only to find out that there was an 8 year waitlist to do so, and then wishing to adopt a Black child but told not to do so because they would not be able to instill a sense of racial pride in them. Growing up in all-white communities was very challenging. Her parents' approach to raising Becky and her adoptive brother was that they were their children and not Korean, which affected how she saw herself when peers would harass and make fun of her appearance. Becky reflects on her self hatred and insecurities she had while growing up, even going to believe that Korea didn’t exist because she had never seen anybody who looked like her growing up, until she went to an all-womens’ college where the population was actually ⅙ Korean. Although her Korean peers wanted to connect with her, Becky was afraid of them because did not know what being Korean meant, growing up in an all-White community. But also college taught her that her background and upbringing is not exclusive to herself, but others too– which guided her passion for activism and organizing which allowed her to connect with peers like herself. But it was not until she arrived in Chicago when she discovered and got involved with her Korean heritage through the Korean American Resource and Cultural Center (KRCC). Although it was tough for her to get used to the space, the words of her mentor Curtis Muhammed and the lens of social justice she discovered at the center encouraged her to stay. Her journey taught her that we all have our individual and diverse experiences that all blend together to become one story, and hers is just one of the many stories. The present marks her 26th year of involvement with KRCC and her 6th year as the co-director for the National Korean American Service & Education Consortium (NAKASEC), forming groups to actively support Korean adoptees. These groups work socially and politically, one aspect of it being helping adoptees achieve citizenship. She hopes people find a home, a sense of joy and happiness through her organization, a place where adoptees can connect with others to uplift each other.
Keywords: adoption, chicago, koreans, nakasec, korean, transnational adoption, interracial adoption, curtis muhammad, asian american, korean american, diaspora, identity, racial identity, ethnic identity, american, krcc

Soon Ki Bae (Full)
Interviewee: Bae, Soon Ki
Soon Ki Bae, who also goes by Simon, was born in Japanese-occupied Korea in 1935 in South Jeolla Province. He recalls how the country was liberated while he was in the third grade, but political turbulence continued for much longer. The Korean War broke out when Bae was in his third year of middle school, and he recalls how, in Busan, you were hounded by police during the day and by anti-communist partisans at night. Following the war, he graduated from high school; as was typical of the time, he was to enlist in the military to perform his mandatory service, but due to missing documentation his service was delayed until he was 27 years old, in 1961. As a result, he fulfilled his mandatory service around the time that Park Chung-hee’s May 16th coup took place. Due to poor job market prospects, Bae moved to Germany to work as a contracted miner; he recalls the difficult working conditions of drilling through earth to lay subway tracks, hauling buckets of water deep into the mine. Though hard at first, he credits his Korean physique as key to enduring through this period of his life. He also remembers the influx of Korean nurses, mostly women, who lived alongside the mining communities. At the end of this three-year contract, he was informed by a pastor that Germany would annually review, with the possibility to extend, the working contracts of those married to nurses, but Bae instead made arrangements to relocate to America with some friends he had made in the mines. Before leaving, he married and had a child (born January 1, 1968). When he arrived in Chicago, he estimates there having been around a hundred Korean students studying, who would have to leave the country after their university studies; the onus for building a Koreatown in Chicago landed in the hands of those who would permanently settle in the area, many of whom had been contracted miners in Germany like Bae himself. During holiday celebrations, Bae’s community—which had by then set up businesses along the North Side of the city—invited Korean students in the city to partake in festivities with them. Along the way, he’s founded and joined a number of Korean cultural organizations, the first of which was a performing samulnori (traditional Korean folk music) troup which traveled all around Chicagoland and the American midwest. As time passed and performing samulnori became difficult due to the aging members of the troup, he founded a saxophone sextet as a means of staying in touch with his arts and performance hobbies. Today, he spends his time doing all of the activities he wants to do—dancing, gardening, playing saxophone. He remembers fondly the friends he’s made who have since passed, and reflects on how he is now the oldest member in his immediate community. Even still, he maintains a youthful vibrancy which he credits to him seeking out leisurely activities.

Kyung Bin Bae (Full)
Interviewee: Kyung Bin Bae
Kyung Bin Bae was born in Seoul in 1953, at the heel of the Korean War. In childhood, he recalls having a comfortable upbringing, with his family able to afford private elementary education. In middle school, his father’s business underwent some financial troubles which forced the family to move. He remembers watching his mother struggle as she picked up work, and becomes aware of a degree of class consciousness. As the eldest son, though, his mother tries to hide the family’s finances from him to save him the burden of worry. In middle/high school, he was involved in a number of activities, and was near the top of his class. After losing a student election, he dropped to near bottom in shock. He applied to SNU but was rejected; studied for a year at Namsan library to prepare for the next application cycle, where he is rejected again so enrolls in Sungkyunkwan University to study journalism, joining the student newspaper. This is during the Yushin era (1970s). Fearful in partaking in “demos” (protests), he fulfills his part in student movements through his work with the newspaper, interviewing democratization activists and artists such as poet Kim Chi-ha. After military service, he prepares to study as an international student at Michigan State University in May of 1980 (Gwangju). Studies are expensive, but receives some amount of financial support from family back home. But still needing more funds, he road trips to New York City, which he falls in love with. He finds work in sales at a clothing business run by a Korean businessman in Brooklyn. After returning from New York, his ambitions for his career shift, and he decides that he should go into the clothing business as well. He marries his now-wife, a chemical engineer whom he met at Michigan State, and moves to Astoria, Queens. He recounts the run-down apartment that they first lived in, but only has fond memories of his time in the neighborhood. Tries to find work in Manhattan when his wife becomes pregnant; proceeds to look for work with more urgency and finds a modest job as a salesman in the clothing business, though this time the job requires him to attend a variety of fashion shows to promote his workplace’s sampling of textiles. When his daughter is born, the growing family moves to newly built rowhouse in Flushing; with a newly bought oldsmobile, they roadtrip around the United States. Looks very fondly upon this period of his life. After accumulating some savings, the family moves to Woodbridge, New Jersey, having bought a condominium. Begins attending church. One day, he receives a call from a businessman (non-Korean) who asks if he’d want to work for him as he expands his clothing business into the Asia-Pacific, an offer which he considers and later accepts. He recalls how at the time, the bulk of the clientele for Korean clothing businesses lived in Harlem and the Bronx, which required him to often drive to different parts of the city to close sales deals. At his new company, he’s given the opportunity to stop by Korea to visit some family before continuing with business trips in Hong Kong and Indonesia; in Korea (~1985), he resolves to work to bring his mother to the United States one day after seeing her working in her aging years, though this would never come to fruition. Relocates to Chicago following his daughter’s acceptance to university in the city, where he has lived in peaceful retirement ever since.

Jackie Faye (Full)

Jackie Faye, born and raised in Dallas, Texas, comes from a richly diverse heritage, being Korean and Chinese. Her family history is deeply rooted in East Asia, with her mother originating from Incheon, South Korea, and her grandparents living through the Korean War. Jackie’s ethnic background is Chinese, and her family has been in Korea since the late 1800s, making them fourth-generation Chinese in Korea. This blend of cultures saw her growing up in a household where Korean was spoken, despite the Chinese ethnicity. Jackie's family ran one of the first jajangmyeon shops in Dallas, a testament to their integration and influence within the local community. Her older sister, who is six years older, was a significant influence during her upbringing, especially as Jackie was navigating her own identity and experiences. Tragically, Jackie’s father, who was pursuing a career in Taiwanese pop music, passed away when she was just nine, after her parents had divorced when she was two. Her early memories include recording music with him, an activity that deeply influenced her lifelong passion for music. Music continued to be a part of her life, with piano lessons provided by her church adding to her musical upbringing. Jackie’s connection with her grandmother, a Chinese Korean and the family matriarch, was particularly strong. Her grandmother not only ran the restaurant but also maintained an active lifestyle, which left a lasting impression on Jackie. Navigating her identity extended beyond her ethnic background to her sexual orientation. Jackie, who identifies as gay, knew about her orientation from a young age but only came out a year after college. The period of hiding her true self felt burdensome, and her eventual coming out marked a significant turning point in her life, lifting a weight off her shoulders. At 17, Jackie's journey toward self-discovery and independence took a dramatic turn when she ran away from home. This decision led her to pursue college education on her own terms. Post-college, she reconnected with her mother to come out formally, which, despite initial difficulties, led to eventual acceptance. Today, Jackie is committed to authenticity in her music career, striving to be honest in her lyrics and maintain integrity in her artistic expression. She continues to embrace her father’s legacy in music, using it as a medium to express her emotions and thoughts. Jackie’s story is one of embracing one's true self, overcoming adversity, and the power of resilience and acceptance in shaping a fulfilling life.

Andrew Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Andrew
Andrew Ungal Kim's journey from Gyeonggi-do, Anyang-si, Korea, to the outskirts of Baton Rouge, Louisiana, encapsulates a life of profound transitions and self-discovery. Born and raised in South Korea, where he spent his childhood playing soccer and badminton with neighborhood friends, Andrew moved to the U.S. at the age of 14, bringing with him dreams influenced by the allure of the American lifestyle. Initially thrilled by the prospect of a new life in America, Andrew soon faced the realities of assimilating into a vastly different culture and language environment. His relocation to Clinton, Louisiana, a place where his family was a rarity, presented unique challenges. His father, a former car mechanic in Korea, adapted by joining and eventually taking over his sister's beauty supply business. Meanwhile, Andrew navigated his adolescence with limited social opportunities due to the geographical and cultural constraints of his new hometown. The local Christian private school Andrew attended became a sanctuary of sorts, shielding him from bullying and allowing him a semblance of stability during his formative years. However, beneath the surface, Andrew struggled with a personal turmoil that contrasted sharply with his external environment—coming to terms with his sexuality. Andrew realized he was gay during his early teens, a revelation that conflicted with his Christian upbringing. For years, he prayed for change, a resolution that never materialized. It wasn't until college at Louisiana State University that he began to truly confront and accept his identity. Despite his acceptance, Andrew kept his sexuality hidden from his family and community, driven by fear of rejection and the need to support his widowed mother financially. His mother's eventual discovery of his sexuality in 2016 led to a traumatic period, with her seeking help from their church community to process the news. Remarkably, the church did not ostracize him but instead offered unexpected acceptance, marking a significant point in Andrew's journey towards openness. Professionally, Andrew pursued a career as a Control Engineer in the oil and gas industry, a role that took him to Houston in 2018—a city that promised a fresh start and more opportunities. Houston brought new freedoms and the chance to explore his identity more openly. He became involved with a LGBTQ+ based running group called Frontrunners, which helped strengthen his community ties. In 2022, Andrew married, solidifying a personal commitment that was once unimaginable to him. He lives with his husband and three dogs in a townhouse near downtown Houston, envisioning a future in a home with a yard where he can continue to care for his mother and share his life openly with his family. Andrew's story is one of resilience and transformation, reflecting the challenges and triumphs of navigating cultural shifts, personal identity, and acceptance. His life underscores the importance of community, whether found or fostered, and the enduring quest for authenticity and happiness.
Keywords: homosexuality; coming out; gay; lgbtq+; Chrisianity; Korean church community; immigration; Baton Rouge, Louisiana; Houston, Texas; gay marriage; Louisiana State University; control engineer;

Joseph Yoo (Full)
Interviewee: Yoo, Joseph
Joseph Yoo's journey through life encapsulates the theme of navigating expectations and forging his own path amidst a deeply religious family background. Born in South Korea and moving to the U.S. at the age of six, Joseph experienced the nomadic lifestyle of a pastor's family, living in diverse locations such as South Carolina, Santa Barbara, and Hawaii. These moves, while challenging, helped forge a resilience and adaptability in Joseph, qualities that he values highly as an adult. Raised in a family where both paternal and maternal lines were deeply embedded in the ministry, the expectation for Joseph to follow in his father's footsteps was prevalent from his childhood. Despite this, during his teenage years, Joseph found himself resistant to the idea of becoming a pastor. However, his perspective shifted after attending a pivotal Methodist conference/retreat in Chicago, an experience that Joseph describes as a turning point in recognizing his own calling to ministry. Choosing to lead a multiethnic church rather than a Korean-specific congregation, Joseph has sought to establish professional boundaries that were often blurred in more culturally homogenous religious communities. His approach to ministry is shaped by his diverse upbringing and his father's influence, who despite the family's frequent relocations, always provided a stable and loving home environment. In 2016, Joseph moved to Houston, attracted by the affordability and job opportunities. His career took another turn here; he transitioned from being a United Methodist pastor to an Episcopalian pastor. This change marks another chapter in Joseph’s life of embracing new beginnings and reflecting on his spiritual journey. Joseph’s personal life took a transformative turn when he and his wife, after facing difficulties in having biological children, decided to become certified foster parents. They welcomed Nathaniel, an autistic child who was half white and half Hispanic. Initially facing complex issues with Nathaniel’s biological parents, they eventually adopted him, bringing immense joy and fulfillment to their lives. Despite initial reservations, Joseph’s parents became supportive of the adoption, recognizing the profound happiness Nathaniel brought into their lives. Joseph’s story is one of self-discovery, resilience, and embracing life’s unexpected turns. His narrative weaves through the challenges of living up to family expectations, finding personal faith, and building a family under unconventional circumstances. His life illustrates the beauty of adopting new cultures and traditions, as seen in Nathaniel’s love for Korean food, and underscores the profound impacts of love and acceptance. Joseph Yoo’s life journey invites reflection on the nature of calling and the transformative power of embracing one’s true path, no matter how unexpected it might be.
Keywords: Houston, Texas; pastor's kid; Korean church community; United Methodist Church; Episcopalian; adoption; autism; christianity; South Korea; religion; South Carolina; Santa Barbara; Hawaii; foster parents; faith; prayer;

Jason Cho (Full)
Interviewee: Cho, Jason
Jason Cho, a 43-year-old native of Houston, Texas, has roots deeply entrenched in martial arts and entrepreneurship. Growing up in a low-income neighborhood, his early life revolved around his family's Tae Kwon Do school, where discipline and structure were part of his daily regimen. This upbringing not only honed his athletic skills but also instilled in him a robust emotional resilience that later translated well into his professional life. Jason’s childhood was significantly influenced by his uncle, a renowned Tae Kwon Do master, which naturally led him to excel in sports throughout high school. His deep connection with martial arts guided him to become a Tae Kwon Do instructor himself before venturing into the hospitality industry, driven by a passion for food cultivated alongside his sister, a fellow food enthusiast. The pivot to the hospitality sector was sparked during a visit to New York where his sister introduced him to Korean fried chicken. Captivated by the dish and unable to find a similar offering in Houston, Jason was inspired to open his own restaurant specializing in Korean fried chicken. Despite his father's initial skepticism due to Jason's lack of cooking experience, Jason's determination led him to sell his house and car to finance the venture—a decision that paid off when his father, after tasting his son's cooking, gave his full support. Tragically, his father passed away as construction began, a profound loss that Jason felt deeply during this critical period of transitioning to a new business. In addition to running his restaurant, Jason manages two Tae Kwon Do schools, maintaining his ties to the martial arts community. His aspirations don't stop at the restaurant business; he dreams of shaping Houston's culinary scene by becoming a prominent figure in promoting Korean food concepts and hopes to one day establish a Koreatown in the city, continuing his father's legacy of community building. Jason's personal life also faced significant challenges, particularly when his father battled and initially overcame lung cancer, only to succumb later to complications from pneumonia. This period was one of intense emotional strain for Jason, who regrets not maintaining a more positive outlook during his father's final days. This experience taught him the power of the human mind and the impact of positivity on overcoming life’s hurdles. Today, Jason remains a devoted father to his daughters and a dedicated business owner, striving to balance his professional ambitions with personal commitments. His story is one of resilience, cultural pride, and the pursuit of dreams fueled by family legacy and personal passion.
Keywords: martial arts; tae kwon do; dojang; entrepreneur; korean fried chicken; hospitality industry; restaurants; loss; mourning; lung cancer; death; family; business; Houston, Texas;

Jason Cho (Edited)

Jason Cho was born and raised in Houston, Texas, in the Aleaf area. His father owned a Taekwondo School business and was the first to do so in the 70s in their respective area. It was an all-in situation for Jason and he persevered through his journey through the lessons he learned with Taekwondo. Discipline, structure, leading by example, and being a man of action are only some of what he learned. Jason now aspires to be a representation of Korean concepts in Houston through his restaurant Dak & Bop and carry on his father’s dream of buying land and building a community by creating a Koreatown in his city in the future.

David Hee Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, David Hee
David Lee's life is a profound story of resilience and transformation. Born and raised in Los Angeles, California, within a devoutly Christian Korean American family, David encountered significant challenges from a young age. His father, a pastor, had first come to the US in the 1970s for leukemia treatment at UCLA, and later, he brought David's mother over from South Korea. David's childhood was shaped by this deeply religious environment, which initially provided a sense of community but eventually felt stifling as he grew older. The most significant struggle in David's early life came from within his own home. His mother suffered from bipolar disorder and terminal breast cancer, conditions that led to periods of intense abuse directed at David. Growing up in a low-income household, David was acutely aware of the harsh realities of poverty, which further complicated his home life. Driven by a desire to escape the turbulence of his family life, David worked diligently in high school, earning a full scholarship to Georgetown University—a decision that marked his first major step away from his past. Once at university, far from the pressures of his family, David decided he would sever ties permanently with his birth family, a decision catalyzed by abuse, the lack of support, and understanding for his identity as a gay man. During his college years, David supported himself through multiple part-time jobs, an endeavor that underscored his independence and determination. It was also during this time that he formed a life-changing friendship with Kelly, a fellow student. When David faced the prospect of nowhere to go during the holidays, Kelly invited him to spend it with her family in Houston, Texas. This gesture of kindness evolved into a deep and lasting bond. After several years of growing closer to Kelly’s family, and experiencing the warmth and unconditional support he had longed for, David was legally adopted by them when he was 26 years old. This adoption came during a tumultuous time. David's biological mother passed away in 2017, an event that forced him to confront his past. During this period, his biological father apologized for the abuse David endured from his mother, a moment of closure that was significant yet complex. David also came out to his biological father around this time, who, unlike others in his biological family, fully accepted his sexuality. David's story is a testament to the power of chosen families and the profound impact of embracing one's true self. Now co-founding Texas Signal, he channels his experiences into advocacy and storytelling, aiming to give a voice to those who feel marginalized. His journey illustrates not only personal resilience but also the transformational love that defines family, not by blood but by bond.
Keywords: adoption; abuse; sexuality; lgbtq; poverty; low-income; Georgetown University; Texas Signal; donor; politics; politcal science; journalism; media; family; Houston, Texas; Los Angeles, California; cancer; Korean church community;

David Hee Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, David Hee
David Lee's journey from a troubled upbringing in LA, marked by his mother's bipolar disorder and abusive behavior, to finding solace and a new beginning is a testament to resilience and the search for identity. Raised in a devoutly Christian, Korean American household, he faced intensified challenges after his siblings left and his mother was diagnosed with terminal breast cancer, bearing the brunt of her abuse while serving as her caregiver. Recognizing education as his escape, David excelled academically, earning a scholarship to Georgetown University and leaving his difficult past behind. There, he formed a life-changing friendship with Kelly, whose family eventually adopted him, offering the love and stability he didn’t have growing up. This act of adoption not only provided David with a new family but also helped him to start healing from his past traumas. Embracing his complex identity as Korean, LGBT, and adoptee, David's story is a powerful narrative of overcoming adversity through the support of his chosen family and the strength of the human spirit.

Terry Yun (Full)
Interviewee: Yun, Terry
Terry Yun was born “Theresa Yun” in Neungju-myeon, Hwasung-gun, South Jeolla Province, where she was raised until the age of 11. She remembers how her grandparents told her that their ancestral home was in Gyeonggi Province, and that her great grandfather was a relative of Joseon-era royalty. As a child, she remembers being a “tom-boy,” hanging out with the other boys in her village, and enjoying playing out in the dirt. She recalls that her father owned ducks and goats, and would deliver goat milk around the village. She also recounts a harrowing story about a time when she was given an errand (going to the pharmacy), only to fall into the village well on the way back; she states that this was the first time she turned to her faith in earnest. As an only child, her older relatives would often tell her family to have a son, and growing up Terry wanted nothing else than to be a boy. On September 17, 1978, Terry’s family immigrated to Oklahoma, where her aunt had moved a year prior with her American GI husband. However, after ten months of living in Oklahoma, the family relocated to Houston, seeing as her aunt and her father didn’t get along very well. In Houston, they were taken under the care of a Presbyterian Church (Terry’s family is Catholic), who found them a room in a 2-bedroom apartment leased by another Korean person, “Anshi Ajuhssi.” She recalls driving through the Houston highways and being in awe of the layers of overpasses above her. Her parents found work cleaning the town mall, but one night her father and ahjussi went out and didn’t come back for three days; it wasn’t until the pastor of the church called Terry’s home that they found out that he had been in a car accident, put into a coma and hospitalized. Because the family had no insurance, they quickly took Terry’s father out from the ICU and brought him home, where he was bedridden for months. Terry remembers how the church took care of their family during this difficult time. Eventually, as Terry graduated high school and began college at UT Austin, she was faced with the question of her career. After trying out premed, she landed on studying fine arts. In 1988, she married her husband who she had met through by matchmaking—she wanted to early marry, seeing that her father was in poor health. After marriage, her husband’s family moved from Korea to live with her, a period she found difficult in maintaining both a traditional Korean household while also exercising her own freedoms, but she says that there’s no other person who knows her better than her husband. In their 40s, she and her husband moved to Birmingham, Alabama, for six years where they operated a sushi business, before coming back to Houston where she worked briefly at the Korean American Association and Community Center of Houston. After a year working there, she and a friend opened a nonprofit organization called Uri Juntos; with an eye towards the future, she envisions herself making more art, and translating religious texts.
Keywords: Houston; Catholic; Oklahoma; Jeolla; Jeollanam-do; South Jeolla; Hwasung; Neungju; church; aunt; highway; overpass; matchmaking; marriage; Birmingham; Alabama; Coma; sushi; nonprofit; Korean American Association; Uri Juntos

Terry Yun (Edited)
Interviewee: Yun, Terry
Terry Yun was born in Neungju-myeon, Hwasung-Gun, Jeollanam-do, and immigrated to the United States when she was 11 years old. She was originally named Yoon Dereza by her father and grandmother in hopes that she would grow up to be like St. Therese of Lisieux, but the people who did paperwork in the U.S. upon immigration put “Terry” as her first name,  “Sa” her middle name,  and  “Yoon” as her last name. She describes her life after immigration being a struggle. They first arrived in Oklahoma but relocated to Houston after her father heard about better job opportunities in Houston from a neighbor. Through the help of other people, they were able to find a job and also find solace in the Korean Catholic Church where she would meet even more people who helped her through other hardships. She kept close to her heart she was told by a church member, “Down the road, when you are in a better position and you see somebody who needs help, just help them." Now, Terry works as a service coordinator for the nonprofit organization Uri Juntos, and in the future she sees herself retiring from her position, translating English spiritual books into Korean, and also creating more art.

Bark Boo Moon (Full)
Interviewee: Moon, Bark Boo
Bark Book Moon was born on January 1945 in Seoul. When he was five years old, an American family moved into the home just behind his, and they brought with them a child who was around the same age as Bark who would pellet him with his slingshot, despite Bark’s desire to befriend the new child. A couple of days before the war broke out, the American family moved away, and after fighting started Bark’s family sought refuge in Masan, where he encountered American and British soldiers for the first time. He recounts a funny story of him and his friends finding what they thought was a balloon on the ground, blowing it up when an American GI smacked him on the back of his head, telling him that the balloon was actually a condom. He also remembers how different soldiers treated the kids differently; whereas Americans would throw them chocolate and gum from the out the backs of their moving trucks, British soldiers would always pull over and hand the sweet treats to the children directly. He returned home for middle school and high school, where he initially found English an easy subject. While serving time in the military, a senior of his who needed to pass a written examination asked Bark if he could instead take it for him, which he did. He was caught, however, and was given the choice between serving time in jail, or serving time in Vietnam, and he chose the latter. After completing his training for Vietnam, he remembers how he and his friends received a paycheck which they used to go out and party before they were deployed, and he recounts how many of his platoon mates got STDs that night. In Vietnam, he was tasked with transporting medical supplies, and would often have to make harrowing treks through gunfire to deliver the supplies where they were needed. He remembers standing in the marshes, too, where it would often flood, and how standing chest-deep in water he felt as if he could see the ripples in the water emanating out of his heartbeat. In 1972 he arrived in the United States where his first job was in an ice cream parlor, before joining the U.S. Army at the age of 28 as infantry. He found training to be a breeze and when asked where he wanted to be stationed, he said he’d go back to Korea; back at home, he met his wife at church, and together immigrated to the United States. They had two kids, and Bark decided to leave the Army to work in the post office so he’d be able to spend more time with family, where he worked until his retirement. Today, he takes care of his ailing wife in Texas.
Keywords: Seoul; Korean War; American; soldier; British; Masan; child; chocolate; gum; condom; church; balloon; test; jail; Vietnam; Vietnam War; gunfire; American Army; infantry; military; Armed Forces; post office; Texas; ice cream; stationed; dementia

Bark Bo Moon was born in Seoul in January 1945, just before the Korean War. He reminisces on his journey to finding refuge in Masan-si alongside his family and being faced with much violence and hardship. Airplanes that flew above that would shoot down them and having to protect themselves, but the family still spread warmth to one another. After the War, he went back to school and focused on his academics, especially English. Time passes and he joins the Korean Army and experiences the Vietnam War. After more changes in his life in homes and occupations, he now is retired, but still lives with a drive to help others for the greater good. He emphasizes that his goal in life is to give smiles and spread kindness, and warm gestures.

Dr. Sam Jae Cho (Full)
Interviewee: Cho, Sam Jae
Dr. Sam Jae Cho was born in Seoul, inside his father’s company residence. The oldest of 5, he has two younger sisers and two younger brothers; during the onset of the Korean War, the family was unable to flee south but instead hid in the basement of the company residence until the September 28 Offensive, which was when the family evacuated to Daegu. The family stayed in Daegu for the duration of the war, and Dr. Cho finished attending middle and high school there as well. After graduating from high school, Dr. Cho matriculated as a student in the Mining/Engineering Department at Seoul National University, which during his undergraduate time reorganized itslef into a Mineral Resources/Petrol Engineering Department. Following graduation, he took a job at a mining and engineering company which asked that he communte into the mines; he quickly realized that working in the mines wasn’t for him, as the air was unclean and he’d emerge daily coated in ash to the point where he was unrecognizable. He left the job to take a governmental one in the national department of energy; it was around this time that he saw a televised broadcast of President Park Chung-hee who announced that oil fields had been discovered off the coast of Pohang, and that a new intiative would be put into place for Korea to become self-sufficient in energy; every year, 100 Korean students were sent abroad to be educated in energy technologies. Dr. Cho left to study at UT Austin after studying English for two years. After graduating with his master’s, he intended to return to Korea to help the country’s fledgling petrol industry, but received a job offer that had little to do with what he had studied while in Texas, and so chose to remain in America to continue studying for a doctorate. The school asked of him to receive field experience before beginning his dissertation, and so he spent six months in New Orleans working for Texaco where as a technical assistant where he discovered a virgin (untapped) oil field. He then returned to school to complete his dissertation, and stayed in Texas to work for various Oil companies. Later into his career, he launched an organization which would provide resources, networking support, and community for international Korean students seeking to study energy sciences in Houston so that they might be able to return home and introduce emerging technologies in Korea.
Keywords: Seoul; Company; basement; Korean War; Daegu; 928; September 28; Operation Chromite; Inchon Landing; Incheon Landing; SNU; Seoul National University; Engineering; Petrol; Mining; ash; Energy; Mineral Resources; Petrol Engineering; Department of Energy; Pohang; Park Chung-hee; UT Austin; UT; Texaco; Houston; disseration; PhD; Masters; Master’s; oil; New Orleans

Dr. Sam Jae Cho (Edited)
Interviewee: Cho, Sam Jae
When Dr. Sam Jae Cho was just a year old, his family fled to Daegu, where he resided until he returned to Seoul to study at university. He details the trajectory of his academic career, starting as an undergraduate at SNU studying Mineral and Petroleum Engineering, to completing a PhD at UT Austin after receiving a government scholarship. After finishing his studies, Dr. Cho sought to return to Korea but upon feeling that the position he was offered wasn’t a right fit, he made the critical decision to remain in the United States choosing instead to work at energy companies where the efforts of his dissertation could be better realized. Even still, wanting to contribute to the advancements in energy technology for his home country, Dr. Cho worked at creating an organization which would provide a platform for Korean students studying energy sciences in Texas to come together, share discourse, and propose emerging technologies in energy management to their home. In his golden years, Dr. Cho remains steadfast in his conviction that we can inch toward a better future through scholarship.

Dae-Duck Cha (Full)
Interviewee: Dae Duck Cha
Dae-duck Cha was born in Gangwon Province, Hongcheon, the youngest of nine siblings. When he was six, the family moved to Seoul; during the Korean War, all of his older brothers died, leaving just him and his older sisters. As a child, he moved around a lot—he attended elementary school while living with his oldest sister, and middle school through college while living with his middle sister, and so he believes he didn’t do well in school because he never had time to adjust to one singular educational environment. His parents passed during the Korean War, not as casualties of war, but as victims of a massacre by fellow Koreans who were going around the country in search of supposed communist-sympathizers. Before Cha was born, they were both heavily involved with the independence movement, and after liberation, both parents were involved with democracy movements; before they were “taken away” by their captors, Cha rememembers them telling him that they would “see him later.” He reflects on the irony that is chinilpa, or Japanese collaborators, getting away with little to no punishment, and whose families are still well off today, whereas those individuals who were directly involved with the liberation and democratization of the country were treated like garbage. In school, he was often told that he was a gifted artist, and so he took art classes and attended Hongik University, widely known for their fine arts programs. He then completed his mandatory military service, then graduated from college, after which he left for the United States to work as a member of the Peace Corps. After his role in the Peace Corps came to an end, he met his wife at a Korean church and lived in Philadelphia for a while, operating a wig and hairstyling store. After 7-8 years, he realized that he hadn’t come to the U.S. to immigrate, but to work, and moved back to Korea for 1.5 years. However, the family—who now had two kids—decided to move back to the U.S. for their kids, and moved to Houston, where they knew nobody. Cha continued his art, and was recognized by a local news channel (Channel 2), after which he became well known in the Korean community within Houston, with his art presented in various galleries around the city. He soon opened a frame-making businesses, which he worked until retirement.
Keywords: Gangwon; Gangwongdo; Seoul; Heongcheon; Hongik University; Hongik; Art; Peace Corps; school; Korean War; chinilpa; communist; communism; communist sympathizer; Japanese; Collaborator; Houston; Philadelphia; wig; hair; hairstylist; neighborhood; dangerous; daughters; Channel 2; Galleries; Rice University; frame; frame-making

Dae-Duck Cha (Edited)
Interviewee: Dae Duck Cha
Born in Hongcheong-un of the Gangwon province, Dae Duck Cha was forced to flee to Seoul at age 6 due to the Korean War. His father led a March First Demonstration in 1919 in his hometown and this passion of his was handed down directly to Cha. Growing up amidst the war was rough for Cha, he had a hard time keeping up with academics due to frequently moving around and living with his older sisters. Through this period, drawing became his solace, leading him to pursue art at Hongik University and eventually join the Peace Corps as an artist which brought him to the United States. He continued to go through many more changes and relocations in his life until eventually settling in Houston. Recognition and fame within the Houston Korean Community came slowly, with his artworks showcased in galleries of others and also his own. Cha’s story reminds us of the importance of being passionate and pursuing our dreams no matter what comes our way.

Justine Yoon (Full)
Interviewee: Yoon, Justine
Justine Yoon was born in Los Angeles, California, to parents who had come to the United States as young adults: her mother arrived when she was twenty-five, and her father arrived in America shortly after. She describes her family as being tight-knit and nuclear and describes her early childhood growing up in the Los Feliz area as lively and active. Her parents would register her and her siblings for sports camps and a variety of extracurriculars so that they might become immersed and exposed to American culture in their outside lives; she remembers biking up and down her neighborhood with her friends and being surrounded by a diverse group of children. At the same time, the family was raised speaking Korean, and as was the case for many Korean American children, sent their kids to weekend Korean school at the local Korean church. She recalls how both parents were active and well known within the Korean community, with her father operating a private practice as a clinical psychologist in the area. Both parents placed a strong emphasis on their children’s education, which Justine appreciated while understanding that the generational difference in their values could be explained by sympathizing with the fact that her parents were doing the best with what they knew, in a country they had immigrated to. She entered university at UCLA not knowing what she would study—those around her suggested business—and sampled a variety of courses before finding an interest in Sociology, which she chose as her major. Around the time of her graduation, her parents retired but made the transition to work in the restaurant business, where she’d often lend a hand. When describing what it means to her to be Korean American, she first says that there is a belief around the idea that working hard will actualize into success, and that Korean people seem to be filled with a lot of hope to begin with (the example she uses is her parents’ opening of a third restaurant, right before covid). Today (at the time of recording), she lives in Koreatown which she says was not planned, but has been “convenient” in how there’s access to good food, though she laments that the neighborhood has become increasingly gentrified since she was a child.
Keywords: Los Angeles; California; UCLA; Sociology; church; Korean; Korean school; nuclear; restaurant; covid; corona; gentrification; food; diversity; Los Feliz; education; values; tension

Justine Yoon (Edited)
Interviewee: Yoon, Justine
Justine Yoon was born in Los Angeles to immigrant parents. She grew up to the north of Koreatown in Los Feliz, and describes her childhood as having been lively and active: from a young age, her parents put her and her older siblings through a variety of local leagues, sports, and extracurriculars, in part so that they might grow up immersed within American culture. Her parents were active within the Korean community as well: her father operated a private practice in Koreatown, and after his retirement ventured into the restaurant business with her mother which they’ve continued to this day. Justine would often work in her parents’ restaurants, whether it was by peeling and cleaning vegetables or going out into the markets to secure the best deals on wholesale ingredients. Today, she has taken a smaller role at her parents’ restaurants to pursue her own passions, something which exemplifies a larger internal struggle that children of immigrants must personally and uniquely reckon with: how we might actualize our own dreams—which our parents worked tirelessly for—while also honoring the sacrifices that went into creating opportunities for the next generation.

Legacy Project - Young Hee Shon (Full)
Interviewee: Shon, Young Hee
Young-hee Shon was born in 1953 in North Gyeongsang Province, South Korea. During her childhood, her parents ran a “bed and breakfast”-like business, operating a motel and diner for commuting workers who either missed their connecting bus back home, or finished their work too late to catch the bus back home at all. Their hospitality business, which included the motel and restaurant across the street, also included a ticket booth and an intercity bus which could dock near the motel to take the workers back home in the morning. The restaurant, which her parents called “Seong Buk Dong Gukbap,” still exists today. After graduating from high school, she met her husband who had a business of his own, and the couple later married and had children, though her husband’s business went bankrupt. Through a turn in fortune, the family was able to successfully apply for, and was granted, visas and later a Green Cards to stay indefinitely in the United States, where the couple moved for their children’s education. The family chose to move to Los Angeles upon hearing from her sister that Los Angeles’ Koreatown was a “good place to find a job,” and although Mrs. Shon states that she never worked before moving to America, she was surprised that she was able to find work rather easily in Koreatown. Her first job, which she admits she worked at for only a single day before moving elsewhere, was to work in a kitchen making banchan. The next day, however, she began working in the kitchen of the Palace Hotel, introduced to the opportunity through a chef that she knew. However, upon experiencing emotional hardships within the workplace, she chose to quit and find similar work at the Rotex Hotel instead. She worked at the Rotex for a while, but after a while began experiencing swelling and pain around her arms and elbows, something she believes was due to her not being used to working for such extended periods of time. She tried looking for a less physically demanding job, eventually finding work at a long-distance phone calling company, which she enjoyed. After her son was married, she resolved to open her own restaurant, following in the footsteps of her parents and in line with the jobs she first had after moving to Los Angeles. From her apartment, she began making homemade doenjang to use at her newly opened ssambap restaurant, though after complaints of the smell from her neighbors, her landlord told her she couldn’t make doenjang in her unit. Not too dismayed, she looked into taking out a mortgage to buy a house, where she might be able to make doenjang without the complaints of neighbors. Her restaurant business took off after she bought a home, which she attributes less to the advertisements she took out in the Korean newspapers but rather to word-of-mouth: “Advertisements in newspapers are as flowing water, whereas reputation which accumulates in customers’ mouths stay.” She named her restaurant “Seongbukdong” after her parent’s own diner, and today it stands as a beloved part of K-Town known for providing Gyeongsang-style Korean food.
Keywords: Gyeongsang; North Gyeongsang; motel; parents; intercity; bus; restaurant; Seong Buk Dong Gukbap; Seongbukdong; green card; bankrupt; Koreatown; Los Angeles; Palace Hotel; Rotex Hotel; call center; ssam; ssambap; food; sister; advertisement; reputation; banchan

Legacy Project - Young Hee Shon (Edited)
Interviewee: Shon, Young Hee
Young-hee Shon was born in 1953 in North Gyeongsang Province, South Korea, to parents who ran successful business in the hospitality industry. At the time, workers who missed the last intercity bus out of the city would have nowhere to spend the night, so her parents operated a motel where late-night workers could spend the night. They also ran a restaurant directly next to their hotel called “Seong Buk Dong Gukbap,” which still exists today. Mrs. Shon immigrated to the United States with her husband and children when they were presented with the opportunity to obtain a green card. Upon hearing from her sister that L.A.’s Koreatown was a good place to find a job, the family chose to move to Los Angeles, where Mrs. Shon’s first job was in making banchan at the Korean market. From there, she switched jobs to work in the kitchen of the Palace Hotel, but after experiencing certain emotional hardships, found a job at the Rotex Hotel instead. After working there for a while, she began experiencing physical discomfort in her arms and shoulders, and so found work at a long-distance phone calling company, which she enjoyed. Yet the job she enjoyed most was in opening her own restaurant, which she named after the one her parents ran back in Korea: Seongbukdong. A beloved part of K-Town, she prepares each dish with love and care, knowing that the success of her restaurant isn’t dependent on advertisement space in newspapers, but in the reputation which is built in the mouths of her customers.
Keywords: Gyeongsang; North Gyeongsang; motel; parents; intercity; bus; restaurant; Seong Buk Dong Gukbap; Seongbukdong; green card; bankrupt; Koreatown; Los Angeles; Palace Hotel; Rotex Hotel; call center; ssam; ssambap; food; sister; advertisement; reputation; banchan

Legacy Project - Aileen Kim (Full)
Aileen Kim was born in 1972 in Yanji, Jilin Province, China, and is a third-generation Korean Chinese. Her family traces their identity as “Joseon-jok,” a term literally meaning “of Joseon ethnicity” but more generally employed when referencing ethnic Koreans living in China, back to Aileen’s grandmother who sought refuge in China in 1917. Both of Aileen’s parents were born in then-Manchuria and entered the civil service. Growing up, Aileen recounts a middle-class childhood in the Korean-speaking Chinese prefecture, converting to Christianity in high school (partly because the school was Christian-affiliated), and attending Yanbian University of Science and Technology, where she studied English. After graduation, she worked for the school as part of its human resources team, where her linguistic and cultural fluency in Chinese, English, and Korean made her a valuable resource for the university’s outreach efforts. During this time, she met her husband who had briefly moved to China to further study Traditional Medicine; after his studies, the couple married and settled in Korea, where Aileen worked as a Chinese language instructor for both children and adults in Ulsan. She paused her work after having their first child, and the couple soon immigrated to Los Angeles when she was pregnant with their second child for her husband to pursue his career in America. Through a Radio Korea, she would find work at a restaurant whose owner also happened to be Korean Chinese, where her language skills could again be leveraged to her advantage. Although the family hadn’t moved to Los Angeles with the idea of finding a Korean Chinese community in mind, they later found out that the city had a vibrant Joseon-jok community; 70% of the customers who came to the restaurant were Korean Chinese. When asked how she feels about the term “joseok-jok” which, depending on context, could be used disparagingly (such as “gyopo”), she says that like America, China is also home to many different ethnic groups, and hopes that when customers call her “joseok-jok” she hopes they don’t mean it in a rude way. Perhaps owing to her own experiences as an ethnic minority in China, she explains how she relates to her own children who, growing up Korean American, must learn to navigate their own hyphenated identity. She hopes that her children retains the Korean language.
Keywords: Yanji; Jilin; China; Korean Chinese; joseon-jok; Manchuria; middle-class; civil service; traditional medicine; oriental medicine; English; Yanbian University of Science and Technology; YUST; Ulsan; Korea; Los Angeles; Radio Korea; restaurant; ethnic diversity

Legacy Project - Aileen Kim (Edited)
Aileen Kim was born in Yanji, Jilin Province, China, in 1972, and she is a third-generation Korean Chinese: her grandmother sought refuge in China during the Japanese occupation, and her parents were born in Manchuria. She talks about having a relatively middle-class upbringing, with both parents both working as public servants, as well as attending Yanbian University of Science and Technology, where she studied English. After graduation, she worked for the school’s human resources team—her fluency across languages and cultures meant that she could work in translation and in cultural introductions with visiting students and scholars. Her husband was one such visiting student, and after the two married they lived in Ulsan, Korea, for six years, where Aileen worked as a Chinese instructor. While pregnant with their second child, the family moved to Los Angeles for Aileen’s husband’s career. Through Radio Korea, she later found work in a restaurant whose owner was also Korean Chinese; here, too, her fluency in Chinese, Korean, and English proved useful in working in front of house. Although she wasn’t aware of it at first, she came to find a sizable Korean Chinese community in Los Angeles, and takes pride in her identity. She relates to her children in understanding what it’s like to grow up Korean in a non-Korean-majority environment, and just as she held onto her heritage through the Korean language, she hopes for her children to continue using it as well.
Keywords: Yanji; Jilin; China; Korean Chinese; joseon-jok; Manchuria; middle-class; civil service; traditional medicine; oriental medicine; English; Yanbian University of Science and Technology; YUST; Ulsan; Korea; Los Angeles; Radio Korea; restaurant; ethnic diversity

Legacy Project - Cathy Yi (Full)
Interviewee: Yi, Cathy
Cathy Yi was born in 1950 in Gyeonggi Province, some thirty minutes outside of Seoul. After the Korean War, her family moved to Seoul for her education, where she graduated from high school; soon after, she moved to the Tennessee, where some of her relatives had immigrated to. She had heard stories of the racial climate in America while living in Korea, though she never anticipated that she’d ever directly experience it herself. While she was attending the University of Tennessee, Knoxville, she one day entered a local grocery market where she made eye contact with a young child, who (supposedly scared by the stranger’s appearance!), ran away to his mother in tears. Despite the upsetting encounter, she resolved to complete her studies to make a better life for herself in this new country she had moved to when she was so young—perhaps because she immigrated as a young adult, she says, becoming adjusted to adult life in America was easier than she had initially thought. After graduation she met her husband with whom she moved to New York, starting a business in Philadelphia. Although New York was exciting to live in as a newlywed, cheaper rents and proximity to their business prompted them to relocate to New Jersey, where the couple lived before moving to Los Angeles in 1986 to start a new business in wholesale. The family business prospered for a while but owing to difficult and strained relationships with her business partners, eventually closed. She appreciates life in Los Angeles surrounded by more Koreans than in any other city she’s lived in; when asked about her decision to immigrate to the United States, she explains that although the younger generation sees Korea as a highly developed nation with modern amenities and luxuries, the Korea she remembers and left behind was one broken by war and afflicted by poverty; immigrating to America was something to be both desired and envied. From retirement, she feels as though she worked too hard when she was young for her business to close the way it did, but feels grateful that she was able to live the comfortable life she did, fully embracing what she felt as a distinctly American culture of individualism from the moment she landed in the States.
Keywords: 1950; Gyeonggi Province; Gyeonggi-do; Seoul; high school; Tennessee; Knoxville; University of Tennessee Knoxville; racism; tears; young adult; Los Angeles; New York; New Jersey; Philadelphia; retirement; business; partner; manager; wholesale

Legacy Project - Cathy Yi (Edited)
Interviewee: Yi, Cathy
Cathy Yi was born in 1950 in Gyeonggi Province, Korea, just outside of Seoul. At a young age, she moved with her family to Seoul, where she received her primary and secondary education; after graduating from high school, she moved to the United States to live with some of her relatives who had already immigrated to the country. She had heard stories of discriminatory attitudes towards minorities while living in Korea and was disheartened to experience such attitudes for herself in places as unassuming as the local grocery market, but was nevertheless resolved to make a better life for herself in spite of the racism she encountered. When she was 27 years old, she married and moved to New York where she and her husband lived for five years before moving to New Jersey to be closer to her business, which was located in Philadelphia. In 1986, she moved to Los Angeles where she and her family got by comfortable while working in wholesale, though a change in fortune would later cause her to close the business. Today, from retirement, Cathy appreciates how she was able to acclimate to an American way of life early on, recognizing that although there exists cultural differences between the Korea she remembers and the America she’d immigrate to, the choices we make as individuals are not predetermined by the society we live in.
Keywords: 1950; Gyeonggi Province; Gyeonggi-do; Seoul; high school; Tennessee; Knoxville; University of Tennessee Knoxville; racism; tears; young adult; Los Angeles; New York; New Jersey; Philadelphia; retirement; business; partner; manager; wholesale

Legacy Project - Esther Kang (Full)
Interviewee: Kang, Esther
Esther Kang was born in Detroit, Michigan, to a family who moved often when she was young in pursuit of her father’s education and career. The oldest of three siblings, she explains that the family lived in Detroit initally so that her father could pursue a postdoctoral position at Wayne State University before relocating to the northern part of Glendale in January of 1992, before Saigu. Although she was still young, she remembers fragments of April 2nd, 1992, when the Los Angeles Uprising began; she recalls waiting for her father to come home, as well as having formative conversations with her parents about race in the family. Afterwards, the family moved to Dallas, Texas, for her father to attend school at UT Dallas. In the meantime, Esther herself had begun attending school as one of two asian students at her school. She has a distinct memory of being made fun of for her lunch, and so showed up to school one day with lunchables which she later regretted, as she thought they tasted bad. After graduating from high school, she moved to Chicago where she attended university, and since then has lived in San Francisco, Baltimore, Brooklyn, Pittsburg, Perth, and Durbin, though she moved back to Los Angeles to work as a researcher, educator, and Professor of Design focusing on questions about ethical design, and equitable processes. Through her research as a scholar and academic, she’s come to understand Design as a political, cultural, and social force that has profound impacts on the environment, whose product is both a process and the the ability to reach broad groups. She also belives that design requires a critical and sensitive understanding of its own consequences, as well as reflections on how we as humans relate to one another. Having lived in Los Angeles for almost two decades, she explains that she’s always been drawn to Koreatown, a neighborhood which has helped her hold onto memory while also letting go of memory, recognizing that her own understanding of Koreatown fits into a broader patchwork of communal experiences. Today, she works as a Professor of Design Studies at the University of Wisconsin, Madison.
Keywords: Detroit; Dallas; Chicago; Los Angeles; Glendale; San Francisco; Baltimore; Brooklyn; Pittsburg; Perth; Durbin; Design; Ethics; Processes; Saigu; race; lunchables; Koreatown; Madison; Wisconsin; Professor; lunch; critical inquiry

Legacy Project - Esther Kang (Edited)
Interviewee: Kang, Esther
Esther Kang is a researcher, educator, and Professor of Design Studies at the University of Wisconsin. As both a child and adult she moved often, experiencing life across a diverse range of cities spanning from Los Angeles and San Francisco, to Dallas and New York, and abroad in Perth, Australia, and Durbin, South Africa. Having lived in Los Angeles the longest and throughout her formative years, she considers the city to be her hometown; her family moved to L.A. in January of 1992, just months before the L.A. Uprising in April. Kang recounts how Saigu was a catalyst in her family’s discussions about race, the idea of which stuck with her as the family moved around the country: while attending school in Dallas, she was one of only two Asians students in the entire student body. As a researcher and educator today, Kang’s work centers on the political, cultural and social implications of Design, incorporating the recogition that design is more than just a means of communincation, and that in many ways, the process of design itself is the product as well. She continues to ask what an equitable design process looks like, and what ethical design ethos is, questions she compels us to think about with critical and sensitive understandings of their consequences.
Keywords: Detroit; Dallas; Chicago; Los Angeles; Glendale; San Francisco; Baltimore; Brooklyn; Pittsburg; Perth; Durbin; Design; Ethics; Processes; Saigu; race; lunchables; Koreatown; Madison; Wisconsin; Professor; lunch; critical inquiry

Legacy Project - Kyungbin Min (Full)
Kyungbin Min was born in Seoul, Korea, and moved to Southern California (around Torrance) where her aunt lived. It was while his family briefly lived with his aunt that he discovered his passion for food, as well as his calling to be a chef; he remembers always wanting to be involved with food preparation and service from an early age, starting first as a child helping to set up and take down the dinner table, before moving onto slowly learning his way around his aunt’s kitchen. After graduating from high school, he worked as a server to save money for culinary school in Orange County, which he describes as a great opportunity in how it exposed him to a side of the restaurant scene he hadn’t encountered before. After completing culinary school, he decided to try opening his own restaurant that incorporated techniques he learned from culinary school while staying true to his own flair—part of what appealed to him about cooking was the instant gratification of service, something he describes as giving him the positive motivation to keep on doing what he’s doing to make others happy. When the pandemic hit he was furloughed from his job, and so, alongside some friends, decided to begin a dry aging meat company completely online, which was successful. With some other friends in the restaurant business he then opened “Hanchic,” a brick-and-mortar restaurant that started with outdoor dining only due to covid restrictions before transitioning inwards, a progression he describes as “completely backwards.” He attributes the success of Hanchic (which was, notably, BYOB) to the community at large, which he feels grateful to for giving a shot at Korean flavors presented in a familiar manner; he considers himself not as someone trying to squeeze into the existing Korean restaurant scene, but more as a middleman hoping to introduce Korean flavors beyond just kbbq or bibimbap to a broader customer base in hopes that they give the “OG mom and pop shops” a try in LA’s Koreatown.
Keywords: food; Koreatown; chef; Hanchic; Korean food; Korean flavors; kbbq; bibimbap; culinary school; Orange County; aunt; Torrance; server; restaurant; dry aging; meal prep; friends; middleman; brick and mortar; mom and pop; pandemic; ecommerce

Legacy Project - Kyungbin Min (Edited)
Kyungbin Min is a chef based in Los Angeles. He was born in Seoul, Korea, and immigrated with his family to SoCal where his aunt lived. He knew from an early age that his calling was in food service: as a child, he grew to love the instant gratification of being a part of dinner service, even if it it meant simply setting up plates at the table (even today, he says that it’s his customers’ positive reviews that “gives him the pump” to keep on going). He worked in restaurants right out of college, and after saving enough money paid his way through culinary school, after which he worked in a variety of kitchens, from fine dining to upscale casual. Though he enjoyed his time in culinary school, he says that it wasn’t necessary for him; the food he creates today, he says, is a mashup of what he learned in the kitchens with his own added flair. During the onset of the pandemic, he and his partners were furloughed, and so they set about starting their own food businesses: one of his partners started a meal prep company, while he and another partner together launched a dry aging meat company; everything, he says, was ecommerce. Soon, he opened “Hanchic,” which to his surprise was picked up by outlets such as Eater, Thrillist, and The Infatuation: what began as an ecommerce business soon turned into an outdoor patio, which then transitioned indoors. He owes the success of his enterprises to his community, which he tries to serve by introducing Korean flavors beyond the typical kbbq or kimbap that many are already familiar with. In acting as a middleman of sorts, he hopes more people will become comfortable ordering food from the “OG mom and pop” restaurants in L.A.’s Koreatown.
Keywords: food; Koreatown; chef; Hanchic; Korean food; Korean flavors; kbbq; bibimbap; culinary school; Orange County; aunt; Torrance; server; restaurant; dry aging; meal prep; friends; middleman; brick and mortar; mom and pop; pandemic; ecommerce

Legacy Project - Laura Park (Full)
Interviewee: Park, Laura
Laura Park was born in Seoul, Korea, in 1964, to parents who made a comfortable living running a fabric store at Gwangjang market near Dongdaemum, one of Seoul’s largest and most well-known traditional markets. A generational business, her family had gotten their start selling fabrics and textiles when her own mother was 18; back then, she says, hanbok was daily attire that people wore everyday, and so business was good. By the time Laura was born, their family’s business had become ingrained into the fabric of the busy market, but Laura herself was unsure if she wanted to continue the family tradition, or if she wanted to go into business at all. She expressed a keen interest and talent for learning languages, and so she studied abroad in Japan, learning Japanese; upon graduation, she found a job working in translation and diplomatic correspondences ahead of the Seoul Olympics of 1988. The itch for business returned, however, and she soon immigrated to Los Angeles where her uncle lived in hopes of opening a successful venture abroad. She quickly found out that English didn’t come as naturally as Japanese, but nevertheless persisted in Koreatown to open her own hanbok store which she called Lee Hwa Korean Traditional Dress Store. She soon started selling wedding dresses as well, changing the store’s name to Lee Hwa Wedding and Traditional Korean Dress. When Korean culture reached global appeal in the mid 2000s, she made one final name change to the store, rebranding it as simply Lee Hwa Wedding and Hanbok (the idea being that “Hanbok” was now ubiquitous outside of Korea as well). During this time she got married and had a daughter who upon finishing college expressed a desire to continue her schooling, but put off graduate studies for eight years to help the family business, a sacrifice Laura doesn’t take lightly. During the pandemic with the sudden cancelation of orders, her daughter helped her in setting up an online webfront for the business, and together they used the fabric reserved for hanbok to make and distribute masks from home. Today, her store has three sections: one for traditional hanbok, one for wedding dresses, and one for hanbok-inspired streetwear, taking care to keep the balance between the different groups of customers her store appeals to.
Keywords: Seoul; Gwangjang; Gwangjang market; Dongdaemun, Eastern Gate; market; traditional market; textiles; fabric; hanbok; traditional Korean dress; Japanese; Olympics; translator; Japanese university; Los Angeles; uncle; business; Lee Hwa Korean Traditional Dress; streetwear; wedding; pandemic; masks; daughter; sacrifice

Legacy Project - Laura Park (Edited)
Interviewee: Park, Laura
Laura Park is a hanbok business owner based in Los Angeles. She was born in 1964 to a family which operated a textile store in Gwangjang Market in Seoul, and recounts a comfortable childhood containing memories of time spent at her parents’ store. She explains how the store had been in operation since before she was born: her mother had begun working in the market when she was 18, and by the time Laura came around, was running a textile business that had become woven into the fabric of Gwangjang. Laura initially expressed an interest in languages, and so worked as a translator for diplomatic correspondences between Korea and Japan ahead of the 1988 Olympics, but realized that her true passions lay in business; with the money earned from working as a translator, she moved to Los Angeles, where her uncle lived. Following in the footsteps of her parents, she opened a hanbok store which she called “Lee Hwa Korean Traditional Dress;” before long, she was selling wedding dresses as well, and so renamed the store to “Lee Hwa Wedding and Korean Traditional Dress.” As more and more non-Koreans became familiar with what a hanbok was, she renamed her store to “Lee Hwa Wedding and Hanbok,” a final DBA change that reflected shifting attitudes toward Korean culture in the United States. Today, the majority of her customers are non-Korean—compared to her clientele being 90% Korean when she first began her business—and her store caters to three styles of hanbok: traditional attire, modernized, and streetwear.
