Legacy Project - OG Chino (Kyumin) Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Kyumin
Kyu Min Lee was born in Seoul, South Korea, during an period of the country’s development when it was beginning to open diplomatic channels with nations. His father, who worked in the Foreign Ministry of the Republic of Korea, was given the choice from the government to temporarily relocate to Bogota, the capital of the state of Colombia, as a part of the South Korean mission to the Latin American country; there, his family was one of a handful of Koreans working to set up a Korean embassy in Bogota. Kyu Min’s family moved to Colombia when he was just 1, and so he grew up speaking both Korean and Spanish; his nickname “Chino” means Chinese in Spanish. When Chino was 10 years old he moved to Los Angeles with his brother and mother, a vibrant city where he felt home both in its Korean and Hispanic communities. Although the family had lived rather comfortably as government employees in Colombia, the comfort did not translate to life in America, and he recounts how his mother had to take up odd jobs here and there to support themselves. After only a year of living with Chino, his mother moved back to Bogota to be with his father, and for the first time in his life, Chino (and his brother) had to learn how to navigate life without their parents. He describes this period of his life as feeling weird, from noticing things such as his mother not being home when he came home from school, to feeling restless when at home; by age 12, he was running with a gang, and getting intro trouble at school. By the time he graduated from high school, he had been kicked out of four schools prior, although during his senior year of high school an art teacher encouraged him to apply to art school; encouraged by this mentor, Chino applied and was accepted to Otis Parsons, which he attended for a year before dropping out. As he was into the growing hip-hop scene at the time, he chose to open a record store (encouraged by his mother) on Slauson, a stretch of town that he describes as not being the safest. He managed his store for two years, during which the store became known as a kind of community center open to restless youth such as himself to kick back, paint, and take a break; afterward, he was reached out to by Rick Rubin, a record producer from New York, who was looking to familiarize himself with the hip hop and record scene in Los Angeles. From there, he started working with artists and music industry management, but before long the music industry changed with the advent of digitization, but craving the age of analog and record tables he took over a former restaurant space in Los Angeles, transforming it into a Colombian-Korean fusion bar replete with a DJ station and a dance floor.
Keywords: Bogota; Colombia; Korean embassy; mother; Los Angeles; Spanish; fusion; Colombian; Chino; high school; gang; gangs; restless; kicked out; dropped out; Otis Parsons; hip hop; hip-hop; Slauson; Ric Rubin; New York; food; bar; DJ; dance

Legacy Project - OG Chino (Kyumin) Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Kyumin
Kyu Min Lee was born in Seoul, South Korea, to a family who worked for the Korean government. When he was only 1 year old, his family moved to Bogota, Colombia, after his father accepted a position to work at the South Korean Embassy, and Kyu Min grew up in Bogota until he was ten. At the time, it was not uncommon for Korean families living in Colombia to send their kids, sometimes accompanied by a relative, abroad for their education. For Kyu Min, his siblings, and his mother, this meant moving to Los Angeles where they soon found out that the economic resources they had in Colombia didn’t translate to living in the United States. His mother had to work multiple jobs to take care of her children in L.A., but soon moved back to Colombia to take care of their father, and for the first time in his life Kyu Min had to learn how to navigate the world without his parents by his side. As a teen, he recounts run-ins with gangs, being kicked out of multiple high schools, and “experiencing everything America had to offer—the good and the bad.” Encouraged by his high school art teacher, he applied, and was accepted to, the Otis School to study art, but dropped out a year later to pursue a career in the music industry, where he worked with a record label for almost 20 years. After the digitization revamped the music world, Kyu Min pivoted to operating a restaurant bar with a space to host various DJs and artists. Although he had little experience with food, he created a menu consisting of Korean-Colombian dishes from his childhood. He remains thankful for how “his life turned out,” while remaining fully aware that there were risks that could have taken him down a very different path.
Keywords: Bogota; Colombia; Korean embassy; mother; Los Angeles; Spanish; fusion; Colombian; Chino; high school; gang; gangs; restless; kicked out; dropped out; Otis Parsons; hip hop; hip-hop; Slauson; Ric Rubin; New York; food; bar; DJ; dance

Legacy Project - Jaesook Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Jaesook
Jaesook Kim was born in Seoul, Korea, in 1948, just two years before the outbreak of the Korean War. Although she was too young to remember in detail the events of the conflict, she talks about how she had an older brother who was drafted to fight in the war who she never saw afterward; it is unclear whether he lost his life, or if he was captured and taken to the North. She describes the Korea she grew up in (in the aftermath of the war) as a barren and destitute place, ravaged by the conflict; her family lived in Seoul, but she describes how they did not live in the “nicer” parts of the city, but in shantytowns near Dongdaemun Gate. Although she didn’t want to leave her family to fend for themselves, her mother convinced her to attend school a ways away from the family in hopes that she might become a teacher. Her mother’s dream came true; after attending primary schools affiliated with a teacher’s college in Seoul, she enrolled into university as an education major and upon graduation taught for twenty years. Soon after graduation, she met her husband who had owned several businesses at the time of their meeting; however, a series of misfortunes, poor investments, and questionable business partners, he lost all of his money, and the couple decided to take a shot at living in the U.S. They first moved to New York, having a connection through her husband’s friend, but Mrs. Kim described it as too chaotic and overwhelming. They then decided to move to Los Angeles where a church friend had moved to, and it was here that she felt that she had returned to her hometown. Because of her English skills, there wasn’t much for her to do, but she tried her hand at managing a snack shop in Downtown L.A., which she found difficult. In time, she started her own restaurant in Monrovia, but found it hard to maintain and closed shop; she then tried one final time to operate a restaurant, this time making Korean food, which was successful for around three years before she closed it due to aching joints. Having worked with kids in education, she wondered if she might be able to work as a babysitter for Korean children (the interview is conducted by a former “charge”); owing to her experience with kids, as well as her time in the food industry, she got along well with the children she babysat, often cooking them Korean food. Mrs. Kim’s husband passed in 2005, and she feels lonely from time to time, but says that she’s had more than enough time to get used to her life now in the States, feeling that the community she has in Los Angeles is great for older Koreans such as herself.
Keywords: Seoul; Korea; shantytown; Korean War; brother; mother; teacher's college; education major; education; school; teacher; teaching; Dongdaemum; New York; church; husband; cooking; restaurant; nanny; babysitting; Los Angeles; interviewee; business; aches

Legacy Project - Jaesook Kim (Edited)
Interviewee: Kim, Jaesook
Jaesook Kim was born in 1948 in Seoul, Korea, just before the Korean War began. Although she was too young to remember the conflict in detail, she remembers how her older brother was drafted into the war, never to return. Despite her family’s impoverished conditions after the war ended, they managed to continue living in Seoul; specifically, Mrs. Kim recalls living in shack near Dongdaemun Gate. Through all of the hardship, her mother desired for her to one day become a teacher, and so sent her to a special elementary school far from home which was affiliated with a teacher’s college—eventually, her mother’s dream came to fruition, and Mrs. Kim taught as a teacher in Korea for 20 years. During this time she met her husband who, after encountering business misfortunes, asked to move to the United States. At first, the couple moved to New York, but Mrs. Kim never quite felt at home in the busy city. They then moved to Los Angeles which she loved, describing it as feeling like her hometown. After experiencing some setbacks while working in the restaurant industry, Mrs. Kim decided to try to find work where she could interact with children, seeing as she’d been a teacher for all those years in Korea, eventually babysitting for children with an appetite for Korean food. Her husband passed in 2005, and she occasionally feels lonely—at times, she longs for Korea as well. Yet feels that living in America has been a blessing as well, and feels lucky to have had this life.

Legacy Project - Nancy Cho (Full)
Interviewee: Cho, Nancy
Nancy Cho was born in North Pyongan Province in what is today North Korea. She spent much of her early childhood there, though she and her family moved to the south when she was 6—this was before the start of the war, but when open borders across the 38th parallel allowed travel between the two occupied states. The Korean War broke out when she was 8 years old, and so she and her family fled from Seoul to Busan, where she remembers a rough train ride to the refugee camp eating rice balls (jumeok-bap) seasoned with a little salt. The family remained in Busan until she completed middle school, after which they moved back to Seoul for Mrs. Cho to attend a high school affiliated with Ewha Univerity. After high school, she enrolled in Ewha University’s education department, and upon graduation met her husband, with whom she had a son. In 1967, her husband relocated to Houston to study for an MBA, and Mrs. Cho and her son followed him in 1968, where she began taking classes to become a certified keypunch operator, graduating and finding work at the Bank of Texas. In the meantime, her husband had finished his studies and begun a position at Houston Natural Gas, by which point their family had grown with another child. She recalls how at the time, there weren’t many Koreans living in Houston (or Texas), and so the family would get their groceries at the local Chinese grocery store; managing finances as students, too, was a challenge. Craving some kind of Korean community, Mrs. Cho was delighted when a church elder pointed them to go to Los Angeles, which had a vibrant Korean cultural scene; after having another child, the family loaded up their station wagon and drove a road trip to Los Angeles from Houston, stopping by sites such as the Grand Canyon along the way. She was amazed to experience just how much Los Angeles felt like Korea; she heard Korean everywhere and was excited to see Korean businesses and food around every corner of Koreatown. She recalls, too, going to a Korean hairstylist for the first time in years and feeling a sense of cathartic relief. Her husband set up a private CPA practice where Mrs. Cho worked at as well, and the family became interwoven into the fabric of the community. When Saigu occurred, she and her husband worked to get Korean businesses get SBA loans to hold onto their businesses. She is convinced that her faith is what allowed her to live the fulfilling life that she did, reminding others that it’s important to keep in mind that there are some things beyond out control, and to worry about those things are detrimental to our wellbeing. Today, she enjoyed watching Korean cooking channels on YouTube, following along to make the foods she wants to each.
Keywords: Pyongang; North Pyongang Province; Pyongang Province; Seoul; Busan; South; Korean War; Middle School; high school; Ewha; education; Texas; Houston; MBA; CPA; SBA; Saigu; chinese; Bank of Texas; keypunch; keypunch operator; Grand Canyon; station wagon; roadtrip; Los Angeles; YouTube; hair; hair stylist; Korean

Legacy Project - Nancy Cho (Edited)
Interviewee: Cho, Nancy
Nancy Cho was born in North Pyongan Province in what is today North Korea, spending her early childhood there. At the age of six, she and her family moved to Seoul, and only two years later had to flee to Busan to escape the violence of the Korean War. She completed middle school in Busan before moving back to Seoul with her family to attend high school, after which she began college at Ewha. After graduation, she met her husband and had their first child, later moving to Houston where her husband had been attending business school. Restless, Ms. Cho studied to become a keypunch operator, passing her licensing examination at the top of her class. In December of 1968 she had their second child, and her husband began working at Houston Natural Gas, though as their family continued to grow, she couldn’t help but notice how barren of Koreans Houston was at the time. Four years after having their third child, the family decided to move to Los Angeles after hearing of its Korean community from a church elder. Living in Koreatown, she remembers thinking that “LA felt like Korea,” from hearing Korean being spoken on the streets to how she could get her hair done at a Korean salon. She worked with her husband at his CPA practice for forty-five years while taking care of their family, and today finds much joy in cooking at home following Korean recipes on YouTube.

Legacy Project - Janet Burt (Full)
Interviewee: Burt, Janet
Janet Burt was born in Seoul, Korea, in 1967. She spent most of her childhood in Korea, but remembers that when she was in elementary school, her parents began the process of applying for visas to live abroad in the United States so that their children (herself, and her siblings) might have access to better education. The visa application process took the family around ten years, so that by the time came for the family to move, her older sister—who, by this point, was almost through with high school—felt that it wasn’t in her interest for the family as a whole to move; Janet herself was approaching the junior year of high school, and had apprehensions about the move as well. Nevertheless, their parents resolved to move the family to Los Angeles, uprooting their life in Korea to join relatives in California. Because of her experience immigrating to the United States “later in life” than Korean Americans whose parents brought them to the States when they were much younger—or Korean Americans who were born on American soil—Janet identifies herself as 1.5th generation Korean American: very much Korean in her private life, but Americanized in her public life, that is, in her workplace and social interactions. Her family continued to speak Korean in the States, and she maintains the language within her own family as well, though she feels already a generational gap occurring within herself. In what she characterizes as a feeling of “losing heritage,” Janet laments how certain traditions, such as the observance of Korean holidays, has been lost. She also states that between raising her family and her workplace responsibilities, visits to Korea have been sparse. Her connection to her heritage, therefore, is rooted in the Koreatown community of Los Angeles, a neighborhood she intentionally avoided until she got a job there working at a law firm. She claims that Koreatown, with all its scars and flaws, exemplifies the spirit of how “community should work,” and wants to encourage the younger generation of Korean Americans in Los Angeles to engage more with their Korean, but also Korean American, roots, a lesson in retaining heritage while still being able to “integrate with the mainstream.” Today, she serves as a board director and the president of many non-profit organizations in the city, and has this to say about Koreatown: everybody’s living in harmony, not to say that everything’s perfect, but what town doesn’t have all those issues?
Keywords: Seoul; Korea; childhood; elementary; visa; long; ten; high school; sister; 1.5; generation; California; law; law firm; fort; relatives; heritage; loss; loss of heritage; holidays; Kroeatown; roots; non-profit; generational gap; public; private

Legacy Project - Janet Burt (Edited)
Interviewee: Burt, Janet
Janet Burt was born in Seoul, Korea, in 1967. When she was a junior in high school, her parents made the decision to move their family to the United States in hopes that their children might have access to better education. After a decade of navigating the hurdles of obtaining American visas, the family immigrated to Los Angeles in 1983, uprooting their life in Korea to join their relatives abroad. Because she moved to America a little later in life, she feels that she straddles the generational gap between Korean and American cultures, identifying as a 1.5th generation Korean American; her family speaks Korean and maintains certain Korean traditions while interacting and working within a larger, multiethnic American sphere. In the time since she’s moved to L.A., she’s gotten married and raised a family of her own; having lived in Los Angeles for over thirty years, she considers it to be her hometown. Today, she serves as the Director of Diversity and Business Development at the largest law firm in Los Angeles and is an active member in a number of community organizations in Koreatown, working to attract the younger generation of Korean Americans to engage more with the neighborhood while its elders hold down the fort.

Lana Yu (Full)
Interviewee: Yu, Lana
Lana Yu was born on March 14th, 1952, in Daegu at the height of the Korean War. The youngest of four children (one older brother, two older sisters), her family had sought refuge in the southeastern city, away from the intense fighting around Seoul, when she was born. In 1957, the family moved back to Seoul from Daegu, and Lana recalls how difficult life was; there was extremely little to eat, and her parents had to restart their family’s lives with little resources at hand; somehow, however, Lana managed to find some joy in singing and music, often performing for guests when her parents’ friends came over (as a child, her dream was to one day become a singer). In 1976, at the age of 23, Lana immigrated to the United States with her two older sisters and her mother; her father had unfortunately passed away. When asked if she ever felt homesick, Lana replies that having her family, as well as ready access to a larger Korean community in Los Angeles, mitigated any feelings of homesickness. This, combined with the fact that for 17 years, Lana lived in Koreatown working a variety of jobs, kept her busy; after 17 years, she moved to Las Vegas to work as a casino dealer for another 23 years, after which she returned to Las Vegas, where she’s been ever since. Lana also reflects on her relationship with her son, who came out as gay to her ~15 years ago. She recounts how she was surprised and shocked—not angry or upset—and how ashamed she felt when he told her that he was under the impression that she had always known. In the time since, Lana talks about seeking out resources in her community, such as literature and organizations such as PFLAG, to find ways to educate both herself and her community on LGBTQ+ issues.
Keywords: Daegu; Korean War; Seoul; Singing; performance; food; Koreatown; Los Angeles; Las Vegas; bank; city hall, women’s apparel; dry cleaners; casino; dealer; PFLAG; LGBT; son; mother

Lana Yu (Edited)
Interviewee: Yu, Lana
Lana Yu was born on March 14th, 1952, in Korea during the middle of the Korean War; after fleeing Seoul for Daegu, she and her family returned to Seoul when she was five years old. She recalls how life after the war was difficult, as food was often scarce, but she found some joy in music and singing. In 1976, she and her family immigrated to LA, where despite initial difficulties with English, Lana worked a variety of jobs including at a bank, the Los Angeles City Hall, and even as a casino dealer in Las Vegas! The most formative experience of her life in the U.S., however, was when her son came out to her; tearing up at the thought that he didn’t feel like he could come to her sooner, she reminds us that “there are way people who understand you than you think,” and encourages all of us to find the people who can support us.
Keywords: Daegu; Korean War; Seoul; Singing; performance; food; Koreatown; Los Angeles; Las Vegas; bank; city hall, women’s apparel; dry cleaners; casino; dealer; PFLAG; LGBT; son; mother

Lori Song (Full)
Interviewee: Song, Lora
Lori Song, 65, was born and raised in Los Angeles to Korean and Japanese parents who arrived in L.A. from Hawaii in the 1950s. he has a twin brother who looks just like her, and a younger brother as well; with regards to her mixed heritage, she describes herself as a “fourth generation person on both sides.” Lori talks about how during her youth, she would feel most comfortable when they wore women’s clothing; back then, she considered herself to be a cross-dresser, though over the years her gender identity has become female.  She recalls, too, how difficult her teenage years were (though she states that “teenage years are hard, no matter the era); eventually, Lori decided to give one last shot in “trying to be a man,” but knew, deep down, that it wasn’t who she really was. She tells us of a particularly poignant memory from her young adulthood where after she returned home early from work one day—before their wife—they secretly dressed “from head to toe” in women’s clothing, after which she felt a natural sense of relief. Although they have since separated from their former partner, neither hold ill will towards the other. Slowly, she began coming out to her friends and family, and although some responses have been more lukewarm than others, she feels joy in how today’s younger generation are accepting of her as who she is. Recently, Lori began hormone therapy, and although she says that changes have been slow because she started later in life, she encourages us to “be brave enough to just do your thing,” no matter how young or old we might be. For her part, at this stage in her life, Lori tells us that as an Asian American transgender person it feels as though all the people who helped her feel true to herself were in a way preparing her to help those who’d come after her.
Keywords: Los Angeles; Korean; Japanese; Hawaii; California; twin; fourth-generation; teenager; HRT; cross-dressing; marriage; partner; transgender; LGBTQ; relief

Jamie Issuh (Full)
Interviewee: Issuh, Jamie
Jamie Issuh was born in 1991 in Champaign, IL, where she spent her early childhood; when she was around 7 years old, her family briefly moved to Korea for a year, then to Tennessee, before settling down in Irvine, California. She recounts about how it was first through crushes that she began to understand her queer identity, though she says too that it’s one thing to be an ally, and another thing entirely to recognize oneself as queer. As a queer Korean American Renaissance woman, she talks about grappling with parts of her identity which felt incongruous with one another—throughout her 20s, she grappled with finding spaces where her Korean heritage and her queer identity could coexist, because for most of her life she had never seen her ethnic heritage and her gender identity occupy the same space; she did come out to her parents, only for them to not be particularly accepting of her. She joined @queerasiansocialclub, a social collective focused on empowering the queer and trans Asian American community, in search of a place which could mediate her identities; today, she serves QASC in the capacity of Creative Director. After moving to Koreatown two years ago, she joined a Korean dance group comprised mostly of imo’s (aunties) her mother’s age; though she is still finding the courage to be fully out, she has let herself be surprised at the kindness and grace which the imo’s have extended to her and her friends from QASC.
Keywords: Champaign; Illinois; Korea; Tennessee; Irvine; California; childhood; heritage; gender; queer; collective; LGBTQ; Renaissance; queerasiansocialclub; QASC; Dance; imo

Vida Marie Adams (Full)
Interviewee: Adams, Vida Marie
Vida Marie Misook Adams was born in 1977 in Los Angeles, California, to a Korean mother and a Black father.; she self-identifies as a second-generation “Korean Blasian,” taking pride in both branches of her family tree. Vida grew up in the neighborhood which would soon become Koreatown, L.A., until she was 6 years old, though her family moved to the Valley (Canoga Park) after an encounter with an armed robber. She remembers how, when she left “Koreatown,” the neighborhood’s Korean culture was just about to set in. As an only child of mixed heritage, she recalls how she felt more of a familiarity with her dad’s side of the family, with her mom’s side often making underhanded comments to her. In retelling one memory from her childhood, she describes how her mother’s family would try to “keep up appearances” and pretend that neither Vida nor her father were related to their family at all, going as far as to exclude her from family gatherings. Vida tells us that in family photos hung around her grandmother’s home, she was in none of them, an overt yet unspoken racism which permeated through the family. Yet at the same time, Vida has maintained a strong and profoundly intimate connection with Koreatown, the neighborhood she calls home and where she attributes the happiest days of her life to. Out of every place she’s ever lived in, she shares how it was in Koreatown that she felt like she “had the right to belong,” taking pride in the community’s history, its staples, and expressing an acute sadness at the fact that it has changed so much since her childhood. Whether one is marginalized on the basis of their ethnicity or gender, Vida feels strongly that “nobody gets to take that [community] away from you. Today, she is heavily involved with Korean advocacy work through the organization “Koreans United for Equality,” or KUE, a group which works to destigmatize LGBTQ-identifying Koreans within the Korean American community,
Keywords: Los Angeles; California; Koreatown; mixed heritage; Blasian; Black; second-generation; racism; homophonia; robbery; Canoga Park; photos; melancholy; Koreans United for Equality; KUE; LGBTQ

Lily Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Lily
Lily Kim was born in 1972 in Suwon, South Korea, as the youngest of four sisters, to parents who worked in healthcare. Growing up, she recalls having been a rather talkative child who took a keen interest in other people, which she attributes to living in a household with three older siblings. When she was 24 years old, she moved to Los Angeles by herself to study, though she soon found a job as a reporter for Radio Korea (she was qualified for the position, but the special quality that gave her the competitive edge was her familiarity with L.A.’s streets). She recalls a conversation she had with a reporter who had been working at the station for a while when she joined the team, saying how he told her that reporting, as a vocation, was a job not only protected by the constitution, but a mandate to extend protections to the rest of the community by writing about injustices which occur everyday. She tells us of certain differences between Korean American media and mainstream media, with the former needing to fulfill journalistic, outreach, educational, and advocacy roles at times. She also describes Korean American media as having a hyperlocal focus, with stories on immigration and public safety catering to the specific needs of Koreatown. All the while, Lily says that there were moments when working for Radio Korea and (currently) The Korea Times that disgruntled individuals would contact her office to complain about stories whose subject matter they did not agree with, such as when she wrote a piece on a Korean American soldier who had come out as gay to his community. As a reporter, Lily has seen how effective journalism produced real and meaningful changes in her community, from the establishment of a police station after the Saigu Uprising to the redistricting of Koreatown so that it could vote as one bloc. Her favorite story from the field, however, is when after she covered a piece on a robbed toy drive, donations from the community poured in to ensure the drive could happen by Christmas.
Keywords: Suwon; Los Angeles; Koreatown; California; college; reporting; reporter; journalism; Radio Korea; Korea Times; police; LGBTQ; immigration; hyperlocal; sisters; advocacy; mainstream media; soldier

Judy Han & Jennifer Chun (Full)
Interviewee: Han, Judy
Judy hui Judy Han (she/they), 51, and Jennifer Jihye Chun (she/her), 50, were born in Seoul, Korea. Judy grew up in Seoul until they were 12 years old, after which their family immigrated to Northern California; Jennifer mostly grew up in Chicago and the surrounding areas. Both Judy and Jennifer grew up with two siblings, and both of their fathers worked in healthcare. Judy recalls how their childhood was filled with books, newspapers, pamphlets, anything that had lettering on it. By contrast, Jennifer preferred to spend the majority of her free time with her friends, even though she had a talent for literature; she attributes “hiding” her academic capabilities as a kind of silent protest against the stigma and stereotypes around school which followed her throughout her childhood. These types of self-denials, stemming from internalized racism and homophobia, would later influence Judy and Jennifer’s activism. After meeting in graduate school, they entered a relationship which was at first kept hidden from each other’s parents, though after a number of years, both parents came around, and fully embraced, the love shared between them. They also talk about their individual experiences grappling with Korean and queer identity, and the complicated interstitial space they’ve had to navigate in reconciling them. For Jennifer, “Korean American” was something she grappled with later in life; she recounts how in her childhood, perhaps owing to the socialization (and objectification) of Asian girls, she was more concerned with her gender. In college, she found the language and space to reflect on her gender identity, a process which she says is ongoing to this day. For Judy, the lines between Korean and Korean American, gender and gender identity were always blurred; everything was so emmeshed with one another. They talk about how they knew they were queer from an early age, but waited until college to express their identity for fear of worrying their parents. They sensed that their being queer would oftentimes disrupt Korean spaces, whether the space be church, family, or with others in their community. Though they’ve never wanted to make others uncomfortable, at a certain point Judy realized that other it was on others to put up with them, not the other way around. Both Judy and Jennifer acknowledge that there are “tensions which arise by accident” by being queer and Korean, but that they believe this discomfort can result in change for the better.
Keywords: Seoul, California, gender, parents, relationship, coming out, literature, reading; identity; incongruous; college; church; community; queer; Korean; discomfort

Chown Soo Cho (Full)
Interviewee: Cho, Chown Soon
Chown Soon Cho was born in Bugok-ri, Gochang-gun, North Jeolla Province, in the midst of the Japanese occupation, around 1937-38. When she was three years old, she was “given” by her parents to be adopted by her eldest aunt, who had been unable to bear children. This was a common practice at the time, and it wasn’t until 10 years later (after liberation) that she learned of this adoption from her grandmother. She recalls feelings of hurt and betrayal against her “aunt”—her biological mother—having always wondered why she was treated so well by her. Soon thereafter, the Korean War broke out on the Peninsula, and Chown Soon stayed at home in Gochang-gun with her grandparents. Her village was invaded by the North Korean army and held hostage, and she recollects the horrors she saw and experienced at the hands of the invasion force. Her uncle, who had been a resistance fighter during the occupation, wanted to flee to Manchuria, but stayed with her grandparents as he was the eldest child. His anticommunist beliefs were exposed to the North Korean army, however, and Chown Soon recalls how his decision to stay by his parents ended up in his imprisonment, and later execution. After the war’s end, Chown Soon married her husband, who was a teacher, and would have eight children for whom she thanks God. On Christmas Even of 1984, she and her husband immigrated, in middle age, to the United States, slowly inviting their kids one by one to join them. Today, she finds the greatest joy in being beside her children and grandchildren, asking for little more.
Keywords: Bugok-ri; Gochang-gun; North Jeolla Province; Occupation; resistance; independence; resistance fighter; grandparents; adoption; North Korea; invasion; Manchuria; Christmas Eve; daughters; grandchildren; betrayal; partisans

Chown Soo Cho (Edited)
Interviewee: Cho, Chown Soon
Chown Soon Cho was born in Bugok-ri, Gochang-gun, North Jeolla Province, during the Japanese occupation. When she was three years old, her mother gave her to her eldest aunt to raise as her own, a common practice at the time. It wasn’t until she was ten years old that her grandmother revealed that she had been adopted by her relative, a surprise which, at the time, developed into feelings of betrayal. Soon after, the Korean War forced Mrs. Cho to shelter with her grandparents, rather than seeking refuge elsewhere. She recalls seeing the North Korean enter her village, as well as the traumatic experience of being held hostage by them. After the war concluded, she married and had eight children before immigrating to the United States in 1984, with her children following her later. Although her life has been filled with moments of profound sorrow, she tells us that today, she finds joy in her children and grandchildren, asking for little more than their own happiness.
Keywords: Bugok-ri; Gochang-gun; North Jeolla Province; Occupation; resistance; independence; resistance fighter; grandparents; adoption; North Korea; invasion; Manchuria; Christmas Eve; daughters; grandchildren; betrayal; partisans

Myung Hee Jung (Full)
Interviewee: Jung, Myung Hee
Myung Hee Jung was born in Yeonggwang County, North Jeolla Province, as the second youngest of eight children. She had five older brothers, an older sister, and a younger sister, with whom she shares a close connection to this day owing to how she often had to take care of her. She recalls fond memories of her older brother getting married when she was 13 years old, though it was around then that her mother suddenly moved to the United States by herself. Though she has since come to peace with her mother’s sudden relocation, she tells us that at the time she didn’t understand why her mother had to leave their family so abruptly; if anything, Myung Hee describes that though she wanted to be a recipient of her family’s love, she just figured that it wasn’t yet “her time” to be on the receiving end of familial concern. After graduating from high school, Myung Hee moved to Seoul where he older sister lived with the dream of attending college, though her aunt preferred that she learn a trade skill so that she could support herself while living in the metropole. As such, she was enrolled into a “computer academy” (by night, however, she would take classes through the local “broadcast university” which disseminated lessons and lectures by radio). She was able to take classes in this way while working as an elementary school teacher, though because her mother was under the impression that she was not in college, she asked Myung Hee to join her in the United States where she might get married and start a family of her own. She arrived in America around 1989-90 without having finished the broadcast university’s curriculum, and married in 1993; even while pregnant, she continued to dream of attending university. While she reminisces on past dreams of education, she expresses how loved she feels by her family here in America, and that she feels as though she has “done right” by her younger sister. She asks of her own children to “live well with others.”
Keywords: Yeonggwang; North Jeolla; marriage; mother; college; university; broadcast; radio; broadcast university; computer academy; trade; teacher; elementary school; 1989; younger sister; older brother; Seoul; aunt; daughter; dream; sacrifice

Myung Hee Jung (Edited)
Interviewee: Jung, Myung Hee
Myung Hee Jung was born in Yeonggwang county, North Jeolla Province, as the second youngest among eight children: she had five older brothers, an older sister, and a younger sister. When she was in eighth grade, her mother moved to the United States by herself for reasons Myung Hee didn’t yet understand, though this meant that she had to become the primary caretaker for her younger sister. After graduating from high school, Myung Hee moved to Seoul where her older sister lived with the dream of attending college; by day, she would work as an elementary school teacher, and by night, she would study and take classes taught at a broadcast university. At her mother’s urging, Myung Hee moved to America around 1989-90 so that she might get married. While she reminisces on the past dreams of a college education, Myung Hee says that if she were given the choice between college or the family she has today, she would choose the latter in a heartbeat.
Keywords: Yeonggwang; North Jeolla; marriage; mother; college; university; broadcast; radio; broadcast university; computer academy; trade; teacher; elementary school; 1989; younger sister; older brother; Seoul; aunt; daughter; dream; sacrifice

Jung Ja Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Jung Ja
Jung Ja Lee, who sometimes goes by “JJ,” was born in Seoul in 1945, growing up up with three older sisters, an older brother, and a younger brother. When the Korean War broke out, she remembers how her uncle unexpectedly arrived at her home with his own family, warning them that it was time for them to head south. Their family sought refuge at Pyeongtaek city, located in the south of Gyeonggi Province, a journey which took 9 days of walking by foot on country roads to avoid running into soldiers. Upon returning to their home in Seoul, Mrs. Lee found that while her home was intact, their furniture had been stripped of its drawers, presumably by individuals who were unable to flee Seoul but needed fires to burn. Later in life, she operated a Baskin Robbins in Toronto, Canada, with her husband, and she continues to call Canada her home. When asked by her daughter, Vivian Lee, if she has a message she’d like to tell her grandchildren, Mrs. Lee says that she wishes to spend more time with them… and that she is a confidential bearer of their secrets!
Keywords: Toronto; Canada; Seoul; Korean War; Pyeongtaek; refugee; ice cream; baskin robbins; military; troops; war crime; furniture; grandchildren; Vivian Lee; news; anchor; New York; secrets

Jung Ja Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Jung Ja
Jung Ja Lee, who sometimes goes by “JJ,” was born in Seoul in 1945. She grew up with three older sisters, and older brother, and a young brother, and recalls being doted on as the family’s youngest daughter. When she was five years old, the Korean War began which pushed her family to seek refuge outside of the big city, in Pyeongtaek. Right before the family fled, her uncle had arrived at her home with his family, urging them to go south. Unbeknownst to Jung Ja or her siblings, her parents had already packed the family’s belongings so that they could leave at a moment’s notice. For nine days, the family walked day and night towards Pyeongtaek by way of country roads, taking care to avoid American, South Korean, and North Korean troops on the main road; they had heard of wartime atrocities committed by all sides, and feared they might be targeted. After the war concluded, her family returned to their home to find the home intact, though the inside had been ransacked; notably, wooden furnishings had been taken, though her family reasoned that they had likely been used as kindling by those unable to flee the city during wintertime. In time, Jung Ja met her husband with whom she immigrated to Toronto, Canada, where the two operated a Baskin Robbins. She resides in Canada to this day, occasionally visiting her daughter in New York (though because of her daughter’s demanding schedule, she laments how each visit doesn’t feel like a trip as it does a business trip). She enjoys spending time with her grandchildren, who she spoils; when asked by her daughter, the news correspondent Vivian Lee, if she’d like to send her grandchildren a message, she tells us that she’d like to spend more time with them, and that she’s a proud bearer of her grandchildren’s secrets!
Keywords: Toronto; Canada; Seoul; Korean War; Pyeongtaek; refugee; ice cream; baskin robbins; military; troops; war crime; furniture; grandchildren; Vivian Lee; news; anchor; New York; secrets

Milton Washington (Full)
Interviewee: Washington, Milton
Milton Washington, formerly known as Milton Park, is a 52-year-old resident of Harlem, New York, with a deeply moving life story that bridges continents, cultures, and personal transformations. Born in a small Korean village near Incheon, Milton's earliest memories involve feeling isolated due to his mixed Black and Korean heritage. His mother, a Korean woman who worked as a prostitute in South Korean military camptowns, faced severe societal backlash, which also fell upon Milton. This rejection rooted in his skin color forced them to move from their village to Dongducheon, closer to a U.S. military base. Throughout his early childhood, Milton grappled with his identity and the absence of his father, who his mother said was in America. This ignited a dream within young Milton to go to America and reunite his parents. However, life grew more challenging when, at six years old, he and his mother were ousted from their village due to racial prejudice. They relocated near a red light district where his mother transformed herself to appeal to Black men at a club she ran, only to face periodic arrests. Despite these hardships, Milton's mother's love for him was unquestionable. At eight years old, feeling overwhelmed and believing Milton was ready to face the world, his mother placed him in an orphanage. It was there that Milton met a Black family from America who came to adopt another child. In a bold move, Milton ran into their car and effectively chose his new family, who then adopted him. Milton moved to the U.S., where he lived in various towns, including a military town in Oklahoma and later Indianapolis. He excelled as a star athlete, playing Division 1 football, though his educational journey was tumultuous, leading him through multiple universities and even a stint in Cook County Jail. At 25, he realized the need to turn his life around. By 29, he graduated from Indiana University with a major in Business and a specialization in Information Technology. In 2002, Milton relocated to New York and founded ROKMIL, a name symbolizing the "Republic of Korea" and "Milton." His ventures include ROKMIL Fitness System, offering strength training, cardio, and flexibility training, a cannabis business to support clients’ wellness, and ROKMIL Studios for photography. Today, Milton is reconnecting with his past, including his 13-year-old son Miles, whom he hadn’t seen for eight years until a recent conversation. This reunion marks a hopeful phase in improving relationships with his family and reflects Milton's ongoing journey of resilience and identity across the landscapes of his turbulent yet inspiring life.
Keywords: US military; military bases; prostitution; red light district; racism; racial prejudice; biracial; multiracial; adoptee; adoption; orphanage; Oklahoma; Indianapolis; Harlem, New York; University of Indiana; family; adopted family; cannabis; entrepreneur; Black; African American; Korean;

Milton Washington (Edited)
Interviewee: Washington, Milton
Milton Washington, now residing in Harlem, New York, carries a poignant story of resilience and identity, starting with his early life in South Korea. Born to a Korean mother who worked in South Korean military camptowns, Milton faced rejection not only for being mixed Black and Korean but for being the child of only one parent. The rejection forced the mother and son to move from their village near Incheon to Dongducheon. Years later, Milton was eight years old and found himself living at an adoption agency after his mother could no longer take care of him. One day, a car pulled up, and a Black family from America stepped out to adopt another child. Milton ran into the vehicle belonging to the family and refused to leave. Feeling an instant connection, this family adopted Milton. As an adult, Milton reflects on the geopolitical forces that impacted his mother's life and other people's heartbreaking and inspiring stories to persist and live.

Jacky Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Jacky
Jacky Lee, who also goes by Stefanie Blandon, carries a complex and poignant story marked by transitions, identity struggles, and eventual self-acceptance. Born in 1957 in Incheon, Korea, Jacky’s first memories are vividly tied to her birth family, specifically the birth of her younger sister. Her life took a drastic turn when, after the last poignant memory of her mother giving her pastries, she found herself at the Seoul City Orphanage and later at the World Vision Orphanage. Her childhood trajectory shifted again when she and her sister were adopted by a U.S. Air Force couple and moved to a military base in Japan. There, Jacky found a sense of belonging and enjoyed her life amidst a new family dynamic, which included an older adoptee brother. The family's later move to California introduced new challenges. Her adoptive parents started a church, and her mother became a minister. In the U.S., Jacky encountered stark reminders of her mixed heritage, facing identity conflicts exacerbated by peers who pointed out her Asian genetics. She struggled with her identity, desiring to fully embrace her Black heritage while feeling forced to hide her Asian roots—a sentiment that persisted until a transformative conversation with her friend Milton enlightened her about her adoptive parents' intentions of giving her a "brand new life." It wasn't until her 30s, during a chance encounter in a beauty store, that Jacky began reconnecting with her Korean heritage. This reconnection deepened in her 40s and 50s, culminating in a trip back to Korea in 2014, where she sought to reconcile with her birth mother's memory, and again in 2017. These visits were transformative, leading her to prioritize her Korean identity as a tribute to her mother and heritage. Jacky’s personal life in the U.S. saw her marrying young and later divorcing, a journey marked by rebuilding her life and seeking new relationships. She became actively involved in the Korean adoptee and mixed Korean communities online, embracing her Korean culture more openly, thanks in part to her son who encouraged her cultural exploration by gifting her a Korean book at the age of 12. Her modeling career took off after being scouted for Refinery29’s disrupt ageism campaign, casting her often in roles that highlighted her Asian identity, which she grew to enjoy. Today, Jacky is a proud grandmother of two, through her son who is married to a Filipino woman. Despite a history marred by abuse and turmoil, including suffering at the hands of her adoptive father and a pastor, Jacky stands resilient. Her life, from the depths of despair to the heights of self-discovery and acceptance, illustrates a vivid tapestry of survival, legacy, and the complex interplay of racial and cultural identities.
Keywords: multiracial; biracial; adopted; adoptee; US military; religion; Christianity; sexual abuse; physical abuse; suicide; California; New York; modeling; ageism; South Korea; orphanage;  identity; self-discovery; Black; African American; Korean;

Jacky Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Jacky
Jacky Lee was born in Incheon, South Korea, in 1957. She recounts her early childhood memories living in Korea with her mother and younger sister, recollecting in vivid detail specific moments spent with family while noting that many of those earliest memories are becoming harder and harder to remember. When Jacky was five years old, she and her younger sister were adopted by an American couple who were stationed in Japan while serving in the Air Force. Though living in Japan presented its own set of challenges—she had to learn a new language, for one—Jacky describes feeling a particular bond with her adoptive mother owing to their skin color, and overall recollects her time in Japan fondly. After the family’s period of service was over, they relocated to California where her parents started a church; it was in America that Jacky felt for the first time a conflict in identity, where she was bullied by the other children at church for her appearance. Resentful that her parents were unwilling to talk about her past in Korea and her biracial heritage, Jacky talks about the confusion and shame that accompanied questions she had for herself. In her thirties, however, while visiting a Korean beauty supply store, a worker recognized her as being Korean; for the first time in her life, she felt able to slowly reclaim bits of her Korean identity. In 2014 she visited Korea with a group of other biracial Koreans, where she rediscovered a love for the land of her birth mother. Jacky went back to Korea in 2017 to nurture this connection, and ever since she’s identified herself as Korean, Black, and proud.
Keywords: multiracial; biracial; adopted; adoptee; US military; religion; Christianity; sexual abuse; physical abuse; suicide; California; New York; modeling; ageism; South Korea; orphanage;  identity; self-discovery; Black; African American; Korean;

Anthony Hull (Full)
Interviewee: Hull, Anthony
Anthony Hull, a 36-year-old native of Mount Holly, New Jersey, has navigated a life rich in cultural intersections and familial bonds shaped by his military upbringing. Born near a military base where his father served as a mechanic, Anthony's early environment was both diverse and transient, offering him a broad view of community and identity from a young age. The diversity was something Anthony found cool, particularly as he saw many kids who looked like him. Anthony’s childhood was marked by frequent relocations—from second to sixth grade, he moved every year but remained sociable and adaptable, traits that helped him forge friendships easily, including a significant one in fourth grade with a Korean boy from Seoul. This friendship notably deepened his connection to his Korean heritage. As his father transitioned from military life to a civilian role as a truck driver, the family settled in Arizona. During this time, Anthony began to confront more pointed observations about his identity, particularly during his adolescent years. However, he maintained a strong sense of self, largely unshaken by external opinions. College presented a new set of challenges and insights as Anthony found himself among predominantly affluent, white peers. Despite the stark contrast in backgrounds, Anthony's identity as a Korean, influenced significantly by his charismatic and protective mother, remained central to his self-perception. His mother, a Korean immigrant who met Anthony's father while working on a military base, has been a pivotal figure in his life, providing both security and stories of her own experiences, which Anthony cherishes and documents. Today, Anthony resides in New York City, where he pursues his passion for storytelling through TV, film, and photography. His work aims to explore and share the nuanced narratives of identity he has lived through. Despite the familial challenges—his mother has not returned to Korea since 1988 due to a painful rejection based on racial prejudice, and his father's family also showed hostility—Anthony's immediate family remains tightly knit. He, his brother, and his parents have navigated these complexities together, forming a resilient unit isolated from their extended families. Anthony's journey reflects a profound engagement with his dual heritage, viewing himself as fully Black and fully Korean, a stance that enriches his creative expression and personal resilience. His life story, marked by movement, adaptation, and artistic pursuit, continues to evolve as he crafts his narrative and fosters connections that transcend traditional racial and cultural boundaries.
Keywords: US military; South Korea; military kid; relocation; truck driver; dual identity; biracial; multiracial; African American; Black; Korean; New York City; film; filmmaker; photography; storytelling;

Anthony Hull (Edited)
Interviewee: Hull, Anthony
Anthony Hull was born in Mount Holly, New Jersey, to a Korean mother and a Black father who worked as an army mechanic. As a child, he remembers growing up in a relatively diverse community with friends who were Asian, or had Asian parents; it was in the fourth grade, when he befriended a transfer student from Korea, that Anthony began to feel a sense of pride and solidarity in his own Korean identity. In college, he remembers struggling to “half” identify with his dual heritage, feeling alienated from in-groups who didn’t see him “Black enough” or “Korean enough:” then and today, he feels that he is both 100% Black and 100% Korean. Following graduation, Anthony moved to New York to pursue a career in acting and filmmaking, where he now has his own production company to tell the stories he’s always wanted to share.

Alexander Chee (Full)
Interviewee: Chee, Alexander
Alexander Chee (he/him) is a bestselling author, essayist, and Professor of English and Creative Writing at Dartmouth College. Born in South Kingston, Rhode Island, to a white American mother and Korean father, his story is one armed with humor and wit; from the way he describes his toddler self as a “financial crisis” (he was “unplanned”) to the way he extracts from the euphemized “How did your parents meet?” the sinister and underlying “Why do you exist?”, Chee articulates with particular sharpness the formative experiences and conditions which made him into the renowned storyteller he’s recognized as today. As a child, he moved around in the Pacific, following his family’s business in fisheries, before the family settled in Maine where he was the target of racism from his peers, to the alarm of his parents. He understood how, for being their child, he was also not like either of them; it wasn’t until 1989 when he moved to San Francisco that he not only met another gay Asian man (he had known only one prior to moving), but other gay men who were mixed-race Asian like himself. Having moved to San Francisco to follow community—community not unlike the one he found as an undergraduate at Wesleyan—he was startled to experience how the community in the Bay was so large that it could “balkanize”: there were bars which excluded women, for example, and bars which historically excluded Asian men, though he did find within such an expansive community his own people. With pleasure as his guide, Chee reminds us that political work isn’t limited to “demonstrations, policy work, and mutual aid”; just as important are “going out dancing with friends, jumping on a stage for a favorite song, and just being goofy.”
Keywords: NYTimes; author; essayist; books; professor; dartmouth; writing; Rhode Island; South Kingston; humor; funny; fisheries; pacific; storyteller; San Francisco; dance; fetishization; Maine; demonstrations; racism

Alexander Chee (Edited)
Interviewee: Chee, Alexander
Alexander Chee (he/him) is a bestselling author, essayist, and Professor of English and Creative Writing at Dartmouth College. Born in South Kingstown, Rhode Island, to a white American mother and Korean father, Chee's journey is a tale woven with humor and wit. Describing his childhood self as a “financial crisis” due to being unplanned, he moved around the Pacific with his family’s fisheries business before settling in Maine, where he encountered racism from his peers. It wasn’t until he moved to San Francisco in 1989 that he found a community. There, he not only met another gay Asian man for the first time, but also others who shared his mixed-race background. However, he was surprised to discover that even within this vibrant community, there were divisions; some gay bars excluded women, while others historically excluded Asian men. Through his experiences, Chee emphasizes that political work extends beyond demonstrations and policy-making. It's also about finding joy and connection in everyday moments like "going out dancing with friends, jumping on a stage for a favorite song, or just being goofy." His journey underscores the importance of finding spaces where you belong, even within the complexities of identity and community.

Mudang Sung Park (Full)
Interviewee: Park, Sung
Mudang Sung Park (he/him) was born in Seoul, Korea, and immigrated to the Ridgewood/Bushwick area with his family when he was young. Growing up, they were the only Korean family—or East Asian family, for that matter—for blocks around, and although Sung tried his best to reproduce the joys of his Korean childhood from within New York, his new environment slowly changed aspects of his personality. Encountering racism as a child, he became withdrawn to the point of not wanting to interact with others and was disappointed that the racism followed him into college, where he was battered with microaggressions ranging from compliments to his English, to students touching his soft hair. He attended a Seven Sister, and recalls how a professor of his once asked if he’d be willing to meet his child, who was a Korean adoptee, a request he found strange and rejected. In time, Sung lost his Brooklyn accent while in college, because his peers would question whether the manner in which he spoke was what it was like to sound “Korean.” It was in college, too, that Sung came out. He always knew that he was born in the wrong body, but being “queer” was something he attributed to whiteness. And there was another complication to his gender: like many Korean Americans, Sung grew up in the church, and came from a family of ministers. After college, he went to seminary, where he obtained a divinity degree, and was under care at a church to be ordained when his father suddenly passed. He wanted to honor him, but because his family had stopped practicing traditional rites long ago, didn’t know how to, and left ministry, having grown resentful of the ways in which the church prohibited indigenous practices in Korea. Wanting to relearn the practices of his spiritual heritage, he rejected an offer to work in ministry, and sought out a mudang to mentor him on museok, and was surprised to learn that he came from a lineage of not only ministers, but mudangs as well. He doesn’t know of other transgender men who are mudang, and isn’t sure if there’s a significance to his gender and his spirituality, but he feels that there must be one, as coming into the Museok tradition has felt like a second homecoming into the queer Korean American community. Looking back, he describes how it was his friends who helped him through this process, both queer and ally. His best friend, who he met in college, invited him over for Thanksgiving dinner with her Korean American family, where he was surprised and touched that they were speaking in gender-affirming language around him. He also describes how his male friends have shown it what being a trans man can be; just because one is “male,” he realized, didn’t mean they needed to act in an emotionally repressed manner, rather, he has seen how his male friends are outwardly affectionate toward their loved ones.
Keywords: Seoul; Brooklyn; Seven Sister; English; racism; hair; church; ministry; seminary; Muism; Museok; indigenous; Mudang; spirituality; father; honor

Andrea Marra (Full)
Interviewee: Marra, Andrea Hong
Andrea Hong Marra (she/her), who often goes by Andy, is a trans activist and executive director of the Transgender Legal Defense and Education Fund (TLDEF). From an early age, she knew who she was as a Korean American person, and as a person of color; adopted into a white family and growing up in a racially diverse neighborhood, she was never surprised to see people who looked like her, or didn’t look like, herself. She recalls how her parents raised her to be in touch with their Korean heritage, having access to community resources which promoted that aspect of her identity within her. Yet there weren’t as many resources available to parents and families about what it means to be transgender, and for Andy this meant that a long journey was in store between her and her parents as she shared with them how her queer identity wasn’t something to be scared of, but something to be treated with care and love. With a commitment to service and leadership instilled in her by her parents, she began volunteering at LGBTQ organizations in and around her hometown, realizing that a life of service and advocacy was what she wanted to do after college, work she’s dedicated the past couple decades to. She reminds us that, as an activist, you often don’t think about ourselves, pivoting to work instead, when the spaces you work so hard to create are just as much yours, too, as a member of the community. She compels us to leave behind our stories so that those who come after might know that they’re not alone, and that leaving evidence behind is an act of defiance in and of itself, a flag in the ground to say we won’t be erased.
Keywords: activist; Texas; Transgender Legal Defense and Education Fund; TLDEF; adoption; adoptee; heritage; transgender; LGBTQ; college; diversity; defiance

Sulkiro Song (Full)
Interviewee: Song, Sulkiro
Sulkiro Song (she/they) is a queer, asexual, and aromantic pastor based in New York. They were born in Seoul, Korea, to a family of ministers: their grandfather was a pastor, and their father was in pastoral training. For the young Sulkiro, the choice couldn’t be more clear: carry on the family line and become a pastor as well. It came as a shock when, at the age of five, their grandfather told them that “women cannot be pastors!” their first encounter with the idea that female-bodied people should not enter ministry. For a long time, Sulkiro had a difficult time identifying with the word “queer”: there weren’t many Asians around them who visibly identified with it, and there seemed to be a certain aesthetic or signaling that they thought they had, yet was always somehow misinterpreted. They had known since middle school that they were asexual, but were teased for it by their peers; it wasn’t until much later on, when they encountered for the first time the ace flag during a pride march, that they felt accepted by the larger queer community. They did end up studying at seminary, not with the idea of becoming a preacher in mind, but strictly to study religion academically—as they put it, “ridiculously expensive therapy”—a way to help themselves through deep religious trauma. Although they didn’t take courses in preaching or pastoral care, they felt comfortable sharing what they were learning in seminary with the rest of their congregation without downplaying anything; today, Sulkiro’s sermons encompass queer, feminist, and anti-imperialist themes.
Keywords: queer; ace; asexual; aromatic; aro; pastor; ministry; seminary; Virginia; New York; Seoul; Union Theological Seminary; UTS; HAN UMC; sermons; imperialist; anti-imperialist; inclusivity; justice; cafe; barista; North Carolina

However, at age five, their grandfather declared, "women cannot be pastors!"—their first encounter with gender discrimination in ministry. Struggling to identify with the word "queer" and navigating their sexuality, Sulkiro felt misunderstood and out of place. Although they didn’t take courses in preaching or pastoral care, they felt comfortable sharing what they were learning in seminary with their congregation. Today, serving as the pastor of HAN UMC, Sulkiro's sermons encompass queer, feminist, and anti-imperialist themes, reflecting their commitment to inclusivity and justice.

Ellie Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Ellie
Ellie Kim (she/her), stage name SuperKnova, is a queer singer and pop musician based in New York City. Her relationship with music began early, when her parents—like many Korean parents—enrolled her in piano lessons as a child. Though her enthusiasm for the piano waned, she took a deep and lifelong interest in the guitar (her family had an old one lying around), which she picked up after quitting piano lessons. Her passion for music continued into college, where she pursued Jazz Studies. While she’d always known since she as a child that she’d been born into the wrong body, it wasn’t until college that she heard the topic of gender and queerness being openly discussed, and a year or two after graduation that she came out. Seeing a lack of Asian American musicians in the mainstream—and queer Asian American musicians even less—Ellie initially chose the “safe route” of medical school, during which she took a leave of absence to continue making music before completing her studies. After medical school, she decided to pursue music full-time as SuperKnova, which served both as a vehicle to process her emotions and as a means to fully espouse her identity on stage. Although her parents have not been fully accepting of her, she has found within the Korean American community a diverse group of people, genderfluid and cis, who have been supportive of her identity and endeavors as an artist. Her advice for the next generation? “Don’t listen to me! I’m just excited for gen Z and for gen A to come up with their ideas, and to make the world better.”
Keywords: SuperKnova; genderfluid; singer; performer; musician; music; New York; New York City; piano; guitar; medical school; doctor; medicine; Gen A; Gen Z

It wasn't until she picked up her first guitar that she fell in love with music and started dreaming of becoming a musician. Due to the lack of Asian American musicians in the mainstream—and even fewer queer Asian American musicians—Ellie didn't think being a musician was a possibility, so she chose the “safe route” and pursued medical school. Ellie completed school and earned a medical degree, but she is not a doctor. She is now living life fully as both Ellie and SuperKnova. Music became the vehicle for her to process emotions and her queer identity, as well as to find a community that embraces her for who she is. So what's her advice for the next generation? “Don’t listen to me! I’m just excited for Gen Z and Gen A to come up with their ideas and make the world better.”

Sammy Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Sammy
Sammy Kim (they/them) is many things: they’re a queer artist, a writer, a healer, a sex worker, a community organizer, a caregiver, and a friend. At a young age, their mother was diagnosed with Parkinson’s, and they had to move in with their Korean aunt and uncle who was white, as their mother’s condition progressed. They remember being confused at first, not seeing their mother as the powerful, “parental” figure that they had been conditioned to perceive parents as; in time, as the condition worsened, this perception was slowly deconstructed. They have memories, too, of their mother crying while praying for their father, who Sammy later learned was going through his own struggles with mental health and addiction. Though he never brought these struggles back home, Sammy had long realized that there was something about their parents that they felt they were hiding from them. Seeing their mother struggle with her health, Sammy naturally came into a role of caregiving, grounding themselves in gratitude while extending empathy towards their family. They have an older sibling as well, who also moved into their relatives’ home witht them, but who Sammy describes as always having been a little more independent than they themselves where; growing up, Sammy would want to stick to their siblings’ side, only to be pushed away, something which Sammy now attributes to their own need to people-please, though through talking with their sibling over the years they’ve come to realize that this act of pushing away was a response by their sibling to the kinds of burdens and responsibilities that they felt as the oldest child. Sammy’s journey with queerness, too, began at an early age: by the time they were in their pre-teen years, they realized that they were queer, and yet having grown up in a Christian household, felt an inextricable need to hide this part of themselves. In those early years, to accept that they were queer meant that they would face eternal damnation, and so they tried to “pray the gay away” themselves, operating out of fear. They didn’t come out to their parents until their mother approached them about it, by which point they had already been living as an out individual for three years. Although their mother told them she couldn’t yet accept this part of themselves, they were still her son, and for Sammy, this just meant that the two of them would need more time in figuring this out, saying that they’ve learned that acceptance can look different in many different ways, depending on the relationship. They extend acceptance to others through their sex work as well. Sammy recalls how they first began taking clients when they were 21 years old, and how what first began as a part time job turned into a realization that often, they were acting as caregivers to men who also just desired to feel wanted, many of whom had never come out. After college, Sammy moved back home to the Bronx (their parents had moved from Flushing), where for the first time they explored the underground scene of the city, meeting individuals who would use the label “non-binary;” though at first Sammy shied away from this term, they realized that it was a more accurate way of describing themselves to others. Rather than feeling bogged down by their personal hardships, Sammy chooses to feel alive in the present, living in the joy that stems from simply being themselves without distraction.
Keywords: queer; artist; writer; healer; sex work; sex worker; community organizer; caregiver; Parkinson's; mother; struggle; caregiving; church; Christianity; shame; damnation; desire; comfort; friend

Emma Kim Stoll-Vanilla Honey (Full)
Interviewee: Stoll-Vanilla, Emma Kim Honey
Vanilla Honey (she/they), who goes by Honey, is a queer architect and community organizer based in New York. They are also, in their own words, a “friend.” Growing up on Long Island, she was never taught Korean by her mother, nor put through Korean school, but language was never an issue in connecting with their family; rather, they’ve found in humor and playfulness a means to connect with their family, as well as with other Koreans. Being biracial and mixed, too, gave them a template for not belonging in certain spaces, but upon them realizing their queer identity, the fluidity they experienced in gender felt familiar to the fluidity they had experienced with race. For Honey, queerness has been, and continues to be, a bridge connecting themselves to the larger Korean American community, and a way to capture a sense of belonging within the community. Partnering with friends and other queer Korean folk in New York City, they helped bring KQT—a national grassroots network aimed at uplifting Queer and Trans people of Korean descent—to New York City, creating virtual “dream spaces” where others might be able find a place like home to belong in. Queer joy for them is rendered in abstractions: a collection of different kind of joys, a warmth in the body, a grin on the face; the feeling of safeness, knowing they’re surrounded by loved ones. They remind themself, and us as well: “You’re not alone. You’re going to find support, you don’t have to always rely on yourself.”
Keywords: queer; architect; community organizer; New York; friend; Long Island; Korean school; language; family; humor; playfulness; biracial; bridge; KQTx; Korean Queer and Transgender Network; dream space; virtual; safety

Yoon Grace Ra (Full)
Interviewee: Ra, Yoon Grace
Yoon (they/them) is a trans/non-binary filmmaker and organizer from communities both in and around New Jersey and New York. Growing up in a family comprised of mixed classes and status, they were from an early age exposed to diversity in identities; for Yoon, the first conceptualization of identity was rooted in their community’s working class background, rather than in race or in gender. It was through a class-based understanding of identity that Yoon mediated other modes of identity, such as queerness. Acknowledging the beauty of romantic love, and romantic queer love, they describe their formative experience with love as one shaped first as in relation to their community, spirituality, and family, through which they developed a means of understanding love as a collective force. As a child, they were always hyperaware that boys and girls were meant to “move” differently, not just in what they wore, but how in movement, the performance of gender was physically embodied. Yet this performance, too, is dynamic: for many trans people, Yoon says, what they look like, how they speak, and how they present themselves can shift radically, on differing timelines. In their own words, queerness is how someone builds relationships outside of what the “norm” may be; queer people are anyone who choose to live outside a nuclear family, “having the liberation to be with whoever is best for you.”
Keywords: transgender; non-binary; filmmaker; organizer; New Jersey; New York; working class; class; romantic love; community; spirituality; family; collective; liberation; performance; nuclear family

Emmett Yoon (Full)
Interviewee: Yoon, Emmett
Emmett Yoon (he/him) is a trans activist and immigrant based in Houston, where he serves as the Executive Director of the Transgender Education Network in Texas (TENT). Born in South Korea, Emmett was adopted into a white family in the Midwest who encouraged open dialogue about gender, sexuality, and identity. As a transgender man who grew up in a transracial family, he often felt out of place and confused, though his mother would remind him that the world which he saw was not the world in its entirety, and that he was a more expansive person than how he felt growing up. He eventually moved to San Antonio as a volunteer in pursuing activist work within trans policy and education in Texas, and in 2017 was situationally tapped by TENT to be Executive Director. Noting a lack of trans infrastructure within the state, and building upon his own unique experiences, he works to expand trans-focused educational policy, legislation, and community resources—“an enormous task” by every metric, but one propelled by his belief that “we’re good, at heart.” Today, in his role in advocacy with his family as his anchor, he continues to create spaces through education where he can meet people where they’re at so that they don’t have to go on their journey alone, ultimately encouraging others to step forward in their convictions, whatever that may look like for them.
Keywords: transgender; activist; Houston; Texas; Transgender Education Network in Texas; TENT; South Korea; adoption; adoptee; midwest; San Antonio; trans policy; education, educational policy; policy; legislation; community

Emmett Yoon (Edited)
Interviewee: Yoon, Emmett
Emmett Yoon (he/him) is a trans rights activist based in Houston, serving as the Executive Director of the Transgender Education Network of Texas (TENT). Born in South Korea and adopted by a white family in the Midwest, Emmett often felt out of place and confused as a transracial adoptee. His mother reminded him that both he and the world were more expansive than he realized, instilling in him a strong sense of social responsibility and community through volunteering.
As an adult living in San Antonio, Emmett noticed the lack of support and the targeted attacks toward the trans community. This compelled him to take action and start volunteering, eventually leading him to become the Executive Director of TENT in 2017. Faced with the absence of trans infrastructure in Texas and drawing on his unique experiences, Emmett continues to expand trans-focused educational policy, legislation, and community resources. It's an enormous task, but he remains grounded by the family he has built and his belief in the inherent goodness of humanity. Emmett hopes that by creating inclusive spaces, the trans community can find their own strength to protect their joy and for the greater community to see each other in fullness to work collectively for a better future.
