Chung Soon Ahn Park (Full)
Interviewee: Park, Chung Soon Ahn
Chung Soon Park, née Chung Soon Ahn, was born in 1935 in occupied Korea. She is a 27th generational descendant of the Sunheung Ahn clan, a reputable family line in Korean whose members include revolutionary fighter Dosan Ahn and his son, Philip Ahn. Park’s father was the eldest of his family and was well-educated (trained in Japan); her mother, too, was educated in Japan. In 1948, at the behest of a family friend who had lived most of his life in Russia, her father began sending his family over the 38th parallel into South Korea, sending first his younger siblings before his children; at the time, they had been living on the bank of the Taedung River in Pyeongyang. Park states that her family lived without worries during the colonial period, in a village called Jinsang in North Korea. She recollects memories of going fishing with her father, as well as her patriotic grandmather who waved the Korean flag out of their home following graduation. She also remembers how the family name “Ahn,” meaning rice paddy, was Japanoized into “Yasuda,” also meaning rice paddy; however, at her grandparents’ insistence, the family did not adopt Japanese personal names. Shortly after the family moved South (to Seoul), the Korean War broke out; Park remembers how her mother asked her to check in on her grandparents in Euljiro (neighborhood); Park, who lived in Sindang (neighborhood), walked over to her grandparents’ home, the road to which was covered in bodies with yellowed feet. In Daegu, she completed her primary education, and after the war ended she matriculated into Yonsei as a history major, though she had wanted to enroll as an English Literature major, “the boys in my grade were too good at English,” even though she had been attending cram school twice a week to learn the language. Following graduation, she worked at the YWCA. While in college, too, she met her husband who had obtained his education in Japan. Following their wedding, the two moved to Japan for her husband’s work (shipping corporation), and had a daughter. He was soon offered a position abroad in the company’s Atlanta office, but at the insistence of Chung Soon, the family moved instead to Washington D.C. in 1965, where Park continued studying under an exchange program, and where her husband began a graduate program at George Washington University to study engineering. She remembers when Park Chung-Hee visited D.C., as well as her excitement when the Korean president called out to her in the crowd from his podium to exclaim how “she was taller than his wife!” In time, her husband found work as an engineer at various companies, and Park herself worked at a number of airlines, where her language skills in English, Korean, and Japanese could be put to use. The couple’s daughter married an Irish-American, who Park greatly adores (she claims that the Irish and Korean are similar in demeanor and temperament), and that they have one grandchild. Nowadays, she enjoys golfing with her friend from home who also coincidently ended up in the DC area, and expresses great satisfaction with her life which she attributes to her faith.
Keywords: Korea; Colonial; Japan; Ahn; Sunheung Ahn; edcuation; parallel; Russia; Taedung; fishing; Jinsang; Daeju; Korean War; Yasuda; YWCA; Yonsei; Atlanta; shipping; George Washington University; Park Chung-hee; President; Sindang; Euljiro; Uljiro

Chung Soon Ahn Park (Edited)
Interviewee: Park, Chung Soon Ahn
Chung Soon Ahn was born in Pyongyang, North Korea, before the outbreak of the Korean war. She lived comfortably next to the Taedong River until her father’s friend informed them that they should move to the South, as the family was at risk of being targeted by a government purge. In 1948, the entire family relocated to Seoul, where Chung Soon was enrolled in school. During the war her family left Seoul, but returned after it ended. Chung Soon, now a university student, returned to school, and upon graduation found work at the YWCA. While working she met her husband, with whom she moved to Japan, following his career. Though her husband’s company wanted to relocate him to an American office in Atlanta, Chung Soon suggested that they move to D.C. instead, and in 1964 she and her husband moved to the United States, where her husband found a new job as an engineer and she as an airline interpreter. Looking back on her life, she remembers fondly the comforts of her childhood in North Korea, but is above all grateful for both the opportunities and the struggles which pushed her to start a new life in America as well.

Seung Hie Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Seung Hie
Seung-hie Kim, née Yoon, was born in Seoul in occupied Korea. She remembers from her elementary school days how if were caught speaking Korean in class—even small exclamations such as “Umma!” (“Oh my!”)—you get points docked from your daily grade. She also remembers how in the fourth grade, she won an origami airplane contest and was able to throw her winning airplane from the top of the school. Her given Japanese name was “Ido Hoshiko,” and following liberation she remembers feeling rather sad: she had undergone primary education in the Japanese schooling system for so long that by war’s end, she considered herself to be Japanese, and couldn’t help but feel confused at her older siblings joyous reactions to Hirohito’s infamous surrender broadcast. There was a wide age gap between herself and her older siblings, a generational gap in the understanding of Korean and Japanese identities. Adding to this generational gap was how, among her older siblings, many of them attended unviersity in Japan, becoming familiar with a variety of ideologies, among them communism (a big no-no in postwar South Korea). Her father, who had been a lifelong Nationalist (anti-communist), was kidnapped by North Korean authorities, never to be seen again—from what she heard in her youth, he was made to work for the Kim Il Sung administration. During the Korean War itself, Kim fled with her family from Seoul to Yeoju, her ancestral village, where she saw the countryside for the first time. The family then fled to another village, further south, where she was hidden from both American and Korean soldiers whenever they passed by in fear that she would be taken away by force to become a yanggongju. After the war, the family returned to Seoul where she met her husband by chance; a graduate of the Air Force Academy, he had just gotten back from America and was in need of lodging, and Kim’s family happened to have an empty room that he could move into. In her words, the two got married out of pity for one another; she had nowhere to go after rejecting an acceptance letter to Ewha University (she didn’t want to leave her mother alone), and he had nothing to do. The two married in 1957. For Kim, the dream after the war was to move to the U.S.; her husband, who had gone multiple times during this time in the academy, was less partial to the idea, but the family moved to Maryland anyway in 1972 as they had relatives who had immigrated to the area in the 1960s. Together, they ran a successful department store for a long time until her husband was diagnosed with ALS, and given a prognosis of two years, though under Kim’s caregiving he lived for 20 years after his initial diagnosis, as Kim held onto the hope that he could lead a happy life after she learned of Stephen Hawking’s own life. When asked how she wants to be remembered by her grandchildren, or whether she has any advice for them, she scoffed and said that she was just happy that they went out of their way to visit her once or twice a year, and that she wants for them to lead happy lives according to how their generation understands happiness.
Keywords: Seoul; Colonial; points; airplane; origami; Ido Hoshiko; Hirohito; surrender; broadcast; liberation; confusion; communist; communism; nationalist; nationalism; anticommunism; Korean War; Yeoju; Maryland; Ewha; Air Force; Academy; department; store; ALS; Stephen Hawking; happiness; Baltimore

Seung Hie Kim (Edited)

Seung Hie Kim was born in Seoul, Korea, during the Japanese occupation. Recounting her childhood memories, she describes what school while under imperial control, such as how penalties would be imposed on students who spoke Korean in the classroom—not even an “Um-ma! Oops!” went unnoticed. She speaks of the Japanese name and identity given to her (“Ido Hoshiko”), and feeling sadness and confusion while listening to Hirohito’s surrender broadcast as she’d considered herself to be Japanese by the war’s end. After liberation, her family was subjected to kidnappings and arrests owing to her siblings’ Communist ties, and during the Korean War she fled from Seoul to Yeoju-si. Shortly after the war ended she met her husband, who had returned from military academy in the United States. The couple married in 1957 and later moved to the U.S. in 1972, settling in Baltimore where fortune granted them a business opportunity in managing a large department store in the greater Baltimore area.

Grace Chunmo Lyo (Full)
Interviewee: Lyo, Grace Chunmo
Grace Lyo was born in 1946 in Gyeonggi Province, Korea. She was born as Yang Chunmo, but halfway through elementary school her grandfather changed her name to Yang Hwasuk after following the advice of a shaman. It was around this time, too, that she moved from the countryside to Seoul to live with her relatives who lived in the city, a move her mother hoped would give her access to better education. Her new peers teased her relentlessly for being a “country bumpkin,” and where shocked into silence when she scored a perfect score—the highest score in the class—on an arithmetic test: the Seoulite kids had underestimated her. She attended high school in Seoul as well, and afterwards entered the workforce as a government employee of the Department of the Treasury. As a government employee, she remembers witnessing Sa-il-gu, or the April Revolution of 1960 protesting President Rhee’s administration, as well as the subsequent May 16th military coup of 1961 which installed Park Chung-hee as President. She was touched by the young people’s activism, but fearing for her own safety as a government employee, she quit her job to work instead in the actuarial industry where she met her childrens’ father. When her kids were around seven years old, the family moved to the Baltimore area in 1981, where they opened a store in nearby Reistertown. It was around this time, too, that Grace began taking night classes at a local community college, which her husband adamantly protested; owing to this difference in opinion the couple divorced and sold the store. Grace then opened a second store in Garrison; by now, she had obtained citizenship and naturalized herself as “Grace Lyo.” Soon after, she opened another store, and then another: at one point, she was running three businesses alone, one of which she gave to her brother when he too immigrated to the area. In 2015, during the Baltimore Protests, one of her stores suffered an arson attack which led to its permanent closure. Despite this setback, Grace persevered and continued working in her businesses, encountering new employees along the way. During the COVID pandemic and the simultaneous George Floyd protests, Grace’s store was again targeted, yet against the insistence of the police department, Grace chose to keep her stores open, keeping her normal hours open (until midnight!). She always looks for the good in people, whether it be the community she serves, the youth in her neighborhood, or her own kids, who she worries for. Grace talks about all the love that local residents have given her and her businesses throughout the years she’s lived in Baltimore, and how she herself harbors nothing but gratitude and a desire to give back to her community and its youth, hoping to one day set up a scholarship fund for those children who always passed through the doors of her store.

Paul Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Paul
Paul Kim's life journey exemplifies a remarkable transition from a humble upbringing to influential business success and philanthropy. Born in Seoul, Korea, and later immigrating to St. Paul, Minnesota in 1966, Paul's early experiences were marked by cultural shifts and economic challenges. His father, arriving in the US with just $20, worked diligently as a janitor before securing a stable job with the railroad, which allowed the family to move to a predominantly white neighborhood. This move was crucial for Paul, who was under strict paternal discipline to excel academically—a pressure that fueled his ambition. Driven by a desire to match his successful aunt, a prominent fashion designer, Paul set a goal to become a millionaire by age 35. His career began at Cargill in Portland as a grain merchant, and though he faced initial financial struggles, his move to trading in Chicago marked a turning point. Paul attributes his subsequent success to his deepened spiritual life, specifically his commitment to prayer and writing out his prayers daily for a decade. This spiritual practice coincided with his financial success in trading, illustrating a profound connection between his faith and professional life. Paul's compassion extended beyond his personal and professional achievements. Deeply moved by the famine in North Korea in 1996, he used his expertise to coordinate grain donations, visiting North Korea to oversee the distribution. His philanthropic efforts expanded globally, including support for orphanages in China and other countries. However, Paul's personal life faced challenges, particularly his marriage. Moving to Newport, CA, in an attempt to revitalize his marriage ultimately led to a divorce after a prolonged separation. This period of his life also saw Paul enjoying a carefree lifestyle, but the realization of his children’s needs prompted a renewed focus on family, leading him to take a more active role in their education by joining the board of trustees at their private school. Reflecting on his life, Paul expresses a wish to have been more ambitious in seeking mentors during his early 30s and advises young people to aim high early in their careers. He also contemplates the choices in his personal life, suggesting a more calculated approach to marriage. Paul Kim's story is a blend of ambition, resilience, and transformation, shaped significantly by his cultural heritage, familial expectations, and personal convictions. His narrative is not just about achieving personal success but also about giving back to the community and finding a balance between personal desires and family responsibilities.
Keywords: philanthropy; St. Paul, Minnesota; immigration; Seoul, South Korea; business; trader; humanitarian efforst; North Korea; famine; divorce; separation; family;

Paul Kim (Edited)
Interviewee: Kim, Paul
Paul Kim was born in Seoul and immigrated to the United States in 1996. His family settled in St. Paul, Minnesota, fulfilling his father’s dream of moving to America. When Paul entered middle school, the family moved from St. Paul to the surrounding suburbs, which Paul recounts as having much better schools than the city, albeit being much less diverse than the city itself. Education was of utmost importance in the Kim family, and Paul chased what he believed was, at the time, the natural pipeline that was getting good grades, getting into college, and landing a high-paying job. Paul graduated from university convinced he wanted to become a businessman, so he moved to Chicago to work as a trader. After two years in Chicago, he relocated to Portland, where he worked as the only Asian worker at his workplace, to trade grain with Asia. While in Oregon, he received an offer from a different firm in Chicago, but it wasn’t until after he moved back to the Midwest that he learned his offer had been reneged. He eventually found work again as a trader but was compelled through his faith to find a higher calling in philanthropy. In partnership with a church in South Korea, Paul leveraged the skills he’d gained in industry to buy grain and ship it to North Korea, over time expanding the type and scale of the humanitarian efforts he found himself involved in.
Keywords: philanthropy; St. Paul, Minnesota; immigration; Seoul, South Korea; business; trader; humanitarian efforst; North Korea; famine; divorce; separation; family;

Jinwoo Chung (Full)
Interviewee: Chung, Jinwoo
Jinwoo Chung, a 41-year-old Maryland native from Gaithersburg, embodies a story of multicultural integration, compassion, and familial growth. Born to Korean immigrant parents who moved to the U.S. in 1974, Jinwoo grew up in a nurturing environment shaped by his father, a dental technician, and his mother, a nurse. His upbringing was deeply rooted in the church community, thanks to his uncle who established churches in Maryland and Virginia. Jinwoo's childhood was marked by a strong sense of belonging and acceptance, facilitated by his mother's fluency in English and her efforts to introduce her children to diverse cultural experiences, such as dining at Indian restaurants instead of Korean ones, since she could cook Korean food at home. His mother also instilled in him a profound respect for women, a lesson that Jinwoo admittedly struggled with in his youth but came to appreciate deeply as he matured. Professionally, Jinwoo was never pressured to follow the traditional paths of medicine or law. Instead, he pursued his passion for helping others by working at a therapeutic group home for boys aged 6-12 right after college. This experience not only shaped his career trajectory—prompting him to consider a master's degree in social work—but also opened his heart to the idea of adoption. Together with his wife, who shared his sentiments about adopting, Jinwoo navigated the complex journey of fertility challenges before embracing adoption wholeheartedly. After several unsuccessful attempts with IUI and choosing not to pursue IVF, they educated themselves about the foster care system and connected with adoption agencies. Jinwoo and his wife ultimately adopted a multiracial daughter, a decision that enriched their lives immeasurably and introduced new cultural dimensions to their family, such as learning to care for their daughter’s curly hair. Jinwoo also made the significant decision to leave his Korean American church in pursuit of a more diverse spiritual community, reflecting his and his wife’s broader values of inclusivity. His reflections on fatherhood and adoption underscore a journey of love, acceptance, and understanding. The couple maintains a relationship with their daughter’s birth mother, which Jinwoo describes as a poignant aspect of their adoption experience, highlighting the complex emotions involved in such relationships. Through every phase of his life, Jinwoo Chung's story is a testament to the power of open-heartedness, the importance of cultural adaptability, and the profound impact of embracing diversity within family life.
Keywords: adoption; family; Maryland; birth mother; IUI; IVF; christianity; Maryland; immigration; church community;

Jinwoo Chung (Edited)
Interviewee: Chung, Jinwoo
Jinwoo Chung was born in Montgomery County, Maryland, to parents who immigrated to the Rockville area from Korea. Holding a strong conviction to help others, Jinwoo studied psychology in college and eventually found work as a case manager and counselor for a boys group home. He recounts how, in forging relationships with youth who had experienced deep familial trauma, his perspective on family-building was deeply impacted. After navigating the emotional journey of fertility treatments with his wife, the family began to look into the process of adoption, a decision spurred by the couple’s unwavering belief in the power of familial love, irrespective of biological ties. When they matched with a birth mother through an adoption agency in Maryland, Jinwoo describes feeling initial anxiety about how his community would receive the family’s adoption of a child who would not present as Korean, but that this fear was quickly dispelled. He recognizes the fears and doubts that come with parenthood, especially through adoption, yet remains steadfast in his belief that every child deserves to be known and loved, through it all guided by his devotion to faith and family.
Keywords: adoption; family; Maryland; birth mother; IUI; IVF; christianity; Maryland; immigration; church community;

Chun-Kyu Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Chun-Kyu
Dr. Chun-Kyu Lee was born in 1928 in South Jeolla province, Korea, during the colonial period. When he was of age to begin attending school—around seven years old—his family realized that the path to and from school was too long for a child to make, and so he deferred his enrollment into elementary school for a year. For that deferred year, he attended a Seodang, or a Classics School where students were trained in calligraphy, aesthetics, and Korean Classics, before enrolling into elementary school a year later. In the years leading up to the end of WWII, the Japanese army forced all able bodied individuals, including middle and high school aged boys, to work in labor to contribute to the war effort, and so he and his classmates would go up and down mountains on “work trips,” carrying logs and doing other tasks. On one such work trip, his classmates heard whispers of some broadcast being sent over the radio waves from Japan, but didn’t give it much thought; it wasn’t until he came back down from the mountain a week later and saw people celebrating that he realized the the war had ended, and that Korea had been liberated from Japanese occupation. On his walk back, which took him through a local village, he encountered a middle-aged woman who, like him, had not known the country had been liberated; Lee was the first person to tell her and the entire village that the war had ended. He recalls the “Manse’s!” erupting as he walked back home from that village. Soon, however, with growing tensions between the Nationalist and Communist factions in Korea, tensions which Lee remembers being palpable for five years, he was faced with another conflict. The Korean War broke out only a week after he began college, and like many college students at the time, he fled to Busan where a provisionary makeshift university had been set up in tents around the Southern city, far from the risk of invasion. He was screened and classified as an “engineering student,” and studied at the school for two years before transferring to medical school at Seoul National University upon seeing flyers advertising transfer applications. After medical school, Lee worked as an army doctor, during which time he met his wife and had three children; after his service in the army, he took up medical posts in Uganda as part of a humanitarian mission where Korea sent three doctors to each of Uganda’s 24 hospitals. The family lodged in the former homes of British colonial officers, and after Lee’s post ended (he was a psychiatrist), his children protested at the idea of returning to Korea, as they’d gotten used to the comfortable lifestyle offered to them by the colonial estates. The family thus chose to move to the United States instead, with Lee enrolling in a medical internship and residency in Lorain, Ohio, before moving to Cleveland to begin his medical career in America. He recalls one particular evening when a supposed patient was brought in by the police (only police were allowed to “check in” patients into the psychiatric ward), who had caught her moments away from taking her own life. The patient was a young Korean woman who couldn’t speak English, and upon screening Lee learned that she was a victim of a human trafficking ring, and had been moved from Korea to L.A., then Chicago and Cleveland. Recognizing that she was heavily in debt, had nowhere to turn to, and was unable to communicate in English, Lee told the officers that she wasn’t mentally ill, but a victim of unfortunate circumstances. After working in Cleveland for some years, he retired in Maryland where some of his family had moved to, and when asked about any potential advice he’d give his grandchildren or to recent immigrants to the U.S., he said he didn’t have any, since the U.S. is a free country and he wouldn’t want to force any particular mode of thinking onto individuals, valuing people’s capacity to make the correct decisions for themselves, whatever they may be. It’s for this reason, too, that he doesn’t lament that his grandchildren didn’t grow up “Koreanized,” as doing so would have been unnatural to them, and caused them unncessary strife.

Chun-Kyu Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Chun-Kyu
Dr. Chun-Kyu Lee was born in 1928 in Jeollanam-do, South Korea, and experienced the hardships of WWII during 8th grade when he was forced into labor instead of schooling to support the Japanese army. Following Japan’s surrender in 1945, Dr. Lee returned to academic life, only to face the outbreak of the Korean War shortly after starting college in Seoul. This led him to transfer to medical school, where he trained as a doctor to aid war efforts, later extending his medical expertise to Uganda during a doctor shortage. In 1972, Dr. Lee moved to the United States to pursue further training in psychiatry, working extensively in Korea, Uganda, and later in Cleveland, Ohio, despite language barriers. After retiring, he settled in Maryland, cherishing the proximity to his family as the happiest period of his life, reflecting a journey marked by resilience and dedication to serving others across continents.

Jim Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Jim
Jim Lee, a 61-year-old financial advisor originally from Namsan, South Korea, has lived a life shaped by diverse cultures and deep personal connections. Born in South Korea, his earliest memory is a grand feast his grandfather hosted for his family during a visit from Uganda—a significant moment, though Jim has no recollection of leaving Korea when he moved to Uganda at the age of four or five. At ten, Jim's journey took him to Cleveland, Ohio, where he enjoyed a fulfilling childhood. In adulthood, Jim met Celeste, who would become his wife, through a mutual friend. Despite some initial uncertainty about their cultural differences—Jim is Korean and Celeste is white, and his family initially hoped he would marry within his ethnicity—their shared core values and mutual respect for each other's selflessness solidified their relationship. They married after six years of dating, and their union was further enriched by their children. Jim and Celeste have two children. Their son, Matthew, was born with hydrocephalus, a condition involving an accumulation of cerebral fluid in the brain. Despite the challenges, Matthew has thrived, thanks in part to a special program called Clemson Life that helps individuals with special needs develop job and life skills. Matthew is currently enjoying a rich social life and even has a girlfriend. Their daughter pursued a career as a speech pathologist, inspired by her brother's experiences and her mother's dedication, and was instrumental in finding the Clemson Life program for Matthew. Jim's career as a financial advisor allows him to emphasize the importance of giving and supporting others. He often encourages his clients to alleviate fears around donating, drawing on his personal experiences of family resilience and community support. Jim's life story is a testament to the power of love, acceptance, and perseverance through the challenges and joys of a cross-cultural family life.
Keywords: Uganda; Cleveland, Ohio; financial advisor; biracial kids; philanthropy; donations; special needs; Clemson Life; speech pathologist; family; mixed marriage;

Jim Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Jim
Jim Lee, 61-years old and born in Namsan, South Korea, shares a story firmly rooted in his dedication to family, a story which begins in college when a mutual friend set him up on a blind date with Celeste, his future wife. Coming from two very different backgrounds, Lee was initially unsure of Celeste's feelings, but their shared values and deep connection proved that the pair would make for a formidable couple grounded in each other as they navigated through life’s joys and hardships. A significant challenge arose with their son, Matthew, who faced health challenges from birth yet overcame them through the family’s perseverance and optimism in sourcing him the support he needed to thrive; today, Matthew leads a vibrant life, having proved those skeptical of his health wrong. Kim recounts how their daughter, inspired by Celeste’s compassion and dedication to Matthew’s well-being, would pursue a career in speech pathology, working as an advocate for children with special needs. Lee emphasizes the importance of giving back, the day-to-day gratitude, and the lasting peace he’s found in family life as he tells his narrative, encapsulating the resilience and enduring love of a Korean American family marked both by struggle but also by triumph.

Matthias Chu (Full)
Interviewee: Chu, Matthias
Matthias Chu, a 25-year-old from Maryland, has navigated a complex journey of self-discovery and identity amidst the challenges of academia and personal beliefs. Raised in a community with a strong Korean presence, Matthias was deeply rooted in his culture from an early age, participating in a Korean church and growing up around a Koreatown. However, it was not until college that he began to form a predominantly Korean circle of friends. Initially enrolled in an engineering program at Virginia Tech, Matthias faced significant challenges that led to a temporary withdrawal from his studies. The isolation and pressure at a remote college contributed to the onset of depression during his sophomore year, which played a crucial role in his decision to drop out. He then spent a couple of years working at Amazon, where a failed promotion attempt prompted him to reassess his academic and professional goals. Encouraged by his parents, he returned to school to study information systems, aiming to graduate, secure a job, and establish his life on the East Coast. Despite not experiencing academic pressure from his parents, Matthias felt a heavy burden of religious expectations, which eventually led him to stop attending church during his junior year. This decision deeply saddened his parents and left Matthias grappling with guilt over diverging from the faith so integral to his family's values. Today, Matthias discusses the complexities of his identity and faith with a candid openness. He reflects on the guilt associated with his educational and religious choices and the evolving dynamics of his relationship with his parents. As he matures, he finds comfort in the improved ease of communication with his family, underscoring that despite the personal trials and the journey of self-acceptance, the bond with his family remains a cornerstone of his life.
Keywords: Maryland; koreatown; Virginia Tech; depression; dropping out; Amazon; family; Christianity; Korean church community; religion; isolation; pressure;

Matthias Chu (Edited)
Interviewee: Chu, Matthias
Matthias Chu, 25, was born and raised in Maryland. His story is one that resonates with many Korean American young adults, with the stresses and pressures of school and adulthood on his mind while also being of the age where he’s coming into his own identity. Part of this journey, he explains, has been navigating through his relationship with Christianity and the church, from interacting with students who attend church to having difficult conversations with his parents about his faith. Another part of the journey has been in learning to navigate stress productively while not getting hung up on overthinking. Matthias talks about the guilt he feels from the difference in faith with his parents, as well as dropping out from college and working for two years; in both cases, he explains that he feels bad knowing what his parents want him to be, versus how he sees himself. Nonetheless, he feels grateful that as he grows older, his relationship with his parents have improved, and that talking to them has become easier—despite the personal hardships and challenges that come with maturing as an adult, Matthias’ relationship with his family has always taken precedence.
Keywords: Maryland; koreatown; Virginia Tech; depression; dropping out; Amazon; family; Christianity; Korean church community; religion; isolation; pressure;

Jennifer Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Jennifer
Jennifer Lee, a 24-year-old from North Bethesda, Maryland, reflects on her transformative journey from Seoul, South Korea, where she was born, to the United States. Jennifer immigrated to the U.S. with her family at the age of 7, following the early death of her father when she was just 3 years old. Raised by a single mother who managed a home-based textile wholesale business for Nigerian fabrics, Jennifer grew up admiring her mother’s resilience and dedication to providing for her family despite the challenges of adapting to a new country and language. Jennifer's brother, who was 12 at the time they moved, faced a tougher adjustment period compared to her. This familial support and her mother’s tireless work ethic deeply influenced Jennifer, instilling a profound respect and gratitude towards her mother, whom she views as a superwoman. Jennifer's close bond with her grandmother in Korea also remained a significant part of her life despite the geographic distance. For her education, Jennifer moved to Pittsburgh, where she attended the University of Pittsburgh and later pursued a master's in Human-Computer Interaction at Carnegie Mellon University. During her time in Pittsburgh, she connected with a local Korean church, which helped sustain her sense of community and belonging. Recently, Jennifer has found a new church in Maryland and is eager to become more involved, seeing the church community as a pivotal part of her life. Professionally, she majored in Computer Science before transitioning to Human-Centered Interaction, a field she is passionate about. Looking ahead, Jennifer harbors aspirations to teach, aiming to share her knowledge and experiences with others. Throughout her journey, Jennifer's narrative is one of resilience, community, and an enduring appreciation for her cultural and familial roots.
Keywords: Maryland; church; Korean church community; UX design; computer science; single mother; immigration; University of Pittsburgh; Carnegie Mellon; teaching;

Jennifer Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Jennifer
Jennifer Lee, 24, recounts her journey from Seoul, Korea, to North Bethesda, Maryland, where she now resides after having immigrated to the United States with her mother and brother. She speaks about what it was like to be raised by a resilient mother who worked to provide for her young family in new country, and reflects on the love, support, and sympathy extended to her following the loss of her father at a young age. She talks about navigating college and career aspirations all the while switching from pre-health to computer science, where she found an interest in Human-Computer Interaction. Although initially conflicted on whether to pursue UX research and design, as this would mean more schooling rather than directly entering the workforce, she was encouraged by her mother to follow her passions instead of feeling burdened by the impossible weight of giving back to family. She says that if she could give her younger self any advice, it would be to have confidence in yourself and what you do, because no matter how you feel toward yourself, there will always be those around willing to show you love and care.
Keywords: Maryland; church; Korean church community; UX design; computer science; single mother; immigration; University of Pittsburgh; Carnegie Mellon; teaching;

Pastor Youngmin Jung (Full)
Interviewee: Jung, Youngmin
Youngmin Jung, a Korean-American pastor, was born in Busan, South Korea, in 1965. Growing up, he aspired to be a movie star, often discussing his favorite films and actors with American GIs stationed near his home. He also enjoyed participating in church skits, showcasing his early interest in performance. Initially, Jung planned to pursue a degree in film or acting. However, a suggestion from his Sunday school teacher led him to consider a different path. After careful contemplation, he chose to attend the largest Presbyterian seminary in Korea. Following his undergraduate studies, he pursued an MDiv at Yale Divinity School, initially aiming to become a seminarian or seminary professor. During his time at Yale, Jung actively engaged in feminist theology and LGBTQ+ activism. He became a member of the LGBTQ Coalition at Yale University Divinity School and advocated for greater understanding and acceptance of these issues within Korean society and the Christian community. After graduating from Yale, Jung moved to Palisades Park, New Jersey, a town with a significant Korean population. While unable to secure a position at a church, he worked as a college admissions consultant and SAT tutor. Simultaneously, he served as the director of Christian education at various Korean churches, gaining valuable experience in communicating with younger generations. Today, Pastor Jung continues to serve his church, promoting a more personal and spiritual approach to faith that transcends traditional institutionalized religion. He remains dedicated to fostering a more inclusive and progressive Christian community.
Keywords: pastor; American GIs; church; seminary school; acting; film; LGBTQ+ Coalition; Yale University; SAT tutor; instutionalized religion; feminist theology; Christian community; School of Divinity;

Pastor Youngmin Jung (Edited)
Interviewee: Jung, Youngmin
Pastor Youngmin Jung was born in Busan, South Korea, in 1965. Growing up, he wanted to become a movie star; when he got the chance, he would talk to American GIs stationed near his home about his favorite movies and actors. As a child, he enjoyed taking part in the short skits his church put on, and when it came time for him to apply to university, he expressed a desire to study film or acting. But when his Sunday school teacher suggested that he go to bible school or seminary instead, he decided to take their suggestion to heart, and after many weeks of prayer and contemplation chose to attend the largest Presbyterian seminary in Korea. After completing his undergraduate studies, he studied abroad at Yale for an MDiv, initially thinking he would go on to become a seminarian, or a seminary professor. He worked as the director of Christian education at a number of Sunday school programs at Korean churches, something he realized provided him with a different kind of scholarship, one where he learned to communicate with younger peoples. Throughout his studies, he also worked as a college admissions consultant and a standardized testing tutor. Today, Pastor Jung is continuing to work as a pastor within his church, emphasizing an approach to faith which shifts away from institutionalized religion towards cultivating personal spirituality.
Keywords: pastor; American GIs; church; seminary school; acting; film; LGBTQ+ Coalition; Yale University; SAT tutor; instutionalized religion; feminist theology; Christian community; School of Divinity;

Soo Chung (Full)
Interviewee: Chung, Soo
Soo Chung was born in Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul, in 1976. From an early age, she expressed a passion, as well as a natural talent, for the arts: she recalls how her mother would often have to hide her arts supplies because of how she’d scribble all over the walls and tabletops of their home. She feels fortunate that her parents encouraged her aspirations as an artist, even supporting her decisions to attend art school abroad in the United States; rather than returning back to Korea after completing her studies, she fell in love with the bustling diversity of New York and elected to stay a while longer to continue her career as an artist in America. From working at art galleries in Chelsea to promoting and selling her own artwork, she describes some of the initial difficulties she experienced as a career artist, at times feeling naïve in her endeavors. Soo struggled to survive in Harlem with her studio space along with an apartment in Queens. Her mom had to stop financially supporting her when she was graduating school because her mom’s business wasn’t doing well. While working as a professional painter, she also began to work as a journalist for a Korean American broadcasting company which was based in New Jersey; from her apartment in Queens, she’d commute to her workplace which then sent her all around New York City to cover the news. Her work as a journalist, too, was a source of inspiration in mediating social issues through visual art, and she feels fortunate that she was able to do both. After she got married, she moved to Palisades Park and today, she still lives in Palisades Park and serves on the board of her child’s school district, and is active in other local and civic organizations while continuing her work as an artist.
Keywords: artist; painter; study abroad; education; palisades park; board member; Seoul, Korea; immigration; art school; New York City; New Jersey; civic organizations;

Mee Jung (Full)
Interviewee: Jung, Mee
Mee Jung (who also goes by Mia) is a dancer who was born and raised in Seoul, South Korea. Her parents were divorced when she was young but still communicated with her father when she started living with her mother. She lived with her mother and brother throughout her childhood, and recalls being doted on by her whole family; for this reason, she thinks she was a “happy girl, and a happy person.” She recalls, too, wanting to always be on stage; she loved to sing, dance, and act, so much so that even her teachers and friends called her “worldstar.” She studied both acting and dance, and decided to move to America in 2005 to pursue her dreams of becoming a dancer. When she came to the US she was really excited and happy with dreams of becoming an amazing dancer. Reality was totally different, she had to learn English and deal with her mother getting diagnosed with lung cancer while she was away, being blamed from her aunt for not financially supporting her mother while she had cancer. Arriving in New York, she continued to study dance at a ballet school for 6 years, where she would audition for placements into dance companies. Encouraged by her partner at the time to begin her own company, she founded i KADA Contemporary Dance company in 2011, through which she later created KoDaFe, standing for Korean Dance Festival, in New York. When the pandemic hit, the dance company and festival temporarily suspended their activities while Mee Jung visited her mother in Korea; tragically, her mother passed at the end of the year, and shortly thereafter her brother as well. In 2021, after an emotionally grueling court trial, she adopted her niece (who had been raised by Mee Jung’s mother), and moved to New Jersey to take care of her new family. Although the past few years have been challenging, Mee Jung says that she’s excited for her future, a truth she wants others—as well as herself—to know.
Keywords: dancing; dancer; small business; dance studio; i KADA Contemporary Dance company;  KoDaFe; Korean Dance Festival; lung cancer; adoption; court; palisades park; immigration;

Kevin Lim (Full)
Interviewee: Lim, Kevin
Kevin Lim was born in Seoul, South Korea, in 1961. The youngest of his family, he remembers being doted on by his three older brothers, three older sisters, and his mother; though he recalls that his family was not particularly wealthy, he admits to having a rather comfortable childhood without much in the way of want or need, unaware of his family’s financial circumstances. His family, concerned for his future, encouraged him to study something which pave the way to a stable career; although he held an interest for literature, he chose to study electrical engineering instead, later affirming that he found it easier to find a job because of his early academic decisions. During his time performing compulsory military service, he took an exam in 1985 to serve as an air force officer on a whim and, upon passing the exam, spent the rest of his time in the Korean Air Force. After his service, he decided to work a corporate job as his family had envisioned for him, but he made sure to apply to a company with an overseas division as he didn’t yet know what he wanted to do. In 1992, after having worked at Samsung trading, he moved to the United States to pursue business school; during this time, he also met his wife, whose family had immigrated to America in the 1980s. Though he never imagined he’d live in Palisades Park, he made himself unintentionally known to the community when one day he showed up to a school board meeting, leveraging his background in finance to ask questions about the district’s fiscal plans to renovate their schools. In 2017 he applied for American citizenship, and began to look for more opportunities to directly get involved with local affairs. Yet he recognizes that there he will always be tied to Korea; around the time that he had begun to settle into his American life, he received a call from his brother that their mother was feeling unwell, and so flew in just days before her passing. Today, he operates his own practice as a CPA in the area and is largely involved with the Korean American community of Bergen County, serving on the board of its school district. He feels that Pal Park is a unique place, comprised of over 50% Korean residents, and is eager find ways to better serve his community.
Keywords: Seoul, South Korea; military; Samsung; military service; airforce officer; business school; Palisades Park; Bergen County; CPA; accountant;

Kyung Wan Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Kyung Wan
Kyung Wan Kim was born in 1940 in Yeondeungpo-gu, Seoul, during the Japanese occupation of Korea. When she was 2 years old, her father was offered a job in Hwanghae Province, today a part of North Korea. The family lived in Hwanghae Province until 1945, when Korea was liberated, after which the family moved back South to a rural part of Gyeonggi Province when she was 6 years old, where Kyung Wan’s grandparents lived and worked on a farm; to avoid rousing suspicion, the family crossed the border in the dark of night. She recalls how, around the age of 16, people began expressing interest in marrying her, and that because food was so scarce, her parents tried to wed her to a stranger; eventually, she ran away to her uncle who resided in Seoul. Her uncle enrolled her in school, but because of a shortage of funds Kyung Wan had to find a way to make money, which she did by offering tutoring services. Kyung Wan recalls that she greatly enjoyed school, which gave her both an athletic outlet in volleyball, as well as a space to perform and sing music with others in a choir. During her third year of high school, she decided to learn a trade in preparation for entering the workforce; she graduated with a license in typewriting, and soon began working for the National Assembly Secretariat, where she worked (while sending money home) until the 5.16 coup of 1961. During the coup, she remembers how because the National Assembly had been barricaded, staff could not enter the building. Soon thereafter, she found another job through a professor at SNU. In 1967, she married her husband who had just quit his job; with no source of income, Kyung Wan made and sold banchan for 2 years, after which she took up tutoring again. Afterwards, her sister—who had moved abroad to Chicago—invited Kyung Wan and her husband to come to the United States, and in time the couple moved to New Jersey, where Kyung Wan became a licensed therapeutic massager, opening up her first clinic in Flushing with her husband as her assistant, and then a second one in New Jersey. Even in retirement, people still visit her at her home for her massages, sometimes bringing small gifts and fruit. Though her life has been marked by many ups and downs, she reminds us to remember that whatever happens—good and bad—will all pass one day; it’s better to find your footing in the present, and just follow the flow.
Keywords: Seoul; occupation; Hwanghae; North Korea, grandparents; border; marriage; banchan; tutor; high school; choir; May 16; coup; National Assembly; typewriting; New Jersey; Flushing; therapy; flow; Chicago

When she was 2 years old, her father was offered a job in Hwanghae Province, which today is a part of North Korea. The family lived in Hwanghae Province until 1945, when Korea was liberated, after which the family moved back South to a rural part of Gyeonggi Province, where Kyung Wan’s grandparents lived and worked on a farm. She recalls how, around the age of 16, people began expressing interest in marrying her, and that because food was so scarce, her parents tried to wed her to a stranger; eventually, she ran away to her uncle who resided in Seoul. Her uncle enrolled her in school, but because of a shortage of funds Kyung Wan had to find a way to make money, which she did by offering tutoring services. During her third year of high school, she decided to learn a trade in preparation for entering the workforce; she graduated with a license in typewriting, and soon began working for the National Assembly Secretariat, where she stayed until the 5.16 coup of 1961. In 1967, she married her husband who had just quit his job; with no source of income, Kyung Wan made and sold banchan for 2 years, after which she took up tutoring again. Soon thereafter, her sister—who had moved abroad to Chicago—invited Kyung Wan and her husband to come to the United States, and in time the couple moved to New Jersey, where Kyung Wan became a licensed therapeutic massager, opening up her first clinic in Flushing with her husband as her assistant, and then a second one in New Jersey. Even in retirement, people still visit her at her home for her massages, sometimes bringing small gifts and fruit. Though her life has been marked by many ups and downs, she reminds us to remember that whatever happens—good and bad—will all pass one day; it’s better to find your footing in the present, and just follow the flow.
Keywords: Seoul; occupation; Hwanghae; North Korea, grandparents; border; marriage; banchan; tutor; high school; choir; May 16; coup; National Assembly; typewriting; New Jersey; Flushing; therapy; flow; Chicago

Kapsong Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Kapsong
Kapsong Kim was born in Seoul, Korea, in 1965, prior to the democratization of the country. He attributes his developing a social consciousness at an early age to his “terrible” childhood which was defined largely by school, where students would be subject to perform “ceremonial marches” whenever board members visited the campus; in an effort to showcase the students’ discipline, teachers and principals would force them to march. Kim, who was the youngest and smallest of his class with poor coordination to bat, would tire before the rest of his classmates and be hit by his teachers, an experience which would shape the rest of his life. As soon as the opportunity arose, he left Korea for the United States, where he dreamed of becoming a journalist and television producer who could make known all of the injustices committed by humans against other people. In 1984 he arrived in America, studying for two years before joining the Korean Resource Center in Los Angeles (1986) to pursue community advocacy and community organization. Here, he met Yoon Han Bong, a leader of the Gwangju Uprising and a storied figure of the Korean democratization movement. He was struck by the man’s humble demeanor, and was overcome with a sense of duty and service for others. Weathering financial hardship, Kim then worked in community organization for ten years, moving to New York in the meantime where he worked at another Korean resource center (Minkwon), after which he got married and had his first child. Having worked as a reporter before, he became a full-time journalist in the city, covering news concerning the Asian and immigrant community; eventually, he became editor-in-chief. After working at the paper for 30 years, he returned to community organization work in 2019, just before the pandemic. When Covid hit, his office began receiving calls requesting translation services for unemployment insurance applications, and over the course of the next year and a half, Kim, who had made his phone number public, personally fielded 50,000 calls; today, people recognize him by the sound of his voice in Flushing and beyond. Although the work he does is difficult, he says that there’s no other sense of feeling like accomplishing something for your community. He leaves us with a message of anti-authoritarian and anti-oppression, though he also reminds us that working to enact change requires deliberate planning.
Keywords: Seoul; democracy; march; school; childhood; teacher; journalist; television; reporter; Minkwon; Los Angeles; New York; Yoon Han Bong; Gwangju; Covid; Flushing; phone bank; newspaper; chief; plan

Stephanie Jang (Full)
Interviewee: Jang, Stephanie
Stephanie Jang was born in South Korea the eldest of three sisters. She describes how, in the 1970s, many Koreans wanted to come to America owing to increasing political and economic turmoil, which would eventually culminate in mass democratization movements across the country. When Stephanie was in 7th grade, her family made an attempt at coming to the United States through an immigration broker, though they ended up being scanned, losing a lot of money. Her family tried for a second time when she was a junior in high school, though because of a family emergency which required their attention, their window of entry into America once again closed. Finally, when she was 19 years old, she and her family successfully immigrated to the United States after her uncle, a professor at MIT, sponsored their arrival. She recalls how life as a “Korean American” began rather smoothly: she attended college, met her husband soon after, and had a daughter. After living in Manhattan for 4 years, she moved back to Korea in 1994 with her husband and daughter; her husband was the eldest son of his family, and her in-laws had pressured her into returning to the motherland where she could attend to the familial obligations set out by her husband’s family. They had a second daughter, which Stephanie solemnly realized was a kind of “disappointment” to her husband’s family, which had been expecting a son. When her younger daughter turned 2 years old, she decided to file for divorce and move back to the United States with her children, beginning a new life (again!) at 39 years old. Empowered by her education, she pursued a career in business, and then in teaching, and today works as a college counselor as well as a Councilwoman for Palisades Park, taking pride in her work to uplift the Korean American community.
Keywords: Patriarchy; Seoul; Chungcheong; MIT; daughter; 1994; immigration; scam; divorce; Palisades Park; college; counselor; teacher; education; business; Councilwoman; local government

Stephanie Jang (Edited)
Interviewee: Jang, Stephanie
Stephanie Jang was born in South Korea the eldest of three sisters. She describes how, in the 1970s, many Koreans wanted to come to America due to political and economic turmoil; when she was 19 years old, she, alongside her parents and sisters, were one such family to immigrate to the United States after being sponsored by close relative in Massachusetts. Her life as a new “Korean American” began smoothly: she attended college, met her husband, and had a daughter. In 1994, she moved with her husband and daughter back to Korea, where she was put through a series of hardship as the sole daughter-in-law (“myeoneuri”) of her husband’s family, an unsolicited title which came replete with burdensome expectations. After having a second daughter, she decided that she did not want to raise her children in the difficult environment, and so moved back to the United States to start her life anew at the age of 39. Empowered by her education, she pursued a career in business, and then in teaching, and today works as a college counselor as well as a Councilwoman for Palisades Park, taking pride in her work to uplift the Korean American community.
Keywords: Patriarchy; Seoul; Chungcheong; MIT; daughter; 1994; immigration; scam; divorce; Palisades Park; college; counselor; teacher; education; business; Councilwoman; local government

Thomas Park Clement (Full)
Interviewee: Clement, Thomas Park
Thomas Park Clement was born in Seoul around 1951-1952, in the middle of the Korean War, to a Korean mother and a white GI father. He recalls how his earliest intact memories are those of war, seeing and hearing flashes of explosions and bombings from his crib; this experience of war, he tells us, has stayed with him through reoccurring nightmares. In July 1953, the noise stopped and at four and a half years old, his mother took him onto the streets of Seoul, telling him to look down the street as she disappeared. It was the last he saw of his biological family, and he was soon brought to an orphanage by a Methodist nurse. He describes how mixed children were treated horribly at orphanages by other children (and sometimes, by staff), and for the next year and a half he endured constant bullying and belittlement before being adopted by the Clement family. Nothing about this process as explained to him—he could only speak Korean—but he remembers finding a degree of solace and groundedness in the cartoon Clyde Crashcup, whose titular character comes up with clever inventions to solve both mundane and ridiculous problems. Like his cartoon hero, Thomas strived to become an inventor and after studying engineering in college, he became an inventor of medical technologies with 77 patents to his name after being hired by a biotech company. In time, he met his wife on a humanitarian trip to Pyeongyang, where he would teach at a medical school; she was his translator. He feels as though Korean adoptees have been overlooked, and has been invited by the Korean government to speak at various adoptee events since 1998. His involvement with his community includes donating DNA testing kits for those curious about their heritage.
Keywords: Adoptee; adoption; seoul; Korea; methodist; nurse; orphanage; war; explosions; nightmare; street; Clyde Crashcup; inventor; engineering; medicine; humanitarian; North Korea; DNA; testing; kit; patents; technology

Thomas Park Clement (Edited)
Interviewee: Clement, Thomas Park
Thomas Park Clement was born in Seoul in the middle of the Korean War. He tells us that the earliest memories he has are of the flashes and noises from explosions outside his crib area, and that the trauma of war would resurface in reoccurring fever dreams for years afterward. When he was four and a half years old, his biological mother led him to a street where she instructed him to look down the road in one direction; this would be the final time he saw his biological family. Soon thereafter, a methodist nurse found and brought him to an orphanage, where he endured constant bullying and belittlement alongside other mixed children. A year later, he was adopted into the Clement family to begin the next chapter of his life in America. Inspired by the cartoon “Clyde Crashcup,” he strived to become an inventor, and today has 77 patents to his name in medical technology, and has donated funds for members of the Korean American Adoptees’ community to receive DNA testing kits for free.
Keywords: Adoptee; adoption; seoul; Korea; methodist; nurse; orphanage; war; explosions; nightmare; street; Clyde Crashcup; inventor; engineering; medicine; humanitarian; North Korea; DNA; testing; kit; patents; technology

Wonsook Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Wonsook
Wonsook Kim was born in 1953 in Busan, South Korea, just after the Korean War ended. On her first birthday, she and her family moved back to Seoul, where her family was originally from. The second of eight children, Wonsook was born into a large family, with aunts, grandmothers, and many, many children living under the same roof. As a child, she remembers feeling a sense of disappointment in herself for not having been born a boy; her father had grown up with only brothers, as did her grandfather and her great-grandfather. While her parents waited for a son, they raised Wonsook in a boy-ish way, dressing her in boy clothing, and cutting her hair short (her younger sibling would be born a boy). She also remembers worrying a lot as a child, anxious to always do the right thing in order to be recognized as someone who was a delight and a joy to be around (perhaps, she teases, this is why she is an empath). Though she says that she wasn’t “good at school,” she always tried her best, though her efforts would often go unnoticed. Later into her career, when she and her family was being interviewed on the subject of Wonsook’s childhood, she recalls how her parents couldn’t provide an answer to the question. “Was she any different?”, with both parents admitting that they didn’t remember much about her as a child aside from how she’d get in trouble for doodling on the walls. In hindsight, Ms. Kim states that it was perhaps due to this distance that she was able to grow as an artist; after studying for a bit at Honggik University, which was known for its arts program, she took a leave to study English for a year so that she might be able to study art in the United States. This distance grew when she came to the United States to pursue college and graduate school at Illinois State University, which today houses the Wonsook Kim College of Fine Arts as well as the Wonsook Kim School of Art. Upon graduation, her career took a turn upon winning the 1976 Elizabeth Stein Art Scholarship which allowed her to relocate to New York City, where she worked a variety of jobs to support herself as an artist. In 1977, a passing curator noticed her studio and offered for her work to be displayed at The Drawing Center in SoHo, and the rest is history; since then, she has displayed her artwork at 67 solo exhibitions and galleries. Wonsook stresses how blessed she has been, and that now it’s time for “pay back;” literally, paying back all those who helped her become the artist she is today.
Keywords: Busan; Seoul; Korean War; large family; aunts; grandmothers; boy; clothing; short hair; art; Honggik; Hongdae; interview; ISU; Illinois; Illinois state university; graduate school; fine arts; Wonsook Kim College of Fine Arts; Wonsook Kim school of art; SoHo; Drawing Center

Wonsook Kim (Edited)
Interviewee: Kim, Wonsook
Wonsook Kim was born in 1953 in Busan, South Korea, just after the war’s end. Upon her first birthday, her family moved back to Seoul where they had lived prior to the Korean War; Ms. Kim recounts growing up in a large family—the 2nd of 8 children—with her many siblings, aunts, and two grandmothers. As the 2nd eldest daughter, she felt as though she was an outsider in many ways, always in the background, while worrying about what she could do be recognized as someone who brought delight and joy into different spaces. In hindsight, Ms. Kim states that it was perhaps due to this distance that she was able to grow as an artist. This distance grew when she came to the United States to pursue college and graduate school at Illinois State University, which today houses the Wonsook Kim College of Fine Arts as well as the Wonsook Kim School of Art. Upon graduation, her career took a turn upon winning the 1976 Elizabeth Stein Art Scholarship which allowed her to relocate to New York City, where she worked a variety of jobs to support herself as an artist. In 1977, a passing curator noticed her studio and offered for her work to be displayed at The Drawing Center in SoHo, and the rest is history; since then, she has displayed her artwork at 67 solo exhibitions and galleries. Ms. Kim stresses how blessed she feels to be able to do what she loves with the full support of her husband and children, and is determined to “pay back”—her words—all those who supported her throughout her  long career.

Sam Hyun (Full)
Interviewee: Hyun, Sam
Sam Hyun was born and rasied in Boston, Massachusetts, where he spent the majority of his childhood and adult years before relocating to Washington, D.C. His father had immigrated to Boston during the 1980s before separating with his first wife; he met Sam’s mother in Korea, and after their marriage they both moved to Boston. Sam has an older sister from his father’s previous marriage, as well as a younger sister, though he describes being raised mostly by his mother who worked two to three jobs at any given moment to support her children and make ends meet while his father was perennially out of the home on “business trips.” Telling his family that “he was so close” to making it big, Sam’s father was absent from most of his life, and the disappointments which accumulated over the years strained his father’s relationship with the rest of his family. He recalls how his mother had expressed a desire to operate her own small business, wanting to buy the laundromat she worked at; in time, her frugal lifestyle and persistent saving made it possible for her to buy the store. Sam’s father, outraged that she had bought the business, expressed his contempt, which spiraled into a physical altercation between Sam and his father; afterwards, Sam tells us that his father left for Korea, for good. He describes the cycle of self-hatred, resentment, and struggle which is often masked by an intoxication with achieving the American Dream, a struggle which he witnessed take over his father’s life. Though his relationship with his father has since been reconciled, Sam talks about how it was his mother who, by asking other adult male figures in Sam’s life to look out for him, that made sure Sam never felt at a loss when it came to his relationships with mentor figures. Today, he is co-founder of 1587sneakers, as well as the Director of Government Relations at The Asian American Foundation, working to change both the narrative of how Asian Americans present ourselves, as well as the harmful narratives to which we subscribe.
Keywords: Boston; Massachusetts; family; divorce; father; strained relationship; mother; laundromat; American Dream; dream; business trip; single mother; single-mother; 1587sneakers; the asian American foundation; director

Sam Hyun (Edited)
Interviewee: Hyun, Sam
Sam Hyun was born and raised in Boston, Massachusetts, where he spent most of his life before moving to D.C. Sharing his family story, he tells us of how the immigrant’s psyche can be worn down and damaged in the pursuit of the American Dream, an experience he both witnessed and encountered through his relationship with his father. Raised by his mother, Sam recalls how she had to endure grueling hours of multiple jobs to care for her family while his father, who traveled often on business trips, was largely absent from his life. He shares with us a memory of the exact moment his father left the family for good: an argument over his mother purchasing the laundromat she had worked at for years, a decision which would improve the family’s financial prospects but one which exposed the pride and insecurities of a father who had failed to live up his myth of an American life. Today, as Co-Founder of @1587sneakers and the Director of Government Relations at @taaforg, Sam works to change both the narrative of how Asian Americans present ourselves, as well as the harmful narratives to which we subscribe.
Keywords: Boston; Massachusetts; family; divorce; father; strained relationship; mother; laundromat; American Dream; dream; business trip; single mother; single-mother; 1587sneakers; the asian American foundation; director

Eugenia Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Eugenia
Eugenia Kim was born in 1952 in White Plains, New York, to parents who had arrived in America between World War II and the Korean War. The second of three children, she recalls how her family had certain privileges owing to her father’s work in the US military government, a job which afforded him the opportunity to relocate his family to America before the Korean War. Her parents arrived first in Los Angeles, where her father’s brother lived and worked as a minister; her own father, too, would study to become a minister. After a year, her parents moved once more, this time to the East Coast, where her father had been offered a job for the Voice of America due to his fluency across English, Korean, and Japanese. She recalls how her parents were early leaders in the Korean American movement in Washington, D.C., during a time when its Korean community was beginning to grow. Surrounded by Korean people and culture, Eugenia often joined her mother in making kimchi to give to Korean restaurants in the DMV area. She felt as though she had, in her own words, “everything but the language,” making a remark about how many hours she had to spend at the Korean church every week. After college, she moved to Miami, Florida, to live with her sister for a while; to make ends meet, she worked at a restaurant, then in hospitality for the next four years until her sister moved to Hawaii. She then spent some time in Fort Lauderdale before moving back home, where she married an school friend who, like her, had grown up between cultures as the child of parents who worked in the foreign service. She then transitioned her career toward Graphic Design, had a child, and separated from her husband on good terms (they remain friends to this day). Feeling a creative itch, she studied writing, graduated with an MFA, and emerged from her program convinced that she was a “Korean American writer.” Believing that creative expression is a powerful means of meditating on one’s identity, she encourages anyone who has ever struggled with the question “Who am I?” to turn to storytelling for an answer. Today, she is the author of two published novels.
Keywords: White Plains; New York; WWII; Korean War; government; US military; military; translator; languages; MFA; creative writing; writing; novelist; books; Graphic Design; Miami; Los Angeles; Washington; church; storytelling; culture

Eugenia Kim was born in 1952 in White Plains, New York, to parents who immigrated to the United States between World War II and the Korean War.
