Joy Lee Gebhard (Full)
Interviewee: Gebhard, Joy Lee
Joy Lee Gebhard was born in Tokchon, Korea, during the Japanese occupation of. Korea. When she was one and a half years old, her family moved from Tokchon to Pyeongyang, a city whose short distance from Tokchon made it possible for her grandmother to move with them. In Pyeongyang, she remembers spending her childhood playing around the massive royal tombs near her home in Nangang-ri; in her words, those royal tombs became something of a playground for her and her friends. When the Japanese military entered the city, Joy saw how the tombs were exhumed for their treasures, systematically removed as for the Japanese to secure Korean artifacts. From a young age, Joy was aware that she was not “Japanese,” and she adamantly expressed pride in her Korean heritage; as a schoolgirl, her class began each instructional day by hailing the Japanese emperor three times in the school courtyard, a ritual Joy refused to take part in (her teacher, fearing retribution, would raise her arms for her). Upon liberation, her father slaughtered the family’s single cow (which had been used for farmwork) so that her neighborhood could celebrate with meat. When the Korean War began soon after, she was in boarding school; after arriving home, a couple of South Korean men were at her door looking for their family. One of the men asked Joy’s mother if she would allow Joy to come to the south with them, fearing violence in Pyeongyang; her mother agreed, and this was the last time Joy would see her. The men brought her to Busan, where she enrolled in a military nursing school (but was treated suspiciously owing to her North Korean origins). She found work as a private tutor, news broadcaster, choir organizer, and interpreter, eventually receiving a sponsorship from a Texan minister to come to the United States. Separated even further from the rest of her family, Joy tried to make contact with her family in North Korea through the Red Cross, the North Korean embassy in Moscow, China, Poland, and the United Nations before receiving a letter from North Korean officials, granting her wish to reunite (if only briefly) with her family. In 1988, she visited North Korea for the first time, and has gone back many times since.
Keywords: Tokchon; North Korea; South Korea; Busan; Pyeongyang; Korean War; emperor; occupation; Japanese; military; nangang; liberation; nurse; Texas; minister; United Nations; embassy; reunite; family; mother; return; tomb

Joy Lee Gebhard (Edited)
Interviewee: Gebhard, Joy Lee
Joy Lee Gebhard was born in Tokchon, North Korea, during the Japanese occupation. When she was one and a half years old her family moved to Pyeongyang, where she remembers spending her childhood around the massive royal tombs near her home, as well seeing their exhumation and excavation by the Japanese Army. Upon liberation, her father slaughtered the family’s single cow, which had been used to work farmland, to hand out cuts of meat to the community in celebrating their independence. During the Korean War, she was brought to the South Korea by a South Korean man visiting Pyeongyang in search of his family, promising her mother that she would be able to live a fuller life south of the 38th parallel; this would be the last time she ever saw her mother. In South Korea, she found herself at a military nursing school in Busan, where both South Korean and American soldiers were fascinated by the fact that she was from the North, at times treating her with suspicion. She worked a variety of jobs to study at Busan University, one of which was as a morning news broadcaster, before receiving an unprompted invitation by a minister in Texas to come to the United States; apparently, news of a “North Korean” nurse in Busan had circulated around the world. She emphasizes how it was never her intention to come to the U.S. as it would further separate her from her family; even from America, her search for her family continued as she sent countless letters to the embassies of countries which maintained diplomatic relations with the newly partitioned country. Upon the invitation of the North Korean government, she was briefly reunited with her family in 1988, and has gone back multiple times since then.
Keywords: Tokchon; North Korea; South Korea; Busan; Pyeongyang; Korean War; emperor; occupation; Japanese; military; nangang; liberation; nurse; Texas; minister; United Nations; embassy; reunite; family; mother; return; tomb

MiRan Powell (Full)
Interviewee: Powell, MiRan
MiRan Powell was born in Texas in 1964 to a Korean mother and white father. At the time of her (and her sister’s) birth, anti-miscegenation laws were still in place in the state of Texas, and would remain in place until Loving v. Virginia ruled such laws unconstitutional in 1967. She and her older sister quickly discovered, however, that if one was deemed physically attractive, the racism which generally accompanied day-to-day interactions in the South would quickly melt away. Yet MiRan recounts how her dual heritage gave her and her sister much grief and confusion across their childhood: their mother, hoping for her daughters to better assimilate into the American milieu, did not teach them Korean aside from a few folk songs. Furthermore, because her maternal relatives resided in North Korea, she was unable to meet them, and so she came to know Korea as a fairy tale, articulated only through her mother’s stories of her own homeland. She would be teased for her name, MiRan, and internalized feelings of self-hate and racism, often feeling that to be Asian was to be undesirable and not beautiful. During her teenage years, she admits to causing her mother some trouble and heartache as she continued to navigate her dual heritage; today, she prefers the term “Eurasian”—a word which, through her mother’s continued usage, became a term of endearment—over “mixed.” She expresses gratitude that her mother as able to not only tolerate their anger (which was often directed at her parents), but shield them from judgement from the rest of the world. In her career as an actress, MiRan has played a wide range of characters including a British heiress, Japanese maiden, and a Greek Princess; however she was casted, MiRan tells us that her goal was always to just become “the best actress that she could be.” She tells us that there are not enough roles to make a living out of playing exclusively “Asian” characters, and would advise her younger self to choose a different career. She finds solace in her sister, who has shared MiRan’s sense of isolation, as someone with whom she can communicate feelings of “not fitting in;” she tells us that the inability to fit in anywhere, compounded by not having someone to understand this struggle, can lead to mental illness. Alongside her work as an actress, she works to bring visibility to the issue of formally ending the Korean War, and speaks pridefully of her experiences attending peace symposiums in Washington, D.C.
Keywords: Texas; mixed; Eurasian; Amerasian; anti; miscegenation; Loving; Virginia; 1967; mental illness; actress; acting; Japanese; British; myth; Greek; Korean War; peace; symposium; isolation; confusion; identity; mother; daughter; sister

MiRan Powell (Edited)
Interviewee: Powell, MiRan
MiRan Powell was born in Texas in 1964, to a Korean mother and white father. At the time of her and her sister’s birth, anti-miscegenation laws were still in effect across the United States, and she recalls how her dual heritage gave her and her sister a lot of grief and confusion in childhood. Her mother, hoping that her daughters would better assimilate into the American milieu, did not teach them Korean aside from a few folk songs, and because her maternal relatives resided in North Korea, she could not meet them; the stories her mother told her of Korea were little more than fairy tales. In her career as an actress, MiRan has played a wide range of characters, including a British heiress, Japanese maiden, and a Greek Princess; however she was casted, MiRan tells us that her goal was always to just become “the best actress that she could be.” On stage, she has had to find and command a confidence which she hoped she had had as a child, navigating the anxieties of her identity, but she has since turned this confidence into a sense of responsibility, advocating for peace on the Korean peninsula.
Keywords: Texas; mixed; Eurasian; Amerasian; anti; miscegenation; Loving; Virginia; 1967; mental illness; actress; acting; Japanese; British; myth; Greek; Korean War; peace; symposium; isolation; confusion; identity; mother; daughter; sister

Peter Sohn (Full)
Interviewee: Sohn, Peter
Peter Sohn was born in the Bronx, New York, to parents who immigrated to the United States between the 1960s-70s. His father was originally from Miryang and immigrated to the United States in the late 60s or early 70s after a failed business venture in Korea; naturally, he thought New York City would be the best place to start a business from scratch. His mother arrived in the United States later in the 70s through a military program which placed Korean nurses in America, arriving first in Chicago before relocating to New York. He recalls his parents telling him about how in the 70s, there weren’t many Koreans in New York; both parents were products of the Korean War, and had endured difficult childhoods before making the difficult decision to move abroad. He tells us of how his mother would catch bugs as a child for something to eat, and that she continued to be frugal throughout her life. His father’s first store, Peter recalls, had shelving constructed out of broomsticks; before long, he was able to buy out a neighboring commercial space. He also operated a grocery store, and soon opened an art supply store. Peter would spend most of his free time helping out at his father’s small businesses, or at his uncle’s SAT prep school. His parents would become frustrated with his practice test scores—he’d draw all over his exams—and it was clear from an early age that Peter had a talent for the visual arts. His parents were resistant to the idea of Peter pursuing art as a career, but his father opened up to the idea when a customer, who worked in animation for a living, suggested that animation was a viable career. His mother, on the other hand, didn’t share his father’s enthusiasm, and would butt heads with Peter on the subject. Yet always working to mend their relationship were movies, which Peter believes contains stories of home which, for immigrant families, is not often openly discussed. After high graduating from high school, he enrolled in CalArts to further study animation and found work as an animator at Disney Pixar. He reflects on fatherhood; as the only member of his family living on the West Coast (the rest around New York City), he feels more sympathetic to his parents’ own early struggles of finding community in a new place. While working on the film Elemental, a story drawn from his own childhood experiences of watching his parents work small businesses, both of Peter’s parents would pass away, leaving him and his brother to keep their memories alive. Their experiences have shaped and informed the stories Peter animates, directs, and creates today, speaking to the valences of immigrant life which he has masterfully captured through film.
Keywords: Bronx; New Rochelle; grocer; art supply; small business; Miryang; artist; animation; animator; Pixar; Disney; New York; New York City; CalArts; Los Angeles; brother; bug; bugs; elemental; dinosaur; Korean War; frugal; movie; movies; immigrant; narratives; storytelling; hollywood

Diana Son (Full)
Interviewee: Son, Diana
Diana Son was born in Philadelphia, though her family moved to Dover, Delaware, when she was young; she attributes most of her formative memories to experiences and interactions in dover. She recalls being especially close with her older brother, with whom she was a self-described “latchkey kid;” after school, she would watch popular talk shows on television which instilled an early passion for narrative, storytelling, and writing. In the fourth grade, her teacher gave the class an assignment asking them to write about what they were “thankful for,” and Diana’s writing gained a recognition from her teacher which she says has stuck with her since, cementing her goal of becoming a writer. She wrote for her middle and high school newspaper; Diana tells us that she had wanted to become a writer until she discovered the theater when she was 18 years old; during the senior year of high school, a certain “cool teacher from New York City” took her English class on a field trip to New York City, where they saw a production of Hamlet at the public theater. And yet, to Diana, the public theater felt like the metropolitan opera house; walking around the big city, she recalls seeing the white and purple flags of New York University, which her teacher insisted she apply to (her parents had given up on the idea that she become a doctor by this point). Around the time she enrolled at the institution, however, her mother had a stroke, which left her in need of care. Diana would spend half of the week in New York City, attending lecture and working part-time at La MaMa theater (where her first professional production was put on!), and the other half of the week at Wilmington, Delaware, which she would get to by train; she tells us that, during this time in her life, she simply wanted someone to tell her that it as ok for someone to live her life. Despite her storied and successful career in production and playwriting, she believes that she is still growing, learning, and “evolving” as an artist, telling us that there’s still so much more she wishes to accomplish; in her own words, “I’m still hungry.”
Keywords: Philadelphia; Dover; Wilmington; Delaware; New York; New York City; NYU; university; theater; writing; teacher; hamlet; metropolitan; opera; La MaMa; lamama; stroke; encouragement; life

Jeanne Yang (Full)
Interviewee: Yang, Jeanne
Jeanne Yang was born in Los Angeles, California, in 1968, the second child to Korean parents who had immigrated to the United States in the 1960s. Her mother had arrived in the country with a mere 20 dollars, while her father had arrived as a student at an American university. She tells us of how while her father pursued his studies, her mother would work 17-hour days across three jobs to make ends meet; as a young family, the four lived in a converted garage with a “converted dresser” packed with blankets for a bed. After the family got on steady footing, they moved to nearby Monterey Park where Jeanne lived until she went to college to pursue studies in pre-law. Her postgraduate life mirrors those of many second-generation children; after college, her parents suggested she find work at a law firm with the expectation that she might return to school in pursuit of a law degree, though after a while in the legal field, she soon became disillusioned by the monotony of the work. In the early 1990s, Jeanne pivoted careers from law into the flourishing entertainment industry of Los Angeles, working as a stylist for music videos, then for magazines, and afterwards for Hollywood. She details racism—both blatant and microaggressions—such as when she got chewed out by a creative director of a styling set for looking out for an intern who had made a mistake. Yet she has left lasting impressions, and continues to make her mark, within the fashion industry, working alongside the likes of Christian Bale, Keanu Reeves, Jason Momoa, Sam Worthington, and other A-list actors in Hollywood to make them look their best both on and off the red carpet. She expresses deep pride in how far the entertainment industry has evolved with respect to diversity and minority representation, while acknowledging that there is much more work that is yet to be done. At times, the work of the trailblazer has been exhausting, such as when she worked for 4 months in a row without a day off alongside a team of Oscar-winning actors and producers, a pace she found “unsustainable.” And yet, she finds it powerful to choose to work a passion, with the question “What will be your legacy?” at the forefront of the decisions she has made throughout her career.
Keywords: Stylist; Hollywood fashion; red carpet; entertainment; wardrobe; celebrity styling; diversity; minority representation; racism; microaggressions; second-generation; immigration; Monterey Park; career pivot; law; creativity; trailblazer; work-life balance; perseverance; legacy

Jeanne Yang (Edited)

Jeanne Yang was born in Los Angeles in 1968 as the second child of immigrant parents. Jeanne’s first career was in law, following her parents’ wishes, but she soon became disillusioned with the legal system and pivoted towards the world of entertainment, where her career as a stylist flourished. Will Yun Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Will Yun
Will Yun Lee was born in 1971 in Arlington, Virginia, though he spent much of his childhood growing up between cities owing to his parents’ rocky marriage. Until he was about four years old, he describes quite the idyllic childhood, saying, in his own words, that his small family lived the “pinnacle of the American dream.” Yet when his parents separated when Will was only four years old, he spent his early youth moving between homes and families while his father tried to gain stable footing in different businesses—during this time, Will tells us that he attended over 20 schools within the span of 5 years. By the time he was 10 years old, his father had resettled in Hawaii, where he had opened a Taekwondo studio; his father had been among the first taekwondo masters to immigrate to the United States in the 1960s. Will moved to Hawaii to live with his father, and the two resided in a motel for a while, though he describes the early moments of their reunion as the beginning of a “true relationship” with his father. Will was recruited to attend the University of California, Berkeley, on an athletic scholarship, but paused his studies halfway through to aid his father with his martial arts studio. During this time, he describes looking up to Jason Scott Lee’s as Bruce Lee in Dragon as an example of wedding talent—martial arts and athleticism—with a burgeoning interest in acting; “If I’m gonna be broke, might as well be broke doing something I wanna do.” After being introduced to a screenwriter who was a student at his father’s studio, Will was inspired to make his way into Hollywood, studying acting in his free time while continuing to help his father’s business. He quickly realized the pervasive nature of typecasting in the industry, with his white friends getting 3-4 auditions a day while he, alongside other Asian American actors, would get maybe one audition a month (all for the same role!) In his first roles he describes being given only three to five lines which, to him, felt like Shakespeare. During his career, he has been cast as the stereotypical “Kung Fu” killing machine, the sympathetic character who dies on screen, to finally starring as the hero, though he acknowledges that the industry has come a long way in casting Asian American actors for a wider variety of roles.
Keywords: Martial arts; Hollywood; acting; typecasting; stereotypes; diversity; representation; auditions; Asian American; immigration; Taekwondo; athletic scholarship; Berkeley; father-son; perseverance; resilience; mentorship; role models; identity; hero

Will Yun Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Will Yun
Will Yun Lee was born in 1971 Arlington, Virginia, though he tells us that he spent much of his childhood moving around. After his parents’ divorce, he was taken cared for by family and friends for a while before moving in back with his father, who operated a taekwondo studio in Hawaiʻi. Taking time off from college to help out with the studio, he started to explore pathways into acting after a conversation with a screenwriter, who happened to be a student at the taekwondo studio. Will tells us that since his career began in 1997, so much around Asian American representation on screen has changed; once relegated to playing doctors or “nerdy” hackers, he is amazed to see Asian Americans in leading roles today.

Kymber Lim (Full)
Interviewee: Lim, Kymber
Kymber Lim was born in Seoul, South Korea, and immigrated to Georgia in 1974; a year earlier, her mother had moved to the American South to work as a nurse under a government initiative which sought to offer expedited citizenship in exchange for workers who could help address a nurse shortage in the region. Kymber, and the rest of her family, followed her mother to Georgia and was raised in a protective household which didn’t expect her to lead an ambitious life. Part of this protectiveness was due to an attempted kidnapping which Kymber survived at the age of eleven, a terrifying moment which drove her parents to encourage her to stay close to home for college. Kymber found some freedom while attending college an hour and a half away from home, though it wasn’t until she learned of, and participated in, a national student exchange program at Cal State Northridge that she felt free to pursue her interests in video production. The early 1990s were an exciting time for the VFX industry, with new editing and production technologies being developed for burgeoning artists, such as Kymber, to explore; while in college, she put these technologies to use across half a dozen projects, including commercials for brands such as Jack in the Box. After college, she found work as an associate producer for film and television, leveraging her background across media technologies within Hollywood. She worked in creating interactive experiences at Disney, which opened the door for working at other film studios such as Paramount, where she worked on Mission Impossible 2 alongside John Woo, an Asian director of a blockbuster film. In 2005, director Bong Joon Ho reached out to Kymber for VFX production in the film The Host, where she and his team brought the aquatic monster to life on the big screen using the latest breakthroughs in American VFX. She has since worked on projects at Netflix and Apple, and was tasked by the Overseas Korean Agency—a governmental division of the South Korean administration—to create what began as a mentorship program for Korean Americans in the entertainment industry; this would lead to her founding Korean American Leaders in Hollywood. “What a difference it would have made,” she says, “to have had someone to turn to for guidance.”
Keywords: VFX; Hollywood; film production; video production; media technology; Disney; Paramount; Mission Impossible; The Host; Bong Joon Ho; mentorship; Korean American; nurse; Georgia; Cal State Northridge; leadership; KALH; studio

Kymber Lim (Edited)
Interviewee: Lim, Kymber
Kymber Lim, raised in Georgia, grew up fighting for her ambitions despite her immigrant parents' expectations of a simple life. Her drive led her to college, where she thrived in production classes, impressing professors and diving into video production. This early passion evolved into a career in visual effects, where she worked alongside industry giants like Tom Cruise, John Woo, and Bong Joon-ho. Beyond her career, Kymber is dedicated to empowering the next generation of Korean American talent through her work with the Korean American Leaders in Hollywood (@the.kalh). Focused on bridging gaps for underrepresented voices, she advocates for diversity, mentorship, and increased opportunities for Korean Americans in Hollywood. Her advocacy has made her a key figure in pushing for greater representation in the entertainment industry.

Daniel K Isaac (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Daniel K
Daniel Kyungmin Isaac was born in 1988 in Los Angeles, California, to parents who separated when he was three years old. Growing up between Fullerton, Diamond Bar, and Koreatown, Daniel describes with melancholy the gradual gentrification of the neighborhoods he spent so much time in, expressing gratitude that he was able to grow up in the communities he did despite the hardships of being raised by a single mother. The two continued to attend the same church where his father was a parishioner, and it was at church where Daniel could see his father—later in life, he learned that his mother withstood the stigma of being a divorced single mom in the Korean American community (and church!) so that her son could maintain contact with his father. She also put him through the boy scouts, where he was unofficially taken under the wing of a white family who attended various family, and in particular father-son, activities with him. In both church and high school, Daniel was actively involved in plays, theater, and stage performances, and it during this time as well that the white family encouraged him to undergo conversion therapy in high school. During college application season, he was told to rescind his applications from universities which were out of state, another decision the family made on his behalf in “fear” that he would acquaint himself with his identity as a queer man. Motivated to attend a college with a reputable theater program, Daniel attended the University of California, San Diego, where he not only auditioned for and joined graduate shows as an underclassman, but was also initiated into the La Jolla Playhouse, a professional theater on the college campus which opened doors for him to pursue acting as a profession across the United States. After his freshman year, a troubling exchange between Daniel and his mother led to her disowning him, and him having to live out of his car and between his friends’ couches for the summer between freshman and sophomore year. He jokes that his own stubbornness as inherited from his mother was what pushed him to persevere until graduation, after which he moved to New York City to work in Off-Broadway shows (his first job was at El Repertorio). Today, he has innumerable credits across film, television, and the theater, and is proud to see more Korean Americans gaining recognition under the spotlight.
Keywords: Single mother; gentrification; Koreatown; church; queer identity; theater; stage performances; conversion therapy; college struggle; UC San Diego; La Jolla Playhouse; professional acting; disownment; perseverance; Off-Broadway; New York City; resilience; Korean American; visibility; representation; family dynamics.

Daniel K Isaac (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Daniel K
Daniel K Isaac was born in 1988 in Los Angeles, California, to immigrant parents who separated when he was a toddler. He recalls growing up acting in church plays and partaking in the Boy Scouts, as well as his involvement with his high school’s theatrical productions. Pressured into conversion therapy in high school, he found in the theater a family and community which he desperately longed for, and ultimately decided to study theater at UC San Diego; at university, he would be involved with a school production during every single quarter of his enrollment. In 2009, he moved to New York City after receiving his La Jolla Playhouse equity card and slowly began his professional acting career: today, he has innumerable credits across film, television, and the theater, and is proud to see more Korean Americans gaining recognition under the spotlight.

So Yun Um (Full)
Interviewee: Um, So Yun
So Yun Um was born in Los Angeles, California, to immigrant parents and grew up with the dynamic city as her playground. Full of fashion, enterprise, and a strong Korean community, she tells us of taking rides in 3-dollar taxis with her girlfriends to explore the various treasures Los Angeles had to offer while her parents worked full-time at the family business, a liquor store. She describes holding a deep pride in her Korean heritage, as the culture was prevalent, visible, and everywhere in L.A., though she also expresses a sadness in witnessing the extent to which Koreatown was, and continues to be, gentrified, isolating community residents who have long called the ethnic pocket of Los Angeles home. She also tells us of becoming conscious of her family’s social class as she grew up, comparing herself to her friends whenever she saw the kinds of vacations or gifts they indulged in. She helped her parents at their business, an environment she describes as not one ideal for a young teenager, and saw both the joys and the sorrows her parents had to endure to run such a business: in one instance, she even recounts how her parents were verbally and physically assaulted, a memory which has not only stayed, but deeply influenced, her work today. When she enrolled in college, she decided to “pick a major she knew she’d be interested in for a while,” and so chose to study film with the goal of watching free screenings on campus as part of her studies. After graduation, she made her directorial debut with the documentary feature film Liquor Store Dreams, a film centered about the experiences of second-generation Korena American children of liquor store owners in Los Angeles while also exploring the various race relations of the diverse city. Released in 2022, the documentary has since been screened at dozens of film festivals, including the 27th Busan Film Festival and Diaspora Film Festival 2023, resonating with both Korean audiences, as well as with communities outside of those the film portrays. Though tricky to create a movie which can speak across multiple audiences, So Yun remains undaunted, excited to capture and share stories from the communities which shaped her.
Keywords: Filmmaking; directing; documentary; Liquor Store Dreams; Korean American; Los Angeles; Koreatown; gentrification; race relations; immigrant parents; small business; social class; film festivals; Busan Film Festival; Diaspora Film Festival; storytelling; community; resilience; representation; identity

Kevin Woo (Full)
Interviewee: Woo, Kevin
Kevin Woo was born in 1991 in Seoul, South Korea, to Korean parents who were US citizens. He comes from an extremely musical family: his father was a pianist with a profound talent for singing, his uncle was a trained opera singer, and his aunts were successful artists. Growing up in the Bay Area of Northern California, Kevin’s upbringing reflected a natural attunement to the fine arts, and together with his sister, Kevin would dance, sing along to the karaoke machine, and perform in little “concerts” for his family (he credits the Spice Girls, Shania Twain, and NSYNC as being particularly formative in his musical journey). His experience being Korean American in the Bay speaks to those who felt as though they had a foot in two worlds, and though this “double identity” began as an object of apprehension, he soon embraced his dual heritage through music. With his mother, he would watch and listen to Korean music shows, which led to an interest in not only the music itself, but in the performance of it as well; in 2004, he asked his mother to take him to Korea for an audition to become a K-pop idol. At the time, he saw very few Korean Americans who were a part of Korean boy groups—there weren’t many foreign idols back then—and he feels as though the double identity of his inheritance was met with positive feedback from the executives he auditioned for. Kevin first debuted with the boy group XING in 2006 before re-debuting in 2008 with U-KISS, where he stayed for nearly a decade from when he was 15 years old. Though he fondly recalls his time in the group as a transformative experience which both challenged, and at times constrained, his own creativity, he chose to pursue a solo endeavor in 2017, branching his talents into acting and musical theater. Moving back to California—this time to Los Angeles—he found that he had to deal with a kind of discrimination which hadn’t existed in his Korean career; moreover, he discovered that not all of his skills from his time in K-pop transferred smoothly into Hollywood. Even still, he chose to surmount these initial difficulties (“trusting the process,” he says), and today has numerous credits across American television, dramas, and even Broadway, performing in the 2022 run of the Broadway Musical KPOP!
Keywords: K-pop; XING; U-KISS; solo career; musical theater; Broadway; KPOP musical; Korean American; Bay Area; double identity; performance; singing; dancing; idol training; Hollywood; discrimination; creativity; perseverance; representation; entertainment industry; cross-cultural

Kevin Woo (Edited)
Interviewee: Woo, Kevin
Kevin Woo was born in 1991 in Seoul, South Korea, to quite the musical family: his father was a pianist, his uncle a classically trained opera singer, and his aunts were artists. Growing up in the Bay, Kevin was naturally attuned to music, singing in his church’s praise band and putting on karaoke performances with his sister at home—favorites included the Spice Girls, Shania Twain, and NSYNC. In 2004, when he was thirteen years old, Kevin decided to move his musical career from the living room and onto the big stage: with his mother, he went to Korea to audition to be a K-pop star, and in 2008, Kevin joined the boy group U-KISS. He fondly recalls his time in the group as a transformative experience which both challenged, and at times constrained, his own creativity, and after nearly a decade with U-KISS Kevin decided to make his solo debut. Back in the U.S., Kevin continues to sing, perform, and act, with numerous credits across television, dramas, and even Broadway!

Harry Yoon (Full)
Interviewee: Yoon, Harry
Harry Yoon was born in 1971 in South Korea, moving to the United States with his family when he was just five years old. He fondly recalls his childhood in the East Bay as one in which he was heavily doted on by his family, with a mother who caught early on how verbal her son was; she would set him on the dining table after dinner and ask him to sing and perform for her. This talent, nurtured from a natural desire to entertain people, was a trait his parents found charming, and so when he announced later in life that he wanted to pursue a career as a film editor, both parents were extremely supportive. Before his career in Hollywood, however, Harry worked in the tech industry for nearly a decade; in college, he had dabbled in some film classes in New York as part of an exchange program, but after graduation his parents’ fortunes in business had begun to decline soon after they opened a video store right before the video store chain Blockbuster began to expand. He describes his early 20s as being a time where his mind went back and forth between supporting his parents and following his passions, which he believed could be a risky, but also selfish, endeavor. Yet at the age of 30, after a successful decade working as product manager for a dot com startup, he left tech to pursue a career in Hollywood as an editor after gaining permission from his parents. It was quite a contrast to go from a position of authority in a managerial position to starting at the bottom of the ladder as an intern, and he tells us of how he actually was fired from his first role in industry as a PA for overstepping bounds. Even still, considering it a learning experience (“nunchi” he called it), he continued to work at becoming an editor, with one highlight of his career working on the film Minari with other Korean American talents such as Steven Yeun and Lee Isaac Chung, a generational change in leadership Harry feels blessed to have witnessed. Harry today is a film editor in Los Angeles, having worked on movies and shows such as Euphoria and Beef, and alongside other Korean Americans in the industry, he tells us that “everything has meaning because it’s part of a story, part of a narrative,” encouraging us to look into the stories we’re invested in to see how they might define our futures.
Keywords: Film editing; Hollywood; Minari; Euphoria; Beef; Korean American; East Bay; tech industry; career change; product manager; dot com startup; video store; passion; perseverance; nunchi; storytelling; representation; mentorship; narrative; generational change

Harry Yoon (Edited)
Interviewee: Yoon, Harry
Harry Yoon was born in 1971 in South Korea, and moved to the United States with his family when he was five years old. He recalls growing up in the East Bay fondly, with parents who recognized and nurtured his passion for entertaining others at an early age. Although he did “a bit little film school” in college, his first career was in the tech industry: having seen his parents sacrifice so much for their kids, Harry says that he initially chose to pursue a stable career over his dream of working in film. Yet at the age of 30—with the encouragement of his parents—Harry left his job and sold everything to break into Hollywood, which meant starting over to learn some new lessons. Despite some early setbacks in his new career (such as getting fired from his first PA job), Harry today is a film editor in Los Angeles, having worked on movies and shows such as Euphoria, Beef, and Minari, to name a few. Working alongside other Korean Americans in the industry, he tells us that he witnessed a generational change in leadership within Hollywood, and reminds us that the stories we tell today play a large part in defining our futures.

Leonardo Nam (Full)
Interviewee: Nam, Leonardo
Leonardo Nam was born in 1979 in Buenos Aires, Argentina, the youngest of three children. His parents had immigrated to Argentina in the aftermath of the Korean War hoping to find opportunity outside of the war-recovering country, and Argentina happened to be a state which didn’t require a visa to move to. His parents ran a fruit market, “maybe a restaurant,” and a fashion store as well, though the family moved from Buenos Aires to Sydney, Australia, when Leonardo was six years old (they brought the fashion store with them to the Sydney). Leo describes how he immediately noticed the ways in which he was racialized differently in Australia as he had been in Argentina, though he also points out that there were wonderful and beautiful people in both countries. His passion for performance emerged early in his life, when alongside his older brother and sister he would dance and put on plays for his family; his passion was nurtured by a teacher in an afterschool program which was separated into a “fine arts” and “performing arts” section. Encouraged by his teacher to act, his first ever role was that of Edmund in King Lear; though separated by age and centuries to the bastard child of the iconic Shakespearian play, he was “moved” by Edmund’s passionate monologues, admiring a “majesty” in his rhetoric. When his home environment became tumultuous, he found community and refuge in the world of acting and its community, and after graduating with a degree in architecture, he moved to New York City to pursue drama studies. His first couple of nights in New York were rough; Leo slept on benches in Central Park, bussing tables to make ends meet, while hunting down auditions he could partake in. All the while, he was mindful to avoid those roles which put the limelight on his ethnicity rather than himself, and in a poetic twist of fate, his first ever professional role was that of Osric from Hamlet… but on rollerblades. Since then, Leonardo has played roles across the stage, the television, and the big screen, with credits in Fast and Furious, The Perfect Score, and the HBO series Westworld. Despite the difficult start to his acting career, he strongly emphasizes both the idea and reality of hope, a principle which has guided his career, relationships, and his family; today, he lives in San Diego with his husband and their two children.
Keywords: Acting; Hollywood; theater; Shakespeare; Westworld; Fast and Furious; perseverance; identity; representation; Argentina; Australia; New York; drama school; Central Park; auditions; hope; community; family; storytelling; ethnicity; resilience

Leonardo Nam (Edited)
Interviewee: Nam, Leonardo
Leonardo Nam was born in 1979 in Buenos Aires, Argentina, as the youngest of three children born to Korean immigrants who left the country in the aftermath of the Korean War. When he was six years old his family moved to Australia, and he recalls how it was in an after-school program in Sydney that he was first moved by spirit of acting while playing the role of Edmund in King Lear. He describes the world of drama and theater as providing refuge and community from a tumultuous household, and after completing college, Leonardo moved to New York City to pursue studies in acting. Aware that he would be typecast for his appearance, he found roles whose characters did not solely rely on their ethnicity both on the stage and on the big screen, with credits ranging from Osric in Off-off productions of Hamlet to Felix Lutz in Westworld; most recently, he stars in the horror film Love is the Monster as its male lead. Although he tells us that making a career as an actor has been difficult at times, he strongly emphasizes both the idea and reality of hope, a principle which has guided his career, relationships, and his family.

Grace Yun (Full)
Interviewee: Yun, Grace
Grace Yun is a Production Designer who was born in New Milford, New Jersey, to parents who immigrated to the United States in the 1970s. She describes her childhood as one which was largely spent in isolation, where she could spend hours upon hours getting lost in her daydreams and thoughts. At a young age, Grace moved from New Milford to River Edge, where she grew up by a creek she would frequent with her friends, and sometimes alone; playing with objects such as twigs, bugs, and leaves, she would make stories out of the environment around her (at times, she would rearrange her family’s home furniture, to her parents’ exasperation). She believes that it was in the way that she entertained herself as a child that she was able to develop a strong inner voice, building and moving sets in her mind; she knew early on that she wanted to study some kind of discipline in design at college, and attended Parsons School of Design in New York City to study within the art department. Out of personal interest and curiosity she enrolled in a digital audio class which fascinated her in its all-encompassing reach, and throughout school she slowly transitioned her focus onto set design, PA-ing and interning on various projects such as short films and music videos. She describes her introduction to filmmaking as a “baptism by fire,” where she had to learn aspects of set design, aesthetic harmony, and how best to achieve a director’s vision on the fly; after working in various Production Assistant, Prop Master, and Arti Directing roles, a producer who was short of change reached out to her asking if she’d wanted to work on commercials with him. Through this producer, she was then introduced to the screenwriter and director Paul Schrader, who after a quick thirty-minute interview decided to hire her to work on Dog Eat Dog and First Reformed (he had been looking, as Grace tells us, for “fresh talent”). As a Production Designer, Grace is responsible for all the visual and aesthetic resonances of a movie, show, etc… working closely with the director of photography, as well as other department heads who scrutinize the imagery of film; the best products, she explains, are created when the designer and director are able to successfully marry their artistic visions. After working with Schrader, she was introduced to Celine Song, who shared her vision for Past Lives, the first film Grace describes as aligning so closely with her own experiences growing up. She hopes to tell others, especially young creative people, that the peculiarly odd yet comforting things one does in their childhood blossoms into talents from which you can derive purpose later in life!
Keywords: Production design; set design; aesthetics; storytelling; Parsons; New York; isolation; creativity; childhood; filmmaking; commercials; Paul Schrader; First Reformed; Past Lives; artistic vision; director collaboration; visual harmony; purpose; talent; inspiration

These narratives allow her to authentically portray cultural nuances, while also embracing universal themes that transcend specific backgrounds.

James Shin (Full)
Interviewee: Shin, James
James Shin is the President of Film and Television at HYBE America, working closely with both local and overseas artists to drive and develop Korean and Korean American talent. Born in Chicago to parents who had immigrated from Korea, James describes his family as one of entrepreneurs starting with his maternal grandfather, who made a living after the Korean War by selling clothing, and later capturing the book market, of Busan. He tells us that his mother carried this spirit of perseverance and independence when she decided to move to the United States with little to her name; it was in America that she met James’ father. James lost his father at a young age and was raised primarily by his mother, who worked tirelessly to provide the best opportunities for him and his older brother of eight years. Growing up, he followed in his older brother’s musical footsteps and picked up the violin, and later the piano; his mother, who recognized James’ talent for music, took lengths to nurture it, and as a youth James was recognized by a Russian violin instructor who enrolled James in circuits of competition across the Midwest. His talent for music carried him to a full-ride at Andover, where he was able to perform alongside Yo-Yo Ma, and later an admission to Princeton University, where he was concertmaster. Although he love for music never faltered, James recognized that he was experiencing burnout in the objects which had paved his life up until college, and so he decided to pivot towards a different path, one in entertainment. His mother did not receive the news well, but through alumni networks he was able to intern under the film producer Mike Medavoy, who then opened doors for James to land his first paid job out of college in the mailroom of a talent agency. James studied different pathways of breaking into entertainment and sought to carve out a niche for himself at the intersection of music and film production, and worked alongside the likes of Bruno Mars (as an assistant manager!), Margaret Cho, and BTS; throughout his storied career, James has also worked in television production on shows such as Community, Breaking Bad, and Dave. Having established himself as a music person within the entertainment industry, and driven by the mutual desire which exists in entertainment for collaborative success, James worked in music production for movies and television before being offered to partner with Scooter Braun, who launched a project which would eventually be acquired by HYBE. Considering himself as a “swiss army knife” who can work across both music, sound, and film, he tells us that there is “no such thing as a wasted experience,” believing that failures, as frustrating as they may be, can always be turned into opportunity. He also believes that one day soon, we will see a Korean American head of an entertainment studio!
Keywords: Entertainment industry; HYBE America; music production; film production; television; talent development; Korean American; entrepreneurship; perseverance; Princeton; Andover; violin; Yo-Yo Ma; burnout; career pivot; mentorship; collaboration; opportunity; resilience; leadership

James Shin (Edited)
Interviewee: Shin, James
James Shin is the President of Film and Television at HYBE America, where he develops local and overseas Korean and Korean American talent across music, film, and TV. Born in Chicago to immigrant parents with entrepreneurial spirits, he was a violin and piano prodigy, earning a full scholarship to Andover and later attending Princeton as concertmaster. Recognizing signs of burnout from his musical career, he chose to pivot to the entertainment, studying different pathways through which he might break into the industry. His first roles in entertainment were grueling and consisted of menial labor; he interned under producer Mike Medavoy for no pay, and later found work in the mailroom of a talent agency, where the storied careers of many entertainment executives began. In time, and through remarkable perseverance and grit, James worked alongside artists such as Bruno Mars, Margaret Cho, and BTS, and today his career spans music production, television, and film, with credits on shows such as Community, Breaking Bad, and Dave. Now a key player in global entertainment, James believes that there is no such thing as a waster experience, and tells us that moments of frustration (and failure) can always be turned into an opportunity. He believes, too, that one day, an entertainment studio will soon be led by a Korean American producer.

Ted Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Ted
Ted Kim was born in Seattle, Washington, to immigrant parents who immigrated to the United States in the late 1950s, shortly after the Korean War ended. His father had been born and raised in Pyeongyang during the Japanese Occupation, and in line with Ted’s grandfather and great-grandfather, who were both ministers, Ted’s father would also become a pastor; at the time, Pyeongyang was widely known as the “Jerusalem of the East” owing to its large Christian population, many of whom would make their way to the South to escape increasing persecution. He recounts a story his father told him of how he had to hide in the mountains for most of the war. His mother was had been born in Seoul to a family of politicians; though both families lost much of their livelihoods during the war, Ted tells us that they were able to secure passage to Philadelphia, Pennsylvania through his grandfather, who worked as a chaplain for the United States army (and thus had access to travel back and forth between the US and Korea), where his father had been admitted to seminary to pursue studies in theology. After his father completed his studies, the couple moved to Seattle, where his mother began doctorate studies in English while simultaneously having Ted and his six brothers and sisters and serving as the pastor’s wife of the church his father pastored. Growing up in the Pacific Northwest, Ted recalls not having many Asian American role models to look up to; there was Bruce Lee, but few others. Following in his mother’s footsteps, Ted pursued an undergraduate degree in English, though at his father’s request he later attended law school as well. A few years into his career as a practicing attorney—during which he worked adjacent to entertainment law, and crossed paths with Asian American filmmakers—he was introduced to the vice chairman of CJ Group, Miky Lee, who suggested he come onboard the new US office of the entertainment behemoth; during his career with CJ/MNET, he worked in film and television for eleven years (during which he served as CEO of MNET America), organizing the first ever K-Con in Irvine, California which, to his surprise, had an unexpectedly large turnout (this was before KPOP, KDRAMA, K-etc… were a thing). The goal of the convention for him had been to stitch together disparate groups of fans and artists and bring them to a safe space, where they might relate to each other through art—“nobody wants to be the person dancing by themselves!”
Keywords: Seattle; Korean War; Pyeongyang; Korean Christianity; immigrant experience; U.S. Army chaplain; seminary education; Korean diaspora; Asian American identity; pastor family; Pacific Northwest; English studies; law school; entertainment law; CJ Group; MNET America; K-Con; K-pop; Korean media; cultural connection

Ted Kim was born in Seattle, Washington, to immigrant parents who arrived in the United States shortly after the Korean War ended. Growing up in the Pacific Northwest, Ted recalls not having many Asian American role models to look up to; there was Bruce Lee, but few others. An aspiring writer, he studied English but pivoted to law school at his father’s urging, and has since leveraged his background as a practicing attorney to create spaces where Korean artists and their fans could connect with one another. He recounts meeting with Miky Lee, the vice-chairman of CJ Group, who tapped him to work in their new American offices. Ted has also served as CEO of MNET America, organizing the first KCON which, to his surprise, had an unexpectedly large turnout of fans. With the entertainment industry having grown and diversified throughout his career, Ted tells us to find our passion, figure out what it is we like to do, and do a lot of it.

Charlotte Koh (Full)
Interviewee: Koh, Charlotte
Charlotte Koh was born in Utah as the only child to parents who immigrated to the United States around the turn of the decade in the 1960s/70s; they had gotten engaged in Korea in 1969, shortly after which her father immigrated to Utah as a graduate student before her mother joined him three years later; they got married on New Year’s Eve, 1972, and Charlotte was born a year later. She tells us that her father jumped at the first chance he got to study in the United States, with neither parent very familiar with the academic landscape of America; “we don’t know anything about these American universities” Charlottle quotes her mother as having told her. Soon after Charlotte was born, the young family moved to Chicago, Illinois, where her mother’s brother lived; by a twist of fate, her father had gotten accepted to the University of Illinois in Chicago for a graduate program in the city. Early on in her life, Charlotte recalls how both of her parents emphasized the importance of education, though deep down she had always loved to act, starring in plays from junior high through college; plays which, according to her, were of the “avant-garde” genre, in which she loved the lack of predictability in storytelling. In college, Charlottle studied political science though she also fell in love with independent cinema thanks to her RA (who was a “total film buff”). A freshman in 1991, she made a bet with her parents that if she got into law school she would study to become a attorney; otherwise, she’d be free to try to make a career in entertainment. After graduation, she took a job in London for a management consulting firm whose clientele included MTV Europe, at a time a very rudimentary and underdeveloped station across the pond. Nonetheless, the opportunity opened up pathways for her to pursue a successful and ongoing career in the entertainment industry; she has since worked in executive positions across Fox Searchlight, Marvel, and Hulu. For future generations of Korean Americans who are looking to enter her line of work, she asks that their ambitions be driven by nothing less than an inner authenticity, finding that thing which gives us both fulfillment and joy. She maintains that to this day, her parents don’t really know what she does for work.
Keywords: Utah; Korean immigration; 1970s diaspora; University of Illinois; graduate student life; Korean American family; Chicago; education values; theater arts; avant-garde plays; political science; independent cinema; law school bet; entertainment career; MTV Europe; management consulting; Fox Searchlight; Marvel; Hulu; career authenticity

Stephen Park (Full)
Interviewee: Park Stephen
Stephen Park was born on May 4, 1962 in Clinton Hill, Brooklyn, to immigrant parents who immigrated to the United States shortly after the Korean War. His father, who favored quiet townships over the busy and loud city, moved the family to Waverly, New York, which Stephen found boring and with “not a whole lot to do;” later, the family moved to Vestal, New York, a town near Binghampton. As one of few Asian American students in his class, Stephen describes how he was a “class clown,” relatively popular among his classmates though in a way which he felt was alienating at the same time; were they laughing at him, or with him? After school, many of his “friends” would come over to his home uninvited, which made him reconsider whether he was valued as a friend or if somehow, his Korean identity—his perpetual foreignness—gave the other kids license to take advantage of him. After graduating from high school, he attended Boston University for a few semesters, choosing not to maintain contact with his friends from home; initially going to college with medical school in mind, he found classes to be quite difficult and transferred to SUNY Binghampton to be closer to his family, though he admits that he felt aimless for a while. Considering dropping out, his girlfriend at the time encouraged him to just take a semester of classes which were “fun,” an idea which Stephen had never considered—and something which his parents would’ve surely disapproved of. He ended up taking an acting class, a mime class, and a voice and body movement class, and he found that all of these subjects came naturally to him, perhaps owing to the fact that he had always been a performer for others. Interested in acting in skits, goofing around, and making others laugh, he graduated with a degree in theater, though not before organizing a protest against the department for its racial biases, which culminated in the creation of a student-body “minority rep.” Passionately outspoken on racial injustices in Hollywood as well, Stephen wrote an open letter for the Asian American community to read after witnessing blatant racism on the set of a large show he had been working in; within days, the letter was being openly circulated, and Stephen found himself invited to talks and conferences to speak on the issue of racism and diversity in the entertainment industry. Today, he has numerous credits across film and television, most recently featured in Bong Joon-ho’s Mickey 17 and Alex Scharfman’s Death of a Unicorn; away from the camera, Stephen tries to enjoy the spontaneity and surprises of life… though sometime she fantasizes about the role that’s “gonna push him to [the] next stage.”
Keywords: Clinton Hill; Korean War immigration; Waverly, NY; Vestal, NY; Asian American childhood; class clown; racial alienation; Boston University; SUNY Binghamton; theater degree; acting discovery; Korean identity; racial protest; minority representation; Hollywood racism; open letter activism; Asian American voice; entertainment industry; Mickey 17; Death of a Unicorn

Stephen Park (Edited)

Stephen Park is an actor who was born in Clinton Hill, Brooklyn, to immigrant parents seeking to relocate their family away from the busy city; when he was a child, his family moved to a small town called Waverly before settling in Vestal, New York. Stephen describes how he was a class clown during his schoolboy days, a role he had little choice in playing, and one which was assigned to him by “friends” who teased him for his ethnicity. In college, he found racism latent within his school’s theater’s department, which largely casted white actors for the same rotating repertoire of shows; after one casting incident, Stephen organized a protest against the department for its racial biases, which culminated in the creation of a student-body “minority rep.” Passionately outspoken on racial injustices in Hollywood as well, Stephen wrote an open letter for the Asian American community to read after witnessing blatant racism on the set of a large show he had been working in; within days, the letter was being openly circulated, and Stephen found himself invited to talks and conferences to speak on the issue of racism and diversity in the entertainment industry. Today, he has numerous credits across film and television, most recently featured in Bong Joon-ho’s Mickey 17 and Alex Scharfman’s Death of a Unicorn; away from the camera, Stephen tries to enjoy the spontaneity and surprises of life.

Kevin Kreider (Full)
Interviewee: Kreider, Kevin
Kevin Kreider, 40, is a Korean American adoptee whose life story spans continents, cultures, and personal transformation. Born in South Korea and adopted by an Irish Catholic family in Philadelphia shortly after his third birthday, Kevin’s earliest memories include arriving in the United States, experiencing the unfamiliar sensations of a new home, and navigating the fear and excitement of entering a family and environment that was culturally and linguistically foreign. Growing up, he confronted questions about identity and difference, particularly in school, where he experienced both curiosity and racialized teasing from peers. His parents’ approach to explaining adoption and cultural difference was cautious, leaving Kevin to gradually process his identity as an adoptee and a Korean American. Despite early challenges, Kevin’s path toward embracing his heritage was shaped by exposure to diverse communities, first in New York City, where he entered the modeling world, and later through travel to Southeast Asia and Korea. These experiences were both affirming and challenging. While Kevin initially sought connection to his birth culture, he encountered rejection in Korea and felt caught between worlds—not fully accepted as Korean nor as white in the United States. Over time, exposure to the rising global visibility of Korean culture through K-pop, Crazy Rich Asians, and community connections in Los Angeles enabled him to explore and embrace his heritage more fully. His participation in the television series Bling Empire provided a public platform for this journey, allowing him to share personal stories of adoption, mental health, sobriety, and self-acceptance while helping educate a broader audience about the nuances of identity and cultural belonging. Kevin’s story is also defined by his commitment to community and giving back. He emphasizes the importance of contributing time, experience, and mentorship, particularly within the Asian American community, advocating for a culture of support that extends beyond financial contributions. Drawing on personal experiences of isolation, marginalization, and overcoming self-doubt, Kevin encourages others to invest in each other’s growth and well-being, reflecting a deeply held belief that collective support strengthens both individuals and communities. Guided by faith and a philosophy of reciprocity, Kevin Kreider continues to explore his cultural identity, share his lived experiences, and foster understanding and empowerment for adoptees and Asian Americans navigating similar challenges.

Kevin Kreider (Edited)
Interviewee: Kreider, Kevin
Kevin Kreider is a model, actor, and entrepreneur who was adopted from Korea and raised in Philadelphia by Irish Catholic parents. Growing up in a community where adoption was normalized but identity was rarely discussed, he struggled to understand where he belonged—facing racism, confusion, and the feeling of never being “Asian enough” or “American enough.” His path through modeling and reality TV brought its own challenges, including self-hatred and a desire to conform. But through his role on Netflix’s Bling Empire, Kevin began embracing his Korean heritage and speaking openly about mental health, sobriety, and identity. Today, he’s the co-founder of ALLS Productions, a company dedicated to Asian-led love stories, and SANS, a non-alcoholic beverage brand inspired by his recovery journey.  His mission remains clear: to give back through storytelling, community, and advocacy—especially for adoptees and those still learning to love themselves.

Jeehae Fischer (Full)
Interviewee: Fischer, Jeehae
Jeehae Fischer was born in South Korea to parents who moved their young family to Japan when she was only two years old. She lived in Japan until she was six years old, after which the family moved back to Korea—Jeehae tells us that she had to learn Korean, learn that there was a country and people called “Korea” and “Koreans,” and that she had to reconcile her Koreanness with how she considered herself to be Japanese for six years. After living in Korea for a while, the family moved to Teaneck, New Jersey, where she once again had to re-find her cultural footing. She recalls how after moving to the U.S., she often had to act as interlocutor between her parents and American society, communicating on their behalf. She never felt resentful of this role; if anything, she says that witnessing her parents face racism and microaggressions in America confirmed in her a conviction that she had to be “self-sufficient.” In one particular memory, she remembers having to translate for her mother a simple question asked by a store clerk: “Do you want a bag?” In college, Jeehae studied violin performance, and upon graduation, she worked at a nonprofit music school, though she her introduction to the nonprofit world was marred by a sudden budget cut which was soon followed by the loss of many of the organization’s music students. Deciding to continue pursing nonprofit work, she attended the New School to study nonprofit organization and operations, inspired to work for an organization which aided migrant communities. After completing her studies, she was tapped to work as the self-sufficiency coordinator of Korean American Family Service Center, a nonprofit committed to preventing and ending domestic and gender-based violence in the New York Tri-State area. She describes the organization as not being typical in the nonprofit world; many of the clients she works with are Korean American survivors of gender-based violence who are unable to relocate themselves for economic reasons. As the self-sufficiency coordinator, she worked the 24/7 hotline, job training programs, and the computer literacy class, among other things alongside volunteers. She recalls one client who she worked closely alongside who would often not show up to the computer classes; when she did come, she arrived with visible signs of physical trauma. Eventually, she learned that the client’s son was a deployed soldier in Iraq, and through the computer classes she was able to send him an email; afterwards, the son returned, helped separate his mother from her abusive living situation, and she filed for divorce. Jeehae tells us that even 10-15 years ago, Korean Americans were not present at the decision making table for policies which directly impacted their communities, something she sees has changed. When the pandemic hit in 2020, KAFSC’s hotline increased by 300%, signaling to the organization how the inability to leave the home increased rates of abuse. The organization made the difficult decision to work in-office owing to the nature of their work, many risking their lives to do so. Following the presidential election of 2024, Jeehae says that she thought the organization knew what to expect, though when January arrived, the situation turned out to be much more grave than expected: executive orders were being signed so quickly, and things were changing too fast. Of the five federal contracts KAFSC received, two of them were up for renewal, but for an unclear reason, were unable to be renewed under Trump’s reevaluation of funding grants for nonprofits. During these particularly trying times, Jeehae tells us that we must rely on friends and allies, who can lend their support through volunteering and sponsorships, to ensure the safety of our community’s most vulnerable constituents. “We’re Korean American,” she says, “we never give up, we are persistent.”

Jung Won Jung (Full)
Interviewee: Jung, Jung Won
Jung Won Jung was born in Seoul, South Korea, in the early 1960s to parents she describes as “not typical:” many of the childhood memories she cherishes revolve around involve a parent who continually encouraged her not to be afraid. As a child, she enjoyed fishing, hiking, and biking, activities her father encouraged so that she might “become brave,” and learn to meet adversity head-on; early on, she learned that there were no limitations as to what she could do owing to her gender. When she was eleven years old, her family immigrated to Argentina, a state which at the time didn’t have as stringent visa requirements to enter and work in the country as the United States. Though she describes life in Argentina as being “quite different” from Seoul, she nonetheless describes her childhood in South America with fondness. Soon thereafter, she moved to the United States by herself to pursue an education, driven by her early childhood convictions of confidence and self-worth, despite some initial homesickness. After graduation, she ended up getting married (per her parents’ wishes), and began building a family with her husband; all the while, she found herself wanting to work, and later learned how to open a business while taking care of her child. One day, while she went to the post-office on an errand, she picked up a brochure for Korean American Family Service Center which called for volunteers. When she called in to KAFSC to inquire about the volunteering position, she recalls that the interviewer was rather confused at first; “why do you want to volunteer?” Over 20 years later, Jung Won is still with KAFSC as an advocate for survivors, meeting with women and children survivors of domestic abuse, accompanying them to court, and filing protection orders at the precinct offices. She has also led classes on computer literacy, a crucial skill in the step towards survivors’ self-sufficiency and independence. Nowadays, she tells us that much of her work has been in outreach, connecting with community members and local politicians to stress the urgency of KAFSC’s mission. Stressing that financial independence is key for survivors to remove themselves from abusive environments, Jung Won tells us that the work of the organization builds in their clients the courage to say, “I can do this.”

Yonsuk Dallas (Full)
Interviewee: Dallas, Yonsuk
Yonsuk Dallas was born on May 28, 1940, in Tokyo, Japan, during the Japanese colonial period. Her given Japanese name was Shizue Jin, and she resided in Tokyo until she was six years old. Her childhood memories in Japan are closely linked to her wartime schooling, during which—like many Korean students in the Japanese schooling system—she only spoke Japanese, and quickly got used air raid sirens going off during class time (the students would hide in the closets whenever planes flew overhead). She also recalls how students were given “bagged bread” (white bread), a precious commodity during wartime. When she was six years old, her family moved to Seoul, which she considers as her second hometown; her ancestral hometown is in nearby Pyeongtaek-si in Gyeonggi Province. Having weathered two wars, she completed schooling and was married and had four sons, though tragically, she would lose her husband to medical complications stemming from diabetes in 1996, and her second son only a year later after a hard-fought battle with leukemia after five years of treatment. Her cousin, who lived in Los Angeles at the time, invited her to stay in the United States for a while to ease her mind, and Yonsuk has been there ever since. She attended church, joined a music club and art exhibition club, and one day found herself enamored at a gallery with a painting created by a stranger with whom she later would share an extremely close companionship, and later marry; their initial spark arose when she found out that he had a close linguistic and cultural connection to Japan, though he was African American. They later separated, but Yonsuk and he remained close friends until his passing. She expresses gratitude for everyone she’s met in the United States who helped her adjust to life here, particularly the workers and volunteers at Korean Community Center of the East Bay, which she describes as a place to go to feel a sense of peace during trying times. Having immigrated to expand her own cultural experiences, she takes pride in the fact that Korean culture is appreciated by a diversity of people, and tells the younger generation of Korean Americans to never forget their roots.
Keywords: Tokyo; 1940s childhood; Japanese colonialism; wartime Japan; Seoul migration; Pyeongtaek-si; Korean diaspora; Japanese schooling; air raids; widowhood; leukemia loss; Los Angeles; Korean church; art and music clubs; intercultural marriage; African American allyship; cultural memory; Korean Community Center; immigrant resilience; generational roots

Joanna Kim Selby (Full)
Interviewee: Selby, Joanna Kim
Joanna Kim Selby was born in 1931 in Pocheon, Gyeonggi-do, during the Japanese occupation. She recounts how, because her father was an officer graduate of an Imperial Japanese University, his high station meant that the family would often have to move according to where he was assigned to work; thus, she tells us that she never had a particular sense of a “hometown.” Rather, of all the places her family moved to, she fondly remembers the “Bon-dong” neighborhood, where she attended an all-girls high school which had both Japanese and Korean students. Her father was transferred to Seoul shortly after, and had made plans for his family to join him when Hirohito surrendered, and prime minister Hideki took his place. Joanna recalls how life completely changed for her; because her father was an officer, and of high station, she acknowledges she lived in extreme comfort amidst the occupation of Korea, with even Japanese workers referring to her as “lady.” In December of 1950, her family evacuated from Seoul to Busan to avoid the increasingly dangerous circumstances of the Korean War; after armistice became law, the family moved back to their home in Seoul, where they found their home “gun-shot riddled!” With little around to eat, she remembers how her mother dug-up earthenware jars of kimchi she had put into the ground before the warm some 3-4 years prior; for weeks, the family subsisted off of this kimchi. After completing school, she joined a government program to train as a social worker, which entailed traveling abroad to Japan, the Philippines, and Hawaii to experience social work in states where the field had had time to develop. Back in Korea, she was asked by a Catholic Relief service worker if she had ever considered moving to the United States, a question which in actuality was a marriage proposition. She ended up marrying the relief worker and moving to Oakland, witnessing the growth of her small “Korean village” in the 1970s, all the while feeling like an “enfant in an adult’s body.” Wanting to expand her personal autonomy in a country with which she was culturally unfamiliar, she enrolled herself in an ESL course in Oakland, took a vocational exam, and was hired to work for the IRS. Living by an ethic of “making herself something,” she continued working after retiring from her federal job in serving on Alameda County’s Commission of Aging, where she secured grants towards the creation of a Korean senior center in the Bay. Always happy to serve others, she expresses joy in the younger generation of Korean Americans she has met working for KACF-SF, and hopes others will also choose a life of service.
Keywords: Pocheon; 1930s Korea; Japanese occupation; officer’s family; Bon-dong; all-girls school; Korean War; Busan evacuation; postwar Seoul; buried kimchi; social work training; global travel; Catholic Relief; Oakland immigration; Korean village; ESL classes; IRS career; Commission on Aging; senior center advocacy; service ethic

Alex Hahn (Full)
Interviewee: Hahn, Alex
Alex Hahn was born in February, 1941, in Seongbuk-dong, Seoul, as the fourth of eight children. Though born during the Japanese colonial period, the memories he recalls of war revolve around the Korean War, during which his parents had to take on a particularly entrepreneurial spirit to support their large family; he tells us of how they rain a mill business, selling barley, millet, rice, and other such grains. When asked what his dream was in childhood, he tells us that it was simply for “the war to be over;” if he had any dreams beyond that, it was to one day study abroad in the United States. After completing primary schooling across tumultuous years, he attended Kyunghee University—where, following in the footsteps of his parents, he studied economics—on a fencing scholarship. His successful fencing career would span the 1967 World Fencing Championships in Montreal, as well as coaching the South Korean national Olympic fencing team. Fencing proved to be his ticket into the United States as well, where he was admitted to and further honed his training at a fencing academy in Hollywood, all the while working at a wig shop in Los Angeles, which he found to be too demanding and unsustainable. In pursuit of his childhood dream to study in the United States, he later moved to the Bay Area to study hospitality management at SF State Hotel Restaurant Management School. Moved by the drive to create a Koreatown in Northern California, which had great telegram access; he was one of the earliest Korean Americans to start a business in Oakland, beginning with a grocery chain. Recognized by the city for his efforts, he later went on to buy the Acorn Shopping Center in West Oakland, revitalizing the old mall. He tells us that the particular hardships the first generation of Korean immigrants experienced revolved around an unfamiliarity with the language, as well as the law: he recollects how the first businesses immigrant entrepreneurs opened were labor intensive, such as grocery stores and restaurants. Alex is also committed to bridging relationships between the Korean and Black communities in the Bay Area, not wanting to recreate the racal tensions he witnessed during his time in Los Angeles. He considers himself to be lucky that he was able to accomplish all of what he sought out to do; he hopes to continue building out the region’s robust Korean community. He warns us against conflating success with money or power, instead encouraging us to live in harmony, and not transactionally, with our communities.
Keywords: Seongbuk-dong; Korean War; grain mill; Kyunghee University; economics; fencing scholarship; 1967 Championships; Olympic coach; Hollywood fencing; wig shop; SF State; hospitality management; Koreatown advocate; Oakland business; grocery chain; Acorn Shopping Center; immigrant hardships; Korean-Black relations; community building; non-transactional values
