Kevin Yoo (Full)
Interviewee: Yoo, Kevin
Kevin Yoo, whose Korean name s Yoo Kun-Bae, was born in a small village (desig: Donam) in Banpo-myeon, Chungchungnam-do, Korea. Born in 1949, he describes his childhood as one wrought with the anxieties of the Korean War; he tells us that only dream he had was to escape his family’s poverty and live a simple life. Like many families from the country’s heartland, Kevin was able to escape the worst of the fighting, though he tells us that economic and financial restraints were precariously and constantly felt. In the first grade, he moved to the nearby metropole of Daejeon (between North and South Chungcheong provinces) to start his schooling in the first grade; even after finishing his studies, he remained in the school system, working as a schoolteacher in Korea for five years before arriving in the United States, where he continued working a variety of jobs until he was twenty-seven years old. Although Kevin is now retired from his last venture, a real estate business which he worked for over forty years, he has lived many lives in the Bay Area: he worked in the restaurant business, running Samwon Galbi and Gogi Time, as well as other smaller ventures such as selling wigs, working a grocery chain, and a health foods store. At the urging of his friend, Alex Hahn, Kevin moved from San Francisco to Oakland, bringing Samwon Galbi with him; he describes the city as a dynamic place with many people from many different backgrounds who tried their best to get along with one another (though sometimes, Kevin says that everyone can do a lot better). Having set up his business at the start of Oakland Koreatown’s growth, he has since helped organized Korean festivals across the Bay Area, including in Alameda, Concord, the East Bay, and everywhere in between. Telling us that we must dig our wells deep for water to flow in, he looks back at his life with thanks and gratitude (hoping that his children might want to take over his real estate business someday!).
Keywords: Korea; Banpo-myeon; Daejeon; Chungcheongnam-do; war; poverty; dreams; education; teaching; immigration; Bay Area; Oakland; Koreatown; entrepreneurship; restaurants; Samwon Galbi; Gogi Time; wigs; groceries; health food; real estate; resilience; community; gratitude; legacy

Kevin Yoo (Edited)
Interviewee: Yoo, Kevin
Kevin Yoo, whose Korean name is Yoo Kun-bae, was born in a small rural village in South Chungcheong Province, Korea, in 1949. Growing up poor, he tells us that his dream was to escape poverty and live a simple life; in the first grade, he moved from the countryside to Daejeon to begin his primary schooling. He would hold education close to his heart long after completing school, working as a schoolteacher in Korea for five years before moving to the United States, where he continued attending school while working a variety of jobs until he was twenty-seven years old. Like many Korean immigrant entrepreneurs of his generation, Kevin’s ventures into small business were wide and varied: he worked a wig shop, a grocery store, health food store, and a restaurant before pivoting into his current work in real estate, in which he now has 40 years of experience. He lived in San Francisco for twenty of those years before being urged by his friend and business partner Alex Hahn to come to Oakland, where a small Koreatown was beginning to plant its roots. He marvels at how dynamic Oakland is, as well as how diverse its people are, and finds joy in the fact that the Korean community has gotten along well with their neighbors. Having seen the growth of the Korean community throughout the East Bay, Kevin hopes that his descendants will continue their family business, continuing to build and support their community across Oakland, Alameda, Concord, and beyond.

Pat Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, Pat
Pat Lee, whose Korean name is Lee Kyung Hee, was born in 1951 in Seoul, Korea, the fifth daughter in a bustling household of seven children. As a young woman, she admits to having been impulsive and carefree—a spirit of youth that would ultimately shape her journey. After attending high school in Anyang and marrying young, she immigrated to the United States with her husband in 1972, landing in Oakland with no clear plan and few resources. Her husband, still in college, juggled night work to support them, often returning home past midnight. Isolated and far from her family, Pat found herself thrust into adulthood—loneliness, necessity, and survival pushing her to grow up fast. Seeking a path of her own, Pat enrolled at Laney College’s cosmetology program while raising two small children. Around the time she was set to receive her license, she made a spontaneous decision that would define her entrepreneurial future: she purchased a small neighborhood market near what would later become known as Koreana Plaza. At the time, she hadn’t grasped how difficult running a business would be, but she threw herself into the work—seven days a week, early mornings at the produce market, long evenings at the shop. Her customers were her teachers, helping her gain confidence and resilience in a new land. Over time, the area around her store began to flourish. A hairdresser, a video store, a billiards hall, and an acupuncturist opened nearby, creating foot traffic and a vibrant Korean hub. In the early days, Korean immigrants in the East Bay found each other through necessity and warmth. Without Korean restaurants or cultural centers, new arrivals were met with shared meals and open doors. “We didn’t have much,” Pat says, “but we had each other.” By the late 1980s, her business had grown immensely—at one point, customers lined up at the door, the cashier overwhelmed with demand. Even an arson fire in 1990 couldn’t stop the momentum; thanks to good insurance and sheer determination, she rebuilt. The plaza became a destination for Korean Americans across the Bay Area—from San Francisco to Fremont to Walnut Creek—who came seeking a taste of home. Today, Pat reflects on a dramatically changed Oakland and a Korean American community that has evolved. Yet she still believes in the value of hard work, honesty, and patience. “Things may not be as easy now,” she says, “but if you work sincerely, your time will come.” Her story stands as a testament to the enduring spirit of immigration, entrepreneurship, and the quiet strength of community built from scratch.
Keywords: Seoul; Anyang; Oakland; immigration; 1970s; marriage; motherhood; cosmetology; Laney College; entrepreneurship; Koreana Plaza; market; resilience; Korean community; East Bay; small business; arson; foot traffic; diaspora; hard work; sincerity; adaptation; nostalgia; food sharing; Korean Americans.

Pat Lee (Edited)
Interviewee: Lee, Pat
Pat Lee, whose Korean name is Lee Kyung Hee, was born in 1951 in Seoul, Korea, the fifth daughter in a family of seven children. She admits that in her youth she was often impulsive, a characteristic which gave her a taste for life and would drive her to immigrate to the United States with her husband in 1971. Landing in Oakland, Pat tells us that her husband had to attend college then work in the evenings, often getting home at midnight; without her family and friends nearby, Pat found a need to mature quickly to support their young family. After enrolling herself at in cosmetology school, she had two children and in 1977, she made the decision to buy a small market around what would become Koreana Plaza. She tells us that her fellow entrepreneurs, as well as her customers, helped her gain her footing in running a small business: over time, a hairdresser, video store, billiards bar, and even an acupuncturist moved into vacant stores nearby, and Pat’s own business greatly benefitted from the increase in foot traffic. Soon enough, the plaza grew so much that customers would drive in from San Francisco, Concord, Fremont, and other towns beyond Oakland to get a sense of home. She tells us that in the early days of the East Bay Korean community, the arrival of a fellow Korean immigrant would be cause for a gathering with shared food and stories; there were no Korean restaurants, but the community had one another. Although much has changed since then, Pat encourages the younger generation to work hard and with sincerity in building the lives they want to live.

June Lee (Full)
Interviewee: Lee, June
June Lee is the Executive Director of the Korean Community Center of the East Bay (KCCEB), where she leads with a deep commitment to equity, empowerment, and care. From a young age, June defied expectations—she was outspoken, full of opinions, and uninterested in staying within the bounds of what was expected of a “typical” Korean girl. Growing up in Korea with two brothers and a sister twelve years her senior, she looked up to her father, a businessman in international trade whose frequent foreign guests opened her eyes to the wider world. Through his example, June developed an early curiosity about global experiences and cross-cultural stories—seeds that would eventually guide her life path. Though she originally came to the U.S. without a fixed career goal, June worked in advertising for a time, navigating the professional world while cultivating a desire to live with intention. During her pregnancy, this desire crystallized. She began to ask: *What would I want my daughter to understand about my life?* This moment of reflection—what she jokingly calls a “chemical change”—led her to reevaluate her values and ultimately set her on a new course in nonprofit work. When she joined KCCEB in 2010, June was drawn to its mission of empowering Korean and other immigrant communities across the Bay Area. What began as admiration evolved into leadership. Over the years, she has helped transform the organization’s model from a program-centered structure to one grounded in community outcomes and impact. Under her guidance, KCCEB has championed a community health framework—one that integrates mental health, public benefits, and social services to meet the complex and evolving needs of immigrant communities. June’s most meaningful moments have come from witnessing this impact firsthand. She recalls a senior client battling depression who, through dramatized storytelling and peer support, was able to reclaim his agency and later became a helper to others. She shares the story of another elder, unhoused and unknown to the system during the pandemic, who was discovered through KCCEB’s meal delivery program. Through sustained support, he secured housing, regained health insurance, and transformed from suicidal despair to becoming a volunteer serving meals to other seniors. These stories, for June, are more than anecdotes—they are proof of possibility. “Empowerment,” she says, “means helping people live to their fullest potential. That’s everybody’s right.” As the needs of the Korean American community change with time, June is committed to ensuring that KCCEB changes with them—remaining a vital, trusted, and responsive force in the lives of those it serves.
Keywords: Korea; Oakland; KCCEB; nonprofit; leadership; community; empowerment; impact; mental health; social services; elder care; meal delivery; public benefits; immigrant support; storytelling; international trade; cross-cultural; Bay Area; resilience; transformation; visibility; pregnancy; identity; advocacy; adaptation.

June Lee (Edited)

June Lee serves as the Executive Director of Korean Community Center of the East Bay, where she works to reify the organization’s key tenets of community service, research, and engagement. Growing up in Korea, she tells us that she was a rather outspoken child with strong opinions who looked up to her father; he worked in international trade, and would often bring non-Korean guests over to their home. Intrigued by what felt like a limitless world of experiences and opportunities outside of her native Korea, June endeavored to explore the world for herself; she immigrated to the United States where she worked in advertising for a while. At the time, she did not know that she would pivot careers towards the nonprofit world, but she tells us that during her pregnancy, she began to think about what her life story would be for her daughter. Joining KCCEB in 2010, she was inspired by its mission of empowering Korean and other immigrant populations in the Bay Area, and has since worked to implement an impact-based, community health framework alongside the service work which KCCEB had long done. For June, the most memorable moments on the job are when she sees the real, tangible impacts of KCCEB’s work in the lives of the individuals they serve, telling us how she “hopes the Korean community and individually and collectively live their lives to the fullest… this is everybody’s right.”

Han Jik Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Han Jik
Han Jik Kim, born on December 20th, 1965—Dongji, or the winter solstice—in Liberation Village, Seoul, is a doctor of Oriental Medicine who has dedicated his life to practicing and teaching traditional Korean medicine in Central Los Angeles. The eldest son of North Korean parents, with a father employed in broadcasting, Han Jik had a childhood marked by frequent moves, attending four elementary schools in total. Though soft-spoken and introspective, he remembers this period as a deeply joyful time. Early on, he developed a keen interest in questions without easy answers: in middle school, he dreamed of becoming a philosopher; in high school, a novelist. Theology even crossed his mind. Han Jik immigrated to the U.S. in 1988, joining his family who had settled in Southern California two years earlier. Though he had briefly attended Chung Ang University in Korea, he did not complete his studies before immigrating. After some hesitation and thoughts of returning to Korea, he ultimately enrolled in South Baylo University in 1991 to study Korean medicine—drawn by the rare opportunity to receive instruction in his native language. Guided by his uncle’s advice and a growing sense of vocation, he stepped onto a new path, one that blended tradition with adaptation. Now, decades later, Han Jik runs his own clinic where the majority of his patients are non-Korean. His approach to healing is holistic, blending the principles of *yin and yang*, the five elements, and food-based therapies. “I treat my patients like students,” he jokes, sharing how he insists on whole, traditional foods—especially two boiled eggs for breakfast (“you won’t be hungry until 1 p.m.”), jujube for warming the stomach, and samgyetang for summer balance. “Don’t let me catch you with protein powder,” he warns us! The core of Korean medicine, he explains, lies in understanding and respecting the body’s natural rhythms. “When people become ill, they often become slaves to their illness. My job is to guide them back to themselves.” Han Jik speaks about the importance of tending to one's relationships—whether with family, neighbors, or coworkers—as a form of healing in itself. “Sickness often begins when those bonds break,” he says. And while he acknowledges the challenges of maintaining those relationships in the social media age, he believes firmly that caring for others and oneself is the heart of well-being. Reflecting on his life, he confesses one regret: that he once believed the best way to resolve a conflict or difficulty was to remove himself from it. Today, he sees healing not as separation but as engagement, understanding, and care. “No matter how advanced AI or science becomes,” he says, “we are not just rational bodies. We are humans. And I am the master of my body.”
Keywords: Dongji; Seoul; immigration; Korean medicine; South Baylo; holistic health; traditional food; samgyetang; jujube; yin and yang; self-healing; mentorship; introspection; Liberation Village; non-Korean patients; Los Angeles; boiled eggs; body-mind connection; family; relationships; philosopher; theology; healing; regret; Korean American.

Han Jik Kim (Edited)
Interviewee: Kim, Han Jik
Han Jik Kim is an acupuncturist and herbalist who practices traditional Korean medicine in Central Los Angeles. Born in Liberation Village, Seoul, December 20th, 1965, Han Jik notes that he was born on Dongji, or the Winter Solstice. Growing up alongside his younger brother, he tells us that his family moved often during his elementary school years, where he describes himself as having been a quiet student. From an early age, Han Jik expressed a curious interest in questions which could not be answered simply, as well as how we might interact with those things less material: in middle school, he remembers wanting to be a philosopher, and in high school, a novelist. He considered pursuing theology in middle school as well, though he later enrolled in Chung Ang university to pursue a different course of study. In 1988, Han Jik joined his family in the United States, who had immigrated two years before him, and in 1991, he enrolled in South Baylo University to study traditional medicine; his uncle had informed him that the school offered instruction in the Korean language. Today, Han Jik operates his own clinic, with most of his patients being non-Korean, and reminds us of the importance of looking at things holistically, as well as being master over our own bodies.

Sion Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Sion
Sion Kim, born Kim Seokhyun in 1937 in Icheon, Gyeonggi-do, is a retired Methodist-trained pastor and avid apitherapist who has made Los Angeles’ Koreatown his home since 1980. One of six siblings, Sion spent his early years on a small rural farm, helping raise pigs, chickens, and rabbits in the mountainous countryside. From a young age, he had a deep fascination with animal husbandry, and his passion led him to study livestock care in Japan near Hokkaido, sponsored by the Gyeonggi provincial government. There, he gained firsthand experience in dairy farming—an opportunity that shaped his early aspirations. But upon returning to Korea, he learned that his father had sold the family’s land, effectively ending his dream of a farming life. He moved to Seoul, where he took on various jobs. Though he rarely attended church in his youth, Sion recalls feeling a sudden and powerful call to prayer one day. That spiritual pull led him to seminary, where he began pastoral training, eventually serving in Methodist, Presbyterian, and Lutheran congregations. In April 1980, he stepped off the tarmac at LAX and into a new chapter of his life in Koreatown, where he has remained ever since—becoming both a community fixture and witness to key moments in Korean American history, including the 1992 Saigu Uprising. In retirement, Sion found an unexpected second vocation: bee sting acupuncture, or apitoxin therapy. After witnessing its healing effects firsthand—his own toothache vanished overnight—he became a practitioner, often emphasizing that bee stings, when administered properly, can be more effective than needles. While he cautions that allergic reactions are possible, he also shares anecdotes of patients who, undeterred, returned for more treatments. Now in his later years, Sion reflects on life’s true priorities. “Health is more important than money or honor,” he tells us, mourning the loss of many friends to age and illness. His advice: pray if you can—but more than anything, live with a positive attitude. For Sion, the mind and body are deeply connected: “The way we think and speak,” he says, “manifests in how we live.”
Keywords: Icheon; Gyeonggi-do; Koreatown; pastor; beekeeping; apitherapy; animal husbandry; livestock; Hokkaido; dairy farming; seminary; Methodist; Lutheran; Presbyterian; church; Saigu; healing; positivity; health; immigration; rural Korea; community; acupuncture; theology; spiritual calling.

Sion Kim (Edited)
Interviewee: Kim, Sion
Sion Kim, whose Korean name is Kim Seokhyun, is a retired pastor and beekeeping expert who calls L.A.’s Koreatown his home. Born as the fifth of sixth children in 1937, Sion spent most of his childhood in Icheon, Gyeonggi-do, a small mountainous rural community. Growing up in the countryside with parents who worked as farmers, Sion became deeply invested in livestock care and animal husbandry; his home itself a farm, he grew up raising pigs, chickens, and rabbits. When given the opportunity to study animal husbandry in Japan, Sion was sponsored by the provincial government to attend a school near Hokkaido to familiarize himself with the dairy farming industry; upon his return, however, he found out that his father had sold much of their family’s land, taking away his opportunity to raise livestock. Instead, Sion had to move to Seoul where he worked a variety of jobs before attending seminary to train to be a pastor. Despite not having been a regular parishioner in his youth, Sion tells us that he felt compelled to become a pastor by God. In April of 1980, Sion immigrated to Koreatown, Los Angeles, where he has resided ever since, and after a long career working in the church, is now a passionate beekeeper and proponent of apitoxin, or bee sting, therapy. As he’s aged, Sion tells us that the thing he realized was most important in life—above money and honor—is health and a positive attitude, reminding us that the way we think, speak, and carry ourselves all manifests in our wellbeing.

Dr. Junwoo Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Jun Woo
Dr. Jun Woo Kim is a doctor of Oriental Medicine who also serves as the OMC Director at Dongguk University Los Angeles. Born in 1985 in Daegu, South Korea, he recalls having a rather peaceful childhood both before and after he immigrated to the United States as a high school student; when asked about his hometown, Dr. Kim tells us that he considers La Mirada home, and Los Angeles his second hometown. Though he tells us that he did not feel “stressed” upon his move from Korea to the United States, he noticed that nearly everything, from the way society operates to the cultural expectations placed on others, were different from what he was used to. Studying biology in college, he was drawn to pursuing psychiatry because of his interest in treating psychological and emotional disorders using non-invasive methods, though he would later find his calling in traditional medicine practicing whole body therapy, a treatment approach which he describes as integrating Western and Eastern medical practices. When asked what he sees as the largest difficult for the field of traditional medicine, Dr. Kim says that it is largely the field’s reputation (or lack thereof) in states outside of California, where accredited licensures are harder to come by; he suggests that outreach for the practice can be achieved by insurers, who can opt to insure patients for traditional medical care, as well as by having practitioners engage with their communities more.

Dr. Junwoo Kim (Edited)
Interviewee: Kim, Jun Woo
Jun Woo Kim, born in 1985 in Daegu, South Korea, is a practitioner of Oriental Medicine and the current OMC Director at Dongguk University Los Angeles. An only child raised primarily by his mother, uncle, and grandmother while his father—a doctor—was away, Jun Woo immigrated to the U.S. as a teenager. He recalls his early childhood in Korea as peaceful, and though his move to La Mirada, California, wasn’t stressful, he was struck by how different American life was—from societal structure to interpersonal dynamics. Today, he considers both La Mirada and Los Angeles to be home, with a strong affection for Koreatown’s cultural vitality. Initially interested in psychiatry, Jun Woo studied biology in college but found that Western medicine often “masked” illness instead of healing it at its root. He began searching for what he calls a “whole therapy”—an integrative approach that attends not just to the body but also to emotional and spiritual well-being. He eventually found that in traditional Eastern medicine, which emphasizes holistic healing and incorporates the Five Elements framework and energy flow within the body. His commitment to this path was further shaped by a deeply emotional case in which a stroke patient made a full recovery under his care. A Christian, Jun Woo believes in a “third force”—a spiritual grounding that nurtures mental health and encourages healing. He describes his faith as a journey, one that parallels his commitment to mental, emotional, and physical well-being. To him, a healthy life is inseparable from a healthy mind. Despite his professional achievements, Jun Woo notes that public understanding of Oriental Medicine remains a challenge, especially outside of states like California where licensure is more robust. He argues that expanding insurance coverage, increasing community outreach, and continuing to educate patients unfamiliar with Eastern concepts are all necessary steps to raise the field’s profile. Through his role at Dongguk University, he encourages students to stay committed to this often misunderstood yet profoundly impactful discipline.

Dr. Sejun Oh (Full)
Interviewee: Oh, Sejun
Sejun Oh is a doctor of traditional Korean medicine based in Los Angeles, California. Born in Seoul in 1965 as the eldest of three children, Sejun describes his younger self as a quiet, bookish child who often felt gloomy. He spent much of his youth immersed in difficult philosophical questions, reading books as a way to escape the monotony of daily life. Despite this introspection, he now considers himself a free spirit—someone who values joy, openness, and the richness of human connection. After graduating from Seoul National University, Sejun worked in small business and accounting, but he always held onto the dream of becoming a doctor or opening a nursing home to care for the elderly. In 1996, at the age of 32, he moved to the United States, inspired by a visit to a friend who had settled in California. Rather than entering a conventional American career path, Sejun saw his immigration as a chance to pursue his long-held ambition in the healing arts. Upon arriving in L.A., he immediately began training and practicing traditional Korean medicine. Today, Dr. Oh runs a Korean Medicine clinic and is in the process of establishing a community health center that will offer basic Western medical treatment alongside traditional care. His approach is integrative: start with familiar procedures, then introduce patients to Korean medicine in a relaxed, open setting. Like other practitioners in his field, he notes that many people still lack access to traditional treatments due to limited insurance coverage or unfamiliarity with Eastern philosophies. Sejun emphasizes that Korean medicine differs significantly from its Chinese counterpart, especially in its focus on long-term bodily recovery and internal balance. He believes that if one can understand their own constitution—like having a personalized health manual—they can engage in preventive care more effectively. For him, the body is not a machine but a treasure, and medicine should minimize harm while maximizing holistic well-being. He describes America as a place where he’s been able to live more freely and soulfully than in Korea, where formality often governs social life. One of his fondest memories is watching the 2002 World Cup with crowds of fellow Koreans in Koreatown. When asked what brings him the most joy, he names his daughter and her commitment to environmental justice, as well as peaceful drives along the Pacific Coast Highway. Dr. Oh’s philosophy centers on service: individual happiness is fleeting, but the happiness found in helping others is enduring. He encourages people to live joyfully and to pursue good not just for themselves, but for others.
Keywords: Seoul; 1965; Los Angeles; Korean medicine; traditional healing; free spirit; Seoul National University; philosophy; immigration; Koreatown; Western medicine; community health; integrative care; constitution; body as treasure; preventive care; childhood dream; service; holistic; public access; insurance coverage; joy; environmental justice; Pacific Coast Highway; doctor.

Dr. Sejun Oh is a doctor of traditional medicine in Los Angeles, California. He encourages us to do the things which make us happy to our heart’s content, and to serve others just as vigorously, if not more; lasting happiness, he tells us, comes from treating others as treasures.

Lauren Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Lauren
Lauren Kim is an urban planner from Orange County, California, who seeks to bring joy, beauty, and equity to cultural and community spaces. Born in Long Beach and raised in Irvine, Lauren says that she grew up in a “placeless place,” a phrase she uses to depict immigrant childhood life in suburbia. From a young age, she was a curious and questioning child—something her mother fondly remembers—always searching for a deeper sense of wonder in the world around her. Lauren studied environmental and urban studies, driven by the feeling that there had to be “more” to being Korean than shopping at H-Mart. Her mother is a master fermenter who delivered homemade foods to Korean grocery stores, while her father, Han Jik Kim, runs an acupuncture clinic that also functions as an apothecary and public education space. Lauren has encouraged her father to transition his clinic into a nonprofit to better serve the community with a model of care rooted in access and reciprocity, though the current space isn’t quite there—yet. Professionally, Lauren works on projects like an arboretum in San Antonio, helping communities reframe and reclaim spaces that have been overlooked or underserved. She believes all communities deserve places filled with joy and wonder, and she views her work as both practical and poetic—creating connection, beauty, and purpose in the built environment. Having often been one of the few people of color in her academic and professional circles, Lauren is acutely aware of the importance of representing communities that aren’t often included in planning conversations. Though she doesn’t consider herself a “healer” in the traditional sense, she describes her role as one of connection: helping people access systems, navigate power, and imagine better futures. She is especially passionate about the agency individuals should have in shaping their own health journeys—particularly in the face of generational divides around mental health or wellness. Lauren’s relationships—especially with her older sister and her parents—have shaped her deeply. While her bond with her family is loving, it is also marked by mutual growth and complexity. She is grateful to her parents for raising her to be an independent thinker, and she wants them to know that her path, while rooted in their legacy, is her own. At the heart of her work is a continual return to the question: “What is the voice I want to use to positively inhabit my community?”

Lauren Kim is an urban planner from Orange County, California, who strives to bring joy to community and cultural centers. Gwang-hun Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Gwang-hun
Gwanghun Kim, 56, is a doctor of Korean Medicine who has lived in Los Angeles’s Koreatown for nearly a quarter of a century. Born in South Korea, he spent his childhood in the countryside before moving to the port city of Busan for his schooling. He recalls those years as relatively “average,” without any grand ambitions other than one day becoming a salaried worker and earning a stable income. It wasn’t until much later—after immigrating to the United States in 2000—that his life took a very different direction. Arriving in Koreatown as a first-generation immigrant, Gwanghun was in his early thirties and confronted by the intense stress and uncertainty of starting over in a new country. Though he did not develop a major illness, the toll of long hours, economic pressures, and cultural adjustment manifested physically: his body ached constantly, and fatigue weighed him down. These hardships prompted him to think more seriously about health and wellness, not only for himself but for others in similar circumstances. When he sought out acupuncture as a patient, he was struck by how much it alleviated his pain. This experience inspired him to train as a doctor of Korean Medicine, transforming personal hardship into a lifelong vocation. Today, Gwanghun’s practice extends beyond his clinic walls and into the wider community. Twice a week, he travels to Long Beach Port, where he offers free acupuncture, massage, and health advice to sailors and dockworkers who often spend months at sea and return with chronic aches and injuries. Many of these workers lack reliable access to healthcare, and Gwanghun sees his volunteer work as both a calling and a responsibility. His involvement in his local church further reinforces this mission of service; he does not see a contradiction between faith and medicine, but rather an alignment in the shared work of healing and care. Reflecting on the healthcare landscape, he notes differences between Korea, where traditional and Western medicine are treated as distinct systems, and the United States, where holistic or non-pharmaceutical treatments are sometimes recommended by Western doctors for patients seeking alternatives to medication. For Gwanghun, this blending underscores the importance of offering people choices in how they manage their health, especially in moments of anxiety or pain. Now in middle age, with children raised and his parents growing older, Gwanghun thinks often about legacy. Koreatown, he says, was built through the labor and sacrifice of his parents’ generation, who struggled to establish themselves in America and make life better for their families. His own generation, he believes, carries the responsibility of stewarding that legacy—ensuring the community remains strong, vibrant, and supportive for the generations to come. Beyond Koreatown, he sees this duty extending outward: to share skills, knowledge, and compassion wherever in the world they might be needed.
Keywords: Koreatown; Los Angeles; Long Beach Port; Busan; South Korea; countryside; Korean immigrant; first generation; Korean Medicine; acupuncture; herbal medicine; massage therapy; dockworkers; sailors; healthcare access; immigrant health; community service; church; traditional medicine; Western medicine; alternative medicine; holistic care; stress; fatigue; middle age; legacy

Gwang-hun Kim (Edited)
Interviewee: Kim, Gwang-hun
Gwanghun Kim is a doctor of Korean Medicine from Koreatown, Los Angeles, a community he has called home for the past 25 years of his life. Gwanghun describes himself and his childhood as being relatively “average”: born in South Korea’s countryside, he later moved to the port city of Busan where he attended school, after which he tells us he had no particular dreams other than becoming a salaried worker. However, due to a change in circumstances, Gwanghun immigrated to the United States in 2000, arriving in Koreatown where he has lived and worked in ever since. He recounts how he became rather unwell while settling into his new home: he was stressed, his body ached, and, carrying the burdens of being a first-generation immigrant, he was constantly fatigued. Though he was only in this early 30s, Gwanghun noticed how lethargic he had become, and sought out treatment. After a few visits to an acupuncture clinic in his community, Gwanghun was surprised at how well his body had responded to the treatment, and decided to pursue a career in traditional Korean medicine. Drawing from his own initial hardships as an immigrant navigating a complex and often opaque healthcare system, Gwanghun is committed to paying it forward for others in his community, volunteering his massage and acupuncture services for sailors and dock workers at Long Beach Port who often return home with painful aches and muscle knots from performing labor while months away at sea. Now in middle age, Gwanghun believes that all of us have a responsibility to serve and shape our communities into environments we’d be proud to pass onto the next generation.

Sue Park-Hur (Full)
Interviewee: Park-Hur, Sue
Sue Park Hur, 53, is the co-founder of ReconciliAsian, a non-profit peace center in Los Angeles dedicated to equipping Korean diaspora and Asian American communities and churches with tools for conflict transformation, restorative justice, and trauma healing. Born in Seoul, South Korea, Sue immigrated to Los Angeles with her family at the age of eight. As a 1.5-generation Korean American, she recalls her childhood ethos as one of dutiful responsibility—“to do a good job”—shaped by her parents’ experiences and the broader immigrant context. Her father, who had been a young teenager during the Korean War, carried with him both the resilience and traumas of his generation; her uncles even referred to him as “Buddha” for his wisdom and composure, yet Sue also witnessed the lingering hauntings of those past experiences in her family life. A life-changing moment came at the age of sixteen when her mother passed away. This loss prompted Sue to reflect deeply on family narratives and memory, realizing that while her mother’s life held moments of tragedy, she was also fully human—excited about dates with her husband, capable of joy and ordinary pleasures. Sue describes this process as “befriending” her mother: learning to see beyond the static stories we hold of our parents and recognizing their complexity, frailty, and humanity. This awareness of familial histories, combined with reflections on her father’s efforts—“In spite of all of that, you tried. And that is enough.”—deeply shaped her understanding of care, legacy, and accountability. Sue’s college years at UCLA marked another pivotal period. In 1992, as a sophomore, she witnessed the acquittal of the LAPD officers in the Rodney King case and observed the ensuing media narrative framing Koreans and Black Americans as adversaries. She recalls seeing her father on the rooftop of their family store with a gun during this period, a moment that crystallized for her the intersection of systemic racism, community defense, and generational trauma. These experiences prompted her to consider how structural oppression manifests not only in society at large but also within families, churches, and faith communities. With a background in sociology and theology, Sue and her husband have devoted their lives to fostering peacemaking and reconciliation within the Korean American and broader Asian American community. Through ReconciliAsian, they provide education, mentorship, and advocacy around restorative practices, encouraging communities to transform trauma rather than perpetuate it. Sue emphasizes that peace work is relational and practical: it begins with confronting the histories we inherit, understanding the human dimensions of those around us, and creating spaces for healing and courageous dialogue. Her life and work reflect a commitment to ensuring that trauma is acknowledged, transformed, and never passed on unexamined.
Keywords: Seoul; South Korea; Los Angeles; Koreatown; Korean diaspora; Asian American; 1.5 generation; immigrant experience; Korean War; family trauma; restorative justice; conflict transformation; peacemaking; trauma healing; ReconciliAsian; non-profit; church; theology; sociology; community advocacy; racial justice; Rodney King; media representation; intergenerational trauma; parenting; reconciliation

Sue Park-Hur (Edited)
Interviewee: Park-Hur, Sue
Sue Park-Hur is the co-founder of ReconciliAsian @reconciliasian, a non-profit peace center in Los Angeles which equips Korean diaspora and Asian American communities and churches with  rehabilitative and transformative tools to be courageous peacemakers. Born in Seoul, South Korea, Sue moved to Los Angeles with her family when she was eight years old; a dutifully hardworking 1.5th generation Korean American, Sue tells us that as a child immigrant, her job was “to do a good job.” Her relationship with her parents, as well as her immediate environment, strongly influenced her to pursue work in peace advocacy today: as the child of Korean immigrants who lived through and remembered the Korean War, Sue was acutely aware of the unique combination of stressors which burdened her parents. During her sophomore year of college at UCLA, Sue witnessed how communities around her responded to the acquittal of the LAPD officers tried in the Rodney King case, and was disheartened by how the media portrayed the Uprising as a racial conflict which pit communities against one another. She describes the memory of seeing her father take up arms alongside other Korean men, having realized that many of them were already familiar with firearms from their time in the Korean military. Some had even lived through the traumas of the Korean War, as did her father. She began thinking of how trauma could be transformed, and not transferred, wondering what that looks like within families, within churches, within communities. With a background in both sociology and theology, Sue and her husband founded ReconciliAsian to advocate for conflict transformation, restorative justice, and healing, educating and promoting the culture of peacemaking and reconciliation within the Korean community.

Joy Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Joy
Joy Kim (Lee Jeong-hyun), born in 1953 in the countryside near Cheonan, South Chungcheong Province, is a pioneering librarian and curator whose work has left an indelible mark on Korean Studies in North America. Raised in a conservative Confucian household after the early death of her father, Joy grew up under the guardianship of her grandfather, who believed that girls did not need an education beyond high school. Despite these limitations, her intellectual curiosity and love of literature drove her to pursue higher education, eventually studying Library Sciences at Ewha Womans University in Seoul. In the late 1970s, Joy immigrated to the United States to join her husband, who had gone ahead to study journalism. Her early years in America were marked by both hardship and determination. She began her life in Los Angeles as a live-in babysitter earning $75 a week, using the quiet hours of childcare to teach herself English by watching television and reading the Bible. Within six months, her language skills were strong enough for her to enter UCLA, where she completed her master’s degree in Library Sciences while also starting a family. In 1984, Joy began her professional career at the University of Southern California’s East Asian Library. Over the decades that followed, she advanced to become Curator of the Korean Heritage Library (KHL), where she was instrumental in building one of the most significant Korean Studies collections outside of Korea. Under her stewardship, the KHL not only expanded its holdings but also became a vital center for research, teaching, and cultural preservation. Beyond USC, Joy’s leadership extended nationally: in 2007 she became the first Asian woman elected President of the Council on East Asian Libraries, an acknowledgment of her trailblazing role in a historically underrepresented field. Her contributions were further recognized in 2011, when she received the National Order of Civil Merit from the President of South Korea, one of the nation’s highest civilian honors. Alongside her professional achievements, Joy has also contributed significantly to Korean American history preservation. She played a central role in USC’s Korean American Digital Archive and worked closely on the Legacy Project with Korean American Story, ensuring that the voices and experiences of Korean immigrants and their descendants would be preserved for future generations. Joy’s personal journey has been marked by resilience as well. She navigated a difficult marriage, raised two daughters—one of whom was diagnosed with Asperger’s—and made the courageous decision to pursue divorce when her children urged her toward a safer and more stable home environment. Through these challenges, she found strength in her faith and in her vocation as both a mother and a scholar. Now retired, Joy continues to be recognized as a leading authority in Korean Studies librarianship and as a trailblazer for women and immigrants in academia. Her career reflects a profound dedication to cultural preservation, scholarship, and community service, and her legacy endures in the institutions she shaped and the histories she safeguarded.
Keywords: Joy Kim; Lee Jeong-hyun; Cheonan; South Chungcheong Province; Ewha Womans University; UCLA; University of Southern California; Korean Heritage Library; USC; East Asian Library; Korean Studies; academic librarian; Korean American Digital Archive; Council on East Asian Libraries; National Order of Civil Merit; Korean immigrant; Korean American women; Confucian household; education; divorce; motherhood; Asperger’s; Christianity; Korean American Story; heritage preservation; Los Angeles

Joy Kim (Edited)
Interviewee: Kim, Joy
Joy Kim is an academic librarian, curator, and renowned authority on Korean Studies librarianship from Los Angeles, California. Born in the Korean countryside of South Chungcheong Province in 1953, Joy—whose Korean name is Lee Jeong-hyun—grew up in a strictly Confucian household led by her grandfather, whom she describes as a conservative man who believed that women shouldn’t receive an education beyond high school. Despite this, Joy, who was a voracious reader, applied to attended Ewha Womans University in Seoul, where she studied Library Sciences. While attending university, she would meet her husband through a friend, though he would soon leave for the United States to study journalism. In time, she later joined him in America, where she found work as a full-time babysitter, learning English by watching television and reading the Bible while her infant clients were asleep. After only six months, Joy’s English had improved considerably, and in 1980, she enrolled into a graduate program at UCLA to continue her studies in Library Sciences. During her studies, Joy had her first child, and after graduation she began working as a cataloger at the University of Southern California’s East Asian Library. She was soon named the Curator of USC’s Korean Heritage Library, and under her stewardship, the KHL transformed into one of the premier Korean Studies libraries in North America. Joy’s service to the academic community continued to flourish in her role as the President of the Council on East Asian Libraries—the first Asian woman to hold the appointment. Her work was nationally recognized in 2011 when she awarded the National Order of Civil Merit by the President of South Korea. And, of course, Joy has been instrumental in the archival process of Korean American Story’s Legacy Project within USC’s Korean American Digital Archive. Now retired, Joy enjoys reading, foraging, and spending time with her two daughters.

Rosa Chang (Full)
Interviewee: Chang, Rosa
Rosa Chang, known in Korean as Jang Kyung-Hye, is the Executive Director and co-founder of the Korean American Special Education Center (KASEC), a nonprofit serving Los Angeles and Orange County. Born and raised in Seoul, South Korea, she credits her father with instilling in her two guiding principles that shaped her life’s work: that women can pursue any path regardless of gender, and that one should never live solely for oneself. Inspired by these lessons, Rosa studied psychology in college, where she first encountered clinical psychology and the study of autism and developmental disabilities. At the time in Korea, disabilities were heavily stigmatized—children with such diagnoses were rarely seen in public, and formal education on the subject was limited. Determined to expand her knowledge and serve those marginalized by stigma, Rosa came to the United States for further study. In 2000, alongside behavioral clinicians and social workers, she established KASEC to address the lack of culturally and linguistically accessible services for Korean American families. Many parents, limited by language barriers, either did not know their child had a developmental disorder or struggled to navigate care systems. KASEC became a bridge for these families, offering screenings, therapies, educational support, and a community in which parents could share both struggles and small victories. Rosa recalls moments of joy when parents cried together over incremental improvements in their children, underscoring the emotional weight of KASEC’s mission. Her advocacy was tested during a tragedy in 2015, when a student named Paul died after being left in a locked school bus on an extremely hot day. Paul’s inability to communicate his distress highlighted systemic failures, galvanizing Rosa and the community to push for reform. Their efforts led to the passage of the Paul Lee School Bus Safety Law, requiring safety devices on buses to ensure drivers check for students before locking vehicles. Reflecting on the slow pace of change, Rosa has likened her efforts to throwing eggs at a rock: progress often seems impossible, but over time, stains accumulate and change begins to take shape. Over 25 years, she has witnessed a gradual increase in awareness and empathy surrounding developmental disabilities within the Korean American community, though she emphasizes that ongoing support—particularly financial resources—remains critical to sustaining KASEC’s programs and professional staff. Her work has been sustained by her family’s sacrifices. Rosa’s husband carried the financial burdens of their household so she could dedicate herself fully to the cause, and her daughter, now studying neuroscience, grew up inspired by her mother’s work with children with autism. Rosa credits her family’s quiet support as the foundation that enabled her to persevere.Today, Rosa hopes to be remembered as a trailblazer—someone who carved a path through uncharted terrain to make space for others. For her, the core lesson remains simple yet profound: no one lives in the world alone. Her vision is of a community bound by empathy and mutual responsibility, where individuals with developmental disabilities and their families are supported, respected, and empowered to thrive.

Rosa Chang (Edited)
Interviewee: Chang, Rosa
Now more than ever, it’s crucial that we come together to support organizations in our community in need. As a fellow nonprofit, we believe in uplifting one another. We encourage you to learn more about KASEC and consider supporting their vital work.

Today, we’re highlighting the Korean American Special Education Center (KASEC), an incredible non-profit based in Los Angeles and Orange County which provides a wide range of integrative services and resources for individuals with developmental disabilities, from pediatric screenings and behavioral counseling to special education and community empowerment programs.

Rosa Chang, whose Korean name is Jang Kyung-Hye, serves as the Executive Director of Korean American Special Education Center (KASEC).

Born in Seoul, South Korea, Rosa tells us that her work was inspired by values instilled in her by her father: that gender shouldn’t limit one’s aspirations, and that one should never live solely for oneself. After studying psychology in college, she pursued further studies in the United States, as there was a dearth in the acceptance of, and education on, developmental disabilities in Korea. In 2000, Rosa co-founded KASEC with behavioral clinicians and social workers to serve Korean Americans in Los Angeles with developmental disabilities who did not have access to language-specific and culturally-sensitive care.

Over the past 25 years, Rosa shares that the community’s knowledge and interest in developmental disabilities has changed, albeit slowly; at times, she tells us that she feels as though she is throwing eggs at a rock, hoping it will break. Explaining that both children with developmental disabilities, as well as their parents, have a diverse set of needs, Rosa emphasizes that KASEC’s work is reliant on community empathy, awareness, and financial support. “Even if the rock doesn’t break,” she says, “we can at least leave a stain and bring about change.”

Kevin Chang (Full)
Interviewee: Chang, Rosa
Kevin Chang, 27, is a dedicated community ambassador for the Korean American Special Education Center (KASEC) and serves as a program coordinator for Asians and Pacific Islanders with Disabilities of California. Born and raised in La Palma, California, Kevin grew up navigating life with an autism spectrum diagnosis. He recalls being placed in special education classrooms until the 5th grade and struggling to feel a sense of belonging among peers during elementary and middle school. Yet, even when socializing felt difficult, Kevin always valued the idea of community and the importance of having spaces where everyone could feel included. A graduate of CSU Fullerton, where he fondly remembers his senior literature seminar, Kevin has since dedicated himself to creating environments that welcome and empower people with disabilities. His work spans multiple roles—coordinating programs that foster job readiness and social skills, volunteering at food banks, and helping individuals build connections with one another. Music has been a central thread in Kevin’s life. He began playing piano in second grade and clarinet in fifth, later joining marching band in high school. It was through his music that he first connected with KASEC, when he was invited to perform at a charity event. That performance led him to help grow KASEC’s Harmonia Ensemble from a small group of five musicians into a vibrant community orchestra with strings, woodwinds, and percussion. For Kevin, both music and community work embody the same lesson: when everyone contributes, something beautiful is created together. Known for his patience and generosity, Kevin emphasizes that people with disabilities should not be defined solely by their diagnoses but celebrated as full participants in community life. He models this belief through his own daily practices, from mentoring younger participants to encouraging others to “take that chance, take that leap” in contributing to their communities. Reflective and hopeful, Kevin notes that if he could speak to his younger self, he would remind him to appreciate what he has and to treat everyone with kindness.

Sun Chong Kim (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Sun Chong
Sun Kim, a committed leader in both business and community service, currently serves on the Advisory Council of Family Touch, a Bergen County nonprofit dedicated to supporting immigrant families through affordable counseling and educational programs. She previously served as Board Chair for the organization and has extended her leadership to other community initiatives, including as a founding member of the CSK Charitable Foundation and as a board member of Englewood Hospital Medical Center. Professionally, she is the President of Bethel Industries, Inc., a textile firm that supplies materials under contract to the U.S. Department of Defense. Born in Seoul in 1949, Sun immigrated to the United States in 1976 with her husband, at a time when Korean emigrants were permitted to take only $500 per person abroad. With those limited funds, the young couple entered the textile industry, working diligently to establish a stable foundation while raising their two sons. She credits her resilience and achievements—both in business and in nonprofit service—to her steadfast Christian faith, which she identifies as the driving force in her life and her commitment to others. Guided by this faith, Sun has consistently sought to give back to her community. She founded scholarship programs to support students’ education, has contributed to initiatives aimed at improving the health of Korean Americans, and has offered her expertise to organizations like Family Touch. Having both experienced and witnessed the cultural, psychological, and intergenerational challenges faced by Korean immigrant families, she regards Family Touch’s mission as deeply personal and vital. Her conviction that “when families are healthy, societies will be healthy” grounds her service. She recalls witnessing the transformative power of counseling: from repairing strained relationships between relatives to guiding families through the hardships of divorce. These moments reaffirm her belief in the beauty of life’s journey—a belief she often frames through Thornton Wilder’s Our Town, which reminds us that too often we fail to realize life’s beauty until it has passed. For Sun, supporting families in times of struggle ensures that they not only endure challenges but also rediscover strength, dignity, and hope along the way.

Suki Park & Lauren Yoo (Full)
Interviewee: Park, Suki
Suki Park is a Korean-born mother whose experience of migration, marriage, and single parenthood illuminates the quiet struggles many immigrant families face—and raises the quiet possibilities for healing. Raised in Samcheonpo (Sacheon City) in South Gyeongsang Province with two older brothers and her grandparents, Suki moved to Seoul for work after college, where she met and quickly married the man who would become the father of her children. After only a brief courtship—three meetings—she followed him to the United States. The marriage eventually ended in divorce, and Suki found herself navigating life in an unfamiliar country while raising two young daughters. Confronted with language barriers, cultural displacement, and the practical burdens of single parenthood, Suki describes feeling overwhelmed and apologetic—sorrowful that she could not create the harmonious family she had wanted for her children. Through family counseling at Family Touch, Suki discovered that talking with an empathetic, nonjudgmental professional offered relief and new tools for relating to herself and her children. Counseling reshaped her understanding of help: what she once thought of as something only for “when everything is broken” became a place to learn, reflect, and interrupt harmful patterns. A central insight for Suki was the conscious refusal to pass down generational trauma; drawing on the contrast between her own upbringing in a small Korean fishing town and the vast anonymity of life in the U.S., she learned ways to parent differently—deliberately cultivating emotional openness and repair rather than repeating the strict or silent scripts of earlier generations. Her conversations with her daughter Lauren are heartfelt and candid: Suki offers apologies for the hardships they endured, acknowledges the limits of what she could provide in a new country, and affirms shared growth. She frames the family’s journey with a sober, humane question—“Everyone has times when life flows in directions they want or don’t want… but would I be the only one feeling this way?”—inviting empathy and the recognition that struggle is often collective, not solitary. Suki’s story underscores how accessible, culturally-sensitive counseling can create space for reconciliation, intergenerational learning, and renewed family bonds—transforming remorse into repair and isolation into a shared path forward.

Sammy Kim & SNIX (Full)
Interviewee: Kim, Sammy
Sammy and Jiwon’s friendship began in the energetic nightlife of Brooklyn, where their natural chemistry quickly turned casual encounters into something deeper. What started as evenings spent together in clubs, laughter spilling into late nights, grew into a connection that blurred the line between work and play. Their synergy extended into creative collaborations, where projects like Sammy’s K-pop party bias and community spaces like KISS became outlets for their shared vision: building environments where Korean Americans, queer communities, and their allies could gather, celebrate, and heal. Though they sometimes joke about spending “too much time together,” both describe their relationship as timeless, filled with the joy of feeling like kids again. Jiwon, whose family did not always support her journey into womanhood, reflects that she feels most at peace with Sammy—whether inside a club or standing together outside after being kicked out. Their companionship has become a space of safety and affirmation, where vulnerability and acceptance thrive. Frequently asked whether they are “dating,” Sammy and Jiwon reject the idea that their bond must be legible through conventional relationship categories. Instead, they view their love for one another as proof that deep care need not seek validation from social constructs like marriage or traditional partnerships. Their friendship demonstrates that intimacy, commitment, and joy can flourish outside of binaries, existing instead within chosen family and community. Travel companions, best friends, and collaborators, Sammy and Jiwon frame their work and their relationship as part of a broader responsibility: to model alternative ways of loving, organizing, and building community. From shared meals in Koreatown to performances on stage, from nightlife to activism, they highlight how collective care, storytelling, and friendship itself can become tools of liberation. Looking forward, they hope that future generations will embrace a more expansive understanding of love and family—one that celebrates relationships in all their color, variety, and possibility.

Sammy and Jiwon first crossed paths in Brooklyn nightlife, leaving deep impressions on one another: radiant confidence, an ability to light up a room, and joy that was infectious. From bowls of soondubu in Koreatown to performing at “bias,” a K-pop party founded by Sammy Kim to spotlight Asian talent, their bond shifted from casual encounters into collaboration. Together, they helped grow spaces like bias and KISS into platforms where Korean Americans and queer communities could gather, celebrate, and heal. Both see their shared journey as part of a larger responsibility: to model alternative ways of being, loving, and organizing for those who come after them. The Son Family (Full)
Interviewee: Son, Diana
Diana Son—acclaimed playwright, television writer, and mother—sits down with her three sons, Nate, Dash, and Wilder, for an intimate conversation about family, love, and joy. At the heart of their discussion is Diana’s relationship with her partner, Casey, and how her journey of embracing queer love has intersected with her children’s lives as Korean Americans. When asked about their friends’ reactions to Casey, the boys respond with humor and ease. Nate notes that his friends never remarked on it, while Dash shares with a grin that his friends found it “iconic.” Wilder explains that in their New York circles, alternative family structures are not only accepted but normalized—reflecting a generational shift in attitudes toward love, relationships, and queerness. What might have sparked shock or disapproval in the past now feels natural, even celebrated. For Diana, this ease is both personal and communal. She reflects on how important it feels to share with the Korean American community that her relationship has been “not an evil thing, not a disruptive thing, not an unholy thing, but a joyful thing.” Her sons echo this affirmation, describing how meaningful it has been to witness their mother’s happiness and to take part in that joy. Wilder emphasizes how special it feels to be included in her journey, while Nate underscores that love means supporting the choices that bring happiness to those we care about. Together, their dialogue reveals the resilience of love across generations and the power of family to adapt, grow, and expand. Diana’s children recognize her relationship not as a rupture but as an enrichment of their family life. For Diana, the greatest joy lies in seeing her sons embrace her happiness as their own. By inviting them into this conversation, she highlights how queer joy can ripple outward—reshaping understandings of family, modeling acceptance, and affirming that joy shared is joy multiplied.

The Bailar Family (Full)
Interviewee: Bailar, Schuyler
Schuyler Bailar—activist, entrepreneur, and swimmer who made history as the first transgender man to compete openly in NCAA Division I swimming—joins his parents, Terry Hong and Gregor Bailar, for a conversation on family, identity, and advocacy. Together, they reflect on the journey of coming out, navigating public visibility, and shaping a family culture rooted in love, patience, and growth. Schuyler recalls that in college he resisted the label of “advocate,” preferring instead to describe himself as a storyteller: someone who shared personal experiences with the hope that others might find permission to embrace their own. What began as self-expression gradually became advocacy, a process his father identifies as rooted in agency—the courage to claim one’s own life path, whether in facing down an eating disorder, remaining committed to the swim team, or later embracing gender transition. For Schuyler, the act of making vulnerability public became its own form of leadership, one that inspired others even before he recognized it as advocacy. His parents, in turn, share their pride in watching their son transform his personal courage into a platform that has impacted countless lives. Family, they emphasize, has been central to this journey. The Bailars describe their guiding values as love and growth, resisting stagnation and instead striving to update tradition while remaining grounded in cultural heritage. For Schuyler, this grounding meant that home was always a safe place: he was never told he was “too much” or “not enough,” never asked to mute the complexities of being a biracial, transgender Korean American man. This sense of belonging allowed him to ask for what he needed, to invite his parents into his journey, and to trust that their patience would meet his vulnerability. Schuyler reflects on how unusual this safety is, noting that many young people he meets feel that their core selves are inadequate—something he never experienced at home. The Bailars acknowledge the road was not without missteps. Schuyler’s coming out first as gay, and later as transgender, brought challenges as well as learning curves. His parents “fumbled” through the process, taking cues from him while navigating their own uncertainties. Media attention following Schuyler’s transition amplified both opportunities and pressures, and yet through the turbulence, their family’s center held. Even extended relatives, including Schuyler’s grandmother—whose views had once seemed rigid—shifted in surprising ways, reminding them all of the possibility of change over time. Looking back, Schuyler sees the courage, patience, and love within his family as a rare gift, one that enabled him to step fully into himself. For the Bailars, their story is not only about one child’s historic journey but about a family’s ongoing commitment to embrace growth, challenge norms, and remain steadfast in love.
Keywords: Schuyler Bailar; Terry Hong; Gregor Bailar; transgender; Korean American; queer identity; Division I swimming; Harvard; firsts; activism; advocacy; storytelling; agency; courage; vulnerability; mixed race identity; family values; growth; tradition; love; patience; safe home; acceptance; media visibility; intergenerational change; resilience
